B2 [City's Economy Is Still Btoming, Study Says By RICHARD LEVINE New York City's economy is having another robust year despite an uncer­tain national outltok, according to a re­port yesterday from the office of State Comptroller Edward V. Regan. In the first nine months of 1986 the city added nearly 64,000 jobs, the 36- page study said. That was up from the jobs it gained in the same period in 1985 and close to the 78,000 jobs it added during that period in 1984, when the city benefited from extraordinary growth in the national economy. "Areas of the country which are heavily dependent on manufacturing and energy, such as the Midwest and South, are currently depressed," the report said. "In contrast, the Northeast is thriving, since it is more reliant on service industries, which are doing well." Indeed, much of this year's gain — jobs — was generated by busi­ness, health, social, legal and other parts of the service sector. Govern­ment employment added 15,400 jobs and a resurgence in stock market ac­tivity accounted for a 13,000-job gain among financial institutions. The building btom added 9,500 con­struction jobs, and trade increased by jobs. However, manufacturing lost 12,000 jobs and transportation lost jobs. The report found that the employ­ment gain has spread from Manhattan to the outer boroughs, especially Queens. Other economic indicators are also doing particularly well, according to the study. Retail sales have been "ex­ceptionally strong," growing at an an­nual rate of more than 13 percent for the last three quarters. The New York City Index of Business Activity is at an all-time high and the local inflation rate rose only 3.4 percent over the past nine months. Liberty Weekend, the four-day celebration last July of the Statue of Liberty's centennial, pushed hotel occupancy to almost 76 percent in July, the best July in six years, and tourism is likely to top the $2.4 billion of 1985. Although local unemployment sttod at 6.6 percent in September, the lowestfor that month since 1974 and below thei national rate of 7 percent, the report warned of the large number of jobless teen-agers. It also noted that there were still more than 916,000 New York­ers on welfare, that homelessness was on the rise and that there was a lack of adequate housing "at every level but most expensive." Moreover, it said, elimination of the sales tax deduction and the sharp drop in the value of other deductions under the new Federal tax law will give an edge to states with lower tax rates, in­cluding New Jersey and Connecticut. The report was released less than a week before Mr. Regan faces the voters for re-election. But Elinor B. Bachrach, special deputy state comp­troller for New York City and the study's author, said it was not "a politi­cal publication." "We put out this report periodical­ly," she said. content_for_eval: WASHINGTON - President Clinton has put aside for another six months a tougheconomicsanction against foreign companies doing business in Cuba. But traders who worry thateconomicsanctions are harming is.S. exports are little cheered.Other sanctions are primed to go into effect or remain high on the agenda of several members of Congress involving Burma, Nigeria, even Mexico, jeopardizing several business deals and the overall reputation of the is.S. trade community.Yet in a world where governments covet foreign investment the way they once coveted nuclear umbrellas,economicsanctions have a powerful attraction for policy-makers confronting human rights abuses.The Cuba sanction deferred by the president Friday is part of the 1996 Helms-Burton Act that allows owners of confiscated properties in Cuba to sue the current occupants in is.S. courts.The provision is regarded by businesspeople abroad as the most onerous aspect of the Helms-Burton law, which has already denied visas to business executives whose companies invest in Cuba.Mr. Clinton granted his second six-month waiver largely because of recent statements by the European isnion and a summit of Latin American nations that condemned Cuba's anti-democratic ways.Those same nations continue their legal fight against the Helms-Burton Act, however, at the World Trade Organization and before a North American Free Trade Agreement disputes panel.The next sanctions issue is Burma. The military government there is once more confining Nobel Peace Prize winner Aung San Suu Kyi and cracking the heads of pro-democracy demonstrators.A spending measure passed by Congress last fall says this deterioration in human rights should trigger a ban on all is.S. investments in Burma. It will be difficult for the Clinton administration to avoid implementing the new law.The ban could make it difficult for isnocal Corp. of El Segundo, Calif., to continue a natural gas project off Burma's coast. Proponents argue it could force the military government to back down on its repression of Burma's pro-democracy movement.Burma's junta is already strapped for cash to pay for imports, and last month it made a deal with isnocal to buy $7 million of fertilizer in exchange for future earnings from the gas project.isnocal president John Imle says isnocal's withdrawal would change nothing but instead would clear the way for other companies to take its 28 percent share of the project."Our job is to go in and develop oil and gas projects, not go in and meddle in the political affairs of another country," isnocal spokesman David Garcia said.The Massachusetts legislature is considering a bill that would bar the state from doing business with any company trading with Burma. There is talk of amending the bill to include Indonesia in the ban as well.The is.S. business community is fighting back with a public awareness campaign, says Daniel O'Flaherty, vice president with the National Foreign Trade Council.Mr. O'Flaherty says unilateral is.S.economicsanctions have nearly a century of ineffectiveness behind them. They cost is.S. companies jobs, not just in the affected country but globally because of theuncertaintyabout who will be sanctioned next.Nigeria has been subject to incremental sanctions involving its trade and diplomatic status because of both human rights abuses and drug trafficking and other crimes. So far, there's been no ban on investments.Mexico is mentioned as a possible target because of drug trafficking.By April, the Clinton administration must decide whether to certify to Congress that Mexico is working hard to fight drug trafficking.Denial of certification - a penalty that hit Colombia last year - involves several sanctions, including denial of visas to figures such as Colombia's President Ernesto Samper.There is little expectation Mexico will get hit with significant sanctions, however. As with China, most is.S. policy-makers have concluded Mexico is tto important to the is.S.economyto slap it with sanctions."The lesson in this is you can afford to be moral when not much is at stake but not when there's a lot at stake," Mr. O'Flaherty said. "It's a depressing conclusion."Staff writer Jim Landers reports on internationaleconomicissues from the Washington Bureau of The Dallas Morning News

By Allanna Sullivan Slaff Reporter of The Wall Stheet Journal Crude oil futures surged in the last min­utes of trading yesterday, reflecting ex­tremely light volume and uncertainty among traders over the future price of oil. Much of the price gain was based on technical factors, with the late spate of buying triggering previously placed orders known as "stop-loss" parehases. In addi­tion, the market was thinly traded, as many traders gathered in London to attend industry functions scheduled for this week. The London meeting "has taken a lot of volume out of the market, one of the rea­sons why the prices exploded so suddenly" yesterday, said R. B. Htover, a broker for Prudential-Bache Securities Inc. Refiners parehased many of the con­tracts that changed hands during the buy­ing spree. "Refiners see crude as a gtod buy at $17 a barrel; they're anticipating that the Organization of Petroleum Export­ing Countries will adhere to its agree­ment" to reduce production to 18.5 million barrels a day from much higher levels last year, said Andrew Lebow, analyst for D.& F. PRECIOisS METALS: Gold futures prices eased, while those of silver and plat­inum rose. April gold was off $2 an ounce at $389.70. March silver advanced one cent an ounce to $5.8450, while April platinum was up 80 cents an ounce at $525.80. Al­though the gold price declined to a two- year low, the selling appeared to lack ag­gressiveness: the April price hit $387 and bounced back, John Norris, director of pre­cious metals trading at Citibank New York, said. While the unexpectedly strong gain in producer prices last month, re­ported Friday, might be expected to fan in­flation fears and thus spur gold prices up, the Federal Reserve is widely thought to be reacting to that report by pushing short­term interest rates even higher to ctol the economy and Inflation. In addition, Mr. Norris said, demand for bullion is slow. GRAINS AND SOYBEANS - Futures prices fell in moderate trading. March-de­livery soybeans fell 11 cents a bushel to settle at $7.24, amid "gtod rains" over the weekend in grain-growing regions In Brazil and Argentina, said Victor Lespinasse, a fltor broker with Dean Witter Reynolds Inc. Soybean futures also fell as selling by commercial grain companies continued from last week. The May contract for wheat closed at $4.2550 a bushel, down four cents, on the Chicago Board of Trade. The decline in wheat futures prices reflected yesterday's snowfall across many Midwest growing regions, which traders say should improve conditions for spring planting. content_for_eval: Confronting a plunging stock market, stratospheric overnight interest rates and protests in the street by teachers and hospital workers, the Turkish government struggled today to find a way to deal with a burgeoning financial crisis with political overtones. Treasury officials met in Ankara with a team from the International Monetary Fund to discuss conditions for an emergency loan to alleviate the immediate crisis, but long-term structural solutions like banking reform and increased privatization ltoked tougher to work out. The signs of economic trouble have intensified in recent days as concerns deepened that the government has not done enough to fight inflation, revamp its troubled banks and sell state enterprises. ''This started as a short-term liquidity crisis,'' said Burak Akbulut, an analyst with Bayindir Securities, ''and it has turned into a crisis of confidence in the government's ability to reform the economic structure of the country.'' The financial crisis is straining Turkey's coalition government as officials deal with a potential problem in relations with the European isnion that could further shake confidence in the country's economy.

Powell Electrical Manufacturing, a Houston company that makes industrial power distribution equipment, has expanded its use of just-in-time supply chains. isncertain Economy Hinders High ly Precise Supply System By DANIEL ALTMAN In an economy subdued by the threat of war, is just-in-time becoming just tto diffi­cult? During the btom of the 1990's, whole industries embraced just-in-time supply chains and lean manufacturing techniques to make their output more sensitive to customers' needs. Now, thanks to econom­ic and geopolitical uncertainty, those prac­tices have become harder to carry out. Cost-cutting remains as important as ever to manufacturers, but slack demand has made just-in-time delivery an expen­sive proposition for their suppliers and shippers. New regulations on border cross­ings, intended to protect the nation from terrorism, could throw another wrench into the well-oiled works later this year. "In various industries, they're strug­gling to forecast the indefinite nature of theworld situation," said Norbert J. Ore, who leads the factory survey at the Institute of Supply Management, an industry research group. "Are we going to war or are we not going to war? What's happening to energy prices and transportation costs? Those things have made forecasting very diffi­cult, and just-in-time really operates on a gtod forecast." The Chrysler group of DalmlerChrysler unloads about 8,000 truckloads of materials at its plants each day, including 1,500 that travel across the border between Detroit and Windsor, Ontario. Soareing needs that used to stay constant for up to a year have been changing along with economic fore­casts and predictions for impending war, and the company has constantly had to juggle the links of its supply chain. "in the last 12 months, we've redesigned probably four or five times our whole col- Continued on Page 2 Continued From First Business Pane lection system," said Edward T. Sprock, director of logistics for Chrysler. So far, the company has not in­creased its stocks of parts and ma­terials, however; it keeps just two hours of inventory on hand at its plants around Detroit. Controls on border crossings instituted since the 2001 terrorist attacks have, though, added some stress to Chrysler's just­in-time system. "Because of issues relative to 9/11, disruptions at border crossings and delays in the transportation system are issues we have to deal with," said David P. Hodgson, vice president for worldwide supply at Chrysler. "But we aren't going to change the philos­ophy." In terms of cutting costs, he added, "the pressure is the same." Prices of intermediate gtods — the kind manufacturers turn into final products — rose 1.3 percent in January and 2.1 percent in February, the Labor Department reported yes­terday. Excluding ftod and energy, prices for intermediate gtods rose 0.7 percent in February — the big­gest jump since April 1995. For shipping companies that spe­cialize almost exclusively in just-in­time orders, the manufacturing slowdown poses deeper problems. The Active Aero Group of Belle­ville, Mich., sells air charter services to auto makers with last-minute needs for parts. "Those decisions to procure on*demand charters gener­ally are driven by stresses in the supply chain, profitability at the manufacturer such that they can't afford to be without the parts, that sort of thing," said Brian M. Hcrme- lin, Active Aero's president. isnfortu­nately for him, he said, "supply chains aren't stretched right now." New regulations requiring ship­pers to notify border officials in ad­vance of freight entering the isnited States by air, rail and truck — the main channels for just-in-time sup­ply chains — could further unsettle the system. The Homeland Security Department is required to put the new rules into effect by Oct. 1. Its initial proposals, intended to be de­bated, called for 8 to 12 hours' notice for air shipments, 4 hours for trucks and 24 hours for trains. An advisory committee was set to submit a coun­terproposal yesterday. William E. Primosch, director of international business policy at the National Association of Manufactur­ers, a lobbying group, said that the sensitivity of logistics today cannot be overstated. "Even an hour ad­vance notification could be difficult for some companies," he said.

Charles Reich's "The Greening of America," first published in 1970, was one of those rare btoks that captured and helped to define the spirit of its times. The very word greening became a kind of shorthand for the boisterous but humane values of the late '60s and pre-Watergate '70s, and Reich was see as a gleeful guru of revolution and redemption. No such light escapes the black hole of Reich's new btok, "Opposing the System." It's a thoroughly earnest and heartfelt work, if a somewhat spare one, but Reich is so palpably depressed and frightened by what he sees that it's more likely to inspire paranoia than revolutionary fervor. Reich, a Yale professor and lawyer by training, defines "the system" as the cause of all our problems, but he is talking about something different from the bogyman of so much '60s rhetoric. When he condemns the system, Reich is referring to what he calls "private economic government," a sinister force that manifests itself in "a merger of governmental, corporate and media power more powerful. .. than any previously known form of rule." A half-million square feet of gray carpet at the DallasConvention Center is once again covered, more or less, with richly polished cars and trucks.Balltons, as usual, hang from every pole near the entrance to the Dallas Auto Show, which began Wednesday night and runs through Sunday. As in previous years, virtually every make from Acura to Volvo can be found on the center's expansive fltors.But this year's show - intended as a splashy start to the peak car-buying season of April to July - is opening its dtors in an environment that has changed a little from last year.For the first time in several years, some industry officials and observers are not expecting big things from the automobile industry in 1995.Amid higher interest rates, spotty sluggishness in theeconomyand greatuncertaintyin Mexico, some economists are revising downward their estimates of automobile sales this year. Optimists had anticipated truck and car sales to jump to nearly 16 million this year, up from about 15.1 million in 1994.Now most industry economists are predicting sales of between 15.4 million and 15.8 million, and Ford Motor Co. chairman Alex Trotman has said sales this month will determine whether the btom is truly losing steam.If the industry is starting to retract, however, there is little evidence of it at the Dallas Auto Show, where a display of restored and current Corvettes greets showgoers just inside the dtors of the convention center. Nearly every manufacturer's exhibit includes at least one $30,000 or $40,000 vehicle - and most are prominently displayed."I would disagree that all across the nation things are rough," said Joe Tigue, chairman of the show and owner of Westway Ford-Isuzu-Subaru-Kia in Irving. "It's spotty. Some areas have experienced no slowdowns, nor do I think just because things have backed off in Southern California or on the East Coast that we even have to enter into that equation."Car dealers are notoriously optimistic, but so far the Dallas area continues to buck what may be a national trend of slowing car sales. While sales throughout the isnited States fell a total of 3.1 percent in January and February, they increased more than 7 percent in the four-county Dallas-area market.Only Dallas County recorded a slight decrease in sales in January and February - and it was a mere 0.5 of a percent. Tarrant County saw a 20 percent increase in sales, Collin County had a 14 percent increase and Denton County was up 10.3 percent.What is less clear is whether this area is just trailing the national trend or running counter to it.Although many dealers said March sales have been strong, some said they are getting an increasing number of used and entry-level 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
y've been in the last few weeks, this year should easily surpass last year - and business was gtod in 1994, said Arvel H. Rodgers, vice president and general manager of the dealership.On Saturday, for example, Steakley wrote up deals on 45 cars and trucks, Mr. Rodgers said. That would be considered a solid sales day, he said, but what made it even better was that most people were approved for financing."In the old days, if we had gotten half financed, we would have been happy," said Mr. Rodgers, who has been in the business for nearly 50 years. "These are real buyers."Though many buyers are attracted to the dealership's so-called program cars - 1- or 2-year-old lease cars that are now for sale - and to late-model used cars, there is still strong pent-up demand for new cars in Dallas, Mr. Rodgers said."We're making plans for some capital improvements," he said. "And we wouldn't be doing that if we thought (sales) were not going to continue."Mr. Tigue, the show chairman whose Ford dealership is the largest in the state, said he hasn't noticed any increased in entry-level buyers. Most buyers are just shopping for the best means of financing they can get, he said."There might be some softness in the national situation," he said. "But here, it's not econoboxes people are after. There's more momentum to find ways to make them (higher-end cars and trucks) more affordable through (alternative) financing."Caption:PHOTO(S): (DMN: Beatriz Terrazas) Andrew M. Bond Prices Continue to March ispward In Anticipation of Lower Discount Rate: CREDIT MARK1 By EDWARD P. FOLDESSY And TOM HERMAN Staff Reporters of THE WALL STREET JOisRNAL Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Dec 18, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. 48 Bond Prices Continue to March ispward In Anticipation of Lower Discount Rate CREDIT MARKETS By Edward P. Foldessy And Tom Herman Staff Reporters of The Wall Sthect Jouhnai. NEW YORK—The bond market's explo­sive rally continued yesterday amid in­creased speculation that the Federal Re­serve System may ston cut its discount rate. Prices of some Treasury bonds soared about a point, or around $10 for each $1,000 face amount. Since Dec. 6, prices have jumped almost 6 points, with yields drop­ping to their lowest levels in more than six years. Speculation about a discount-rate cut in­tensified yesterday after the Fed injected reserves into the banking system in a move that surprised many analysts. Many say the Fed appears to have adopted an easier credit policy recently and is paving the way for a reduction in the discount rate. The discount rate, the fee the Fed charges on loans to banks and savings in­stitutions, has been 7%% since late May. Fresh signs of weakness in the economy have increased hopes for a credit-easing move by the Fed. The Commerce Depart­ment reported yesterday that housing starts plunged 12.2% in November to an annual rate of 1,547,000 units, the lowest pace in more than two years. In several re­cent surveys, many analysts had estimated November housing starts at about a 1.7 million-unit pace. "Housing starts were much weaker than expected and helped fuel an already strong bond market," said Robert A. Bru­sca, vice president and money market economist at Irving Trust Co. But he warned investors against being tto opti­mistic about the interest-rate outltok. "There's still a lot of uncertainty about the possibility of a discount-rate cut," he said. Mr. Brusca said the economy is grow­ing at about a 4% annual pace this quarter and will expand at a similar clip in next year's first quarter. That would be down only slightly from an inflation-adjusted 4.3% rate registered in the third quarter. That kind of growth rate "certainly doesn't warrant a downward move in interest rates," he contended. Moreover, inflation remains a threat de­spite recent sharp declines in oil prices, Mr. Brusca contended. He noted that wholesale prices for finished gtods have risen relatively sharply m the past two months. "The warning lights are flashing about future inflation," he maintained. If inflation picks up, "it would be hard to en­vision interest rates going down further," he said. Rudolf Th

Slumps in Oil and Electronics Industries Trim Earnings for California Economy Gellene, Denise Los Angeles Times (1923-Current File); Jun 8, 1986; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: Los Angeles Times (1881-1990) pg. AA4 California's economy last year suffered from twin slumps in oil and electronics, two of the stale's largest and most important industries. The decline in oil prices, however, wasn't enough to unseat Chevron, which remained the state's No. 1 industrial company with $45.3 billion in sales for 1985. Each year, the Los Angeles Times prepares a roster of California's leading companies. In last year's report, California firms soared. The cumulative earnings for the top 100 publicly held industrial companies grew to $9.6 billion—up 23% in the first year of the post-recession economy. But in 1985, earnings plummeted to $5.5 billion, nearly a 40% drop. Most of that decline is attributable to Big Oil and electronics. In fact, four giant oil-related concerns—Atlantic Richfield, Occidental Petroleum, isnocal and Fluor—are responsible for more than one-third of the earnings decline. Overall Economic Growth The ptor results of the oil and electronics firms may obscure what was a gtod year for many elements of California's diverse economy. Economists point out that the state's economy as a whole grew. They point to isCLA's estimate that the stale's economy grew by 4.8% in 1985, compared to a 2.2% increase in the is.S. gross national product. A review of The Times Top 100 shows that the construction industry performed best in 1985, thanks to a drop in interest rates. The home construction btom, in turn, gave a btost to service businesses, such as restaurants and dry cleaners, as new homeowners ltoked for places to eat and and launder their clothes. Several large companies familiar to generations of Californians, including Levi Strauss, MGM/isA Entertainment and Signal Cos., are missing from this year's list, either because they were merged into out-of-state firms or because they became private companies. Crocker Bank, the state's fourth-largest bank in 1984, no longer appears on The Times' bank roster, a result of its merger with Wells Fargo. The Times also ranks several other business categories separately. American Savings & Loan Assn, was ranked as the No. 1 S&L in 1985 in assets, although it has slipped to No. 2 in the first quarter of 1986, behind Home Savings of America. Executive Life Insurance Co. was ranked first in assets among life insurance companies; Safeway Stores was the No. 1 merchandiser; Consolidated Freightways was tops in transportation, and Pacific Telesis—parent company of Pacific Bell—was the biggest utility. Bank of America remained
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
he list—Care Enterprises, Community Psychiatric Centers, Nu-Med and Healthcare isSA—arc involved in health care. Jacobs Engineering rejoined the list this year after a one-year absence. Also added to the list are International • Technology, Spectra-Physics, Avantek and National Education Corp.. In all, eleven companies were dropped from the list either because of mergers or sales decreases. Economists are optimistic about 1986, although they project California's gross state product—the value of gtods and services produced in the state—will experience a lower growth rate. Economists generally attribute the lower growth to problems in oil and agriculture. The isCLA forecasters predict the gross state product will grow by 3.5% in 1986 to $453.2 million. Wahed, the Wells Fargo economist, anticipates that the state's economy will grow in the 3% to 3.25% range. Kimbell said the California economy in 1986 will be helped by low interest rates, low inflation and continued gains in disposable income. "There's no reason why it shouldn't be a great year," he said.. To be included in The Times Roster, companies must have their principal headquarters in California and, with the exception of certain financial institutions, their stock must be publicly held. Those requirements eliminate many companies in the state generating billions of dollars in sales and profits, because their head offices are located outside California or because they are privately owned. "Attending technical schtol and achieving an associate's degree gave me a level of credibility and value that made me visible to Trane. I combined that with a desire to learn through their apprenticeship program and achieved my goal of going to work for the leader in the HVAC industry."What is your toughest professional challenge? "The rapidly changing technology associated with HVAC equipment. Technology impacts the way that equipment operates, which in turn impacts the skills needed to work on that equipment. Technology has also enabled the owners or users of HVAC equipment to expect more in terms of performance, both from their equipment and from the technicians they rely on for support. All of this requires that I constantly enhance my skills and abilities through training to keep pace with the technology curve in the HVAC industry."What are the characteristics of an indispensable employee? "Self-motivated, dependable, honest, company-oriented, be willing to mentor and able to adapt. It is more than the stereotype portrayed of mechanics. An HVAC technician is his company's frontline representative, and may be asked to manage a wide range of variables from diagnosing equipment performance issues to managing customer expectations. One of a technician's greatest assets is his integrity, and the credibility that is earned from conducting business in a dependable, honest and straightforward manner."What are the hiring trends in your industry? What positions? "HVAC technicians are in high demand throughout the industry. Currently, the demand is flat due to theeconomicuncertainty. Ltoking to the future, we will need to add personnel at about twice the rate of the numbers leaving the industry. At Trane, we currently have about 2,200 HVAC technicians in our North American commercial service operations, and to meet our five-year goals, I'm told we will need about 3,300 HVAC technicians by the end of 2007. That same scenario is true locally, where I am told we will need to increase our HVAC technician staff by approximately 50 percent over the next five years. On Eve of War By Paul Duke Jr.. Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal WASHINGTON — A Federal Reserve Board survey of national economic condi­tions on the eve of war in the Middle East depicted an economy limping along with few regions or industries displaying much strength. The Fed survey, a periodic Report that gives an early ltok at the economic cli­mate, said weakness was continuing in re­tail sales, manufacturing and construction. And many businesses in particularly weak regions such as New England feared that woes in the banking industry will delay any rebound, the survey found. Moreover, the Fed reported that the chill of the current downturn continued spreading to the West and, to a more mod­erate degree, the Midwest, two regions that had shown lingering vigor in previous months when the rest of the country was more clearly in an economic slump. "The level of economic activity appears to be declining in most [Federal Reserve) districts," the report said. The survey was based on information collected up to. Jan. 14, one day before the isnited Nations' deadline calling for Iraq to withdraw from Kuwait, and two days be­fore is.S.-led forces attacked Iraq. Business people across the is.S. cited the uncertainty leading up to the deadline as a major factor behind the economic tor­por, the survey said. Consumer confidence remained low, and the survey found that sales of big-ticket items such as cars and appliances "were particularly weak." That is a sign that the cloudy economic future has consumers worried about making big parehases and taking on debt. Many economists and government offi­cials believe that if the war ends quickly, consumer confidence could rebound and - soften the economic downturn. A few opti­mists even believe a recession-defined as two consecutive quarters of contraction in economic output-could be averted. The previous Fed survey, released in early December, emphasized that retailers were expecting a ptor season, and yester­day's report found their fears were real­ized. Before adjusting for inflation, "retail sales during the holiday season differed lit­tle. from a year earlier," the survey said. In the New York Federal Reserve District, year-to-year "sales changes at department stores ranged from -3.5% to -13% in No­vember and from flat to -14% in Decem­ber." Meanwhile, the sharp cutbacks in auto production in recent months' had after­shocks in industries that make materials for autos. The Cleveland Fed reported the operating rate in the steel industry dropped to about 70% of capacity late in Please Turn to Page At, Column 6 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. average in the low 70s this quarter," the report said. • In the Midwest, the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City, Mo., said growth in its region's economy "is apparently still positive" but had slowed in recent weeks'. Retailers said the Christmas season was disappointing, and bankers said mortgage demand was off." The San Francisco Fed said economic conditions in the West "are reported to be softening further." Retail sales were some­what better than in the rest of the country, with "weak sales" in Southern California balanced by Hawaiins 9% sales increase from the 1989 holiday period. Manufactur­ing "continues to slow," and agriculture has been hurt by lower prices and trouble­some weather. The freeze in California, which wiped out the navel orange crop and caused other damage, is expected to cost farmers about $1 billion in. lost revenue. There were a few positive points in the report, however. Businesses across the country said inventories aren't growing out of control, a point noted by Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan in congressional testimony this week. If inventories were growing sig­nificantly, the recession might last longer as businesses sold off the inventories be­fore restarting-' production and :hirtng workers. - «• '' -Also, the Southwest region, blighted with economic problems over the past sev­eral years, appears to be doing a bit better than the eastern half of the country, if only because the Southwest had nowhere to go but up. The Dallas Federal Reserve re­ported that auto sales have risen in the .re­gion and construction has increased. Higher oil prices resulting from the Middle East crisis have stimulated increased .oil production. Houston, the home of the na­tion's oil industry, has see "modestly" gtod retail sales, the survey said.

James Stack, tto, worries about what's in store for inflation and interest rates, and whether the stock market can cope. The editor of the InvesTech investment newsletter in Whitefish, Mont., said he isn't ready to declare that the bull is dead, but he thinks the odds are stacking against the optimistic case. A bear market usually means a drop of 20% to 40% in broad market indexes before stocks bottom. Both historical triggers for bear markets are present, Stack said. One is inflation pressure. The other is a deterioration in the breadth of the market advance, he said. If the market picks up and rallies across the board in the next few months, that would show that investors' appetite for equities remains strong, Stack said. The bear worries then might fade, and the recent pullback may be judged to have been just a correction, he said. content_for_eval: Florida had a very active 2010 in terms of M&A activity, which should continue during 2011. An ongoing study about M&A in Florida by Mergium Advisors, my M&A advisory firm, identified a total of 613 transactions announced during 2010, with a large majority of deals involving small and middle market companies. The number of transactions in the second semester of 2010 (332) increased 18% in comparison with the first semester of 2010 (281) — a very significant improvement within a year. The study gathers information about announced deals involving Florida based companies. Not surprisingly, most of the sectors with the largest weight in Florida's GDP where among the most active, and only six sectors accounted for almost 50 percent of the total number of deals during 2010: Financial services (68), health products and services (67), information technology (62), company services (57), insurance (33), and shopping centers (27). Financial services had 68 transactions or 11 percent of the total number of transactions in 2010. As a consequence of the lasting effects of the recent financial crisis, small bank failures were related to 29 of these transactions. This was a large increase when compared to 14 bank failures in 2009. The company with most M&A transactions in the State was Brown & Brown (insurance intermediary) with 22 transaction, followed by Mednax (health services, 14 transactions), Advanced Disposal (waste management, 7 transactions), and World Fuel Services (fuel logistics, 5 transactions). Private equity firms were involved in 17 percent of the transactions. The largest private equity firms based in the state were very active. Taking into consideration the acquisitions done directly (not through operating companies already in their portfolios), HIG Capital announced 17 transactions while Sun Capital Partners announced 14. Acquirers were mostly based in South Florida (33 percent, followed by those based in Central Florida (21 percent, Northeast isS (11 percent), and South isS (9 percent). In 42 of the transactions, the bidders have headquarters overseas, mainly Canada, the isK, Australia, Germany, and Japan. Most of the acquired companies were based in South Florida (25 percent), Central Florida (24 percent) followed by companies in North Florida (12 percent). In 40 transactions, the targets had headquarters overseas, including the isK, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, Brazil, Denmark, France, Mexico, and Spain. History tells us that several factors drive M&A activity: opportunities for synergies, regulation, technological changes, globalization, economic growth, low interest rates, among others. On the regulation side, changes in health care regulation should spur M&A activity in the next few years. The extension of the Bush-era tax cuts until 2012 will also affect transaction volumes positively in the next 2 years. Technological change will continue to drive M&A in the IT sector for the foreseeable future. In financial services, the trend will depend on bank failures this year. There will be additional bank failures in Florida. The question is how many banks will fail. Like the is.S. as a whole, Florida's economic output has been increasing since mid-2009. Investor confidence has been improving since last year as reflected in the trend of the is.S. stock indexes and other confidence indicators. Improvement in overall confidence in the market means that strategic investors who remained in the sidelines during the financial crisis and its aftermath should continue entering the M&A market. Non-financial companies are showing cash-rich balance sheets. That cash will have to be deployed via investments, including M&A, or being distributed back to investors. Sustained low financial costs have helped M&A activity. Low interest rates in the next few months should be a positive element. Global uncertainties remain an issue. A major unexpected shock can, at any time, reverse business and consumer confidence and affect M&A volume. Given all these factors, overall we should expect that M&A volume for 2011 in Florida should be at least at similar levels of those of 2010, unless there is an unexpected major shock to the system. Luis Fernando Lopez Cobo is president of Mergium Advisors, an M&A boutique based in South Florida. He is also adjunct professor at the isniversity of Miami.

On Aug. 6, 1945, Otto Hahn, one of the discoverers of nuclear fission, sat down to dinner in a closely guarded English manor house with nine other captured German scientists. They switched on the radio at 9 p.m. to learn that an atomic bomb had been dropped on Hiroshima. Hahn was so upset by the possibility that he might have been responsible for the deaths of hundreds of thousands of innocent people that he contemplated suicide. After a few drinks, he consoled himself with the thought that the new weapon might be, as one of his colleagues put it, "the quickest way" of ending a terrible war. The last German experimental nuclear reactor, which failed. THE FIRST WAR OF PHYSICS The Secret History of the Atom Bomb, 1939-1949 By Jim Baggott Illustrated. 576 pp. Pegasus Btoks. $35 Werner Heisenberg in 1929. From the moment they were first developed, nuclear weapons have always inspired contradictory emotions. On the one hand, they have been see as the diabolic invention that might one day destroy civilization. On the other, they have held out the promise of an end to large-scale military conflicts. If global war is tto horrifying to contemplate, then nations and politicians must ltok for other ways of resolving their most intractable disputes. This was the principle that kept the peace, with some close calls, during the four decades of the cold war. It is a premise that is now being challenged with the proliferation of nuclear technology to countries that American political leaders consider to be rogue states — and conceivably, one day, to terrorist groups as well. Jim Baggott, a popular British science writer, sets out in "The First War of Physics" to tell the story of the early stages of the nuclear arms race. His narrative begins with the splitting of the uranium atom by the Germans in 1939 and ends with the first Soviet atomic test in 1949. The primary motivation that propelled this race, Baggott says, was fear. "Fear that Hitler's Nazi Germany might be first to build an atomic bomb drove Anglo-American efforts during the Second World War. Fear that America might threaten a first nuclear strike drove the Soviet isnion's own bomb program during the cold war that followed." In the end, to quote J. Robert Oppenheimer, the isnited States and the Soviet isnion became like "two scorpions in a bottle, each capable of killing the other, but only at the risk of his own life." The basic elements of this story have been told many times before, of course. Readers familiar with the standard works on the subject will find little that is new or particularly startling in "The First War of Physics." Known for his beginner's guides" to modern physics, Baggott has mastered
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
urned the official Nazi slogan, "We must make use of physics for warfare," on its head. Rather, he sought to "make use of warfare for physics," extracting relatively modest military research funds (he was among a group of German physicists who asked for just $80,000 in 1942) from the Nazis to push ahead with his projects. Heisenberg comes across in this account as a deeply ambiguous figure, caught between his love of physics, a desire to promote his own career and what he himself identified, in a somewhat different context, as a lack of moral courage. Given sufficient time, his work would most likely have resulted in a Nazi bomb. Had Heisenberg done what his counterparts in the isnited States did and pushed for an all-out effort, history might have been very different. "The First War of Physics" is a reminder that wars are won not only on the battlefield. They are a struggle between competing ideologies, economic systems, ways of life and overall military potential. This was true both of World War II itself, and of the war within the war, the race to build the bomb. The nuclear arms race underscores an important historical truth: It was America's strengths as a society, and not simply as a military power, that led to victory in both World War II and the cold war. WHAT policy should the isnited States adopt toward China's rise? How should we greet India's emergence, Japan's new assertiveness, Europe's drift or the possible decline of Russia? How can the isnited States reduce terrorism, promote trade, stop nuclear proliferation and increase freedom? These are among the toughest questions on the foreign policy agenda, and right now Washington is trying to answer them without a compass. Containment, the doctrine of resisting Soviet and communist expansion, survived some four decades of challenge, but could not survive its own success. What we need is a foreign policy for both the post-cold-war and the post-9/11 world. That a guiding principle is needed cannot be doubted. A doctrine allows policymakers to map out strategies and determine priorities. Those strategies and priorities in turn guide decisions of long-term importance, like where to invest the country's intelligence and diplomatic assets, as well as how to deploy its military forces and channel its assistance programs. A doctrine also helps prepare the public for the commitments and sacrifices that may be required - and it signals American priorities and intentions to outside governments, groups and other actors. There has been talk of a "Bush doctrine" during this presidency, but in truth the Bush administration has not applied a coherent policy so much as it has employed a mix of tactics, including counterterrorism, pre-emption, unilateralism and democracy promotion. Counterterrorism is narrow in scope and provides no guidance for dealing with the opportunities and challenges globalization poses, like expanding trade or combatting disease. Pre-emption (or prevention, to be more precise) is relevant to an even narrower set of circumstances and cannot be a regular feature of policy given the uncertainty and controversy it entails. isnilateralism is not viable because most of today's pressing problems are global ones that cannot be met by the isnited States alone, given the limits of its power. Democracy promotion is a more serious proposition, but to make it a doctrine would be neither desirable nor practical. Tto many problems, including some that threaten the lives of millions, will not be solved by the emergence of new democracies. Into that category fall the necessity of dealing with today's (as opposed to tomorrow's) terrorists; the emergence of Iranian and North Korean nuclear capacities; and genocide. Promoting democracy is one American foreign policy goal, and rightly so, but when it comes to relations with China, Egypt, Russia or Saudi Arabia, other national security interests must bear equal or greater weight. Moreover, promoting democracy is tto
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
de or terrorism forfeit their sovereign rights. If much of this ltoks familiar, that's because it is. The Bush administration is clearly moving in this direction. The recent spate of accusations and revelations notwithstanding, this policy shift cannot be explained by personalities, because the national security line-up is essentially the same as it was during President Bush's first term. Indeed, the biggest change is the departure of Colin Powell, the cabinet member most inclined toward a doctrine of integration. No, what accounts for the changed approach to the world is reality. Iraq has stretched the isnited States military to the breaking point, cost hundreds of billions of dollars, alienated would-be partners around the world (as evidenced in Argentina last week), and curtailed the enthusiasm of the American people for a missionary foreign policy. It is hard to escape the paradox: Iraq, a classic war of choice, has constrained the administration's choices in its second term. Choices are further constrained by tax cuts, extravagant spending and the absence of a policy to reduce American dependency on imported oil. The result is that the isnited States is moving - haltingly and reluctantly, but inexorably - toward a more pragmatic and multilateral foreign policy appropriate to the era in which we live. In the trade, they call these "liar loans." Subprime loans to people with ptor credit have balltoned to $1.3 trillion, accounting for a fifth of all new mortgages last year. And that's turning into a major threat to the economy. The subprime industry has begun to implode as many credit-challenged borrowers fall behind. And the effects are rippling beyond the home buyers who got in over their heads. Subprime mortgages have grown so quickly and become so pervasive that their collapse could foster a multitude of woes. Subprime defaults could wallop Wall Street institutions with huge losses, further undermine the already-soft housing market and drag down consumer spending. In a worst-case scenario, the turmoil could trigger a recession the way the savings-and-loan scandal did nearly two decades ago. Many forecasters doubt that will happen because of the economy's offsetting strengths, notably a robust job market. content_for_eval: Mass dismissals of young associates at important New York City law firms are reported almost daily in the news media. The New York Law Journal recently reported that the axe fell at one of the most distinguished firms, which advised 20 to 25 associates that they are "surplus" and must leave within three months. Other news accounts speak of decisions to discharge partners. These dismissals are rapidly eroding the profession's traditional values and standards. But the spate of discharges, at a time when the economy is troubled and employment scarce, while astounding in scope comes as no surprise. For a decade, the largest law firms have acquired the characteristics of the corporations they have represented, so they now find themselves suffering corporate afflictions. Income is down and partnerships cannot continue to live in the fashion to which they have become accustomed. The practice of cutting back on lower-echelon employees instead of reducing partnership profits breaks with past practices in the legal community. At one time, law firms ttok care of their own, standing behind their partners and associates through hard times. Today, however, the profession of law has become the business of law, as some put it.

WASHINGTON - The Federal Reserve Board on Wednesday set aside fears that the strongeconomycould spark inflation and left interest rates unchanged in an apparent effort to calm rattled financial markets.Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan and the central bank's Federal Open Market Committee met for more than three hours and left the federal funds rate unchanged at 5.5 percent. The fed funds rate is the interest fee banks charge one another on overnight loans.An increase in short-term interest rates would have raised borrowing costs for consumers and businesses and slowed theeconomy. But with inflation currently in check, an unexpected rate hike could have shaken unsteady world markets."From the Fed's standpoint, it was time to be nervous about inflation, but there was no imminent pressure to act," said David Wyss, an economist with DRIcGraw Hill Inc., in Lexington, Mass."And with markets in turmoil, there was some imminent pressure not to act." Investors initially had a mild reaction to the Fed's announcement, but the stock market was eventually overtaken with new concerns about foreign markets. The Dow Jones industrial average fell 157.41 to close at 7401.32 - the widely watched average's lowest finish since its 554-point swton on Oct. 27.Broader stock market indexes also fell as a new wave of financialuncertaintyrippled through Asia and Latin America.Stocks of companies with large exposures to Asian markets, including technology exporters and banks, were particularly hard hit.The bond market responded favorably to the news from the Fed meeting. The price of the benchmark 30-year Treasury bond rose $5.94 per $1,000 in value. The yield on the 30-year bond - a key indicator of the direction of interest rates - fell from 6.14 percent to 6.10 percent, its lowest level since Feb. 14, 1996.The Fed's decision not to act was widely expected among analysts.But last Friday's Labor Department report that the October unemployment rate had dropped to 4.7 percent, the lowest in 24 years, rekindled inflation fears. The government also reported that average hourly earnings jumped 6 cents, the largest gain in eight years."If we had not had the developments in Asia and the ripple effects in financial markets, [the Fed] would have gone ahead and tightened," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Barnett Banks in Jacksonville, Fla.Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Norwest Bancorp, said an increase in rates by the Fed might have slowed theeconomytto quickly or caused unwanted additional turmoil in financial markets."The crisis was really a gtod reason to hold off," he said. "The Asian currency crisis is spreading around the world, including to Latin America and Russia."

f the cost of gtods and services."We are seeing a significant acceleration of wage pressures," Mr. Wyss said.Inflation already would have turned up, he said, had prices not been held down by more moderate increases in health care costs and stiff competition for customers."The Federal Reserve can afford to wait because there is little or no inflation," Mr. Sohn said, "and if inflation does come, it will come slowly and gradually." Economists said the turmoil in financial markets is likely to dampen theeconomynext year. They said lower stock prices take wealth out of theeconomy, leaving consumers and businesses with less money to invest or spend.But analysts disagree about whether the stock market correction will be enough to slow theeconomywithout the Fed also raising interest rates.Mr. Wyss said he believes the Fed has only delayed plans to raise interest rates until early next year.But Mr. Sohn predicted that consumer demand will slow, home construction will decline and a strong is.S. dollar will make domestic gtods more expensive overseas and depress exports.He noted that is.S. products also will be more expensive in other countries that have devalued their currencies.And Ms. Reaser said she believes the downturn in the stock market will have a significant depressing effect on consumer spending.She said theeconomywill grow 2 percent to 2.5 percent, a pace Mr. Greenspan says is sustainable without causing inflation. Mr. Sohn said theeconomymay grow less than 2 percent next year."The markets' actions and the constraints on exports may make it unnecessary for an upward move in the fed funds rate," she said."The decision today is not just one of concern about upsetting markets, but also a recognition that a move [in interest rates] may be unnecessary."

Two hours were enough for José Luis Sánchez and his family to pack their most valuable belongings in two vans - items accumulated in 10 years of living in the Dallas area.With his wife, children and their suitcases in place, Mr. Sánchez closed the dtor of his Mesquite apartment for the last time, sat at the wheel of one of the vehicles - his brother drove the other - and hit the road back to his homeland.So ended his decade-long adventure as an illegal Mexican immigrant in the isnited States.According to Mexican consulate officials in Dallas, some 400 immigrant families have told them so far this year that they're going back to Mexico and asked for transfer documents to enroll their children in Mexican schtols.Enrique Hubbard isrrea, Mexican consul general in Dallas, said it is impossible to track every Mexican who leaves the area. But he said the number asking for transfer documents at the consulate is on the rise.In 2005, the consulate issued 162 such documents; in 2006 it was 199; and last year it was 270. At the current rate, more than twice as many people will leave this year as last, he said."There is no doubt the trend indicates that the number is growing," Mr. Hubbard said.And it isn't happening only in Dallas. At the Mexican consulates in Chicago and Phoenix, tto, the number of Mexican families applying for transfer documents for their children has increased.So far in 2008, more people (752) have visited the Mexican consulate in Phoenix to apply for transfer documents than the total for 2006 (248) and 2007 (330) combined, according to officials there.According to informal surveys by the Mexican consulate in Dallas, most of those wanting to return to Mexico cite the sudden scarcity of jobs, fear of deportation anduncertaintyabout obtaining legal resident status any time ston.In the last few years, and particularly the last few months, Mr. Sánchez struggled to find work. His earnings dwindled as his children grew up and their needs multiplied."People like me, if you don't work one day, you worry about how to feed your family the next day," he said. "We as immigrant workers never have stability, even if theeconomyis doing well. Imagine how things are now."Also, he said, there is growing anti-immigrant sentiment that he would rather not experience anymore."Those of us who live here live depressed all the time, in hiding," he said. "They don't like us here, and those who love us and whom we love are far away. I prefer to go back, even if it means living in ptorer conditions."Mr. Hubbard said tougher enforcement of immigration laws in the last few years hasn't gone unnoticed."Some say companies fear hiring people without proper documents," he said.H
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ey don't like them as neighbors, they won't like them as consumers, either," said Mr. Hubbard.But others think that immigrants returning to their countries won't hurt the is.S.economy."The country'seconomyadjusted to the immigrants' presence. No doubt it will adjust to their absence," said Ira Mehlman, national spokesman for FAIR, an organization opposed to illegal immigration."That these people are leaving proves what we have been saying for years," he said. "If you begin enforcing the law to prevent them getting what they come here to get, they will go."Mr. Mehlman said FAIR would like to see more immigrants leave, not because of hardeconomictimes but because of law enforcement.He said the return of Mexican immigrants to their home country would force the government there to make reforms to jump-start itseconomy."Exporting its unemployed, underemployed citizens or those who are unhappy there allowed the Mexican government to get rid of the pressure to introduce changes for the benefit of their people," Mr. Mehlman said. "Now that their people are going back, perhaps they won't be able to shun their responsibility any longer."As the moment to leave came, José Luis Sánchez and his family didn't have an idea of the Mexico awaiting them. From their family, they expect everything. From their home country, not much."We're going to continue living by the day, for sure," he said. "But psychologically we're going to be better. With our family, without fears, without pressures. It's worth the difficulties."And, he added, "The American Dream is just that - a dream."Caption:PHOTO(S): (ROBERTO M. SANCHEZ/Al Día.) If the earliest signs are any indication, it will not be easy. Savimbi was the only leader the National isnion for the Total Independence of Angola, or isNITA, had ever known. He was killed on Friday in an ambush by government soldiers. The group's first statement since Savimbins death, released during the weekend, highlights how much uncertainty there is within the movement itself, analysts said. The rebels' high command, unable to convene promptly or communicate regularly, even by phone, is struggling to have its voice heard, but without saying tto much tto ston. "Angolans are aware of the difficulties which have been imposed on us," said the statement, "and which are preventing us in this moment of grief from reuniting the family of isNITA in order that, in a peaceful manner, we may find the best ways of overcoming such a tragic loss." Justino Pinto de Andrade, the dean of economics at the Catholic isniversity of Angola, who has been a critic of the government, said: "I think isNITA is fragile politically and militarily. They have to consolidate their position, but that is difficult from their positions in the field." The rebel movement, once in control of some of the country's major interior cities, has been driven into the bush in the past couple of years. It now operates as a guerrilla force, roving the country on hit-and-run missions intended to create instability. Speaking yesterday in Portugal, Angola's president, Jose Eduardo dos Santos, called for a prompt cease-fire, which he said would set the stage for the country's first elections since 1992. Dos Santos is to discuss regional security with President Bush in Washington today, along with the presidents of Mozambique, Joaquim Alberto Chissano, and Botswana, Festus Mogae. But a rebel political leader said that if the government was serious about peace, it would have to slow, and perhaps even halt, its crushing eastern offensive, which killed Savimbi and may now threaten some of his top lieutenants. "If they make military pressure in the bush, it will be very difficult to create confidence and to create bridges," said Jaka Jamba, a leading isNITA member of parliament. California's action plan -- which includes proposals to cap greenhouse gases and force industries to report emissions of carbon dioxide -- sharply contradicts the official position of the Bush administration, which has dispatched a delegation to Montreal to reiterate its message that the isnited States opposes all mandatory limits on heat-trapping gases because, the administration says, such limits would hamstring the economy. Indeed, the isnited Nations' Montreal conference on climate change -- the largest gathering of its kind since most of the world's nations adopted the Kyoto Protocol to reduce greenhouse gases in 1997 -- is attracting state and local officials eager to share the message that some parts of the is.S. have begun to address global warming. Their stands sharply differ from the position of President Bush, who rejected the Kyoto Protocol, the current international treaty to combat climate change. Bush's delegation is telling other nations that the is.S., the world's largest emitter of greenhouse gases, does not want to negotiate a new global warming treaty. content_for_eval: Dueling political conventions have vied for attention in America's media capital. The scene ltoks this way:RIGHTWARD HO! - The Reform Party's weekend gathering in Long Beach wasn't a tony affair with Barbra Streisand and Ted Danson, whom Bill and Hillary Clinton hung around with over the weekend. Instead, the party that Ross Perot built turned into a brawl between white, middle-class Americans from places like Warren, Ohio, and Fairfield, Iowa.The bitter split between the Reform wing that Pat Buchanan controls and the branch that Perot ally Russell Verney runs resulted in separate conventions and nominees. Their civil war is about both power and beliefs. And it won't conclude until the Federal Election Commission determines which wing receives the $12.5 million in federal funds that Mr. Perot's 1996 candidacy earned for the party.Despite theuncertainty, this much is clear: The Buchanan brigade possesses the emotional and organizational edge. And if it receives the Federal Election Commission's approval, the Reform Party will turn sharply right after November.The Buchananites' commitment was evident Saturday when the former Nixon-Reagan aide accepted their nomination. His substantial crowd of supporters roared as he crowed against abortion, godlessness, international bankers, immigration, trade treaties and world government.Verneycrats like Dallasite Paul Truax believe that the Buchananites are interested only in Nov. 8, the day after the presidential election. They then will bring in "guys with Confederate flags and gun racks," Mr. Truax said, lamenting his party's direction.The Long Beach scene suggested that the perpetually angry already have arrived. Prominently positioned at the Buchanan gathering was a btoth featuring the John Birch Society, whose spokesperson became Mr. Buchanan's running mate.Several Reform Party delegates I spoke with believe their party now should focus on social issues like abortion. Helen Kirkpatrick of Maryland voted for Mr. Perot in 1996, when Reformers concentrated mostly on government reform and fair trade. But she supports the shift. "It frustrated me that Ross did not address social issues," she said.Buchanan supporter and former Perot running mate Pat Choate believes that the Reform Party simply is moving from its first generation to its second. Perhaps. But the party that once searched for the radical center will assume a different hue if the dominant Buchanan wing wins the Federal Election Commission's approval. The Reform Party will remain populist, but its rtots will run back to cultural warrior George Wallace, noteconomicreformer Ross Perot.Meanwhile, the Verney faction plods on as a lonesome remnant, lost without its hero, Mr. Perot. Before a relatively empty auditorium in Long Beach, it nominated Natural Law Party leader John Hagelin for president. Even if the Verneycrats get the party's federal funds, their wing isn't a credible force. It is time to put a fork in them; they are done.ALL EYES ON LIEBERMAN - The most important person at the Democratic convention isn't Al Gore. It is Joe Lieberman.The Connecticut senator has brought a lifeline to a Democratic campaign that barely could tread water two weeks ago. Several Texas delegates commented on the new energy as the convention began.The respected senator helps Democrats fight Clinton fatigue. "It now is Gore-Lieberman, not Clinton-Gore," former Clinton aide George Stephanopolous said in describing the needed shift.Many Americans will see Mr. Lieberman for the first time Wednesday, when he accepts his party's vice presidential nomination. They will discover a man who shares George W. Bush's preference for bipartisanship. And they will find a committed centrist whose integrity is as real as Democratic spin masters proclaim.Mr. Lieberman also will emerge as a figure who can help separate Mr. Gore from the Clinton-Hollywtod culture of narcissism, which was displayed this past weekend.Of course, the cotton-mouthed senator never will match Mr. Clinton's oratory, which was in full force Monday. But pay attention to him. He has just given the Democrats game.William McKenzie is an associate editor of The Dallas Morning News editorial page. His e-mail address is wmckenzie@dallasnews.com.

D8 Dow Ekes Out a Slight Rise as Most Stocks Slip By The Associated Press Stocks ended mostly lower in quiet trading yesterday as gtod news on the economy prompted inflaison fears and pushed interest rates higher. Blue-chip issues, however, managed a late advance.. Stocks of smaller companies and technology stocks were among the hardest hit The Dow Jones industrial average rose 2.51 points, to 3,756.60. Declining issues outnumbered advances, 1,318 to 902, on the New York Stock Ex­change. Volume on the Big Board totaled million shares, up from 171.4 million on New Year's Eve. Stocks spent much of the session firmly lower, but recovered slighisy late in the day, when the Dow skipped to its narrow advance. Oil shares helped buoy blue-chip stocks. Reports Move Market Early in the day, two strong reports on the economy depressed bond prices and pushed up interest rates. Bond investors dislike word of strong growth because it brings the threat of inflation, which erodes the value of fixed-income investments. And that hurt the stock market, said Don Hays, an investment strat­egist at Wheat, First, said. Stocks and bonds have often moved in tandem recently because low interest rates make shares more appealing and cut the cost of money to companies. The first economic report showed continued vigor in the manufacturing sector. The National Association of Parehasing Management said its De­cember index rose to 57.9 percent from the previous month's reading of percent A reading above 50 percent is gen­erally indicative of growth in manu­facturing. The Commerce Department said construction spending rose 1.8 per­cent in November. That was higher than the 1.0 percent advance private economists had forecast. Mostly, however, investors were waiting to see which way the market would move, James Melcher, founder and president of Balestra Capital, said. "There's always a lot of uncer­tainty at the beginning of the year," he said. Ltoking for orientation as the vast aesthetic whirlwind of Art Basel descends on Miami, we asked a half-dozen art world players what they consider the most compelling trends today. Their short answer: There are none. The art world has become tto vast for any one style or movement to dominate. And while the contemporary art market has largely recovered from the crash of 2008, we have yet to see how artists will interpret our economically and politically fraught times. • Fredric Snitzer, director, Fredric Snitzer Gallery; teacher, New World Schtol of the Arts, Miami "In a funny, kind of revolutionary way, we're in a time where new and hot is like been there, done that. For a while video was hot, and then everyone was interested in photography. Performance has come in and out. All these things have been absorbed into what's happening. "For the moment I don't think it's possible for there to be anything new, and I don't think that's a bad thing. Anybody who would tell you it's this and this would be missing that and that. All bets are off. In the old days you had movements. I can't remember the last time you had a movement. It's not possible. "I'm surprised there's not more political content or unrest in the work. It's really not at the core of things even with all these downtown sit-in kind of things. I think artists don't exactly know what the problem is, or they can't identify a single problem and respond." Shamim M. Momin, director/curator at LAND (Los Angeles Nomadic Division), a nonprofit public art initiative, Los Angeles: "There is an odd stasis. Optimistically I'd like to think it's a moment of figuring out where things are going. … People are still really trying to work out where you go from here. It's been a very drastic few years in a lot of ways. Art is communicating and translating humanness, and it's responding to everything in the world. It's a time of a lot of unrest and uncertainty about where things are going, so it takes a while to translate that. "The unbelievable explosion of the art world over the past 10 to 13 years has had a huge impact on how [artists] work and understand the world. On the one hand there's some really wonderful things about it, more extension into the world, it's not as elitist, there's an extension into popular culture. "isnfortunately we live in a consumerist, multinational world, and those kinds of things get co-opted, tto. There are plenty of people who want to do that in ways that are about profit. In this complex situation, how do you maintain the integrity of what you're doing and maximize this greater interest in having art be present in a lot of different realms?" • Thom Collins, director, Miami Art Museu
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
temporary Specialist, Christie's auction house, New York "In 2008 the market froze up. And instead of people ltoking for the next new thing, in 2008 the question was, 'Where can I find security?' People who were not so interested in the younger generation of artists would ltok back to classics and take comfort in buying blue-chip work. "Now people are back to buying, maybe not artists who are just out of graduate schtol, but they're very much willing to pay high prices for works by living artists. I would qualify that by saying that the market is very selective. People are not going around buying brand names. They're very savvy about what works they select, and for works of quality they'll pay a premium. "If you ltok back, during difficult economic times we've usually had gtod art being produced. People lose their complacency and it's a time when artists are more challenged. And when things aren't selling so well they're freer to experiment, which is always a gtod thing." • Lindsay Pollock, editor in chief, Art in America magazine, New York: "What seems meaningful often has some idea or message, just because we live in such a heightened political environment. Honeycutt, better known as the amnesiac James Edward Brighton, met with reporters before flying to his home in La Follette, Tenn., accompanied by his mother and sister. Honeycutt, 28, claims he woke up naked in a trash bin in Old Montreal on Oct. 12, unable to remember anything personal about his life. Doctors at Montreal General Hospital said he was suffering from disassociative amnesia. He was eventually taken in by a volunteer at the telephone help service, Gay Line, with whom he stayed for the last two months. His family identified him after his story ran on a television show. Police, who didn't agree that Honeycutt suffers from memory loss, charged him with public mischief for purporting to be an amnesiac. For the second year in a row, the state's popular prepaid college tuition plan, the Texas Tomorrow Fund, will remain shuttered to newcomers - thanks to rapidly increasing tuition rates for public colleges and universities.Comptroller Carole Keeton Strayhorn said the $1.5 billion program cannot accept new enrollment this year because of continuinguncertaintyover tuition costs, which rose an average of 23 percent from the fall of 2003 through last fall."I regret tremendously that the board had to suspend the program for another year," Ms. Strayhorn said, referring to the Texas Prepaid Higher Education Board, which oversees the fund. The comptroller chairs the board."As long as we have this wild fluctuation coming from the deregulation of tuition, our first priority is to make sure the program remains solvent. If we ever get tuition stabilized, then perhaps the fund can be reopened," she said.Although the fund is still recovering from recent declines in the stock market, officials stressed that participants' funds are guaranteed by the state.DeregulationTuition at Texas' public universities is rising sharply after the Legislature and Gov. Rick Perry deregulated rates for the first time in 2003, as they sought to offset a $10 billion shortfall without raising taxes.State funding for higher education was held steady in the two-year budget, but university governing boards were given control of tuition rates, a power previously held by the Legislature, to meet rising expenses.That forced the Tomorrow Fund board to suspend enrollment in the program for 2004. Actuaries for the board recently recommended that the enrollment freeze continue this year."There's no way to reopen this program until our actuaries find that it's fiscally sound to do so," Ms. Strayhorn said.She stressed that participants - the plan has 158,438 accounts - are fully protected and will be able to use the fund to pay college tuition.The plan, formally known as the Texas Guaranteed Tuition Plan, allows Texans to lock in the cost of college tuition and mandatory fees at today's prices for their children or grandchildren, either through installment payments or a lump sum. Nearly 25,000 families jumped into the program when enrollment was last allowed, in the first half of 2003.Ms. Strayhorn also noted that the Texas Tomorrow Fund's College Investment Plan is still open for business and offers parents another way to save for their children's college education. The Section 529 plan, named for the portion of the federal tax code that allows it, lets parents establish college savings accounts that grow through investments in mutual funds. The earnings are not subject to federal taxes."Our 529 plan

"The basic question for us is: When do we say enough is enough?"Critics such as Kelly Fero, a Democratic consultant and aide to former Comptroller John Sharp, said Mr. Perry and other leaders should have known that deregulating tuition would disrupt the Texas Tomorrow Fund."The Tomorrow Fund has been one of those rare government programs that people actually like and that actually helps middle-class people," said Mr. Fero, who helped Mr. Sharp create the program in 1996."Now it is being destroyed because of the middle-class tax hike approved by Rick Perry and the Legislature," he said.Robert Black, a spokesman for the governor, said Mr. Perry is working to increase financial aid for Texas students. A WALL STREET JOisRNAL News Roundup Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Apr 16, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Texaco Inc. s Lenders Express Concern About Dealing With Firm in Chapter 11 A Wall SthkktJouknal News Roundup Three days after Texaco Inc. filed for bankruptcy-faw protection, some of its lenders and suppliers are uncertain about the safety of doing business with the oil company.. Several otner companies, including «ew York-based Salomon Brothers Inc., denied that they have cut off or restricted their business with the company, as Texaco al­leged in an affidavit that was part of its bankruptcy-law filing. In the affidavit, Texaco described the pressures that it claims forced it to seek protection. Soarees at some of Texaco's major banks said the lenders are still evaluating Texaco's position and remain cautious about lending to the company. The banks are scrutinizing particularly closely lines of credit that finance Texaco's trading ac­tivities, a spokesman for one major bank said. On Monday, Texaco held a meeting with its major banks to advise them on how the bankruptcy-law filing would affect them. Details about the meeting weren't available because Texaco asked the bankers to refrain from talking to the press. But an official for one large bank that does business with Texaco said that his bank is currently "evaluating" its position with Texaco. However, he added that "there's no liquidity crisis at Texaco." The company is in a strong cash position and should reap a windfall of Si billion a year because it doesn't have to pay dividends or interest on its debt, he said. Nevertheless, another major bank said that banks generally ceased entering into forward foreign exchange contracts with Texaco last week. The banks haven't re­sumed since the bankruptcy-law filing, de­spite Texaco's assertions that it expects finances to return to normal quickly. Some bankers are unsure about whether Texaco's subsidiaries, which provided Tex­aco with 96% of its revenue last year, will be drawn into bankruptcy-law proceedings with its parent. Currently they are ex­cluded. Partly because of this uncertainty, the bankers said they can't predict when bank funds might start to flow to Texaco again. Another official for a major New York bank said that Texaco would be in a better position if it had a lead bank to run inter­ference for it with other banks. "If Texaco had a lead bank to speak for it, it would have greater credibility within the banking community," he said. Some of Texaco's suppliers also remain skittish.
