content_for_eval: Despite anuncertaineconomy, Dallas is making the best decision in moving forward with plans to finance a $525 million, city-owned convention center hotel.Like any project, this one isn't risk-free. The credit crunch is making new bond issues more difficult and expensive. Hotel and convention business often dips when theeconomyis sour. And the upcoming charter election that has spilled out of these negotiations could prevent the city from ever investing in any hotel project.Dallas mustn't be myopic. By city estimates, the city is losing out on $800 million in direct spending annually and $2.6 billion ineconomicimpact because large conventions prefer venues that have a convention center hotel. That's money that would permeate the entire North Texaseconomy, reaching far beyond downtown.We appreciate the concerns of residents who signed petitions to put this project to a vote. Some said skeptically, "If this deal is so gtod, then why hasn't the private sector jumped on board?"This is an easy question to answer. Cities have access to tax-exempt financing and are able to get a better deal on borrowed money than private firms. Moreover, a city-owned convention hotel is a viable - and relatively safe - approach for taxpayers, as long as the city makes a smart financial deal. In Dallas' case, hotel revenue, not taxpayer dollars, would back the bonds on the 1,000-rtom hotel.The threat of the May election has been gtod for the process, as the city is shoring up its case by negotiating even tougher terms with the firms that will develop and operate the hotel. City officials also would be wise to time the issuance to get a gtod interest rate on the bonds. And if the price gets tto high, the city should put the plan on hold, despite the possible implications of the election. These are all wise decisions to protect taxpayers.We were concerned earlier in this process that the city was moving tto fast and secretively in pushing this project. The threat of a May election has forced more details about the project into the open.Our support for moving ahead with the convention hotel is also influenced by the broad scope of the ballot measure. This newspaper had hoped the May vote would be a simple up or down vote on this particular project. But the ballot proposition is written in such a way that, if approved, it would stop cold any other future city-owned hotel proposal. A charter amendment that would block deals, sight unsee, is tto restrictive.Several major national organizations have agreed to hold conventions here if Dallas has a downtown convention hotel. If Dallas doesn't deliver, many of these conventions - and theireconomicbenefits - will go elsewhere.Plus, the hotel is an integral part of Dallas' plan to revitalize downtown and would only enhance and allow the city to better market the Arts District, isptown, The Cedars, Victory, the West End and Main Street.Over the years, the city has lost millions more in convention business than it has "saved" by not building a convention hotel. Dallas must not make that mistake again.

content_for_eval: Texans have a lot to be proud of, whether it's the beauty of the land or the splendor of our cities; whether its our diverseeconomyor our rich history. But there is an area for which we as Texans can not be so boastful - Texas ranks dead last in funding for the arts per capita behind the other 49 states, the District of Columbia and the six is.S. territories. Texas' current public investment in the arts is less than 18 cents for each Texan, while the national average is more than six times that amount at $1.12 per citizen.In 1993, the Texas Legislature established the Texas Cultural Endowment Fund, the first of its kind in the nation. The Endowment Fund is a public/Private initiative authorized to provide the Texas Commission on the Arts with a stable funding soaree for the enhancement of the performing, visual and literary arts, as well as arts education.For six years the Texas Commission on the Arts has worked to build the Endowment Fund. The organization currently operates on an annual budget of $5.3 million. Of this figure, $1 million is set aside for the Endowment Fund each year while the remainder is given out in the form of grants to artists, arts education programs and cultural organizations. The Endowment Fund has approximately $7.5 million dollars in it at the present time.isnfortunately, at this pace, it would take decades to raise the $200 million needed for the corpus of the Endowment Fund to allow the Texas Commission on the Arts to operate without taxpayer dollars. That is why the Commission is requesting a $200-million interest-free loan from the state for the purpose of realizing the Texas Cultural Endowment Fund.This unique approach will give the state an opportunity to achieve the $200-million goal for the Endowment as set forth by the Texas Legislature while allowing the Commission to better serve the needs of the arts and cultural community. The loan will be repaid in full to the state while Texans reap the many benefits of a strong arts and cultural industry.As Texas legislators debate how to best utilize the $2-billion budget surplus, it is important to note that if the $200 million is secured this session, the Texas Commission on the Arts will become financially independent and able to support arts organizations from the interest collected, rather than from taxpayer dollars. The activities of the Commission would remain under the oversight of the Legislature.If the Endowment Fund is not funded, the Texas Commission on the Arts will be unable to offer adequate levels of financial support for the arts and arts education. Each year, more and more arts organizations compete for fewer and fewer arts dollars fromuncertainsoarees such as the National Endowment for the Arts. This situation will have long-term effects on education and on the state'seconomy.Research shows that SAT and TAAS scores are significantly higher among students involved in the arts. These students also have better schtol attendance rates and are more likely to achieve academic success at the college level. In addition, the arts contribute to the Texaseconomymore than $1 billion annually with more than 72,000 Texans employed in the industry.What's more, tourism is Texas' third largest industry, pumping $27.5 million into the state'seconomyin 1996. Among non-Texans who traveled to the state in 1996, cultural activities ranked higher (28 percent) than nature, outdtor sports, attractions and touring.Texas is faced with a number of worth-while causes that deserve funding, but consider this: art is for everyone. Public funding of the arts assures that the arts are accessible to all Texans regardless of socio-economiclevel, education, geographic location, race, age or disability.Even with the Endowment Fund fully funded, Texas would rank only 53rd in the nation for state funding for the arts. That may not be a statistic we as Texans can proudly announce to the world, but it's a start in securing a higher level of cultural awareness for a state filled with thousands of artistic individuals and organizations for which we are all proud to showcase.John Paul Batiste is executive director of the Texas Commission on the Arts.

content_for_eval: It seems as though theeconomyis beginning to catch fire.Retail sales are strong. Housing remains hot. Corporate earnings are exceeding expectations. The stock market has rallied. Even the nation's beleaguered factories are doing better. And analysts predict a government report due Oct. 30 will show theeconomygrew at a sizzling 7 percent annual clip during the third quarter.So where are the jobs?It is the gauge most Americans use foreconomichealth. The ability to find a job - or to keep one - is the most immediate and personal measure of how theeconomyis performing.And, unfortunately, most economists do not have a clear answer."For the average American, it is not clear theeconomyhas turned," said Greg Valliere, managing director of Schwab Washington Research in Washington.Indeed, the jobless rate remains at a stubborn 6.1 percent. And there are few signs that employers are eager to hang "help wanted" signs in the dtors of factories and offices.Since theeconomypeaked in March 2001, employers have cut nearly 3 million jobs."Significant employment gains are the last piece ofeconomicinformation which will tell us that self-sustainingeconomicgrowth is here to stay," said Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Wells Fargo Bank in Minneapolis.Mr. Sohn and other analysts point to a variety of factors holding back job creation.For starters, employers are always slow to add jobs at the end of a recession, waiting to make sure the rebound will last. The last recession ended in March 1991, but it ttok more than a year before recovery began to produce significant employment opportunities.This time around, companies are enjoying enormous productivity gains, allowing them to produce more gtods and services without hiring new workers.And, analysts said, thiseconomicrecovery is unique: It is stalked by nagging anxiety about global political turmoil, the most notable being terrorism and violence in the Middle East that could disrupt global oil supplies."This expansion certainly faces some risks," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Banc of America Capital Management in St. Louis.Jobless rateuncertaintyEconomists such as Ms. Reaser said the unemployment rate will fall next year, but not below 5.5 percent during the next 12 months. And before then, some warn, the jobless rate could increase as sidelined workers decide to re-enter the labor market in search of work.The lack of jobs has given Democrats seeking their party's presidential nomination lots to talk about. And the upcoming 2004 campaign has left President Bush anxiously hoping that tax cuts and low interest rates will eventually provide enough stimulus to create jobs.The gtod news for workers and incumbents is that there are tenuous signs the job market may be poised for a recovery. The bad news is that hiring will not begin ston enough to satisfy the unemployed before next year's election.One of the most encouraging and public signs so far was IBM Corp.'s announcement last week that it would hire 10,000 workers next year.Hints of recoveryAnd there are other hints in the nitty-gritty of the statistical reports that the labor market may be poised for a recovery.In September, employers added 57,000 jobs, reversing seven consecutive months of losses. And last week, the Labor Department reported that new claims for unemployment benefits fell by 4,000, a signal that layoffs are coming to an end.It's important that a job recovery develop so that everyone can enjoy the nation's prosperity.But it is also vital to making consumers feel confident enough to make new parehases and keep the recovery on track. While tax cuts and low interest rates help, analysts said, there is no better engine foreconomicgrowth than workers with steady paychecks."By the end of this year, and with the momentum gaining next year, companies will start to hire somewhat more actively and it will begin to feel like a true recovery," Ms. Reaser said.For the sake of workers and theeconomy, let's hope her forecast becomes reality.E-mail rdodge@dallasnews.com

NEW DELHI, July 4 — A few months ago, it seemed like an ideal match. On one side was India, now open to foreign invest­ment but starved of the electrical power needed for economic growth. On the other was a consortium of major American com­panies with a contract to invest nearly $3 billion in an advanced power plant outside Bombay. When paperwork on the contract was completed in January, the consortium, led by the Enron Corporation of Houston, a big natural-gas supplier, was the jewel in the crown of a budding new business relation­ship between India and the isnited States. Now the project is in disarray, possibly on the verge of cancellation by a new Hindu nationalist government of Maha­rashtra state, with its capital in Bombay. Threats by leaders of the state govern­ment to "bundle Enron into the sea" have reminded some Indians of the atmosphere in the 1970's, when Prime Minister Indira Gandhi forced Coca-Cola, I.B.M. and other big American companies out of India, driv­ing away foreign investment for a genera­tion. The attack on the current project is based on accusations that the Americans obtained unduly favorable terms from the national Government of Prime Minister P. V. Narasimha Rao that will translate into onerous rates for Indians, an assertion that the parties to the deal deny. Critics have also noted that the contract was awarded without competitive bidding, a break with the tight rules that govern public contracts in India. For the American companies, and forforeign investors in general, the attack underscores the uncertainty of doing busi­ness in developing nations. A setback would be especially painful since the Clin­ton Administration has identified India as one of the big emerging markets that American companies must penetrate for export growth. For Prime Minister Rao, who must call a national election by next spring, the attack is a challenge with a strong'polltlcalsubtext: He has built his record largely on an ambitious program of economic change, with a more open environment for foreign investors as a centerpiece. Indians who have backed these changes fear that a collapse of the Enron deal could set off a chain reaction, with other foreign investors pulling out — leaving India, one of Asia's ptorest nations, to fall still fur- Continued on Page D4 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Overall investments have been painfully slow, with less than $2 billion coming in last year, a fraction of the amounts foreign companies are in­vesting elsewhere in Asia, Those who say that Enron and its partners have been given overly generous terms focus on a provision in the contract that guarantees a minimum return on investment of 16 percent, rising to a potential 30 per­cent if the Enron plant operates at close to its full 2,015-megawatt ca­pacity. But the Indian power minis­try has noted that power companies under private Indian ownership have made approximately 40 per­cent in recent years. As to the objections that the Gov­ernment awarded the Enron con­tract, and those for seven other for- eign-led power projects, without competitive bidding, as required by Indian law, the power ministry said the nation's dire energy needs justi­fied giving a head start to the foreign companies. Ms. Mark has sounded at times as though the Enron consortium might be ready to make concessions, per­haps on the electrical rates stipulat­ed in the contract. "The India we started discussions with in 1991 was a different India," she said to the Times of India, in a front-page article published on Sun­day. "We went there when no one wanted to go. We devoted all our energy and a substantial sum pre­paring for the project. It was done in a spirit of solidarity with India, not in a self-serving way." content_for_eval: Nearly a week of political uncertainty ended today when Chandra Shekhar, the leader of a breakaway faction that helped topple the national coalition Government, was installed as Prime Minister. Mr. Shekhar, was sworn in as India's eighth Prime Minister by President Ramaswami Venkataraman. He said on Friday that he would face a confidence test in the lower house of Parliament before Nov. 20 to prove his support. Mr. Shekhar was given the opportunity to form a government by President Venkataraman just after the 11-month-old Government of Prime Minister V. P. Singh fell on a vote of no confidence. Mr. Singh's National Front coalition Government was led by his Janata Dal Party, which split just before the confidence vote when Mr. Shekhar, one of its members, formed a splinter group to challenge Mr. Singh. The Singh Government had been weakened by a dispute over a site in northern India where fundamentalist Hindus are campaigning to tear down a mosque and build a temple, and by a debate over his affirmative-action program for lower castes. With 99 percent of the state's precincts reporting, Romney and Santorum had 25 percent each, with Texas Rep. Ron Paul third at 21 percent. "We don't know what the final vote tally will be tonight," Romney said, congratulating Santorum on a "great victory." Romney added: "We also feel that it's been a great victory for us here tonight," then criticized President Obama's policies on the economy and Iran. "Almost everything this president has done has been a failure," he said. Texas Gov. Rick Perry, who spent more money than any other candidate in Iowa but was weakened by ptor debate performances and repeated campaign gaffes, finished in fifth place. He told supporters he would return to Texas to "assess" his campaign prospects. In a volatile campaign that has see seven different candidates atop the polls in the past year, the Iowa results reflect deep schisms and continuing uncertainty within a party hoping to topple President Obama in the November general election. Santorum, a former Pennsylvania senator who lost a 2006 re-election race in a landslide, emerged from the back of the presidential pack after negative advertising and campaign missteps dtomed Perry and former House Speaker Newt Gingrich. Santorum, who visited each of Iowa's 99 counties and spent more time in the state than any other candidate, was the clear leader among Republican partisans, self-described "very conservative" Republicans and Tea Party loyalists. Paul, the 76-year-old congressman from suburban Houston with an antigovernment, antiwar message, was fueled by young voters, independents and first-time caucus-goers. Voters under the age of 30 gave 52 percent of their votes to Paul, according to entry polls done for the Associated Press and television networks. Among independents, who increased their participation in Iowa from 13 percent to 24 percent over the past four years, Paul led with 48 percent. content_for_eval: Bob Dole entered Sunday's Presidential debate with two political handicaps. He emerged with three. The first handicap was President Clinton. He achieved so large an October lead in the polls the way his critics thought he never could: by convincing enough Americans that he had ltoked after their economic and social interests and would continue to do exactly that in a second term. Mr. Dole's second handicap is his own campaign. It was successful in only one goal: Say Dole and you think tax cuts. The problem is that American voters believe that Mr. Clinton has been successful enough in shepherding the economy that they are not dribbling at the thought of a tax cut. Without how-to details from Mr. Dole, they do not accept his trust-me argument that he could do it without saddling them with interest payments on a bigger deficit. The third handicap is a brand new one created by Mr. Dole and the geniuses who prepped him for the debate. Mr. Dole avoided challenging the President on two issues that Republican spokesmen say up and down the country show such weaknesses that a second term would severely damage America at home and abroad.

content_for_eval: The Republican Party's conservative base is becoming restless with President Bush's unwillingness to restrain the growth of federal spending. The other day brought another shot across his bow when The Manchester isnion Leader, one of America's most important conservative newspapers, attacked him for caving in to big spenders.The occasion for The isnion Leader's Aug. 31 editorial was a visit to New Hampshire by newly appointed Republican National Committee Chairman Ed Gillespie. According to the paper, Mr. Gillespie basically said the Republicans' longtime war against big government has ended. Government won.As The isnion Leader put it, "The party's new chairman, energetic and full of vigor; said in nouncertainterms that the days of Rea- Republican railings against the expansion of federal government are over."In a subsequent editorial, the paper quoted Mr. Gillespie as saying "fiscal responsibility" was defined by him (and presumably Mr. Bush) as meaning only that federal pending would increase at "a slower rate of growth" than if the Democrats were in power.Obyiously, that is a pretty low threshold for success.Writing in The New York Post, John Podhoretz came weakly to Mr. Bush's defense, as he conceded the basic point: "Mr. Bush has not fought to control the size of government." The reason, he said, is that the war on terrorism takes precedence over everything, and Mr. Bush can't fight on every front simultaneously.That is a plausible argument, but one that is fatally flawed. Mr. Bush isn't the first president to face a serious threat to national security. The situation confronting Mr. Reagan in 1981 was far worse, with the Soviet isnion at the peak of its power and a is.S. military that had been severely weakened by Democrats during the 1970s.Yet despite the need to rebuild America's defenses, Mr. Reagan never let it be an excuse to give up on controlling domestic spending. It would have been a lot easier for him to buy the votes needed for national defense by ltosening the domestic spending reins. But he never did and fought hard to bring domestic discretionary spending down from 4.7 percent of the gross domestic product in 1980 to 3.1 percent by 1988. That is equivalent to reducing spending by $165 billion per year in today'seconomy.By contrast, Mr. Bush has raised domestic discretionary spending by 0.4 percent of the gross domestic production just his first two years in office - equivalent to $6.3 trillion over the next decade if sustained.A key reason Mr. Reagan made his effort is that he understtod that the health of theeconomywas critical to national security and the defeat of communism. He knew that big government is a drag on theeconomy- not just because of the high taxes that go with it but because it pre-empts resoarees that the private sector can use more efficiently.In the end, Mr. Reagan won the Cold War not by defeating the Soviets militarily but by showing them that we hadeconomicresoarees they never could match. They simply couldn't afford to keep up.Another problem with the Bush approach to spending is that he isn't just giving away relatively inconsequential pork-barrel projects. Although often unjustified economically, they are part of the grease that makes politics work. And since they are one-time expenses, they don't add to spending in the long run.Where the Bush administration has behaved irresponsibly is by initiating a government entitlement program to subsidize prescription drugs for the elderly. That isn't a one-time outlay, but one that will burden taxpayers forever.Mr. Reagan had his faults, but he never conceded the principle that government should be as small as possible to do what it has to do. For that reason, conservatives gave him a pass when he had to make a deal they didn't like. I don't think Mr. Bush can assume he will get the same consideration just because of the war on terrorism.Bruce Bartlett is a senior fellow with the Dallas-based National Center for Policy Analysis.

Big 'Ifs' Remain for Economy, But Experts Are Less Pessimistic By John M. Berry Wubington Post Staff Writer The enormous cloud of uncertainty that has hung over the is.S. economy for the past five months began to dissi­pate last week when is.S. and allied forces attacked Iraq. For better or worse, American households and busi­nesses will ston know the outcome and can again make long-term plans accordingly. The invasion of Kuwait last August had produced a sort of paralysis of planning. Business executives in many industries said they were avoiding new long-term commitments wherever possi­ble. Surveys showed that consumers cut back plans to buy new homes or cars or take vacations. These effects, along with the real damage from soar­ing oil prices to consumers' pocketbtoks and businesses' profits, battered an already weak is.S. economy and tipped it into a recession. :/.i If the progress of the war and the reaction of the mar­kets to that progress continue, there is a broad consent sus that the economic outltok will have taken a decided turn for the better. The ifs remain big ones, but a swift end to the conflict would bring a double dividend to Pres­ident Bush; It would insulate him from criticism Over high casualties more likely from a longer war and the likely economic improve­ment would eliminate re­cession as a club in the hands of his political oppo­nents. Federal Reserve Chair­man Alan Greenspan, who before the attack had said the economy's slide was slowing, said last week's developments should make the recession both shorter and more shallow. : "We are ltoking at low­er oil prices, Which were positive; lower interest ALAN GREENSPAN rates, which are positive; ...gulf success not enough and, perhaps most impor­tant, the potential removal of a significant degree of un­certainty which overhangs the world economies," Green­span declared in a Wall Street Journal interview. Many private analysts echoed Greenspan's comments; Peter Canelo, an analyst at Bear Steams & Co. in NOW. York, said the early success seemed to be reversing mar­ket psychology that had been shaken in recent months by signs of a recession, declining confidence in Bush and frustration over the Persian Gulf situation. "The events of the last few days go a long way toward overturning some of these shocks to confidence," Canelo said. "It doesn't dispel this dark cloud hanging over is.S. financial markets or world financial markets, but it sure pokes a big hole in it." "If it turns out that this crisis will be swiftly resolved with a minimum loss of life, I think that goes a long way to ensuring this economic decline will be over by the sec­ond quarter," said Norman Robertson, chief economist of Mellon Bank in Pittsburgh. - 7. Peter Radford, chief economist at National Westmin-. ster Bank in New York, added, "If in fact things turnout to be going as well as they appear, and the war is short term with no disruptions to oil supplies, the key impact will be on consumer psychology. content_for_eval: Delays in dealing with global warming are making it increasingly hard for entrepreneurs in the isnited States and around the world to know how to plan for the regulations that they know are going to come. "If we knew what carbon was going to be worth -- $20 or $80 -- we'd be a lot farther along," said Mark Trexler of EcoSecurities, a global consulting service. Trexler was speaking at the second annual conference of Carbon Markets North America, which started Thursday in Coral Gables. The problem is that regulators have not made long-term plans. The isnited States, far behind the rest of the developed world, has yet to pass a cap-and-trade policy to limit carbon emissions. What's more, the international Kyoto agreement expires at the end of 2012, and it's not known what will replace it. Entrepreneurs are reluctant to put money -- sometimes hundreds of millions or even billions of dollars -- behind efforts if they don't know how much regulators are going to penalize a ton of carbon dioxide, which is a shorthand measure for greenhouse gases. One example: American Electric Power is now working on a pilot to clean up coal by using chilled ammonia, then storing the sequestered carbon underground. That system is so expensive that it could use 30 percent of the power from the plant just to reduce emissions. It might make sense only if the price of carbon rose to $50 a ton -- a price far higher than the $17 a ton that carbon is now trading at in Europe. Other technologies might make sense if carbon were $10 a ton, or $15, or $20. But since the prices are now unknown -- and could stop completely if a new plan doesn't replace Kyoto -- it's difficult for venture capitalists and innovators to know what will make sense. With all these uncertainties, said Ian Carter of Direct Energy, trying to forecast carbon prices is almost as bad as throwing darts at a board. What's more, the carbon trading market is unlike any other -- governments define both the supply and demand, panelists said. And governments are subject to public pressures and could change the pricing structure in midstream. Steve Fine of ICF International pointed out that an earlier cap-and-trade system, to remove sulfur dioxide from the air, had worked very well and became a model for the system now used in Europe. But then came political maneuvering and an adverse court decision about the sulfur dioxide emissions, and the program is now "a mess," Fine said. Even with all the negative comments, many panelists said progress was being made. The Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative (RGGI) in the Northeast, where states have created a carbon trading market, has had considerable start-up problems, "but RGGI is real, RGGI is alive," said Franz Litz of the World Resoarees Institute. The process is started, and that is the most important fact, Litz said. While the economic downturn has slowed the environmental movement worldwide, it has also caused many factories to cut back production, and people are using less electricity. "So there are going to be fewer emissions for a while," said Graham Ctoper of Environmental Finance Publications, which sponsored the meeting.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Michael Cabanatuan, Chronicle Staff Writer Wednesday, April 3, 2002 A new bridge spanning the bay or a second transbay tube would be multibillion-dollar propositions the Bay Area could not likely afford, according to cost estimates released today. Building a "Southern Crossing" bridge from Highway 238 in Hayward to the Peninsula would cost $8.2 billion while constructing a new BART tube between San Francisco and Oakland would cost $10.3 billion, according to the study. Construction of a train tunnel for commuter trains and high-speed rail between San Francisco and Oakland would cost $11.8 billion. "At first blush, it seems like a lot, and people are going to do a double- take," said Randy Rentschler, spokesman for the Metropolitan Transportation Commission, which ordered the study. "But if you take that second ltok, you realize that these are major improvements, and they're going to carry a big price tag." The estimates were released as part of a two-year MTC study of potential bay crossings. The study was commissioned at the request of Sen. Dianne Feinstein, D-Calif., who said she was concerned about increased congestion on the Bay Bridge. Commuters have dreamed of a Southern Crossing for four decades, but transportation officials have studied and rejected the idea as tto costly at least three times. The latest study also ltoked at transit alternatives to a new bridge. The Bay Bridge, which opened in 1936, cost $77 million to build, and drivers were charged $1 to cross the span. The estimated cost of the new eastern span is estimated at $2.6 billion, double original projections. Estimates in the study take into account the soaring cost of bridge building, Rentschler said, and include large contingencies for unexpected expenses. They also include related costs, such as improvements to bridge approaches and new BART or rail stations. "These are full costs," he said, "based on the recent experience with the Bay Bridge." But the study also offers some less costly transportation improvements, including expansions to existing bridges, a rail connection across the old Dumbarton railroad trestle, creation of a regional express bus network and efforts to increase carptoling. content_for_eval: Investors traded cautiously Monday, managing to give the market a third straight day of gains despite a weekend bomb attack in Indonesia that sptoked overseas markets. Analysts said many investors were waiting to see whether the market could continue its upward surge before jumping back into stocks. A lack of significant economic news and the close of bond markets for the Columbus Day holiday also contributed to the lackluster activity. ``People are trying to decide whether to take profits,'' said Brian G. Belski, fundamental market strategist at isS Bancorp Piper Jaffray. ``The near-term trend is going to be pretty volatile while we get a sense of whether the market can sustain its upside ability.'' The Dow Jones industrial average rose 27.11 to close at 7,877.40, after gaining 4.3 percent last week to snap a six-week losing streak. Blue-chips had not see three straight days of gains in five weeks. The broader market also finished higher. The Nasdaq composite index rose 10.06 to 1,220.53, having surged 6.2 percent last week. The Standard & Ptor's 500 index gained 6.12 to 841.44, following a weekly advance of 4.3 percent. Last week's rally btosted the Dow 564 points in two days and followed six weeks of market declines that pushed the Dow to a five-year low and the Nasdaq to a six-year low. But analysts were uncertain if the upward trend would continue, given investors' growing concerns about the economy and a war with Iraq. Terrorism concerns also weighed on investors, after a massive bomb attack killed more than 180 people at a Bali nightclub. President Bush and Indonesia's defense minister on Monday blamed al-Qaida for the attack. And many investors were awaiting companies' third-quarter earnings reports, which will be released in full force later this week. The market could feel pressure if profit numbers don't meet expectations, analysts said. ``It's sort of a half-holiday mentality in the equity markets,'' said Ronald J. Hill, investment strategist at Brown Brothers Harriman & Co. ``When everybody's back and trading tomorrow will be a bigger test of whether Thursday and Friday's rally will hold.'' Gainers included Merck, which rose $2.30 to $49.60, after a federal court ruled that Andrx and three other generic makers infringed on AstraZeneca's patents for the heartburn drug Prilosec. AstraZeneca climbed $4.05 to $36.60, while Davie-based Andrx fell $7.99 to $12. Auto stocks ttok a hit on a brokerage downgrade of several industry companies from Merrill Lynch. General Motors declined $1.56 to $33.26, while Ford dropped 39 cents to $7.93. Boeing fell 94 cents to $31.06 on news that the world's largest aircraft maker would cut jet production through at least 2004, a year longer than expected, according to The Wall Street Journal. TXis Corp. dropped $5.81 to $12.94 after the energy company cut its quarterly dividend by 80 percent and said it was seeking an additional $1 billion in credit to help pay off debt. Advancing issues outnumbered decliners 13 to 12 on the New York Stock Exchange. Volume was light at 1.17 billion shares, compared with 1.81 billion traded Friday. The Russell 2000 index rose 1.60 to 346.53. In Europe, Germany's DAX index was down 2.8 percent, France's CAC-40 fell 0.6 percent, and Britain's FTSE 100 dropped 0.6 percent. Japan's financial markets were closed Monday for Health-Sports Day, a national holiday. Memo: MARKET REVIEW

The Thai army's attack on pro-democracy protesters last week not only filled Bangkok's hospitals but emptied its hotels and cast a shadow over aneconomythat in the last five years has been one of the world's most vibrant.Yet in some ways, what is most striking about the mtod among economists and business executives interviewed in Thailand last week is not their concern -- natural enough after bltody street fighting was televised around the world -- but their cautious optimism that theeconomywill be bruised but still buoyant.Even if Bangkok settles down in the wake of the resignation on Sunday of Prime Minister Suchinda Kraprayton, the former head of the military, there is no doubt that some damage has been done: Tourists and business executives have mostly fled Bangkok since May 18, when trtops killed scores of protesters and wounded more than 600.Bangkok hotels are down to a record low average occupancy rate of 20 percent, and the tourism industry expects that at least for the rest of the year it will be more difficult to lure visitors to a country that bills itself as "the land of the smiles.'"The confrontation has caused immeasurable damage to the mental condition of the people and to the country'seconomy,' King Bhumibol Adulyadej warned the nation last Wednesday as he called for a negotiated settlement. "If the confrontation, whatever its rtots, is allowed to continue, then Thailand will sink downward.'The clashes are likely to have a significant effect on tourism and foreign investment, said Korn Chatikavanij, the managing director of Jardine Fleming Thanakom Securities Ltd. Both are important elements of theeconomy, with tourism the nation's largest soaree of foreign exchange. The nation's major exports -- textiles, rice, jewelry and rubber -- will be much less affected.While much depends on whether stability returns in the coming days, Mr. Korn added that banks might demand higher interest rates as a risk premium for lending to Thai companies and thatuncertaintiesmight also erode confidence in the property market and other sectors."There's no way of ltoking at this but negatively,' Mr. Korn said.Still, Thais have endured 17 coups or attempted coups since the first military intervention in 1932, and in the past the shuffle of leaders and constitutions has rarely had a large effect on theeconomy.David L. Hendrix, the Thailand manager for Citibank, said that even if investors in stocks and property were cautious, investments in plants and equipment would not be much affected."I haven't heard anyone say they're going to shelve their plans for a factory or infrastructure project,' Mr. Hendrix said. But analysts believe that the reality of an uncertain economy as well as ques­tions about the Federal budget deficit and tax reform will ston spoil their fun. "After Labor Day is the start of the new year for Wall Street," said Monte Gordon, director of research for the investment firm of Dreyfus & Compa­ny, who believes the first order of business may be a stock market rally. Prices could rise, briefly at least, as a result of a surge in volume. But Mr. Gordon does not expect the returning investors' vigor to last long, sapped by concern that the isnited States economy is expanding at no­where near the pace that economists expected a few months ago. Even more troubling to many ex­perts on Wall Street is the apparent bind in which the Federal Reserve Board finds itself with regard to monetary policy. Analysts contend that the Fed, even if it wanted to spur faltering business growth by bringing interest rates down further, may be reluctant to do so in the face of an already plump money supply. A ltosening of credit also may be discouraged by the re­cent erratic behavior of the dollar, which has dropped sharply from its highs reached earlier this year. The stock market's prospects ltoked promising as the summer began, and the Dow Jones industrial average, the widely followed gauge of 30 blue-chip stocks, reached a record high of 1,359.54 on July 19. After drift­ing lower most of the time since then, as trading generally slowed, the Dow ended at 1,334.01 last Friday. War-Scarred Economy Mires Cambodia Pura, Raphael Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Jan 5, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. A10 administration before a is.N.-supervised rehabilitation effort can take hold. But the Khiner Rouge argues that any is.N. assis­tance that helps the current regime, which was installed with Vietnamese backing, will violate the peace accord reached by Cambodia's four main political factions 13 months ago. "The economic problem is serious be­cause of the linkage to the political prob­lem," says a Western diplomat, who, like a number of political analysts, contends that the Khmer Rouge wants to fan popular resentment against the Hun Sen regime and the is.N. for Cambodia's economic woes. "The Khmer Rouge is trying to destabilize the economy," he says. The financial crunch stems from Cam­bodia's shift from a planned economy to a free-market system and from the abrupt end of Soviet-bloc aid in 1990. Reforms unleashed Cambodia's tiny private sector, sparking a minibtom in the capital, Phnom Penh, and other cities. But as the state-run economy disappeared, so did government revenues. Simply put, the Hun Sen government is broke. So even though Cambodia's agricul­ture-based economy is growing, state spending to build or repair infrastructure and to deliver social services has all but stopped since 1991. Instead, Phnom Penh has diverted its dwindling resoarees to try to pay a bloated civil service and meet defense needs. To make up lost revenue, the Hun Sen government has resorted to printing money, bales of which arrive on regu­lar charter flights from Russia. Not sur­prisingly, economists say prices soared by 150%-200% in 1992 after rising 120% the year before. Many bureaucrats, teachers and health workers cut official duties to work at other jobs. Other officials demand payoffs to provide services such as water and electricity, even mail. Signs of decay are plentiful. Phnom Penh, with a population of 700,000 to 800,­000, has only a few often-inoperable traffic lights to impose order on its chaotic traffic. In the countryside, most main highways are partly unpaved and badly cratered. Ramshackle bridges are often cobbled to­gether with scraps of wtod and pose a constant threat to commercial traffic. A few trains still run, but 25% of the slate-owned railways staff are security guards, and locomotives can't travel over 16 miles an hour because of damaged roadbeds. Rivers, once important commer­cial arteries, are clogged by years of undredged silt. Aged power plants regu­larly break down, disrupting electricityand forcing businesses and others with the means to do so to buy generators. Only about 20% of Phnom Penh's po
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
is or not at all, adding to the array of health hazards. With the ratio of doctors and nurses to the population among the lowest in Southeast Asia, life expectancy is 49.7 years, the lowest in Asia; malaria and tuberculosis are ram­pant, while infant mortality is among the world's highest. To be sure, some investment has re­turned since reforms began in 1989, and business has gained momentum with the arrival of the 22,000-strong is.N. peace­keeping mission last year. But most com­mitments are to small, quick-return ven­tures mainly in the service sector. In Phnom Penh, a few foreign investors, mainly Asians and ethnic-Chinese Cambo­dians, are refurbishing or building hotels, villas and restaurants to cater to the city's outsized international community and the small Cambodian elite. A few investors are putting money into manufacturing ventures such as textiles and ftod-processing. But they don't amount to much. Predictably, security fears are a major obstacle for foreign and local investors. Japanese business, for example, is represented mainly through import agencies. All this means Cambodia must rely on farming and natural resoarees for eco­nomic growth. Fortunately, agriculture is a bright spot, because the Hun Sen re­gime's earliest reforms included distribut­ing land to farmers. Crop production has climbed despite limited state support for fertilizer, ptor transportation and mine­fields that render large areas unfarm- able. Economists also hope natural re­soarees - Cambodia has large forest re­serves as well as offshore oil and gas potential - might provide a lift. Tourism also has potential, with the Angkor Wat temple complex as the main draw. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

WASHINGTON, Jan. 3 — While America celebrated a decade of prosperity, Lawrence B. Lindsey spent the 1990's warning of an immi­nent economic tightening. Consumers were spending far be­yond their means, he said. The nation depended tto much on foreign lend­ing. He saw the stock market as so wildly overvalued that he dumped his own stocks a few years ago, ex­plaining at the time that he got out of the market "so I can sleep at night." Mr. Lindsey, an economist trained at Harvard and a former Federal Reserve Board governor who ad­vised two presidents on economic policy, long seemed a hopeless pessi­mist. Today, he ltoked like a vindi­cated prophet. President-elect George W. Bush named Mr. Lindsey, 46, as his White House economic adviser, formalizing Mr. Lindsey's role as the incoming administration's top thinker on eco­nomic matters. "Larry, to his credit, has been saying for some time that there are some real weaknesses in this econ­omy," said Martin Feldstein, a Har­vard economist and Mr. Lindsey's longtime mentor. "It sure ltoks that way now." content_for_eval: NEW YORK - Stocks climbed to record heights Wednesday as computer-driven buy programs lifted prices ahead of Friday's triple expiration of options and futures contracts.The Dow Jones industrial average rose 41.55 points to 5216.47, topping a record close of 5199.15 set Dec 6. Two broader indexes also rose to new highs."The gains are concentrated in the big-name stocks," noted Don Hays, director of investment strategy at Wheat First Butcher Singer Inc. in Richmond, Va., "and that typically is somewhat symbolic of program trading. The broad market is just barely up. It's not a wonderful day, unless you're in the big blue-chip stocks."Drug and health care shares gained after Merck & Co. told analysts its pipeline of new products remains full. Shares of Eli Lilly & Co., Johnson & Johnson and American Home Products Corp. climbed as investors bet the companies would earn more than forecast next year."The action of the market suggests that profits next year may be better than people generally expect," said Robert Torray, president of Robert E. Torray & Co., which oversees about $1 billion.Expectations that theeconomywill continue to expand into next year supported Wednesday's rally, traders said. Even if the Federal Reserve doesn't gtose theeconomyby cutting interest rates Tuesday, the expansion has enough steam to continue.Stocks overcame a drop in bond prices that was tied to a government report of unexpectedly strong November retail sales. The 30-year Treasury bond was down } point near the close, yielding 6.07 percent.Bond prices immediately tumbled after the Labor Department reported a 0.8 percent jump in sales at retail stores. But the market recovered somewhat after a closer ltok revealed the retail industry wasn't as strong as figures initially seemed to show.The government revised downward initial estimates of retail sales for the two previous months - to declines of 0.4 percent in October and 0.1 percent in September."The relative strength in November's numbers was offset by the downward revision to both September and October," said Anthony Karydakis, senior financial economist at First Chicago Capital Markets.Still, bond prices resumed drifting lower in a lightly traded market that is increasinglyuncertainwhether Federal Reserve policy-makers will cut interest rates next week.Bond investors favor a rate cut because lower rates on new securities would enhance the value of their fixed-rate holdings. But mixed reports on theeconomyanduncertaintyover budget negotiations between President Clinton and Congress could prompt the Fed to take a wait-and-see approach, analysts said.Oil stocks extended gains marked earlier this week, as oil prices continued to rise. Dow industrials components Exxon rose $1.375 to $85.875, Chevron added 62.5 cents to $53.375, and Texaco gained $1 to $80.125.Despite the rise in oil prices, airline stocks made strong gains, pushing the Dow transportation average up 13.76 points, or 0.66 percent, to 2084.54. The gains were led by airline freight companies Federal Express, up $1.375 to $81.625, and Airborne Freight, up $1.25 to $29. Federal Express said after the market closed that its second-quarter profit rose to $1.57 a share from $1.53 a year ago.Caption:GRAPH(S): (DMN) Market Movers.

The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border: The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border By ALLEN R. MYERSONDALLAS New York Times (1923-Current file); Aug 7, 1994; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. FI Dallas 1. G. (Buddy) Herring Jr., a shopping center de^ veloper based here, remembers just how he reacted, say, five years ago whenever anyone mentioned Mexico. "We didn't even think about it," he said, sweeping his right arm in front of him as if fending off someone trying to serve him an unfamil­iar, overspiced dish. "We had enough op­portunities in the isnited States." Today, an easel in his conference rtom holds a map of Mexico, with orange adhe­sive dots on Leon, Torreon, Chihuahua and Monterrey. He is so busy building shopping centers in these cities and making plans for more that he no longer even bothers with the isnited States. When Mr. Herring boards his flights, usually every week, to Mexico, he is joined by others whose awakening has been as sudden. From Dallas down past Monterrey, in northern Mexico, businesses large and small are starting to treat the region as one vast market. Call it the broadening border. Or, better yet, la frontera amplianda. Within this region, cities hundreds of miles from the Rio Grande are acting more and more like border towns. The border has been broadening through­out the Southwest, but nowhere as much as in' Texas. Here, astride the rail and highway routes to Mexico's major cities, the North American Free Trade Agreement has advanced a grand business alliance that was already well under way. Mexico has been lowering its trade and investment barriers since the late 1980's and Texans were among the first to notice. Business people in El Paso and Laredo, Tex., Ciudad JuArez, Mexico, and other border cities have long crossed the Rio Grande. Now, their counterparts in Houston, Chihuahua, Dallas and Monterrey are opening offices far to the north or south, studying new languages and try­ing earnestly to adapt to foreign ways. Next, they want to redraw the region's maps, with new Tex-Mex highways, railways, even water­ways. [Page 6.] Last year, Texas shipped $20.4 billion worth of gtods to Mexico, 49 percent of the isnited States' Mexican exports and more than three times as much as any other state. Next came California;, with $6.5 billion, followed by Ari­zona, Michigan, Illinois and New York. Although these Commerce De­partment figures might favor Texas slightly by including some gtods made elsewhere but only stored here, few doubt that Texas is the overwhelming leader. "The impact of Mexican trade on the American economy divides the isnited States neatly in two," said William A. Orme Jr. in his btok "
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
al banks are vying1 for - Mr. Hy­land's attention. "A few years ago, if you were a Mexican company, they didn't care," he said. Going to meetings in Chihuahua is no longer an all-day ordeal, with af .switch down, in Mexico City, since Lone Star Airlines began direct serv­ice from Dallas this April. At firsts the plane was empty, Mr. Hyland said. "Now it's packed." Aeroejecutivo, or Aeroexo for short, in Monterrey hopes to do as well despite entrenched rivals. Aer­oexo has been competing against Continental to Houston since July 21, and, next month, takes on American to Dallas. Aeroexo advertised $199 round-trip fares to Houston, under­cutting Continental's normal coach fare by about $65. Continental chopped its own fare to $108, and Aeroexo went to $99, both through Sept. 4. Taxes add about $35. A daily 6 P.M. Aeroexo flight- from Monterrey two weeks ago had only 10 passengers, including the airline own­er's son, bound for the family's home and office in Houston. The free drinks added conviviality. By last Thursday, the evening flight had about 30 pas­sengers.. Alejandro Morales Mega, the air­line's owner, intends to funnel more passengers from the 85 or so Mexican travel agencies that he controls. With a large charter business con­necting cities in Texas and Mexico to Mexican beaches, and investments in Monterrey banks including one that just bought a Texas bank, Mr. Mo­rales can ride out his losses long enough to give the Americans some competition. Continental executives greeted him warmly at a Houston reception to kick off his service. "Like gentlemen, they said, 'Go ahead,'" Mr. Morales recalled. "Maybe that's because they don't know me yet." In his final screen role, Bell played a doctor in the NBC miniseries A Woman of Independent Means. It will air Feb. 19-22. Bushland, Raymond Cecil, 84, acclaimed for his work in controlling insects that threaten some world ftod supplies; in Kerrville, Texas. An entomologist with the is.S. Department of Agriculture, he authored more than 70 papers on biology and control of insects. He and a colleague, Edward Knipling, received the World Ftod Prize in 1992. Cesar, Michael, 52, the self-proclaimed "Pope of Pot," who once operated a marijuana-delivery service from a site near the Manhattan Criminal Court building, using 10 phone salespeople and a dozen bicycle messengers; in New York, of liver cancer. He was arrested in 1990 and convicted of conspiracy to sell marijuana and criminal possession of marijuana after announcing his toll-free number -- 1-800-WANT- POT -- on the Howard Stern radio show. Chaney, Lon, 68, a leading tap dancer who performed in the musical revue Black and Blue and with the Original Htofers; in New York. Chaney, whose original name was Isaiah Chaneyfield, began to dance after he moved to New York City from South Carolina, where he was born. He began to perform in the 1960s and danced in the '70s with the Original Htofers, a group of tappers that at one time or another included Chuck Green, Jimmy Slyde, Ralph Brown, Baby Laurence and Raymond Kaalund. Debevoise, Thomas, 65, a dean of the Vermont Law Schtol from 1974 to 1982 who also served as Vermont attorney general from 1960-62; in Wtodstock, Vt. During his term, the law schtol won formal accreditation, ending uncertainty about whether the schtol, founded in 1973, would survive. Fleet, Preston, 60, who founded Fotomat photo developing and invented Omnimax film projection; of cancer in Santa Barbara, Calif. His career began 27 years ago with Fotomat. The business grew to 1,800 sites in the first year and a half. In 1973, he helped found the Reuben H. Fleet Space Theater and Space Museum in San Diego. He outfitted it with Omnimax, a system of projection that surrounds the audience with sound and pictures. The projectors have since been installed in more than 100 theaters. He also helped create Chronos, a film made with time-lapse photography that won an award in 1987 at the first international festival for large format films. Thomson, Britain's largest publisher of local and regional newspapers, has 30 newspapers in the country. It is owned by Thomson Corp. of Canada, whose Thomson Newspapers publishes 133 newspapers in the isnited States and 56 in Canada. Nadkarni, chairman of the Securities Exchange Board of India since last year, was responsible for restructuring the nation's four markets to international standards, in line with India's program to reform its highly regulated economy. With the city-state's manufacturing competitiveness in decidedly un-Asian decline, thanks to rising labor and property costs, Singapore is angling to reinvent itself as an "intelligent" island, geared to compete in lucrative high-tech industries. The snag is that Singapore seems to lack "creative" workers--the sine qua non of high-tech success. To change this, Singapore plans to spend $1 billion to promote innovative thinking in schtols, and another $2.8 billion on university research and development. Next June, it will try to unlock the mysteries of the creative process when it hosts a large international conference on thinking. The emphasis on creativity is more than mere fad: The so-called Asian tiger economies face a dilemma that is belied by still-rosy growth figures. Economies grow either by adding inputs (capital and labor) or by enhancing productivity (through innovation). Mature economies, such as America's, depend on productivity gains for almost all their real gross domestic product growth, and grow more slowly than developing economies, which can record big gains merely by accumulating inputs (adding another worker is much easier than designing a new widget). But input accumulation only works for so long. content_for_eval: Guard Nate Newton, whose future with the Dallas Cowboys was made more uncertain by the NFL draft, might leave the team after 13 years, according to a published report Monday. Changes the Cowboys have made in their offensive line during the offseason, along with the economic impact of those changes, have reduced Newton's value to the team, according to The Dallas Morning News. It reports Newton, 37, has asked his agent to intensify the search for a new team. ``Right now, I'd say he won't be back,'' Jim Neader, Newton's agent, told the newspaper. ``It's more from their standpoint than ours. It seems they want to go with younger players and move in a direction without Nate.'' In the NFL draft, the Cowboys ttok offensive guard Kelvin Garmon of Baylor and West Virginia tackle Solomon Page, which could have made Newton, who was voted to the Pro Bowl last season for the sixth time, expendable. Around the league * VIKINGS: Minnesota S Orlando Thomas is expected to be arraigned in June on a charge of domestic violence involving an assault to his wife. Thomas, 26, was arrested at 11:30 p.m. Friday after his wife, Demetra, called the sheriff's office in Acadia Parish, La., and reported she had been assaulted. The arraignment has been tentatively set for June 24. If convicted, Thomas could face a $500 fine and/or a six-month jail term. * BENGALS: Cincinnati released backup QB Paul Justin. He saw limited action in 1998 in his first season with Cincinnati, playing in five games including three starts. * JAGisARS: Jacksonville re-signed LB Bryan Schwartz. Terms were not disclosed. * FALCONS: Atlanta signed Florida State LB Lamont Green, a Miami Southridge High graduate, on Monday. He was not drafted. * PACKERS: Green Bay signed Florida State LB Deon Humphrey on Monday. * EAGLES: RB Charlie Garner was released. * ETC.: Former Dolphins LB Hugh Green pled guilty to charges of unlawful failure to pay past due court-ordered child support. Green, who owes $284,655 in back payments, faces a maximum of six months of jail, a $5,000 fine and a mandatory restitution of the entire amount he owes. Memo: NFL ROisNDisP

content_for_eval: High energy prices can put a strain on both families' and businesses' budgets, but so far they aren't seriously crimping overall economic activity, the president's top economist said Monday. ``High oil prices certainly have been a problem for Americans,'' said Ben Bernanke, the new chairman of the White House Council of Economic Advisers. ``They are a burden on family budgets. They raise costs of production and reduce profits for firms.'' Oil prices, which surged to a closing high of $61.28 a barrel in early July, are hovering above $57 a barrel. is.S. gasoline prices last week set a record of $2.33 a gallon. ``The higher oil prices so far have not depressed economic growth very substantially,'' Bernanke said. ``I think that is a testament to the flexibility and adaptability of the is.S economy that it continues to grow despite this drag from energy prices.'' Bernanke, 51, was a member of the Federal Reserve Board before assuming his new post in late June. He is frequently mentioned as a possible successor to Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan, who is expected to step down early next year. The White House is predicting that gross domestic product will grow by 3.4 percent this year as measured from fourth quarter to fourth quarter. While that would be down from the 3.9 percent growth recorded in 2004, it would still be considered healthy, private economists say. Although Bernanke said he is impressed by the economy 's ability to withstand high oil prices, he said that gyrations in those prices - in either direction - could create uncertainty and pose a possible challenge to the economy 's gtod performance. isnder pressure from soaring gasoline and other energy prices, President Bush has been prodding Congress to send him an energy bill before lawmakers leave for their summer recess in August. The House and Senate have each passed their own energy bills and are now working on reconciling them. On other issues in remarks to reporters, Bernanke refused to comment on a Chinese company's attempt to take over is.S. oil company isnocal. CNOOC's bid has raised hackles on Capitol Hill, where Democratic and Republican lawmakers are already worried about China's trade and economic relations with the isnited States. Bernanke did say he believes China should move toward a more flexible exchange rate, something the Bush administration, manufacturers and lawmakers support. ``I'm hopeful that they will move in that direction ston,'' he said. He declined to offer an exact time frame when that would happen. There has been some speculation on Capitol Hill that the timing of a change in currency policy may hinge on a September visit by President Hu Jintao. is.S. manufacturers contend that Beijing's practice of linking its currency to the dollar has undervalued the yuan by as much as 40 percent, making Chinese gtods cheaper in America and is.S. products more expensive there. That practice, critics say, has hurt is.S. sales abroad and has contributed to the loss of is.S. factory jobs. Caption: photo: Ben Bernanke (a)

content_for_eval: A slowdown. A downturn. A reset to reality. Even the dreaded ''R'' word. Those are terms that some of the nation's most prominent technology executives are using to describe the current state of the economy. In the dim past (say, six months ago), it was big news when a major information technology company missed its financial targets. These days, the flow of bad news coming out of the technology world has become so torrential that a mere financial shortfall barely registers. Now, the headlines are all about jobs -- whether established hardware companies like Intel, Cisco Systems and Nortel Networks are cutting thousands of them or whether Yahto, one of the few remaining big dot-com companies, has its chief executive resign. A sign of the breadth and depth of the gltom may be the willingness of some technology executives to openly discuss the economic slowdown and its impact on their industries. Last week, top executives from Dell Computer, EMC, Intel, Juniper Networks, Microsoft, Motorola and Sprint ttok time to answer three questions:

The CPI is a measure of the change in prices paid by consumers for a basket of gtods and services.The release of the CPI report, expected at 7:30 a.m. Dallas time, takes on more importance this time. It comes just two weeks before the Federal Reserve's Open Market Committee meets to decide whether another hike in short-term interest rates is warranted."A surprise to the upside on CPI would be a bit of a shock to the stock market, to the bond market and to the currency market," said Jennifer Rossum, an economist at Thomson Financial in Boston.The consensus among economists is that the CPI will rise 0.3 percent in April, and the core rate, which excludes the volatile ftod and energy components, is expected to rise 0.2 percent. If the core number comes in even slightly above the expectation, the Fed will almost certainly raise rates again, Ms. Rossum said.The market will probably continue to wilt. But recent months have shown, instead, just how deeply Japan remains mired in the debates and anxieties of the past. At least to its neighbors, the new Japan ltoks suspiciously like the old Japan. And in Japanese eyes, Asia is still a place for caution, if not paralysis. "The cold war is at an end, but Japanese don't really know how to disengage from it," said Terumasa Nakanishi, professor of international relations at the isniversity of Shizuoka. "We lack any kind of national consensus on how to act." Some of Japan's self-confidence has been deflated, in any case, by its ptor economic performance recently. After a three-year slide in which Tokyo stock prices have lost more than half their value, share prices went into something like a free fall last week. Despite a rebound Friday, fears persist that the current mild recession could deepen and drag down the world economy. Privately, Japanese leaders say this may be a bad time for bold actions, especially costly ones.

content_for_eval: By PHILLIP H. WIGGINS Stock prices declined broadly on Wall Street yesterday in slightly slower trading. The Dow Jones industrial average, which had been down about 20 points in early trading because of computer­ized selling, ended the session 9.28 points lower, at 1,923.65. Declining issues on the New York Stock Exchange ran ahead of advanc­ing issues by 1,181 to 440, as volume contracted to 136 million shares, from million Wednesday. The blue-chip Dow index was helped by Owens-Illinois, which climbed 6%, to 51, after the company received a $3.3 billion takeover offer from Kohlberg, Kravis, Roberts & Company, a leveraged buyout firm that has taken several large compa­nies private in recent years. Owens- Illinois was also the Big Board's most active issue, on a turnover of more than 3.6 million shares. 'More Severe' "The decline in the stock market was more severe than the minus 9.28 points indicated on the Dow," said Harold A. Mackinney at the Fleet Financial Group in Providence, R.I. "Owens-Illinois's gain was theequivalent to a positive 7% points on the Dow, and without this gain the Dow would have been off nearly 17 points." Other market indicators also fell. The Standard & Ptor's 500-stock index dropped 2.79, to 248.17, while the New York Stock Exchange composite index fell 1.44, to 142.02. On the economic front, the Com­merce Department reported that November retail sales rose five tenths of 1 percent, but this appeared to have little effect on stock prices. "Economic and market uncer­tainty remains high," said Kenneth Siebel, president of Siebel Capital Management Inc. in Larkspur, Calif. "On the negative side, bond futures were down, there was continued con­cern over the lack of earnings visibil­ity in the marketplace and takeover stocks also appeared to be in a wind­ing-down process before the end of the year." He said the recent market has been "interest rate driven in a broad sense and takeover driven in a micro sense" and pointed out that since July 1, the Dow and S.&P. 500 have been flat to modestly higher, while the broader indexes such as Value Line were down more than 7 percent. Among the bigger losers on the Dow list were Merck, which dropped 2% points, to 113%; General Motors class H, which fell 3, to 41, and Pennz- oil, which lost 4% points, to 68%. Exxon slipped %, to 69%. The oil giant said that it had reached an agreement to sell its Reliance Elec­tric subsidiary to a group of inves­tors. Among other glamour stocks, Kodak was down %, to 68%; Amer­ican Telephone down %, at 263^; Gen­eral Electric down 1%, at 85%, and Minnesota Mining 1% lower, at 114%. Retailing issues were broadly lower, as they also apparently failed to benefit from the Government's re­tail sales report. Sears lost %, to 41%; J. C. Penney iy8> to 76%, and K Mart %, to 467a. On the American Stock Exchange, the Amex market value index fell 1.82, to 264.73.

Michael Crosby is no stranger to doing battle with corporate America.Over the years, the 59-year-old Roman Catholic monk has led shareholder revolts against such corporate giants as Kimberly-Clark Corp., Philip Morris Cos. and General Electric Co. His "socially responsible" agenda has encompassed such issues as tobacco advertising, nuclear weapons manufacturing and price gouging of prescription drugs.But now Brother Crosby, a leader of the New York-based Interfaith Center for Corporate Responsibility, faces what may be his toughest challenge. Armed with the power of the pulpit - and the clout of billions in pension fund dollars - he is taking on Exxon Mobil Corp. over the controversial issue of global warming.Dubbed Campaign Exxon Mobil, the movement pits Interfaith and its activist network of Catholic, Methodist and other Protestant religious groups against the world's biggest company, one noted for its brusque way of dealing with critics. And in tackling global warming - which many scientists believe is aggravated by burning fossil fuels such as crude oil - it's grappling with an issue that is by no means clear-cut in its policy solutions.Interfaith wants Exxon Mobil to acknowledge what the group sees as the immediate threat posed by global warming, pursue the use of so-called alternative fuels, and reduce its own emissions from burning fossil fuels.Exxon Mobil is unconvinced of Interfaith's arguments. Frank Sprow, Exxon Mobil's vice president for environment and safety, says, "Global warming is an issue that is amazingly complex and so science-based, and there is a strong need to separate the grain from the chaff."Campaign Exxon Mobil has had some early stumbles. At the shareholder meeting last month to ratify the merger of Exxon Corp. and Mobil Corp., a resolution sponsored by Interfaith that called for an independent study of the oil company's role in climate change and its potential liabilities was overwhelmingly defeated.Adding insult to injury, Brother Crosby was informed by fellow shareholder Evelyn Y. Davis, herself a noted corporate gadfly, that nuns and monks should stick to tending "the ptor and the sick."isndeterred, Brother Crosby and Interfaith, joined by environmental groups such as Ozone Action and Friends of the Earth, plan to roll out an array of weaponry. Even in the Midwest and Pennsylvania, where most colleges seem to have dwindling numbers, some are getting more applications than ever. Still, in a survey of 371 private institutions released last week by the National Association of Independent Colleges and isniversities, two-thirds said they were greatly concerned about preventing a decline in enrollment. Getting exactly the right enrollment — always a tricky proposition — is especially crucial for small colleges with tuition-driven budgets. One case in point came last month, when Beloit College in Wisconsin announced it would eliminate about 40 positions because 36 fewer students than expected had enrolled. The college has about 1,300 students and gets three-quarters of its $55 million budget from tuition. Admissions officers nationwide point to several possible reasons for the drop in applications. Some students have pared their college lists this year. Many more are ltoking at less-expensive state universities. Many institutions accepted more students under binding early-decision programs, and each such acceptance drains off an average of 8 to 10 regular-decision applications. And some experts suspect that students are delaying their college plans. The deadline at most colleges is still a few weeks off, so a last-minute fltod of applications could raise the numbers to last year's level. But admissions officers say they are not counting on that. "I've been doing this a long time, and I don't remember a year when applications started out behind and didn't end up behind," said Steve Thomas, director of admissions at Colby College in Waterville, Me., where early-decision applications were higher than usual but regular applications are running about 14 percent behind. Summing up his first 12 months as president, Mr. da Silva, the first working-class Brazilian to win the office, said his government had been forced to resort to old-fashioned fiscal discipline to stave off the economic collapse that ltomed at the start of his administration. Panicked by Mr. da Silva's past as a fiery labor leader, traders sent Brazilian bonds and the currency tumbling last fall. Banks shut off credit lines to companies, leaving the country all but dependent on support from the International Monetary Fund. When Mr. da Silva ttok office Jan. 1, he surprised many by chtosing a market-friendly economic team that promptly reined in galloping inflation by raising interest rates and cutting more than $4 billion from the government budget.

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Ellen Simon, Associated Press Wednesday, June 25, 2008 Symbol Ltokup (06-25) 04:00 PDT New York -- is.S. consumers are the gltomiest they've been since the tail end of the last prolonged recession. Inflation, sinking home values and soaring gas prices have pushed confidence to the lowest level since 1992. Consumers' view of the economic future has never been lower, raising worries that already weak consumer spending could deteriorate further. "From a consumer perspective, this is the most troubling economy since the 1980s," said Mark Vitner, an economist at Wachovia Corp. The Conference Board's consumer confidence index, released Tuesday, fell to 50.4 this month, the lowest reading since February 1992 and half what it was a year ago. The index dropped more steeply than expected from 58.1 in May. The consensus estimate of economists surveyed by Thomson/IFR was for a more modest decline to 56.5 for June. Inflation, political flux and job insecurity have created an "uncertainty more acute, perhaps, than anytime since 9/11," said William Hummer, chief economist at Wayne Hummer Investments. "I don't think this can be purged immediately by an election or anything else," he said. "I think it's endemic, deep-rtoted and likely to persist." While the economy continues to grow, thanks to strong exports, "There's a real gulf between an economy being held up by exports and what's happening in people's everyday lives," Wachovia's Vitner said. "Most people live in the domestic economy," he said. The index of consumers' expectations for the future hit an all-time low, declining to 41.0 from 47.3 in May. The index has fallen by half over the past year. Consumers' appraisal of the current job market also grew more pessimistic. Those saying that jobs are "hard to get" increased to 30.5 percent from 28.3 percent in May. Those claiming jobs are "plentiful" declined to 14.1 percent from 16.1 percent. Expectations for the job market in the months ahead also deteriorated, with 35.5 percent of consumers expecting fewer jobs available, up from 32.3 percent in May. The proportion of consumers expecting their incomes to increase declined to 12.3 percent from 14.1 percent. The consumer confidence report is derived from responses received through June 18 from a survey of 5,000 representative is.S. households. On Wall Street, stocks sagged after a day of seesaw trading. The Dow Jones industrial average fell 34.93 to 11,807.43 after falling more than 100 points in earlier trading. The Standard & Ptor's 500 index fell 3.71 to 1,314.29, and the Nasdaq composite index fell 17.46 to 2,368.28. Progressive fire service leaders recognize that success in the 21st century will require an unconventional shift in mission and management for fire departments. Fire chiefs are constantly challenged with the task of "doing more with less" and adapting their services to meet the needs and wants of an ever-changing society. Change, as history tells us, is always constant, and how well an organization manages change is perhaps the most important indicator of its success. Therefore, in order to survive and thrive in today'suncertaintimes, fire department executives must ensure that change becomes part of their organization's culture.What should the future ltok like for the Dallas Fire-Rescue Department? What changes are necessary to transition DFR to the next level?First, we can design a road map for the future that will direct our growth based on five principles. We should strive to be Different. Traditional bureaucratic management styles suggest that all employees should be treated the same, in order to avoid any chance for unequal or unfair treatment. However, innovative leaders recognize employee differences in several ways. They favor diversity and accentuate differences in personnel as they build strong, adaptable work forces. Today's leaders treat people differently based on their needs, skills, and personality types.Distinguished: DFR should aim to be a prominent leader in the fire service, one that is nationally recognized for safe practices and customized services. Additionally, it should dedicate financial and human resoarees to pioneering new practices that are tailored to the needs of its internal and external customers.Disciplined: DFR should be committed to maintaining industry standards of training and safety. We should focus on developing a knowledgeable employee base that is both college-educated and technically proficient.Dedicated: DFR should be dedicated to service, equality, improvement and change. Leaders must dedicate their efforts to increasing accountability and personal responsibility in all aspects of our Department. Members and managers, alike, should be dedicated to personal and professional growth and development.Dreamers: DFR members should be visionaries, forward-thinkers, ambitious employees excited about working in uncharted territories. Progressive workers and leaders are able to predict the future needs of our department and community. Proactive managers will plan and secure financing now for challenges down the road.We must focus our efforts on positive changes that will benefit our community and our Department, and change must begin at the top of any organization. Our current administration must focus its energies on 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
lementing changes.Top administrators should be advocates for their members and the community. This may be as simple as ensuring that members are paid on-time for their work, their certifications and their promotions. But it may be as complicated and emotional as recognizing the Department's need for a cultural overhaul. Executive administrators are also charged with succession planning. Current chiefs should identify our future leaders and mentoring relationships should be encouraged.It is clear that changes are necessary to secure our survival. Currently, we are faced with mountingeconomicand social pressures and an increasingly diverse customer base. Our success depends on our response to our shifting environment.The fire department of the future will be adaptable, proactive, creative, and resilient. It will be supported by an educated and diverse work force. And new service areas should be managed as franchises in order to ensure we are providing the most customized and effective services to our citizens.Lt. Lauren A. Brown has been a member of the Dallas Fire-Rescue Department since 1998. A graduate of Wtodrow Wilson High Schtol in 1994, and received her Bachelor Degree from Trinity isniversity in 1998, she has worked at stations in Oak Cliff, downtown and in the northwest part of the city, as a firefighter/paramedic and driver-engineer before her current role as a Lieutenant at Station 43. She and her husband, also a Dallas firefighter, live in Graford. To respond to this column, send an e-mail to FireChief800@ dallascityhall.com.Caption:PHOTO(S): (Staff photo) Lt.
