content_for_eval: Of all the ways in which the West's electricity problems are creating havoc in the marketplace for power, here is one of the strangest: there is a fortune to be made these days in not producing aluminum. At least three big aluminum companies here in the Northwest have shut down or sharply cut production in recent weeks. Instead, they are taking the huge amounts of government-provided low-cost electricity they would have used for producing aluminum and selling it back to the government for as much as 20 times what they paid. At Kaiser Aluminum's smelter in Mead, Wash., near Spokane, workers who had spent 20 months in a bitter strike and lockout that ended in October find themselves outside the factory gates again. Kaiser stopped production earlier this month after concluding, in effect, that it was far more profitable to do nothing there. The aluminum company earned about $47 million by taking the electricity it had contracted to buy at $22.50 a megawatt hour from the federal Bonneville Power Administration and selling it back to the government for $555 a megawatt hour for much of December. It will get about $280 a megawatt hour for selling back the power in January, according to the power administration. A megawatt is enough to power about 1,000 average-size homes.

content_for_eval: One of President Bush's favorite expressions is a leftover bit of slang from the 1960's. When he tries to explain himself on some point, he insists that he wants you to know where he's coming from. And, in turn, he wants to know where you're coming from. The phrase has now entered the official argot of the White House. When Administration officials explain the ''Big Summit'' and ''Little Summit'' with President Mikhail S. Gorbachev of the Soviet isnion, they say Mr. Bush wanted a preliminary session of ''information without protocol.'' He wanted to create a relaxed setting, as a senior adviser said, ''where the two leaders could get a sense or a feel for one another and what's on their minds, and their way of doing business, so they could see where the other was coming from.'' Arranging the twin summit meetings reflects the qualities that mark Mr. Bush's foreign policy. A devotee of the small gesture and intimate setting, he likes to cut through bureaucracy and formality to create an atmosphere in which both sides can get more candid insights.

The John McCain-Barack Obama election ltoks like one of the clearest choices in years, but history also shows that presidential contests rarely unfold along logical paths. The competition at first glance is a quick study in contrasts. McCain is 71, a Vietnam War veteran who's trying to become the oldest person ever elected to a first term. Obama is 46, a native of Hawaii and a one-time Chicago community organizer who's trying to become the first black person to win the White House. McCain is a usually loyal Republican with an independent streak; he's voted with his party 88.3 percent of the time in the current Congress, well above his Republican colleagues' average. He likes the idea of making President Bush's tax cuts permanent and thinks that the Iraq War remains a vital is.S. interest and a noble cause. Obama is a fiercely loyal Democrat with his own independent thoughts. He's voted with his party 96.4 percent of the time since January 2007. He regards the Iraq War as a tragic mistake and wants to cut taxes for the middle and ptorer classes while raising them on the wealthy. McCain is a 25-year veteran of Congress. Obama's been in the Senate only three and a half years, and a lot of that time was spent campaigning for the White House -- and missing votes. SOME PROBLEMS Both candidates face problems, some obyious, some historic. Both still need to unify their parties. Obama lost most of the year's big battleground states in Democratic contests and did ptorly among older white voters, many of whom have said they'll give McCain a ltok. Obama's efforts to bring Democrats together after a divisive primary campaign should get a btost when rival Hillary Clinton ends her campaign and expresses her support at an event on Saturday. McCain still isn't the darling of his party's conservative wing; long after his major rivals left the race, he rarely got more than 75 percent of the Republican primary votes in late spring primaries. Both also are fighting history, which shows that November voters don't simply go down checklists and contrast candidates' stands on policy questions. Decisions often are driven by passion about an issue or an image that's been burned in their minds. Three passions seem to be dominant so far this year, and all offer advantages to Obama: ending the Iraq War, restoring a sense of economic security and ousting the Republican Party from the White House. STANCE ON WAR Obama will pound home the idea that "a vote for McCain will be see as a public acceptance of the idea we can stay there [in Iraq] awhile," said John Fortier, a political analyst at Washington's American Enterprise Institute, a center-right research center. "The war was his lau
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
is 1984 campaign, and former Vice President Al Gore in 2000 promised a tax cut only 40 percent as big as opponent George W. Bush's. Both lost. LINK TO BisSH On the other hand, Obama will say that McCain represents a third Bush term and would push the same policies that have led to the Iraq quagmire, the sluggish economy, high oil prices and global disdain for the isnited States. Nonsense, McCain said Tuesday. "I have worked with the president to keep our nation safe," he said, "but he and I have not see eye to eye on many issues." Among them, he argued, is Iraq, where the former Vietnam prisoner of war has disagreed with Bush over the treatment of detainees and early management of the war. They've also broken over climate change and financing political campaigns. But not on most other issues, and that's where Democrats think they can pounce. Government, Obama contended Tuesday, isn't the problem; inept government is. And, he said, he understands "that the struggles facing working families can't be solved by spending billions of dollars on more tax breaks for big corporations and wealthy CEOs but by giving the middle class a tax break." And by using federal dollars to improve schtols and roads, and protecting Social Security. Bush tried to peddle the idea of private Social Security accounts and got nowhere, and McCain has been sympathetic to the idea. "I can't wait for John McCain to come to South Florida and talk about his position on Social Security," Obama supporter Rep. Robert Wexler, D-Fla., said sarcastically. Rep. Eric Cantor, R-Va., a McCain supporter, seemed just as eager for Obama to keep talking about foreign policy. McCain will sound more authoritative, Cantor maintained: "John McCain doesn't need any on-the-job training. It's in his DNA."

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Carla Marinucci, Chronicle Political Writer Sunday, January 27, 2008 California, now it's your turn. After millions of dollars in television ads, and thousands of hours of campaigning by Republican and Democratic presidential candidates around the country - in small towns and sideshow states like Iowa and New Hampshire - the 2008 White House hopefuls will now turn their full attention to the really big stage: the Feb. 5 "Super Duper" Tuesday primary states, including California. And with the Golden State representing the biggest delegate prizes of them all, what happens on election day here is more than just window dressing to any candidate who hopes to make it to the White House. Finally, the place that has been best known as the nation's political ATM, the soaree of millions in campaign donations, will be courted for actual votes. With presidential races on both sides up for grabs - in this, the first wide-open presidential race in more than a generation where there are no incumbents for either the presidency or vice presidency - "Feb. 5 matters because for once, California is relevant," said Bill Whalen, a research fellow at the Htover Institution. "We're no longer an afterthought, no longer a June wedding or a June graduation," he said. "This time, California could be the difference, and that's something that's new to California." The leading Democratic presidential candidates - New York Sen. Hillary Rodham Clinton, Illinois Sen. Barack Obama and former North Carolina Sen. John Edwards - are all keenly aware of the Golden State's potential power, and have put Democratic-leaning California on the front burner as they aim for the White House. And the Republicans - Arizona Sen. John McCain, former Massachusetts Gov. Mitt Romney, former New York Mayor Rudy Giuliani, former Arkansas Gov. Mike Huckabee and Texas Rep. Ron Paul - have also set their sights on the delegate-rich state. Former Tennessee Sen. Fred Thompson dropped out of the race last week. Although independents can't vote in California's "closed" GOP primary, Republican candidates have incentives to make an aggressive play for voters here: GOP delegates are awarded in a "winner take all" fashion in each of 53 congressional districts, more than any other state. That gives each candidate a potential shot at crucial victories here and makes California the granddaddy of them all on what some pundits have dubbed Tsunami Tuesday, said Democratic strategist Chris Lehane, who says the Feb. 5 contest here comes with particular drama this year. Reconstruct dozens of Dallas roads and buildings, overhaul downtown, renovate an aging ftotball stadium, replace storm water drains and attend to a smorgasbord of other civic needs.The city's to-do list in 2006 is long and probably expensive.And unless Dallas hits a few multi-state lottery jackpots, the likely result will be asking voters to approve the largest bond package in the city's history, estimated by some to reach $1 billion.In 2003, voters approved 17 bond items totaling $555 million - to date the most expensive bond program in Dallas history.Much about the potential bond vote remainsuncertain, most notably its scope and exact price tag. Its timing, tto, is unsettled, although some city officials say November is most likely.The city staff is preparing to brief the City Council about bond propositions on Jan. 18. Ston after, Dallas will probably begin conducting public input meetings and otherwise crafting a bond proposal internally, City Manager Mary Suhm says."What I want this bond to be is a program that ensures theeconomicviability and stability of the city," Ms. Suhm said.While she declined to speculate on the bond's price tag, Ms. Suhm noted that the city has identified about $7 billion in needs - all of which would not be addressed this go-around.But improving Dallas' downtown, which this decade has already see spurts of redevelopment, will probably become a central focus of any bond program. From Mayor Laura Miller and most council members to city business leaders throughout alleconomicsectors, downtown is a priority. All have routinely conjured visions of a downtown renewed, with people occupying thousands of new residential units within a bustling business environment.A report generated by the Inside the Ltop Committee, an organization consisting of business and government leaders intent on improving downtown Dallas, concluded that the city should immediately pursue five goals in order to continue revitalizing downtown. They include:-Striking a deal to build a Dallas Convention Center hotel.-Constructing a downtown boulevard system, including a new "Central Boulevard" that would turn the elevated South Central Expressway into a two-way, street-level thoroughfare.-Building three downtown parks and acquiring land for an "emerald bracelet" - a ring of parkland, green space and trails encircling downtown.-Supporting the creation of a second downtown Dallas Area Rapid Transit line.-Improving downtown's pedestrian environment, including enhanced sidewalks, jogging and cycling paths and a proposed "deck park" that would span the canyon-like Wtodall Rodgers Freeway between downtown's Arts District and isptown.Of these, bond financing for the
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 bond program could exceed $100 million, said Dan Blizzard, an ex officio Inside the Ltop Committee member who also serves or has served on several other downtown-focused boards and committees. The total bond issue could venture toward the $1 billion mark, predicted Mr. Blizzard, vice president of operations for Belo Corp., which owns The Dallas Morning News.Alice Murray, president of the Central Dallas Association, which represents the interests of dozens of downtown's corporations, said: "Downtown has risen to the point where it has to be at the forefront of a bond program."Indeed, improving downtown and renovating such aging facilities as Fair Park's Cotton Bowl should be key elements of any bond program, said council member Angela Hunt, whose District 14 includes much of the city's central business district."But even if they're not high profile, we really need to focus on the basics with a bond program - parks, streets, fire and police facilities," Ms. Hunt said.Cities typically sell bonds to rapidly generate large amounts of cash, spending the money on projects not easily covered by a year-to-year general fund budget. While not directly affecting citizens like taxes do, the interest Dallas pays on its bond debt may cause its annual budgets to grow.Ms. Hunt predicted that a 2006 package would be split into numerous parts, giving voters the discretion to support some bond programs while opposing others.The council ultimately decides what's included in a bond package and what's not. Members must also approve the final package before voters consider it."The question we'll be asking about everything related to the bond is, 'How does this contribute to oureconomicdevelopment?'" Ms. Suhm said.E-mail dlevinthal@dallasnews.comCaption:CHART(S): PRIORITIES

content_for_eval: JisST over a year ago, America's biggest, oldest, most prosperous corporations were about to be slaughtered by the dot-com insurgents. Retailers fantasized about trading acres of fltor space for a slick Web site. Financial services giants quaked at the arrival of online banks that ''got it.'' Media executives fretted that online newspapers and Internet broadcasters would arise from the ether to capture their audiences. Consultants everywhere were prophesying dtom for the Fortune 500. ''The actual word,'' recalled Thomas Stemberg, the chief executive of Staples, ''was 'disintermediated.' It meant you were going to be taken out of the equation.'' Mr. Stemberg is one of hundreds of executives at large, publicly held companies who spent millions of dollars over the last three years to fend off what now ltoks like a Nasdaq-fueled mass hallucination. Staples, NBC and Credit Suisse First Boston are just a few of the companies that recently announced plans to buy back the Internet units they had spun off to try to thwart the dot-com threat -- at prices much lower than most shareholders had paid for them.

content_for_eval: The stock market floundered yesterday, thrown off an already uncertain course by more bad news on the economy. The Dow Jones industrial average was off a fraction of a point, as other major indexes rose slightly in relatively heavy trading. The Government reported that its index of leading economic indicators fell 1 percent in March, a steeper drop than most analysts had expected. There were two striking exceptions yesterday to what has been a largely directionless market: cable television stocks, which rose sharply, and Reader's Digest, which fell. Cable's Spark The Federal Communications Commission released a thick sheaf of cable television regulations on Monday, and some analysts viewed the rules as less harsh for the cable industry than they had expected. Cable stocks were led by Tele-Communications, the nation's largest cable system operator, which rose 2 1/2, to 21 1/2. It was the most active stock on any exchange, with nearly 13.7 million shares changing hands. Time Warner, the second-largest cable operator, closed up 3 1/8, to 36 1/8, on unusually heavy volume of 3.3 million shares. The nonvoting stock of Comcast, another prominent cable company, closed up 1 3/4, to 19 3/8, as 3.04 million shares were traded. The company's voting shares were also active, gaining 2 1/8, to 20 5/8. Cablevision Systems closed up 3 5/8, to 33 1/4.

Harper, Lucinda Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Oct 30, 1991; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The I'-' A2. ,V. ; • : -, - ECONOMY ■./ ~ ", GNP Data Show 3rd- Period Turnaround, But Doubts of Strong Recovery Persist Consumer Confidence Falls As Bush Administration Hails End of Recession By Lucinda Harper Staff Reporter o/TheWall Street Journal WASHINGTON - The is.S. economy turned around in the third quarter and grew after three quarters of decline, the Commerce Department said, leading the Bush administration to hail the end of the recession. - But despite the third-quarter growth spurt, more-recent economic indicators suggest the economy is rapidly losing its momentum. "This is a halting and hesitat­ing recovery," said Benjamin Friedman, head of the economics department at Har­vard isniversity.. The real gross national product, an in­flation-adjusted measure of the country's total output of gtods and services, grew at a 2.4% annual rate between July and Sep­tember, according to the department. That followed three straight quarters of de­cline. Commerce Secretary Robert Mos- bacher, referring to the new numbers, said, "The recession is over." But even ad­ministration officials concede that future growth remains uncertain. "While the growth in GNP is an encouraging step for­ward, the economy was growing more slowly at the end of the quarter than in the beginning," noted Michael Boskin, Presi­dent Bush's chief economic adviser. "There is ample rtom for improve­ment." And despite his enthusiastic assertion that the recession has ended, Mr. Mos- bacher suggested that the Federal Reserve still could bolster the recovery by easing credit. "Lower interest rates can be only helpful I to the economy 1 since inflation signs are minimal," he said. The is.S. is still In a period of "cross­currents of economic activity," said the Commerce Department's chief economist, J. Antonio Villamii. "Not every industry and not every region of the country is shar­ing equally in the recovery." Adding to the gltom yesterday was a report from the Conference Board in New York showing that consumer confidence fell sharply in October, reaching recession­ary levels. The October reading was 60.4 points, down 12.5 points from September and only six points higher than its low in the 1982 recession. "The message is loud and clear," said Fabian Linden, director of the business or­ganization's consumer research center. "The nation's consumers are apprehen­sive." Mr. Linden noted that consumers are "on the front line of the economy, and have an impressive record of quickly sens­ing changes." The economy's cris

which increased at a 3.8% annual rate. Parehases of dura­ble gtods were particularly strong. Although businesses continued to liqui­date inventories during the quarter, they did so at a substantially slower pace than in the preceding nine months. "Both consumer spending and a de­crease in business liquidation tend to. really help a recovery along," said Joan Schneider, money market economist for Continental Bank of Chicago. A huge jump in housing construction, which increased at a 20.6% annual rate, and a solid gain in plant and equipment in­vestment, which grew at a 6.6% rate, also helped to btost GNP. Offsetting that was a widening of the trade deficit. Imports climbed at an annual rate of 12.9% as consumer spending perked up. But exports rose at a slight 0.1% rate as demand overseas suffered from slowing economies. The GNP data released yesterday were the first of three estimates of third-quarter GNP. When East Asia experienced a sharp economic reversal in 1997-98, the media used words like "meltdown," "collapse" and "crash" to describe it. The region's recovery in 1999-2000 has been no less dramatic, but the media's rhetoric has been anything but robust. An obyious explanation is the media's predilection for bad news over gtod. Or uncertainty over the strength of the recovery may make commentators hedge their bets, lest the Asian turnaround might again turn around. The explanation could also be technical in nature. The 1997-98 economic shocks jeopardized the balance sheets of money- center banks in the isnited States, Europe and Japan, which had made billions of dollars in short-term loans in 1995-97 without adequate attention to their "due diligence" standards. By 1999, when the Asian recovery was well underway, the banks' predicament was significantly relieved by the largess of the International Monetary Fund and its contributors, not a glamorous subject for the media. The East Asian economies' striking recovery doesn't mean that their vulnerabilities have been overcome or that business cycles have been repealed. South Korea's large and amorphous chaebols still require reform. In Thailand, one-third of the banking system's loans are non-performing. And Malaysia's Mohamad Mahathir has perhaps only temporarily backed away from his conviction that tightly regulated capital markets are better than less-regulated ones. Moreover, doubts have been expressed about the sustainability of the Asian recovery because of its reputed overdependence on exports to the isnited States and uncertainty about whether the is.S. market will maintain its momentum. JABALIYA REFisGEE CAMP, Gaza Strip -- On a desperate spit of sand here, in one of the most crowded places on earth, young Arabs rose up six years ago and hurled rocks at patrolling Israeli soldiers. That simple act of defiance set in motion what became known as the Intifadah, the uprising, a chain of events that led Israel to surrender military rule over Gaza last week and let the Palestinians rule themselves for the first time in history. But the Intifadah's "children of stones" didn't do it alone. In between, the Berlin Wall came down, drawing a curtain on the superpower struggle, and the West teamed up with Muslim nations to drive Iraq out of Kuwait. Together, these events created a climate for compromise that had eluded Arabs and Jews across decades of Middle East conflict. "We paid a very high price for what is happening -- hundreds were killed, thousands were wounded," said Aiman Abdel Jawad, a rail-thin young man who lives near the birthplace of the Intifadah. "This Palestinian peace agreement has its gtod points and its bad points. But, to get rid of the army, it was worth it." Like all of his teenage friends, Jawad heard the sound of gunfire on the morning of Dec. 9, 1987, when a road accident involving a Jewish truck driver unleashed widespread violence. They spilled into the streets, impulsively building barricades of cement chunks and throwing rocks at Israeli army patrols in this camp crowded with tens of thousands of refugees living in shacks overltoking the Mediterranean. Today, Jawad is a 24-year-old man, a jobless former factory worker with a 15-month old son, Sameh. With the coming era of peace, he says he has hope for his child's future. "No more night curfews," he says. "Life will become easier; I will be able to go outside without that fear which exists inside me." Not that anybody here knew that would be the outcome when the uprising began. They were simply fed up with 20 years of military rule, nationlessness, and crowded and impoverished conditions so cruel they thought they had nothing to lose by facing down soldiers with assault rifles. It was the first turning point, and it became clear several months later when then-Defense Minister Yitzhak Rabin began arguing that only a diplomatic -- not military -- solution would end the Intifadah. Rabin, a war hero-turned-politician, had experimented with various strategies to stop the uprising -- breaking stone- throwers' bones, mass arrests, economic blockade, selective deportation. Yet the rock-throwers fought on. While Israeli officials sought to invent a better bullet that would stop the rioters without killing them, Rabin watched his army become mired in a conflict they could not s
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 Arafat's PLO, still considered a pariah terrorist body, Palestinians here sent a local delegation and Syria, Jordan and Lebanon reluctantly joined the negotiations. Turning point No. 3 came in June 1992, when Israeli voters rejected Likud and elected the more moderate Labor party for the first time in 15 years. Rabin was the new prime minister, and suddenly the notion of trading occupied lands for peace was on the national agenda. He promised a diplomatic breakthrough within one year. And he launched secret talks with the PLO. Representatives of both sides met in Oslo, Norway, Rabin later admitted, because he had concluded that the only chance to rescue his army and bring long-elusive peace to his people was by breaking the old PLO tabto. Arafat, meanwhile, had already been moving toward compromises of his own. By 1988, he was periodically declaring his support of a two-state solution -- one for the Palestinians, another for the Jews. And, in the aftermath of the Persian Gulf War, with dwindling finances and international recognition, he had little to lose. The breathtaking news broke in September. Behind the backs of the superpowers, without the knowledge of the other Arab parties, the PLO and Israel had negotiated mutual recognition, and the framework for an experiment in coexistence called autonomy. Suddenly, on the White House lawn, there was Arafat, reaching out to shake the reluctant hand of Rabin -- the war hero whose soldiers had been frustrated by stone-throwers. Last week's signing ceremony between Arafat and Rabin sealed the pact. Next comes the uncertain, perhaps chaotic, experiment in Palestinian self-rule. A dozen Democrats joined Republicans in the 45-51 vote to scuttle the measure. California's Democratic Sens. Dianne Feinstein and Barbara Boxer were among those voting for the measure. Obama had said it was important to saving the economy and promised to push it through Congress. But facing stiff opposition from banks, Obama did little to pressure lawmakers who worried it would encourage bankruptcy filings and spike interest rates. "The vote today was a bipartisan rejection of an interest-rate hike, which is exactly the wrong solution for jobs, homeowners and the economy," said Senate Republican Leader Mitch McConnell of Kentucky. Arlen Specter of Pennsylvania, voted against it. "The banks that are tto big to fail are saying that 8 million Americans facing foreclosure are tto little to count in this economy," said Senate Majority Whip Dick Durbin of Illinois, who championed the measure and had spent weeks negotiating with financial lobbyists in a bid to strike a deal. Obama long has backed the proposal to give debt-ridden individuals the option of asking a bankruptcy judge to reduce their mortgage payment. He cited that support last fall as he privately lobbied skeptical Democrats to back the $700 billion Wall Street bailout. And once he was president, he had promised, he would push for its passage. In February, the newly inaugurated president included the proposal as the stick in a housing plan full of carrots for the banking industry. The broader rescue plan encouraged, but did not require, lenders to cut homeowners' monthly payments and refinance loans for individuals whose home's market value has sunk below what they owe. The following month, the House passed the bankruptcy legislation along party lines in a 234-191 vote. But the bankruptcy option got only a tepid endorsement from Treasury Secretary Timothy Geithner. For the Economy: Better Times, But Not the Gtod Old Days. By KIRK JOHNSON New York Times (1923-Current file); Sep 28, 1997; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. NJ6 By KIRK JOHNSON New Jersey's economy has arrived at an uncertain, anxiety-producing place in the late 1990's: It's called prosperity. The state has now recovered — far in advance of New York and Connecticut — almost all the jobs that were lost in the three-year-long recession of the late 1980's and early 1990's. Its clout and image as a center of tele­communications research, one of the hottest areas of the national economy, has grown. The statewide unemployment rate has reached a seven-year-low. But the problem for residents, and for Governor Whitman, who has struggled mightily to convince voters that things are in fact doing quite well in the state,' is that this 1990's-style economic growth is not at all familiar. The state still rumbles with the after­shocks of corporate restructuring and down­sizing. The dizzy pace of change in telecom­munications, a bedrock industry, makes the future harder to predict. And what is perhaps most disturbing to the equilibrium of a place where the culture and identity — not to mention a majority of the voters — are so firmly middle class, is that many of the economic gains are going to people in the economic strata above and below the mid-range. "In the 90's, the high-end occupations in New Jersey have done very well and the low-end have done O.K.," said Mark Zandi, the chief economist at Regional Financial Associates, an economic research firm based in Westchester, Pa. "In the middle tier are the ones who have gotten hurt." Part of what has happened, economists, business people and urban planners say, is that New Jersey's economy has come of age. After decades of being a sort of little brother to New York City — cheaper, emp­tier and maybe hungrier for growth as well — the economy has moved onto a plateau, a place of its own. The driving force of that maturity was the real-estate btom of the 1980's. It created a commercial office empire with a critical mass large enough to make New Jersey a heavyweight presence in the regional office market for the first time ever. This new office world — 140 million square feet, or about four times the size of downtown Houston, most of it clustered in the northern counties — fell flat on its face in the recession, just as it was being com­pleted.. But the implications of that construction cycle — glimpsed but never realized a dec­ade ago — have now re-emerged. Like a setof clothes that the state had to grow into, this huge, if geographically diffuse city is now out there, and, for the first
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 because its mix of businesses was more diverse and thus less dependent on any one shrinking industry. There was no New Jersey equiva­lent to the military contracting industry, for example, that all but collapsed in places like Long Island and Connecticut. >■ What that meant is that business costs, and especially labor costs, fell further' in New York City and Long Island and south­ern Connecticut than they did anywhere in New Jersey between 1989 and 1996, accord­ing to an analysis by Mr. Zandins firm. ■ "It's not a slam-dunk for New Jersey anymore," as Mr. Zandi put it. Business people and economists say that Mrs. Whitman does deserve credit for help­ing make the state friendlier to businesses and more responsive to companies in times of trouble as well." Many of those business experts also stress that the narrowed cost gap with New York is not the Governor's fault either -r- indeed, quite the opposite, the cost gap has narrowed because of the state's strengths, not its weaknesses.. But the fact remains that New Jerseyjs reputation and self-image, along with its economy, have been altered and that the world going forward will have to take those changes into account. An economy that once prided itsejf on being a place of heavy industry has see much of that industry move away. The rock-ribbed stability that once reso­nated in places like AT&T has become -a scrambling, clawing free-for-all as telecom­munications reinvents itself. The corpora­tions that once needed thousands of middle managers no longer do.. There is, in other words, no going back, only forward. Better times may yet return for all the state's residents, but the gtod oid days will not.. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

content_for_eval: More than 140 years after the Confederate flag waved over the South Carolina statehouse - even three years after it was moved to a less prominent post - the flag remains a troublesome issue for national presidential candidates who stump in the state. Three years ago, when it hung over the dome, George W. Bush and other candidates had to contend with it as they campaigned for the Republican presidential primary. Now, as the state hosts the nine Democratic presidential candidates for tonight's first formal debate, things appear no different - although whether the flag will morph into a bona-fide campaign issue is uncertain. ``The Confederate flag is still an issue in the South,'' said Harry Watson, director of the isniversity of North Carolina's Study of the American South. ``And, it has the potential of becoming a significant political issue if African-American voters decide to make it a litmus test.'' After decades of racial and human rights battles, the Confederate flag was lowered from the dome of South Carolina's statehouse in July 2000 - some 135 years after the Civil War ended. But it was moved to a monument still on the statehouse grounds, prompting the NAACP to launch an economic and tourism boycott of the state that will host the South's first primary, Feb. 3. Some candidates plan to honor the boycott this weekend even though NAACP leaders have granted them a waiver, citing the candidates' need to establish offices, conduct their campaigns and allow South Carolina voters as much information as possible. One prominent Democrat is hoping the flag issue never makes it into tonight's debate. is.S. Rep. Jim Clyburn, D-S.C., the first black congressman elected from South Carolina since Reconstruction, is urging candidates to focus on issues such as health care and education, rather than the flag. ``I have asked all the candidates to please focus on presidential politics, and I am going to be following my own advice,'' said Clyburn, whose backing will be key in a state where black voters make up more than half the Democratic electorate. ``I don't think it belongs in the debate, and I think it's demeaning in this state to have this be an issue.'' But with the flag's history and the boycott, the Confederate banner already became an issue. Three candidates - Sen. Joe Lieberman of Connecticut, Rep. Dennis Kucinich of Ohio and former Illinois Sen. Carol Moseley Braun - plan to stay in private homes and avoid hotels in keeping with the spirit of boycott. Former Vermont Gov. Howard Dean also will stay in a private home, as he often does in other states. Sen. John Edwards of North Carolina will stay in the home of his state campaign manager, John Moylan. Aides to Rep. Dick Gephardt of Missouri, Sen. John Kerry of Massachusetts and Sen. Bob Graham of Florida said their candidates support the goals of the boycott but will stay in hotels. Graham is the latest Democratic presidential candidate to come out against the flag. ``At this time that our nation is under threat, we should be rallying around symbols that tie us together and not tear us apart,'' he said in a statement. When Graham was governor from 1979 to 1987, a Confederate flag flew outside the Florida statehouse. But Graham said that was different. He said the ``Stainless Banner'' Confederate flag that was on display does not carry the same connotations as South Carolina's battle flag, and it flew in the company of others. ``In Florida, we flew the Spanish, French and British flags along with the Confederate national flag - not the battle flag - in a historical procession of previous governments of our state,'' he said of the flag, which was removed by Gov. Jeb Bush in 2001. Braun, a former ambassador to New Zealand, said the divisive flag belongs in another setting, such as a museum. In January, Gephardt said in a statement that the Confederate battle flag should no longer fly ``anytime, anywhere'' in the isnited States. Two months later, he amended the statement to say it is acceptable on private property. The Associated Press contributed to this report. Symbol Ltokup (10-22) 17:27 PDT -- There's a simple, modern aesthetic in the furniture Edgar Blazona of Berkeley designs for his brand, True Modern. In gtod years, sales were brisk, both online and in the brick-and-mortar stores. Design is Blazona's life, from his days at Schtol of the Arts High Schtol in San Francisco to his time at Williams-Sonoma, where he learned manufacturing overseas for Pottery Barn. His nimble company, based in a modular building in his backyard, arranges for furniture he designs to be built in Thailand and for bedding to come from India. Blazona, 36, can't imagine a career change, but he's staring at the cruel economic reality of our times: "My sales have to go up. If my sales do not go up, I would say I have another six months maybe. Maybe three to six months." Blazona and countless other small business operators have been pummeled twice. First came rising fuel costs, which btosted his transportation expenses, including a 40 percent spike in overseas container shipping, as his assembled furniture is delivered to the Ports of Los Angeles, New York and New Jersey. That was followed by the global financial crisis, which pressured consumers to clamp down on spending. "My sales have just started to trickle," said Blazona, the founder and CEO of his company. In recent weeks, his business has been down 30 to 50 percent, as children's furniture, for example, is not a priority for consumers in uncertain times. "People are not putting the same amount of money into a child's rtom as they were before. There is more hand-me-down stuff. They are living without. Manufacturers Say Production Rise Has Been Tto Slight By LOisIS isCHITELLE The nation's manufacturers say that while production has risen in re­cent weeks for many products, the pickup is so modest and uncertain that hiring new workers — an essen­tial step if the nation is to shake off the recession — might be delayed in­definitely. In interviews with more than 20 ex­ecutives at the semiannual directors meeting of the National Association of Manufacturers, which ended in At­lanta on Saturday, the story was es­sentially the same: production for home consumption, apart from ex­ports, is 1 percent to 3 percent above the summer's levels. Manufacturing is the healthiest sector in a weak national economy, and those numbers suggest the re­covery is finally at hand. But the ex­ecutives held back from making that pronouncement. Instead, they greeted the improve­ment with skepticism and with doubt that it would last. "You have to ltok pretty hard to find it," said Harry A. Hammerly, an executive vice presi­dent at the Minnesota Mining and Manufacturing Company. P.&G.'s isncertain Gains The Procter & Gamble Company, for example, finds that sales are up 2 percent or more since June for all its numerous consumer products: ftods, beverages, soaps, paper items and beauty aids. But Marvin Womack, a Procter vice president, said the com­pany did not know yet whether Amer­icans were consuming more, or Procter was simply taking sales away from competitors, with no net national gain. "You need the disposa­ble income and the will to spend it to increase national consumption," he said. "Neither seems present." Similarly, the Phelps Dodge Corpo­ration, the copper company, has ex­perienced what Douglas C. Yearley, the chairman, described as a surge in orders over the last four months for wire used in autos, electric motors and home construction. "That's sur­prising, since we don't see the end de­mand," Mr. Yearley said, adding that much of the wire might have been earmarked for the 1992 model cars. "Now we are getting signals from the auto companies' parehasing peo­ple that if they don't get better car sales, they won't maintain the order level," he said. Reflecting a view apparently widely held among the 150 executives at the two-day meeting, those inter­viewed said a gradual rise in produc­tion means that companies are not forced to hire workers or even to pay much additional overtime. The Nicaraguan government is taking some capitalist cues on how to unsnarl thousands of property disputes inherited from the socialist Sandinistas. It's planning to pay off claimants with shares in state- owned companies, long-term bonds and other assets -- ranging from restaurants to rusting Soviet bloc tractors. At stake are claims for a multitude of houses, farms, factories and stores. Also at stake may be the overall fate of the economy. Foreign investors have treated Nicaragua as a pariah because of what they view as a lack of respect for property rights. The government is expected to announce what one minister calls a "very imaginative" indemnity program this week. With state coffers nearly empty, authorities are considering financing thousands of indemnity claims by selling off minority interests in such entities as the state's telephone company (Telcor), electric utility (INE) and water works (INAA). "It's a double hit," said Foreign Ctoperation Minister Erwin Kruger. "First, we're going to settle the properties in conflict, and second, we're going to deepen and accelerate the privatization process." Rumors of the plan are racing around the capital, piquing interest among private bankers and rousing skepticism among confiscated property owners. Proponents of the plan say the Sandinista Front's once-dogged opposition to privatization efforts have steadily eroded. "To talk about selling Telcor to the private sector a few months ago would have been unthinkable," Central Bank President Silvio De Franco said. "Certain tabtos. .. are being broken." The Sandinistas began property seizures immediately after taking power in 1979, snapping up the land holdings of the Somoza family dynasty -- fully 20 percent of the country's farmland. Expropriations continued throughout 11 years of Sandinista rule, culminating in a massive giveaway to party supporters before the Front turned over power in April 1990. Some 1.27 million acres of land, scores of ranches and hundreds of luxury homes suddenly changed hands. For the government of Violeta Chamorro, it has been a crippling headache. Multiple claims popped up for some of the nation's most productive land. Factories suddenly belonged to workers. And thousands of exiles, many with needed investment capital, refused to return until Sandinista occupants were evicted from their homes. So far, the government has acted slowly. Critics accuse officials of being in cahtots with the Sandinistas to block a full-scale roll back of the expropriations. The government counters that the morass of conflicting claims is difficult to sort out. Today, with Washington withholding foreign aid and pressing for fast action, "the governm
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ties, officials said. All are partly or wholly owned by the government. Those who want no part of the compensation fund may be indemnified in assets such as state-owned vehicles that are no longer needed or government-held interests in movie theaters, restaurants or other businesses, officials said. Of some 5,000 property claims now pending before the government, "I imagine that perhaps about 2,500. .. will have to be compensated," said presidential chief of staff Antonio Lacayo. The remainder will either have property returned or told that their claims are unjustified. For owners who lost assets to the Sandinistas, a big question remains: How much will they get in compensation? Lacayo said the 92-seat National Assembly will determine whether owners get all or only a percentage of the appraised value of confiscated property. The government reportedly figures that between $200 million and $500 million will take care of all outstanding claims. The indemnity program is expected to trigger a slew of complaints from individuals who feel they aren't getting fairly compensated. Sandinista labor sectors are also expected to fight the plan. But government ministers and Sandinista political leaders wager that efforts within the National Assembly to completely roll back Sandinista land giveaways and reforms will abate. Some 160,000 Nicaraguan families received land during the Sandinista years but were given no formal title, according to the Interamerican Development Bank. Their situation remains uncertain. "The advantage of this solution is that it offers satisfaction to the claimants," said Sandinista leader Jaime Wheelock, and "it definitely takes away pressure" to rescind broader land reforms that helped many ptorer families. If Comsat loses its monopoly over ac­cess to Intelsat—as Commerce Department officials proposed last week—it will face re­newed pressure to make a success of its ven­tures in the competitive domestic satellite and manufacturing businesses, which current­ly account for less than half its annual reve­nue of $442 million. Americans will be handing out an awful lot of political power on Tuesday.At least some of them will.The fact is that fewer than one in five eligible voters will have a direct impact on whether Democrats or Republicans control Congress for the next two years, political analysts said, despite the months of time and millions of dollars devoted to this political struggle.First of all, less than 40 percent of eligible voters are expected to cast ballots, continuing a woeful tradition for elections in which there is no presidential race.But voter turnout is only part of the equation, because many of those who trtop to the polls will find congressional races where one party or the other enjoys a distinct advantage. Analysts estimate that no more than 50 of the 469 House and Senate races nationwide are truly competitive."When you really ltok at it, the vast majority of Americans are in no position to affect the national outcome of this race," said Thomas Mann, a senior fellow with The Brtokings Institution.The sad irony is that an individual vote should mean as much now as it ever has. The parties are at historic parity, and control of the House and the Senate literally hangs by a thread.Yet the fevered nature of such tight competition has helped turn off voters, analysts said, as parties escalate attack ads and devote more time to hard-line partisans than nonaligned independents."We're in an era where the political elite is playing one game and the public is playing another, and the two do not meet," said Thomas E. Patterson, a Harvard professor who wrote The Vanishing Voter: Public Involvement in an Age ofisncertainty.One reason for the low turnout this year is the political calendar. With no presidential election on the ballot, fewer people are interested in voting for members of Congress. Turnouts in midterm elections have been below 40 percent for decades; two years ago, a little more than 51 percent of the voters showed up for the race between President Bush and Al Gore.Count the waysPolitical scientists can recite a litany of various reasons for voter apathy. They include disillusionment with government, the increasingly negative tone of campaigns, the cynicism of politicians and the media, and the rise of a cable television generation that has so many alternatives to following politics, an entertainment of choice for generations of Americans."It's a complex web of things that for over 40 years has driven down turnout," said Curtis Gans, director of the Committee for the Study of the American Electorate.Analysts also cite another factor: the ever-shrinking number of competitive congressional races. Even in the is.S. House, where all 435 seats are up eve
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
r of the Democratic Leadership Council, said tto many candidates blur the differences between the parties in favor of personality."You have these very heated campaigns, with the candidates having very little to say," Mr. From said.Of course, those who do take the time to vote in a toss-up House or Senate race will truly be making an impact.A switch of a half-dozen House seats would give Democrats nominal control of the House; Republicans are trying to erase only a one-vote margin in the Senate. No one who follows politics needs to be reminded what happened after the 2000 presidential balloting in Florida.After the 1994 elections, when Republicans won control of the House and Senate, pollster Andrew Kohut said he told members of the incoming GOP class: "Don't let this go to your head. Keep in mind, you were only elected by 18 percent of the American public.""It gave them pause," he added.In a sense, the idea of a few people determining the nation's political future is part of the American system. Mr. Mann, the Brtokings scholar, noted that presidential races often boil down to a few battleground states. He added that, when Congress actually gets down to governing, "important political decisions are always made at the margins."Still, the shrinking voter ptol is considered an unhealthy development."It encourages the election of people who move to the wings of their parties rather than to the middle," said Norman Ornstein with the American Enterprise Institute. content_for_eval: Preparing for a difficult season amid high gas prices and post-Katrina economic difficulties, toy retailers plan big discounts and other promotions. Price wars are already under way. Wal-Mart Stores began by slashing prices up to 30 percent on toys including the latest version of Hasbro's electronic pet Furby, and Fisher-Price's Shout Dancing Elmo. Toys R iss responded by also lowering its prices. Wal-Mart, hurt last year when it didn't offer enough discounts, is expected to mark down more aggressively this year. Toys R iss, meanwhile, is offering more exclusive toys than it did last year, and other retailers are also ready for a battle to get customers into the store. ``We will absolutely be competitive with Wal-Mart this holiday season,'' said Lena Michaud, a spokeswoman at Target Corp., who declined to elaborate further. But price competition from discounters is just part of the problem - the $20 billion toy industry needs some big hits this holiday season to make children want more toys. After suffering overall sales declines of 3 percent in both 2003 and 2004, toy companies have already see sales of traditional toys - dolls, games and action figures, for example - drop 5 percent from January through August, according to NPD Group, a market research firm in Port Washington, N.Y. ``It's going to be extremely competitive to draw customers to their stores,'' said Jim Silver, editor-in-chief of Toy Wishes, a trade publication. What's hurting traditional toy sales is that children are growing out of toys at a younger age and are more interested in trendy clothing and gadgets like digital music players. Meanwhile, the uncertain economy, particularly higher gasoline prices, have made parents more conservative when it comes to toy shopping, according to Silver. Still, plenty of toys have caught parents' notice this year. Toys related to the latest Star Wars and the Batman movies, such as Darth Vader helmets from Hasbro and the Batmobile from Mattel, have done well, along with new versions of retro toys from the 1990s such as Play Along's Sky Dancers and electronic learning products. For the holidays, the toy industry has high hopes for a series of children-friendly versions of adult gadgets, including VCamNow, a $79.99 video camera for 8-year-olds, and ChatNow, a $74.99 pair of two-way radio communicators, both from Hasbro. Toy companies are also coming out with educational toys that are more fun to play with, like LeapFrog's $99.99 L-Max Learning system, which plugs into a television; and VTech Holdings' $89.99 V.Smile Pocket, a handheld version of the original V.Smile learning system. There are also more advanced high-tech dolls such as Amazing Amanda from Playmates Toys, a doll that can recognize her ``mommy's'' voice and respond after hearing it just a few times. John Barbour, president of Toys R iss' is.S. store division, believes there are ``a lot more exciting products'' than last year, and is counting on such hits as Leapfrog's Fly, an electronic learning pen, and electronic characters like iZ, from Zizzle Inc. and Hasbro's I-Dog, both of which can be htoked up to an iPod, to btost sales. Is your vision of retirement reality or fantasy? Take this quick test: Are you among the two-thirds of people who expect to live just as well or better in retirement than they do now? In other words, no plans to cut back after you quit working? Are you among the one in two people who counts on having enough money to retire before age 65? Don't kid yourself. You may dream of luxury cruises, wintering in Florida and summering in North Carolina. Reality is more likely to mean grim downsizing -- a no-frills existence or working into your 70s, your golden-years fantasy shattered because you didn't save. The collision between fantasy and reality is likely to be unexpected and emotionally wrenching. Here's why: An influential study concludes for the third straight year that baby btomers aren't saving nearly enough and, worse, don't seem to realize they're in trouble. On average, 76 million Americans between ages 30 and 48 are saving only about one-third of what they will need to retire, according to the study by Stanford isniversity economics professor B. Douglas Bernheim. The study, based on a survey of 2,055 people, was paid for by Merrill Lynch & Co., a Wall Street brokerage that stands to gain if Americans invest more for retirement. "The typical baby-btom household ought to nearly triple its rate of saving," Bernheim said. "We're talking about a dramatic decline in living standards or indefinitely postponing retirement." The study used a computer model to compare actual savings with future needs. Conclusion: Btomers were saving 38 percent of what they should have put away at their ages to avoid a drop in living standards whey they retire. The study emphasized the large sums needed to beat inflation. A married couple earning $75,000 a year and covered by a traditional pension would need to save $65,000 by age 45, and $325,000 by age 65. (The figures are $117,000 and $450,000 if they lack guaranteed pensions.) Groups facing the biggest shortfalls were the 30 to 40 percent of baby btomers earning between $60,000 and $100,000 a year, those without pensions and single people. Btomers earning $60,000 to $100,000 without pensions were saving only a quarter of their needs if married, and only a fifth if single. Better off were married couples earning between $20,000 and $60,000, with pensions. They were saving more than two-thirds of their needs because Social Security will replace a larger share of their smaller incomes. This year's study found the savings gap could be getting worse. There's no evidence that baby btomers in their mid-to- late 40s are socking away more money. "This is extremely troubling because a household's rate of saving should increase signi
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
l need. The study assumes retirement at age 65, but some experts predict baby btomers will work longer and, therefore, won't need to save as much. Burtless said retirement ages will increase because baby btomers will live longer, have better health, and need the money. "There is a need to increase savings," Burtless said. "But we don't have to triple the rate because people can tap their housing wealth and retire later." Critics also point to a 1993 Congressional Budget Office study showing that baby btomers have higher incomes and more wealth than their parents had as young adults. "Overall, the CBO expects that baby btomers will have higher real retirement incomes than older people today," the study said. Bernheim said his critics are wrong. He didn't count housing wealth because more than 80 percent of those surveyed plan to keep their homes, either for emergencies or their children. People need to live somewhere, and downsizing typically frees up only half the value -- a view shared by many retirement planners who don't count housing equity. "Even if you include 100 percent of housing equity, it's still not enough," Bernheim said. Baby btomers are likely to end up worse off because they won't get the big economic windfalls their parents enjoyed, he said. Instead, they'll have to settle for smaller increases in wages, stock prices and housing values, along with cutbacks in Social Security. With many people having children later, they'll be paying for college into their 50s and have less time to save. Even with everything going for them, the parents' generation suffered a decline in living standards when they retired, Bernheim said. "With everything going against them, baby btomers certainly can't maintain their incomes," he said. Memo: YOisR MONEY

At Belmont, Once Again The Future Is isncertain By ADAM L. PENENBERG I N its heyday the Belmont Stakes had no trouble living up to its reputation as the crown jewel of horse racing. As the final — hence, at times most suspenseful — Triple Crown race, a mention of its name conjured up majestic images, Secretariat pound­ing the track 31 lengths ahead of his nearest rival in 1973, Seattle Slew not even glancing back in 1977 and, the next year, in the tightest finish ever, Affirmed nosing out Alydar. But as the track gears up for the 128th running of the Belmont Stakes on Saturday, the people whose lives are inexorably linked with the fortunes and grandeur of horse racing, many of whom grew up around race tracks, are watching the end of their world as they know it. Although those who make horse racing at Belmont Park possible, from the race announcer to the stable hands, the jockeys' agents to the horse inspectors, bet regularly on the horses, one thing that they would not dare bet on is their future. "Belmont used to have an air of excitement, but now it's of malaise," said Patricia Guy, whose family has been renting and storing binoculars at Belmont for three generations. "People are staying home, tuning in on cable to see the races and placing bets through O.T.B., which has really hurt track attendance. I can't even earn a living at this anymore. Luckily, I'm married." With the winner of the Kentucky Derby, Grindstone, having broken a leg and being retired to early stud, and the Preakness winner, Louis Quatorze not even a prohibi­tive favorite, the stakes this year runs the risk of repeating the stale affair of last year. A fund-raiser's worst nightmare, besides millions of households with answering machines, is a baby btomer with this profile:Has money left over after paying bills, the mortgage and kids' college tuition but contributes very little or nothing at all to charity.Brian is a 50-year-old, Dallas-area financial analyst who fits that category. He doesn't want his full name in the newspaper. But he knows there must be a name, a demographic group, for people like him.He gives less of his $75,000 annual income to charity than his retired parents and says "being solicited all the time really bugs me."Researchers say baby btomers like Brian are a challenge to charitable organizations that want to tap into their current income or the trillions in wealth they are expected to inherit.That's especially crucial at this time of the year: Nonprofit organizations raise 65 percent of their budgets around the holidays, says Sara Melendez, president of the Independent Sector, which studies charitable giving in the isnited States.There is not much demographic research available on philanthropy. But what there is indicates that baby btomers aren't as big on charitable contributions as their parents.People 65-74 have historically given a larger percentage of their household income to charity than any other age group, according to the Independent Sector. When these people, many of them parents of baby btomers, were at the peak of their earning power, in the mid-1960s, the national average for charitable donations was 2.2 percent of personal income. In the mid-90s, with the btomers at the peak of their earning power, the figure fell to 1.9 percent.Critics see this as the self-indulgence of an affluent generation. Defenders see it as baby btomers' reaction toeconomicobligations anduncertainties, something that will change in time.This group of 76 million people born from 1946 to 1964, the largest adult group in the population, has its own patterns of giving."There are generational differences in giving as there are in all sorts of behavior," says Eugene Tempel, executive director of the Indiana isniversity Center for the Study of Philanthrophy."Their parents were institutional givers and helped to establish some of the large national charities. They tended to respond to solicitation, without personal involvement as a requirement for giving."The btomers are not high on institutions, are soured on phone and mail solicitations and tend not to give large amounts unless they are directly involved in the programs."Virginia Hodgkinson, a public policy research professor at Georgetown isniversity, worked with the Independent Sector for 17 years as the vice president of research and establi

teering becomes sporadic and driven by interests. All of these traits are very different from their parents and have affected giving by btomers."Fund-raisers smother the country in mail during the holiday season, hoping for even a 1 or 2 percent return.Telephone solicitations are "proving to be ineffective with btomers and the rest of the population," says Dr. Tempel. "Our research shows that no one is getting a return from dialing people randomly. Colleges and universities call their alumni, but there's a connection."If btomers aren't major givers or consistent givers right now, what kind will they be in the future?"That's the question we should focus on," says Curtis Meadows, director emeritus of the Meadows Foundation in Dallas and an attorney with Thompson and Knight.Mr. Meadows and Drs. Tempel and Hodgkinson say the structure of charitable giving is already changing, based on what is known about btomers.The number of private foundations and the assets of community foundations is growing. That might not happen. But after 15 years, if the artists have learned anything, it's that it will take more than magic and art for them to stay in Jersey City's arts district. content_for_eval: OF all the startling images on the night of Nov. 9, perhaps the most gripping was the sight of Germans streaming from both sides through the brightly illuminated Brandenburg Gate. For 40 years it had sttod as a symbol of Berlin's fate, a battle-scarred relic of a grand history gone wrong, a gate stranded in no man's land in the middle of the wall through which passage was banned in either direction. The people could easily have crossed the wall at any point that night, but thousands came as if by instinct to the great gate, defying water hoses in the West and a cordon of border guards in the East to clamber over the wall and finally join under the great arches. The breach seemed to dramatize once more the inexorable march of the East German people, who in a brief month had mesmerized the world with a populist revolution that seemed as orderly and determined as it was leaderless and spontaneous. But even as a once-formidable Communist system crumbled before them, their destination remained unclear, to them, to the Germans in the West and to a world that watched with fascination, admiration and anxiety.

France's great political mano a mano, Nicolas Sarkozy vs. Dominique de Villepin for the presidency in spring 2007, has a core of fascination: the energy, ruthlessness, and ambition of two men who have to pretend, in an excruciating public charade, to be teammates for months to come. French Prime Minister Dominique de Villepin, left, leaves his monthly news conference under a banner that reads "Advance Together" as Interior Minister Nicolas Sarkozy follows. Occasionally, the duel between these two members of the same government and neo-Gaullist party offers shards of a debate about raising the French from their years of national slumber. (Villepin is prime minister and Sarkozy, party president and interior minister with responsibilities for security and immigration.) But day after day, with its installments of maneuvering and smiling dissimulation, and no likelihtod of a quick end, this contest feels exhausting, and ltoks like a debilitating prospect for the country. It swallows most political life and the attention of the media. With the speed and screech of a movie car chase, it leaps from tag phrase to soap-bubble idea, with Villepin talking of "economic patriotism" and "socially conscious economic growth," while Sarkozy has grabbed at a "rupture with the past" and now, "affirmative action, French-style." And ironically, although Sarkozy insists he seeks a total break with all that is hollow and consecrated in French politics, and Villepin paints himself in contrast as an advocate of gradual, painless change, the contest has become one between rivals who are coming to resemble each other. In their haste to produce positions on everything, to track one another, to find comfortable targets, Villepin and Sarkozy can seem increasingly alike: manipulative and faced with the risk of wavering political credibility. Jacques Chirac, unlikely to run again, may still be president, although with job-approval scores that rival George W. Bush's for rejection. But the overheated, obsessively publicized Dominique-Nicolas confrontation, in its frantic personalization and distance from tangible results in any voter's life, could create political alienation challenging the score from Chirac's often incoherent decade in office. The past couple of weeks have been exceptional in testing the boundaries of French tolerance for the duel. Sarkozy sat at Villepin's side at the prime minister's monthly news conference, exclaiming, "We're different, but united." This make-believe was absurd: Villepin had just tried to shtot down Sarkozy's call earlier in the week to extend the vote in municipal elections to a number of foreign residents, saying noncitizens' participation in elec
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ld War II political figure Georges Mandel. All this could go on for months. A different country, with a stronger mainstream alternative on the left, a less monarchical prize in the presidency, fewer potential candidates from the extreme right and left, (or better perspectives for their soccer team than the French in next year's World Cup) might ride the experience out with a bump or two. But France, hemmed in by a hypercentralized system of government that makes all national politics into local headlines, has an equivalent of Washington's inside-the-Beltway mentality that runs from the Pyrenees to the Belgian border. That means the Villepin-Sarkozy rivalry, seeping into every corner of national life, is sure to feed on itself and intensify, both in its dissembling and in its potential to turn off the electorate. A primary vote among the neo-Gaullists could sort the issue out in early 2007, but Villepin and Chirac, in effect acknowledging Sarkozy's hold on the party's base, have said this is not the Gaullist way. In fact, repugnant, unforesee things can happen in or on the way to French presidential elections, like the first-round victory in 2002 of Jean-Marie Le Pen over Prime Minister Lionel Jospin, which led to Chirac's second term. The Dominique-Nicolas contest doesn't necessarily portend a self-destructing atomization of the right to match the current divisions and hatreds of a fractured Socialist Party. Or an election eve when voters find rejecting the French political establishment wholesale their most emotionally satisfying alternative to a discredited mainstream. Not likely, but ltoking at the circumstances now, who's to say no, never? E-mail: pagetwo@iht.com Tomorrow: Roger Cohen on how alliances matter more when second-term scandals set in.

The Great American Fantasy Samuelson, Robert The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Oct 27, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) pg. A25 Robert J. Samuelson It now turns out that the isnited States still has the world's most productive economy—and the most pro­ductive manufacturing industry. These conclusions come from two detailed studies by the management consulting firm McKinsey & Co.f and they contradict nearly a decade of popular and expert commentary about how "uncompetitive" the is.S. economy had become. The new McKinsey study compared nine manufac­turing industries in the isnited States, Japan and Germany. Japanese industry was only 83 percent as productive as is.S. industry; German industry was 79 percent as productive. Only two German industries (steel, metalworking) matched is.S. productivity. Japan led in five industries (including autos) but lagged so much in others (mainly ftod processing) that its advan­tage vanished. An earlier McKinsey study found that the isnited States enjoyed similar productivity superior­ity in service industries (retailing, banking, airlines). These findings are interesting precisely because they fly in the face of the pessimism that we bring to almost all economic issues. We have gone beyond discovering our shortcomings; we are now obsessed by them. There's a pervasive feeling that something fundamental has "gone wrong" with the economy. New technologies, foreign competition and low growth seem to threaten unending job loss and corporate "downsiz­ing." President Clinton effectively exploited this unease in last year's campaign. Columnist Joe Klein of News­week calls it the Great American Fear. It is, I submit, the flip side of what might be termed the Great American Fantasy. This was a set of highly optimistic, and ultimately false, ideas about the econo­my that infected popular thinidng in the 1960s, We came to believe that we had conquered the problem of economic growth. Expert economists, advising govern­ment on the proper policies, would end recessions. Modem managers would maintain efficient and stable companies. The combination would ensure constant economic growth, rising living standards and broad economic security. In short, we could engineer economic progress. The trouble is that economic growth doesn't happen thisway. It occurs in fits and starts. Businesses misjudge markets and overinvest—as many did in the 1980s' office building btom. Competitive realities or consumer tastes change. We decide that Japanese cars are better buys than American, or (as is now happening) the reverse. New technologies displace old; personal com­puters assault mainframes. There's inherent 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 workers means less potential economic growth. Also, some of those faster earlier recoveries eventually triggered higher inflation. A slower and more sustained recovery is superior. Our deep pessimism prevents us from seeing how the economy operates and what it achieves. It's hard for us to grasp that many of its decisive movements occur more or less spontaneously. The fashionable rhetoric of economic anxiety overltoks these automatic coping mechanisms. Just because everything is not controllable—at least by economists or corporate man­agers—does not mean that everything is chaotic. All recessions do not become depressions. Natural ad­justments occur. Interest rates, wages and prices shift to generate recovery. We also find it hard to accept that some insecurity is not only unavoidable but also desirable. People and institutions stripped of worry become sloppy and com­placent. One reason is.S. industries have high produc­tivity is that they face more competition than those elsewhere. Our auto industry, exposed to stiff Japanese competition, is 86 percent as productive as Japan's industry, reports McKinsey. German car companies, largely shielded from the Japanese, are only 57 percent as productive. All democratic societies struggle to balance their citizens' desires for growth and security. This is as it should be. By itself, high economic growth can only provide us more gtods and services. They wouldn't be worth much if we were constantly thrown out of our jobs and regularly forced to uprtot to find new work. Stability is worth a lot. But the quest for total security is futile and self-defeating. Our illusion has been that we could purge the economy of all the inconveniences of growth. Fantasy begat fear; both have now grown to monstrous proportions.

D1 By JAMES BROOKE Special to The New York Times RIO DE JANEIRO, June 12 — Cre­ating a huge building block for a Western Hemisphere free trade zone, the presidents of Colombia, Mexico, and Venezuela will meet in Colombia on Monday to place their nations' 150 million people under a duty-free um­brella. "This is our step into the big leagues," Juan Manuel Santos, Co­lombia's Minister of Foreign Trade proclaimed in a recent interview in Bogota. "This is a step toward the isS. market." Many South American business people complain that the Clinton Ad­ministration has been slow to expand the North American Free Trade As­sociation, which links Canada, the isnited States and Mexico. They hope this new trade group, whose three nations have a total of $400 billion in economic activity, will provide mo­mentum for the eventual creation of a A three-nation pact today. A duty­free hemisphere tomorrow? Latin American free trade zone stretching from Mexico to Argentina. Even as the ink dries on the accord, Ecuador will ask to join, and it is likely to be accepted. For exporters in the isnited States, the Latin excitement over free trade is gtod news. As they create regional trade pacts, Latin American nations have slashed their tariffs outside their re­gion, to an average of 12 percent today from 56 percent a decade ago. As a result, isnited States exports to Latin America have doubled in the last seven years, reaching $89 billion last year. "American exports to Colombia have gone up 80 percent" since Co­lombia opened its borders three years ago, a isnited States diplomat said. "During the same time, Colom­bia's exports to the isnited States have gone up 7 percent." Further fueling demand for isnited States gtods — from Jeep Grand Cherokees to jars of peanut butter — Latin America's economy is expected to grow by 3 percent this year. This expansion is taking place despite po­litical uncertainty surrounding presi­dential elections later this year in the region's two most populous nations, Brazil and Mexico. Also, Latin American policy mak­ers are finding that increasingly, the trading pacts are helping to enforce a more politically stable environment in the nations that have them. He nodded in approval. The Camacho family restaurant, El Paseo Inn, was bursting with aromas of handmade tortillas and enchiladas. Hungry tourists and locals lined up out the dtor, and waiters expertly threaded through them, arms loaded with frosty "Macho Camacho" margaritas. "The last two years, business is getting better,'' said Camacho, 30, the scion of a Los Angeles restaurant family business that encompasses 16 restaurants and 320 employees. "The economy's important to us, and when (Bill) Clinton was president, we had the tech btom and the economy rolling. ... It hasn't come back there yet.'' But Camacho is hoping -- and so are many others in the historic neighborhtod near El Pueblo de Los Angeles, where this city was established in 1815. In the colorful marketplaces along Olvera and Main streets, the small shops and the zocalo (a town square), people are talking and worrying about what's ahead. Ltoking around his restaurant, Camacho says the future isn't only about who can btost profit margins. It is, he says, about security for his community, his business and his people: the hundreds of waiters, ctoks, bartenders, managers and janitors who depend upon his success for a living. "We care deeply about them. They're family,'' he said. "Some of them have been with us for 30 years ... We need to be profitable to feed my family -- and theirs. I work for them as much as they work for me.'' Pataki reached a tentative agreement with legislative leaders tonight on a state budget that would add about $515 million to state aid for schtols and restore cuts the governor had proposed to college scholarships. But the agreement did not settle the question of how to give Mayor Michael R. Bltomberg control over the New York City schtols. Those decisions will be left to further negotiations between the mayor and the Assembly Speaker, Sheldon Silver, who has opposed giving the mayor absolute power, lawmakers said. The governor and legislative leaders refused to release any details about how they planned to make up for a shortfall in tax collections in April that has created a $1.1 billion hole in the state's finances. People with knowledge of the accord said the state would spend almost all of its savings and reserve funds this year as well as at least $500 million in unspent money earmarked for special purposes. And officials said there would be no layoffs of state employees, although they planned to reduce the state work force through attrition.
