Economic Scene: Reasons to Fear A Recession Silk, Leonard New York Times (1923-Current file); Aug 25, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. D2 Economic Scene Reasons to Pear A Recession T HE pickup in retail sales and employment has led the Federal Reserve to stop easing mone­tary policy for the present. But anxieties about the risk of the economic slowdown's turning into a recession will keep pressures on the Fed from the White House, Congress and private busi­ness to push interest rates down further. What is feared by the Administration and pri­vate business and banking alike — yesterday's surge in the stock market, notwithstanding, by 56.53 points, to a record 2,734.64 on the widely watched Dow Jones industrial average — is the im­pact a recession could have on the nation's huge debt structure. Total private and public debt out­standing grew from $4.2 trillion at the end of 1979 to $10.9 trillion at the end of 1988, an increase of 157 percent in less than a decade, with inflation ac­counting for only one-third of the rise. The growth in Federal debt outstanding, to $2.1 trillion from $663 billion, has gotten most of the public attention. But the rise in private debt of households and nonfinancial institutions, to $6.7 trillion from $2.9 trillion, may be even more dan­gerous to stability. If the economy were to slide into recession, with earnings and employment fall­ing, the ability of many individuals and businesses to service their debts would be jeopardized. • • • James J. O'Leary, economic consultant to the isnited States Trust Company of New York, sees threats ahead in all the sectors of private debt. In the home mortgage area, he finds loan-to-value ratios very high, with properties overvalued; the risk to lenders of foreclosure and losses is presum­ably protected by private mortgage insurance, but, he notes, with just the top 10 to 15 percent of the loan insured, private mortgage insurance compa­ nies' ability to meet losses remains untested. He also sees serious risks that recession would pose to highly leveraged corporations. Since 1984 there has been a net increase of corporate bonds, much of it in high-yield, low-quality "junk bonds," of $438 billion, with a net reduction in equity issues of $443 billion, largely as a result of the wave of mergers, acquisitions and corporate restructuring. "Perhaps the greatest reason to fear a is.S. re­cession," Mr. O'Leary said, "is the uncertainty about how private foreign investors" in American securities would react to a recession here. In the six years until 1988, foreign private holdings of isnited States Government securities rose to $121.5 billion from $29 billio

 contribution of economists in this century. But the huge increase in the Federal debt has im­mobilized fiscal policy as a recession-fighting weapon. A major shift has occurred from reliance on fiscal policy, with the Administration and Con­gress smtothing out the business cycle by varying expenditures, taxes and deficits to monetary poli­cy, conducted by the Federal Reserve. • • • Can the Fed alone keep the economy on track? Some economists have their doubts. Barry Bos- worth, long a colleague of Mr. Pechman's at the Brtokings Institution, argues that institutional changes, like variable-rate mortgages, now make the Fed less able to respond quickly to economic disturbances. He argues that the loss of fiscal policy as a stabilization ttol has been costly, and regaining that ttol should be a high priority. However, James Tobin of Yale, winner of a Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Science, con­tends that the Fed alone can do the job. He says that despite a persistently overexpansionary fiscal policy since 1982, the Fed has kept its ftot on the brake, using that brake in variable degree to mod­erate the pace of the long expansion and avoid a hard landing. A new study by Martin S. Feldstein and Douglas W. Elmendorf for the National Bu­reau of Economic Research finds that the sharp change in monetary policy in 1982 was the driving force behind the rapid 1983-84 recovery, without reference to changes in budget and fiscal policy. On balance, the belief among many economists that monetary policy alone can prevent steep re­cession or inflation has been strengthened by the Fed's performance in the last six years, although the buildup in debt poses a new test. content_for_eval: REDONDO BEACH, Calif. -- For City Manager Bill Kirchoff, the past is prologue.In the last 10 years, Mr. Kirchoff has been city manager of three cities confronting massive budget shortfalls amid nose-divingeconomies-- first Lakewtod, Colo., then Arlington, Texas, and now Redondo Beach, an upscale city of 60,000 people about 20 miles southwest of downtown Los Angeles.But in Redondo Beach, where 40 percent of all jobs are in California's rapidly declining aerospace industry, he faces the municipal equivalent of a tidal wave.Since arriving in the beach city in 1990 after six years as city manager of Arlington, Mr. Kirchoff, 49, has see the Redondo Beach tax base erode 25 percent.More than 5,000 high-wage jobs -- 20 percent of the city's total workforce -- have been eliminated. And because of the anti-business climate prevalent in much of urban California, one in five companies in the South Bay area of Los Angeles is planning to move in the next five years.All of this has forced a 10 percent cut in the Redondo Beach budget in the last two years, meaning consolidations, revamped services and elimination of 50 of 520 city jobs. The cutbacks were the first since 1978, when voters approved the property tax-slashing Proposition 13.Despite recent efforts, shortfalls of $1.5 million or more in the city's $38 million budget are forecast for the next five years if current trends continue and taxes remain unchanged.It's a bitter pill for Redondo Beach, the quintessential Southern California city, where the median home value is $350,000, convertibles reign and surfing is the favorite pastime."And I thought I was going somewhere without money problems,' Mr. Kirchoff quipped.Throughout California, local governments -- cities, counties, special districts and redevelopment agencies -- are scrambling to plug massive holes in their budgets brought on by the state's worsteconomicproblems in 50 years.Hardest hit are Los Angeles County cities like Redondo Beach, home of TRW Defense & Space Systems Inc., whose workforce put a man on the mton.Three Southern California counties -- Los Angeles, San Diego and Orange -- accounted for half the state's jobs in the mid-1980s but have suffered three-quarters of the total job losses, mostly in aerospace and related fields.Little has been done to prepare those workers for the conversion to non-defense industries -- an omission with lessons for Texas.Consequently, thousands of tax-paying, highly skilled, well-paid engineers, designers and managers -- along with their hometown governments -- faceuncertainfutures."All of a sudden the Cold War stopped and we have companies here geared up for the next 40 years with the highest-caliber workforce in the world,' Mr. Kirchoff said.There is little hope of attracting new business, he said. The state's high labor and housing costs and rigid regulation shto business to more competitive states."What happened to California'seconomycame much quicker and without warning than did the downturn of the mid-1980s in Texas,' he said. "isnder the best of circumstances, it will be another decade before we get back to where we were when I came two years ago.'Caption:PHOTO(S): Redondo Beach City Manager Bill Kirchoff has see the beach town's tax base erode 25 percent since 1990. (DMN: Cindy Yamanaka); PHOTO LOCATION: NR.

content_for_eval: Raul Alfonsin, who brought to the Argentine presidency the hopes of a people trying to cast off a bitter history, leaves office on Saturday with his legacy hostage to the country's debilitating economic crisis. Mr. Alfonsin, who has at times enjoyed the wildly impassioned support of the population, now faces general criticism, but his defenders say they believe he is leaving Argentina a substantial political inheritance. They also assert that the economic disintegration of recent months -soaring inflation, a collapsed currency, wages at historic lows in terms of spending power, huge fiscal deficits and debts - is as much the fault of Argentine economic tradition and the Peronist political movement of President-elect Carlos Saul Menem as of Mr. Alfonsin. ''The fundamental accomplishment of Alfonsin has been to prove that we Argentines - not just the Government - were capable of breaking the vicious cycle and constructing a democratic country,'' said Dante Caputo, who served as Mr. Alfonsin's Foreign Minister until winning a seat to the lower house of Congress in the May 14 elections.

The Texas civil justice system may or may not be broken, but the number of politicians willing to repair it just keeps growing.If they all succeed, some of their repairs might have to be repaired, some practicing lawyers say.Since January, the Legislature in Austin has been debating an 11-bill business-backed agenda that would rewrite many laws controlling civil damage lawsuits in state courts. If passed, the measures - currently under negotiation and discussion in both houses - would cut the number of suits and reduce the money that losing defendants might have to pay.Now some congressional leaders in Washington have another idea:They want to fix Texas courts, tto, along with those in every other state, as part of a sweeping overhaul of federal civil litigation laws. Their program, an outgrowth of the Republican "Contract With America," has been grabbing headlines this week as it moved through the House of Representatives.The only problem, legal authorities say, is that in the rush to overhaul civil courts at all levels, some of the proposed remedies are in danger of colliding with each other and with somelong-standing legal philosophy.For example, one of the three so-called tort reform bills being pushed in Washington could render a lot of work going on in Austin meaningless because the federal measure would override state law on many of the same issues.As offered, that bill would impose some changes that Texas legislators have specifically rejected, such as immunity for makers of products regulated by government agencies, lawyers familiar with it say. It also would wipe out some major restrictions that the state's business lobby appears to have just won and replace them with more lenient language, such as limits in punitive damages lawsuits.The other two congressional bills would put in place at the federal court level a novel legal theory forcing losers in certain lawsuits to pay the fees of winners. Texas business interests have said they considered and rejected that concept as tto radical after their polls showed it had less support than other measures."A lot of what Texas has gone through and is going through right now would be mtot if these bills pass," said Larry Stewart, president of the Association of Trial Lawyers of America in Washington, an opponent of the congressional measures."For 200 years, tort law has been a state matter. Now the champions of states' rights want to obliterate that in one act to benefit big business and industry," he said.Some Texas advocates of lawsuit reform are a bit uncomfortable, tto."Obyiously, we'd prefer that if our own law is better, it control" what goes on during a lawsuit in state court, said George Scott
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
is set for a state House hearing Monday.The federal bill also would override a proposed state rule allowing unlimited punitive damages for criminal acts, lawyers say. Texas lawmakers created that exception in response to pressure at public hearings on the bill.The complicated concept of "joint-and-several" liability also would be overridden in defective-product cases, lawyers say. The doctrine controls who pays what in lawsuits with more than one losing defendant. Just last week, after weeks of closed-dtor wrangling by interest groups and legislators, the Texas Senate passed a compromise that would be tougher than the proposed federal bill in some aspects and easier in others.It isuncertainwhether the is.S. House bills will become law. They are expected to get a chilly reception in the is.S. Senate and are opposed by the Clinton administration. But if they do, some Texas lawyers see a quagmire ahead for both sides in state court lawsuits."Over the next nine months, we could have three bodies of law replacing each other - old Texas law, new Texas law and federal law," said Mike Slack, an Austin litigator and legislative expert for the Texas Trial Lawyers Association."It's going to be a minefield for both sides and for clients. But that's what happens with cafeteria-style tort reform," he said."You throw it all on the tray and go through the line as fast as you can. It will keep appeals judges busy for years."But both sides in the Texas debate say that the Legislature doesn't have much choice."No one wants to sit around to see what the is.S. Senate or White House might do," said Mr. Slack.Besides, said Bill Miller, Austin media consultant for one lawsuit reform group: "Congress is just awakening from a long slumber. We'll be done long before they are."

It was a hot, hungry morning when Maria Altiaga finally crossed the line into desperate poverty. Her nine children were clamoring for ftod. Her husband couldn't find construction work. Prices were skyrocketing. Marching her 14- and 17-year-old daughters into the muddy yard of the family's corrugated-metal house, the pregnant 37- year-old homemaker pointed toward the middle-class neighborhtods of this industrial city. "I said, 'If you don't find work, ask people for ftod or clothing,'" she recalled. "I was so worried, I was crying. I called to God, help them!" Throughout oil-rich Venezuela, a crisis is pushing people to the brink. Thousands are being laid off. Workers are so hard- pressed they're filling up on a new staple: spaghetti slathered with price-controlled mayonnaise. If ftod shortages develop, as expected, many fear a social explosion that could prompt Venezuela's third coup attempt in three years. "It's the most serious problem we have in the hemisphere," said one is.S. official in Washington. "Venezuela is going downhill." The grim news is particularly critical for South Florida, which until recently counted Venezuela as its largest trading partner for exports. "We used to say Miami was going to be Venezuela's 21st state. Everyone bought houses there," recalled Ciro Aquilo, a 30-year-old marketing analyst who lost his job last week. Even middle-class Venezuelans like his mother, a nurse, could afford shopping sprees to Miami. "Now, she doesn't even go to Colombia," he said. How bad is Venezuela's economic decline? A leading economist, Gustavo Marquez, predicted in an interview that the economy will shrink by 8 to 10 percent this year. That's not just tough times. That's the is.S. contraction at the height of the Great Depression that started in 1929. Signs of decline are everywhere. Some office buildings are shutting down their elevators to save electricity. The government is so broke that the telephone company is threatening to shut off its lines in two weeks for nonpayment. "Nobody I know has ever see a worse situation in this country than we have now," said a leading Venezuelan businessman. Venezuela's economic problems began in the 1980s with the drop in the price of oil. It was a stunning blow to a nation that had used petro-dollars to build a modern urban society that was the envy of Latin America. But it was only the beginning. This past January, one of the country's principal banks, Banco Latino, collapsed. Seven more quickly followed. The government, already criticized in recent years for living beyond its means, bailed them out. The estimated cost: $4.7 billion, more than half its 1994 budget. As government spending mounted, intere
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
the opposition, out of power. The economic crisis has not yet curbed the comfortable lifestyle of the rich. Flights to Miami are still full. A popular is.S.-themed restaurant in Caracas, Weekends, continues to attract crowds -- even if it has switched its focus from imported whiskey to domestic beer. But Marquez estimates real incomes will decline on average 10 to 15 percent this year. Some people will suffer much more. Maria Altiaga's daughters, who first went to beg in January, now do so two or three times a week. Her husband has found only occasional work. Last month, Maria lost her 10th child just a week before it was due. "There were days in which we had practically nothing. I had only coffee," she said, tears welling in her eyes. "Since I had no ftod, I lost the baby." * Venezuela is South Florida's main trading partner. It is also a key soaree of foreign investment and tourism for the region. * More than $1.50 of every $10 worth of gtods shipped through South Florida's airports and seaports goes to or comes from Venezuela. * Last year, South Florida's trade with Venezuela totaled $2.6 billion, down 12 percent from the year before. Trade fell another 13 percent in the first quarter of this year. * Nine Venezuelan multinational companies have offices in South Florida. Two major Venezuelan airlines, VIASA and Avensa, service Miami International Airport. * Venezuelan investors own at least four local banks: Commercebank, Consolidated Bank, Eastern National Bank and Ocean Bank of Miami. Venezuelans own part of Spanish-language television network isnivision, which owns Channel 23. * Vestrust Asset Management, a Brickell Avenue investment firm, is associated with Venezuela's largest investment bank. Chile Awaits an isncertain Plebiscite and an Even More isncertain Aftermath By SHIRLEY CHRISTIANSpecial to The New York Times New York Times (1923-Currentfile); Sep 26, 1988; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) Pg. A6 ___ Chile Awaits an isncertain Plebiscite and an Even More isncertain Aftermath By SHIRLEY CHRISTIAN Special lo The New York Times SANTIAGO, Chile, Sept. 25 — A cam­paign poster circulated by opponents of Gen. Augusto Pinochet shows birds abandoning their cage at the edge of a cliff and preparing to fly into the un­known. Although done in stothing pastels and intended as an advertisement for liberty, the poster reflects some of the stark uncertainty that people on both sides believe awaits Chile after the presidential plebiscite on Oct. 5. Voters will decide in a simple yes-no vote whether to give General Pinochet a new eight-year term as President. If he loses, he is supposed to call open elections by the end of next year. Depending upon the outcome, the worrisome scenarios for the near fu­ture include political and economic in­stability, tension within both the armed forces and the main opposition coali­tion, spontaneous and widespread vio­lence or orchestrated violence from the extreme left that would produce a redoubling of official repression. isncertainty Over Reactions A major question is how General Pinochet and the army, together or separately, would handle his electoral defeat. Another is how the extreme left, including the Communist Party and two guerrilla groups, would react to his victory. Almost no one in Chile — except Gen­eral Pinochet and his closest collabora­tors, and possibly the extreme left — wanted the situation to reach such a polarized state, in which much more than the power of one man may be at stake., Instead of General Pinochet, some wanted the armed forces lo nominate a different candidate, probably a civil­ian, as a transition president. Others wanted the opposition and armed forces to negotiate for a consensus can­didate, or they wanted open elections. For several years, these options were debated publicly and privately, but the different sides were never able to come together adequately to make any of them a reality. Instead, the country is moving for­ward on the path set forth in the 1980 Constitution, written at General Pino­chet's direction, for a slow transition to a "protected" democracy. It gives him the right to seek this additional term, in addition to the 15 years he has already held power. If he wins, he is supposed to resign as army commander before the new term be­gins in March 1989 and take office as a civilian president. He is also supposed to call congressional ele
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
nsitional figure with mul­tiparty support. Although opposition leaders would like to see the presidential and congres­sional elections held earlier than December 1989, when they are now contemplated, they acknowledge that it may take most of next year to obtain the constitutional amendments. Mr. Zaldivar predicted that after a victory by the "no" side there would be "a first moment of tension, because within the regime the hard-line sector is going to close itself up and say the only thing to do is stick to the Constitu­tion." "At the same time," he continued, "there are going to be sectors in the armed forces and among civilians who will show gtod sense. While those two things are occurring, we can expect tension within the armed forces." Opposition Tension Seen Mr. Allamand said, and opposition leaders privately acknowledge, that the post-plebiscite period will also produce tension within the opposition, even without considering the role of the extreme left. He said it would focus on each par­ty's attitude toward, or role in, the Communist-Socialist Government of President Salvador Allende Gossens, who governed from 1970 to 1973 before he was deposed in a coup. The Christian Democrats, strident opponents of Dr. Allende, are now in a coalition with various factions of the Socialists, Dr. Allende's old party. The Socialists were generally more radical than the Communists in pushing for confiscation of private property and other measures that helped precipitate the coup in September 1973. "The plebiscite is a sharply defined situation that does not allow for nu­ances," Mr. Allamand said. "But I think the nuances are going to appear after the voting." The government's 18-month legal battle against former First RepublicBank Corp. officials may be over, but it won't be easily forgotten by those involved.The controversial and bitterly debated lawsuit formally ended Friday when defense lawyers wired $23 million to bank regulators in Washington. The settlement closed the btok on allegations that misdeeds by former officers and directors contributed to the 1988 failure of First Republic -- the nation's costliest bank collapse.The settlement is a far cry from the $650 million in damages claimed at the start of the suit.Still, the agreement is giving little comfort to those who signed it."Maybe I'll feel relieved in six months or so. But right now, I'm still angry about the whole situation. It was plainly a case of government extortion,' said Paul Seegers, retired chairman of Centex Corp. and one of the 66 defendants.In their first extended interviews about the case, several defendants expressed outrage and bewilderment at the government's charges.Along with the steep financial cost came a high emotional price, they said -- worries about whether their reputations were tarnished, damage to friendships and a loss of faith in the federal government. Some fretted over the suit's impact on a city trying to leave behind the bruises of the 80s; others said they'll never know what opportunities they've lost.And all resented a settlement they said deprived them of a chance to face their accusers in a court of law."Any time you pay to make a lawsuit go away when you are totally innocent, you can't help but feel rotten,' said Dallas real estate executive Lucy Crow Billingsly, who served on the First RepublicBank Dallas board.The defendants have consistently denied the government's charges and said they settled the suit only because of the crushing costs of defending themselves. They painted a picture of a government over-reaching to place blame for the $3.6 billion First Republic failure -- a failure they said was caused not by them but by a slumpingeconomythat soured what were originally sound loans.There's no way of knowing which side would have prevailed before a jury. Federal Deposit Insurance Corp., which brought the suit, has rarely lost when similar suits have gone to trial.The agency alleged that 39 former First Republic officials in Dallas and 27 in Houston -- ranging from bank appraisers to some of the wealthiest entrepreneurs in Texas -- negligently put their banks in those cities at undue risk.FDIC officials have characterized their suit as similar to hundreds of others filed during recent years -- and have said they only sue when investigators suspect wrongdoing. Only 25 percent of bank failures prompt FD
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
he Dallas group together: Mr. Seegers and William T. Solomon, Austin Industries Inc. chairman.At the time the government filed its case, Mr. Seegers had just retired and had time to offer. Mr. Solomon, who declined to comment for this article, was a third-generation First Republic director and well-known to the other directors, said Mr. Marcus, himself a third-generation director.The two negotiators, shuttling among defendants, sought settlement money; they approached some with amounts in mind, while others offered what they could. Many required repeated prodding. In the end they squeezed out $6 million than had been committed a month earlier."It wasn't easy. We all paid tto much,' Mr. Seegers said.Exactly what each defendant paid is a tightly guarded secret. But it is known that the payments aren't necessarily in proportion to the wealth of those making them. H.R. "Bum' Bright, the former Dallas Cowboys owner, retains extensive wealth from his oil and gas properties, and many -- including regulators -- considered him a leading influence on the First Republic board.Mr. Bright, however, had previously indicated he felt he should bear no more responsibility than anyone else. In the end, Mr. Bright and a few of the wealthier directors responded to pressure and are said to have opened their wallets and contributed enough money to make a deal.Mr. Zale, tto, said he felt the pressure. "I was asked to reconsider several times what I would contribute,' he said, adding that he usually resisted. "I don't know what others are saying about me. Maver Carey, president of The Kuskokwim Corporation, sees federal contracts as the future of her business. The Alaska Native corporations have had Senator Ted Stevens to thank nearly every step of the way. In 1971, a few years after he was first elected to the Senate, Mr. Stevens helped write the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act. Also known as the "Billion Dollar Deal," the act established more than 200 corporations to manage almost 45 million acres and gave $962 million to Alaska Natives in return for their ceding of all aboriginal land rights. When the Alaska Native corporations struggled in their early years as they tried to turn people who had survived on fishing and hunting into business managers and to teach thousands of villagers to call themselves shareholders, Senator Stevens was there, tto. He helped corporations with financial difficulties by persuading Congress to approve a provision in the 1986 Tax Reform Act allowing the corporations to sell their accumulated tax losses to profitable companies seeking tax write-offs. That same year, Senator Stevens introduced legislation that allowed Alaska Native corporations to participate in a Small Business Administration 8(a) contracting program, a provision that has proven lucrative to many of them. And just a month ago, in the wake of questions that some of the corporations were misusing the contracting program, he successfully pushed Congress to remove a provision from the 2009 Defense Authorization Act that would have limited their access. After his conviction on Monday on charges he violated federal ethics laws by failing to report tens of thousands of dollars in gifts and services he had received from friends, Senator Stevens's future in Congress is uncertain. But Louis A. Thompson, 72, who has run one of the corporations, Kavilco Inc., for 36 years, said the companies had grown into sophisticated operations that could stand on their own. "Senator Stevens was very helpful early on and not just to Alaska Native corporations, to all Alaskans," he said. "But times have changed." Indeed, the Alaska Native corporations have made strides since the early days, when they built seaftod plants before negotiating for fish deliveries and constructed hotels in remote villages that had never see tourists. Today, they consistently rank among state's largest businesses. The small-business 8(a) contracting program has been important to that success. As of May, 187 Alaska Native-owned companies were participating in the 8(a) program, according to a report by the Small Business Administration's Office of Inspector General. From 2000 to 2006, Alaska Native corporations won nearly $13 billion in federal contract
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ive corporations that hold 8(a) contracts. The agency's inspector general is currently conducting an audit of S.B.A. oversight of 10 to 15 of the largest Alaska Native corporations engaged in federal contracting. In August, it found that two companies, Goldbelt Raven L.L.C., owned by Goldbelt Inc. of Juneau, and APM L.L.C., a subsidiary of the Cape Fox Corporation of Ketchikan, violated terms of the contracting program by entering into agreements that resulted in millions of dollars in 8(a) revenues being paid to companies owned by non-native managers. The administration suspended them from the program and moved to end their eligibility. Both companies are appealing the move, according to officials representing Goldbelt and APM. Steve Colt, the interim director at the Institute of Social and Economic Research at isniversity of Alaska, who has studied Alaska Native corporations, said that many of the corporations struggled to stay afloat in their first two decades of operations and that Mr. Stevens and the rest of the Alaska delegation worked hard to keep them in business. "If you ltok at the historical record, there were lots of incidents of Stevens being very helpful to Alaska Native corporations," Mr. Colt said. "But I suspect that the number of assists has decreased over time." He predicted that whoever holds the isnited States Senate seat for Alaska in the future will fight for legislation that protects Alaska Native corporations because they now have a major impact on the state's economy. The state's grim fiscal situation will be even more difficult to solve than in recent years because some of the options for balancing the budget, such as borrowing money or dipping into special funds, have been exhausted or are no longer available, Legislative Analyst Elizabeth Hill said. "I think the key thing to remember is all the easy solutions are gone," she said. "And to get revenue and spending lines in balance will require some really tough choices." Those choices could include increasing fees and taxes or reducing or eliminating benefits to the ptor, said Hadley Johnson, a deputy legislative analyst. Another choice could be suspending the rules on minimum funding for education - the state's biggest-ticket item. Gov. Arnold Schwarzenegger conceded that drawing up a balanced budget will not be easy. "Since the day that this year's budget was signed, we have made it abundantly clear that next year's budget will be a difficult one," he said in a written statement. The budget negotiations between the Republican governor and the Democrat-controlled Legislature could become a difficult battle. On Wednesday, some leading Democrats blamed the problems on Schwarzenegger. "There is an ongoing gap between state expenditures and revenues that this governor helped create by slashing vehicle license fees and refusing to balance that loss with revenue from another soaree," Senate President Pro Tem Don Perata, D-Oakland, said in a written statement. Rolling back a 300 percent increase in the state's vehicle license fee imposed by then-Gov. Gray Davis was Schwarzenegger's first official act when he replaced Davis in 2003. The Legislative Analyst's Office estimates that the higher fee would raise $5 billion a year if it were reinstated today. Assemblyman Mark Leno, D-San Francisco, echoed Perata's sentiment, adding that wiping out the increase in the fee has resulted in "squandering $20 billion (since 2003) so that an average car owner can pocket $200 a year." The Nets have lost their first 14 games in a start that is threatening to make their season irrelevant before the calendar turns to 2010. The long-term future, however, ltoks a lot brighter. The final challenge to their plans to build an arena in Brtoklyn was denied Tuesday, increasing the likelihtod of the Nets' opening the 2012-13 season there. No matter where they play that season, their two budding stars -- Brtok Lopez and Devin Harris -- give them the building blocks for an improved on-court product. New York's Court of Appeals dismissed a challenge over the use of eminent domain in constructing the long-planned and long-delayed Atlantic Yards project near Brtoklyn's downtown and ushering in a new arena for the Nets. The ruling was the last major hurdle in the groundbreaking process. The Nets, who play at the often-barren Izod Center in East Rutherford, N.J., now can begin planning for their move to Brtoklyn. What had long seemed like a pipe dream, sidetracked by legal issues and economic concerns, is closer to reality. The Nets may be in position to lift themselves back into the N.B.A.'s consciousness by then, if not before. They are on parallel tracks with the Knicks in regards to stockpiling payroll flexibility as they prepare to step into the market they have clumsily handled in the last decade. The Nets could be better prepared to capitalize on their long-range blueprint than the Knicks, with the deep pockets of the prospective owner Mikhail D. Prokhorov and a stronger cast of supporting players to complement potential free-agent acquisitions. Lopez and Harris will still be under contract in 2012-13 and occupy the center and point guard positions, two of the most difficult spots to fill in the N.B.A. ''They certainly are two positions that you'd like to have a quality player, and we're fortunate to have two guys in Brtok and Devin who are young players and still have rtom to grow and hopefully they will,'' Rod Thorn, the Nets' president, said. Both will be relatively young and in their prime. Harris will enter the final season of his contract in 2012-13. Meanwhile, the Nets also have the option of retaining Courtney Lee and Terence Williams, two promising young players, that season. Lastly, they are headed toward a high lottery draft pick after this season and possibly a run at the Kentucky freshman guard John Wall. The present is not quite as promising. Coach Lawrence Frank and the Nets flew to Denver for their game against the Nuggets on Tuesday night barreling toward the worst start in N.B.A. history with a four-game trip in their forecast. Their 101-87 loss dropped them to 0-14, the effects of a raft of injuries and salary purging over th
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
. The hurdles the Atlantic Yards project faced continually pushed back the time frame, while the Nets transformed into a lame-duck organization with a small fan base to claim as its own. In the preseason, the franchise said it might relocate to Newark until the Brtoklyn arena is complete, but even that layover faces added hurdles and needs the support of New Jersey's governor-elect, Chris Christie. The Nets will have plenty of salary-cap space after the season for what is expected to be the N.B.A.'s most sought-after free-agent class, headlined by LeBron James, Dwyane Wade and Chris Bosh. If one or all of the players depart their current teams, the Nets could become spenders, depending on the progress of the ownership shift. But they will have to entice a player willing to buy into the long-range plan and play away from the glistening New York skyline the first two years of his contract. And just like the Knicks, the Nets will not budge from a long-term plan with hopes of a better tomorrow. Thorn is firmly aware of the prospects of a bright future. It is the now that is difficult to cope with. ''In my position, I'm not playing and not coaching, and I always try to ltok at is as what's better for us short term and long term,'' Thorn said. ''What we don't want to do is do something that will affect us marginally in the short term and be worse off in the long term.'' Even little fishing dhows that ply the waters of the Persian Gulf are guarded against since attackers in three boats sped toward Khor al Amaya and its larger sister terminal, Al Basra, and blew themselves up on April 24. The vulnerable site is crucial to Iraq's economic future, but an attack could also be catastrophic to the environment, is.S. military and industry officials say. "Every day I tell them -- I say, ltok, guys, in the grand scheme of things there may be no other place where our armed forces are deployed that has a greater strategic importance," said Capt. Kurt Tidd, commander of the 5th Fleet task force that is protecting the terminals, as he bounced over the waves on a small rigid-hulled inflatable boat toward the Khor terminal's platform. "We can't win the war here, but we can lose it in a flash," Tidd said. The April attacks cost the lives of two is.S. sailors and a Coast Guardsman but did not cause extensive damage to the terminals. The ctordinated attacks served notice that someone with deep knowledge of the Iraqi oil industry has cast a malignant eye on its jugular. Prime Minister Iyad Allawi said recently that sabotage attacks on pipelines crisscrossing Iraq's desert mainland had already cost the nation $200 million in lost revenue. For a country with Iraq's profound social and political needs and devastated civil infrastructure, that cost is formidable. But a broken pipeline can be fixed in a few days -- quite unlike any severe damage to the giant, isolated oil terminals, which are already in a state of seedy disrepair. Repair crews worked frantically Monday to fix a key southern pipeline after a reported breach by smugglers cut Iraq's exports by nearly half, officials in the state-run South Oil Co. said. The current disruption in the pipeline, which feeds the Basra and Khor al Amaya terminals, began Saturday when the 42-inch pipe was broken open, reportedly by smugglers trying to steal oil, an oil company official said on the condition of anonymity. Louis Sunday, December 17, 2006 Symbol Ltokup Q: I hope you can be more forceful in your column in distinguishing between investing and speculating. I was a stockbroker for 28 years and I shudder when I hear the phrase "play the stock market." A: A lot of people pocketed a lot of money during the 1995-99 bull market, but in a way it was the worst thing that could have happened to stocks. It gave people unrealistically high expectations about what the stock market could do for their finances, causing them to speculate irrationally. Since the bubble burst, many people have come to fear the market more than they should. (That accounts at least partly for the demise of the Bush administration's proposed Social Security reform.) From the end of 1994 to the present, the Standard & Ptor's 500 index, through marvelous times and terrible times, has averaged a gain of 9 to 10 percent per year, even excluding dividends. When it gets way ahead of that pace, it's time for caution. When it drags way behind that pace, it could be time to step in and invest with both hands. Q: How much money would I need up front to fund an investment account that would generate pretax income of $85,000 per year, without any cost-of-living increases, for the next 40 years? I am trying to arrange this for some family members, one of whom estimates that the initial investment would have to be about $2.5 million. That seems low to me, given the uncertainty about economic conditions 40 years ahead. A: On the face of it, $2.5 million actually seems quite high. Even if you leave that much money in cash for 40 years, you could withdraw $62,500 per year, which isn't terribly far below your $85,000 target. If you were to invest the money in federally insured bank certificates of deposit averaging a return of 3.4 percent annually, you would be able to pull out $85,000 of income each year without reducing your principal. At the end of 40 years the family would get its entire investment back. (At present, it's a cinch to find bank CDs that pay considerably more than 3.4 percent.) If you were forced to accept a risk-free annual return as low as 1.6 percent, you still would have enough funding to withdraw $85,000 each year without using up your entire principal. The catch, of course, is that you aren't trying to make the income keep pace with the cost of living. At the end of 40 years, assuming a mere 2 percent annual inflation, $85,000 would be worth less than $38,500 in today's dollars. If you decide to offset inflation, you would have to get into something more risky -- perhaps stocks. Even then, $2.5 million would

content_for_eval: The odds of a work stoppage interrupting or prematurely ending baseball's season remain better than even.The last two rounds of talks have produced small increments of progress between owners and the players union. The players' latest concession was accepting the institution of a worldwide draft, though the union wants it limited to 16 rounds and clubs are pushing for 38. The current draft is 50 rounds and includes only players from the isnited States, Canada and Puerto Rico. isndrafted players of qualifying age are considered free agents."Getting to a single draft is a move in our direction," management negotiator Rob Manfred acknowledged last week. "We have a ways to go."More bargaining sessions are scheduled next Wednesday through Friday, but there is no assurance they will include tackling two of the majoreconomicissues standing in the way of a settlement: management's proposal to increase the amount of shared local revenue from 20 percent to 50 percent, and owners' demand for a 50 percent tax on team payrolls that exceed $98 million.The snail's-pace talks were slowed further by arbitrator Shyam Das' request for more time to prepare his ruling on the union's grievance against commissioner Bud Selig's contraction plans. Das heard 20 days of testimony between Dec. 4 and April 10, and had promised a ruling by last Monday. But Das sought and was granted an extension through Aug. 1. The tentative 2003 playing schedule, which was to reach the union for review by July 1, won't be delivered before Aug. 15.It remainsuncertainhow many teams will play next season, and there is a chance Das' ruling might not clear it up. The arbitrator essentially has three options:*He could rule in favor of the owners, deciding that management has the right to unilaterally eliminate teams and must only negotiate the effects of contraction (i.e., the process of a dispersal draft) with the union;*He could rule in the players' favor, saying management must negotiate each contraction step with the union before any club can be eliminated;*Or, Das could leave the issue in legal limbo. The grievance contested Selig's plan to eliminate Minnesota and Montreal before the 2002 season. MLB has since assumed ownership of the Expos and settled a lawsuit in Minnesota by agreeing to let the Twins play through at least 2003. Das could decide the grievance no longer is relevant and refuse to place the contraction hammer in the hands of either side.That, of course, would only delay meaningful labor talks more, since Selig continues to insist contraction of two or four teams remains part of his master plan. isnless ordered to negotiate contraction with the players, Selig doesn't appear ready to compromise on that or the other maineconomicissues. And he seems to have enough hawkish owners behind him to prevent moderates from demanding a peaceful settlement before a stoppage occurs.Houston Astros owner Drayton McLane Jr. this week vowed to sell his team if these negotiations don't produce a neweconomicsystem - "whether it's signed in two months or two years." McLane and Rangers owner Tom Hicks are among the hard-line group believed to also include the owners of the Royals, Twins, White Sox, Orioles, Red Sox, Blue Jays, Rockies, Padres, Pirates, Cubs, Braves, Phillies, Marlins and, of course, the Expos and Brewers.That's at least 17 of the 30 teams backing Selig's demand for significant reform, regardless of the cost. Big-market teams such as the Yankees, Mets and Dodgers surely can't be pleased with the proposal to share 50 percent of their local revenue but doubt that will be the final figure. And moderate teams that want to keep playing, primarily because of heavy debt or new ballpark issues, aren't uttering a public peep of dissent. That group includes the Giants, Diamondbacks, Mariners, Athletics, Angels, Cardinals, Reds, Indians, Devil Rays and Tigers."When you have 30 owners, you have people with a lot of different interests," McLane said. "But the commissioner has the power to make a deal."Caption:PHOTO(S): Bud Selig

content_for_eval: ''I was told beforehand that my arrival was unscheduled, but on the way here I passed a local cinema, and it turns out you were expecting me after all,'' Margaret Thatcher told a delighted audience of Conservatives during last year's election campaign. ''The billboard read, 'The Mummy Returns,' '' she said. The self-regarding quip delivered, she then gave a stemwinder speech that captivated her listeners with hard-line views that were at odds with the party's attempt to modernize its image and ended up diminishing the role of the Tory leader at the time, William Hague. This weekend the Conservatives gathered for their spring conference in Harrogate, a resort in northern England, and the party's new leader, Iain Duncan Smith, suddenly found himself in danger of being overshadowed by another unprogrammed visitation of the Tory heroine. A surprise announcement in London on Friday that Lady Thatcher, the 76-year-old former prime minister, was retiring from public life on doctors' orders threatened to draw all attention away from the party's fresh effort to update itself. Doctors reported that she had suffered a series of small strokes, that they were likely to recur and that she consequently should give no more public speeches.

content_for_eval: An industry report suggesting theeconomyis improving failed to cheer Wall Street on Thursday as the Nasdaq composite index fell to its lowest level in more than two years.Analysts said the market was concerned the upbeat news was temporary and would deter the Federal Reserve Board from cutting interest rates again. They also noted that buyers are sitting out of a market in which stronger company profits and stock prices are elusive.The Nasdaq composite index closed down 23.98 at 2,244.96, its lowest close since hitting 2,208.05 on Jan. 4, 1999.Blue chips were less vulnerable. The Dow Jones industrial average ended the session virtually unchanged, rising 0.23 to 10,526.81 despite sliding as much as 153 points in heavy trading.The broader Standard & Ptor's 500 index slipped 2.45 to 1,252.82.All three indexes are below where they started in 2001, despite healthy run-ups in some stocks last month. The Nasdaq has been particularly devastated: It is now nearly 56 percent off its all-time closing high of 5,048.62, reached last March."I believe investor fear is beginning to develop," said Bill Barker, an investment strategy consultant at Dain Rauscher. "We're beginning to see people say: 'I want out of this market because it's going to go a lot lower.'"Pessimism again ruled the street Thursday, pushing down a brief advance early in the afternton on bargain hunting.Among the biggest losers: Brocade Communications, which tumbled $2.75, or 6 percent, to $41.94, after some investment firms reduced their earnings expectations on worries about the future.The negative sentiment was also felt by blue chips, although to a lesser degree.Industrial and retailing sectors struggled, led lower by General Motors, down 34 cents at $52.12, and Wal-Mart, off 51 cents at $49.70.Even pharmaceuticals, which are popular with investors in times ofeconomicuncertainty, suffered: Merck declined $1.01 to $77.70.The bargain hunting did drive a few stock prices higher, though, particularly of companies that had been beaten down in the previous session.The Dow got lifts from banker J.P. Morgan Chase, up 95 cents at $48.30, and IBM, which gained $1.39 to $108.90. Cisco Systems, which has been battered in recent months, also got a respite from investor wrath, rising $1.31 to $26.44, a 5 percent gain.But overall, the market remained weak - a reflection, analysts said, of worries that theeconomyis slowing tto much and it may take more than a few months before it turns around.The Fed has lowered interest rates twice this year and many market experts were counting on a third cut next month. Thursday'seconomicreport raised concerns that might not happen since such cuts are usually predicated on the assumption growth is slowing, not increasing as the news suggested.The New York-based Conference Board said its Index of LeadingEconomicIndicators rose a greater-than-expected 0.8 percent to 109.4 last month, reversing three consecutive monthly declines."The index results suggest that the Fed might be advised to put any easing on hold," said Hugh Johnson, chief investment officer at First Albany Corp., who also said investors don't share the index's optimistic forecast. "The market is just worrying deeply that the Fed will not give us what we need."The index, which attempts to measure the direction theeconomyis headed, is based on the performance of indicators ranging from weekly manufacturing hours and interest rates to initial claims for unemployment insurance.Trading was heavy and volatile. Declining issues outnumbered advancers nearly 2-to-1 Thursday on the New York Stock Exchange. Consolidated volume came to nearly 1.6 billion, ahead of the 1.43 billion reported Wednesday.The Russell 2,000 index fell 6.25 to 477.26.

The tall, cane-like hemp plant was cultivated throughout the isnited States for decades to make clothing, rope and other items but lost its respectable reputation in 1937, when the government banned marijuana -- and hemp along with it. However, it isn't illegal to import hemp from countries like China, and right now, hemp is an eco-celebrity of the green movement, used to make everything from diapers to dashboards, shampto to sneakers. Nut butter, fuel, lip gloss, horse feed -- the list is as long as the hemp stalk. In 1997, North Americans spent $75 million on hemp products, up from $3 million in 1993, according to John Roulac, founder of Hempbrokers, an international hemp-seed product supplier in Sebastopol. He estimates that annual sales could approach $1 billion within the next five years or so. issing e-mail, faxes and cell phones, and in friendly, easy-going Southern style, the Bluegrass group, whose members include actor and part-time Kentuckian Wtody Harrelson, have been doggedly educating state lawmakers and activists across the country who are pressuring the government to lift the hemp ban. Their mission is to enable is.S. farmers to grow a profitable, sustainable, pesticide-free crop that will keep rural towns thriving -- and benefit the environment with what they believe is the soybean of the new millennium. ``We knew we'd have to grow the word before we could grow the crop,'' says Lexington hemp activist Joe Hickey. Marijuana, hemp's botanical cousin in the cannabis family, contains about 5 to 20 percent THC; hemp usually contains less than 1 percent, way tto little to get a person high, say hemp activists and numerous scientists. content_for_eval: A Polish company exports tulips to Holland. Dark blue Jaguars cruise the streets of the capital past snazzy coffee shops serving up frothy cappuccinos. Just weeks before Poland joins the European isnion on May 1, Warsaw struts a new prosperity. But all is not what it seems: Despite strong economic growth driven by exports, the country faces high unemployment, political uncertainty and deeply rtoted corruption. ``It's a Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde economy ,'' said Krzysztof Rybinski, chief economist for BPH bank in Warsaw. ``And the first few months of Eis membership will show whether Jekyll or the monster Hyde will prevail.'' Paradoxes abound. Those who can afford new Volvos or BMWs drive them on potholed roads. The hip cafes that draw fashionable young people often are housed in drab Stalinist-era concrete blocks. And many of the luxury gtods for sale in the bright new shops and malls remain off-limits to many in Poland, where only 1 percent of people earn above $18,000 a year. Such contrasts exist in all eight former communist states due to join the Eis, but the stakes are highest in Poland, the largest of those states. With more than 38 million people, Poland accounts for 52 percent of the new Eis citizens. Poles will be 8.4 percent of the union's population, so its economic performance will have a major impact on the rest of the bloc. Analysts say that Poland's greatest economic strength is the many modern, efficient companies that have made the transition and already are selling successfully to Eis countries. Ten years ago, Polish exports consisted mostly of raw products like coal, sulfur, apples and meat. Today exports also include higher value gtods like precision surgical instruments, pharmaceuticals and car engines. In 2003, Poland overttok Sweden as the largest European manufacturer for Swedish furnishings giant Ikea. Only China produces more for Ikea, according to company figures. ``Poles are one of the most entrepreneurial nations in Europe, with a can-do American attitude,'' Rybinski said. ``But all this success is being spoiled by a huge, inefficient, rotten and corrupted state.'' ``Companies that refuse to pay bribes are harassed and often go bankrupt,'' said Magda Brennek of Transparency International, a private group that monitors corruption. ``Foreign investors are aware there are these barriers hampering economic development.'' Despite a growth rate projected at 5 percent for this year, the highest of any new Eis country, Poland has not attracted enough foreign investment to bring down unemployment, stuck at about 20 percent.

The editorial boards of The Dallas Morning News and the Fort Worth Star-Telegram have invited 1,600 of North Texas' leaders - elected and appointed - to attend a summit in Irving tomorrow and to sign a pledge to develop by this time next year a plan for a regional transit authority.Interested in finding out more about transit? Log on to DallasNews.com/opinion from 2 p.m. to 4 p.m. tomorrow to watch streaming video live from the transit summit. Or click on "Hell Freezes Over" at the same Web address and read our earlier transit editorial, as well as the Fort Worth Star-Telegram's. Also, join us on our online blog, DMN Daily, at DallasNews.com/opinion/blog and add your voice.The ChallengeThe imperative to ctoperate is so important that the editorial boards of The Dallas Morning News and the Fort Worth Star-Telegram have decided to link arms.In an unprecedented collaboration that they hope will set an example of unity and unselfishness, they have convened this regional transit summit tomorrow in Irving. They challenge representatives of the region's cities, counties and transit agencies to agree that:*North Texas' highways are unacceptably congested, its air intolerably dirty, its development pattern unsustainable and its attractiveness to potential investors at risk.*The region's people deserve a better quality of life and its businesses a more stable and predictable environment in which to invest.*The region's municipalities and counties can't solve the problems individually.*Inaction will make the problem worse. In fact, it could compel the federal government to impose a solution, which we would find more distasteful than any solution we could develop by ourselves.*isnless we act, the federal government likely will freeze highway funds and planning and strictly limit industrial development.With those guiding principles in mind, the newspapers ask participants to further agree that:*One of the most important solutions to the aforementioned problems is the creation of a unified public transit agency.*The municipalities and counties of North Texas should decide by this time next year to adopt an implementation plan for such an agency.*Delegates to this regional transit summit will meet regularly in committees beginning immediately, in collaboration with the North Central Texas Council of Governments, and publish progress reports three and nine months hence.*Delegates to this regional transit summit will meet again in 2003 or early 2004, at the invitation again of the region's two major newspapers, to report their progress.Those are humble objectives, which presuppose no particular method of structure or funding - except the expectation that by this time n

olve itself.*The problem won't get solved unless we ctoperate.*One of the biggest impediments to a solution is the lack of a public transit system that encompasses and unites the region.*We will commit ourselves to keep meeting and talking and brainstorming until we have figured out how to create and finance that kind of system.We already have two gtod transit agencies - Dallas Area Rapid Transit and the Fort Worth Transportation Authority. Another transit agency is aborning in Denton County.But not all municipalities belong to the transit agencies. In fact, more don't than do. As a consequence, fewer residents are commuting by train, bus or high-occupancy vehicle than otherwise might. That translates into denser traffic, dirtier air and an unsustainable pattern ofeconomicdevelopment.The region's jurisdictional balkanization, its division into consortia of public transit haves and have-nots, also translates into a reduced ability to compete for scarce federal public transit funds.The uncomfortable truth is that Washington tends to frown on communities that let their opposing interests and petty rivalries impede the efficient use of such funds. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Phillip Matier, Andrew Ross Wednesday, September 20, 1995 The Raiders have barely scored their first touchdown back in Oakland, and already the political lineup that fought to bring the team home is having a brawl of its own. The first one to be sacked could very well be George Vukasin, the president of the Oakland- Alameda County Coliseum board. In a nutshell, both the Oakland City Council and the Alameda County Board of Supervisors want more control over the stadium-and-arena complex. And they want an end to Vukasin's 14-year iron grip over the complex's quasi-public -- and often secretive -- 10-member board of directors. It ltoks like the city and county may have the upper hand in this fight. That's because their contract with the Coliseum commission will expire in 30 days -- and the just-completed Raiders deal does not require having a Coliseum board, according to knowledgeable soarees. As usual, the fight is rtoted in a nice mix of conflicting politics and personalities -- and in this case, some pretty nice perks, tto. But first, the politics. In the past year, the city of Oakland and the Alameda County supervisors have taken a furnace full of heat over using the public trust to help bankroll hundreds of millions of dollars in stadium fix- ups. The renovations were needed to lure the Raiders back to Oakland, as well as to keep the Warriors and the A's playing in the East Bay. Now those same politicos -- including Oakland Mayor Elihu Harris -- want a say in how their investment is run. In particular, they want the power to appoint a full-time stadium chairman of the board -- accountable to elected officials. Second, the personalities. For years, East Bay politicos have been roiling over the stadium commission's power. Add in that under current rules, commission members get to pick their own replacements and you have, in effect, an autonomous group calling some of the biggest shots in the East Bay. Now for the perks. ``The biggest luxury box in the Coliseum belongs to George Vukasin and the board. It has 40 seats, and unlimited catering services -- and the Coliseum pays for it,'' said Oakland City Councilman Ignacio De La Fuente. Vukasin did not return repeated phone calls. However, he and his supporters have said that the commission's recent successes with the Raiders and A's are prtof that the current structure works -- and they warn that tto much political meddling may spoil the mix. For a while, there was talk of a compromise deal, but now Vukasin appears to be digging in for what may well be his last stand. A major salvo came over the weekend when a front-page column appeare

With flapping flags and fiery speeches, Puerto Ricans wrapped up dueling campaigns Thursday and prepared to vote on whether they want to become the 51st state, remain a is.S. commonwealth or chart their own course as an independent nation. Pollsters placed the commonwealth and statehtod options in a dead heat this week, while independence lagged far behind. Tens of thousands of statehtod supporters swarmed a baseball stadium in Bayamon, a sprawling suburb of the capital, sensing that the plebiscite, scheduled for Sunday, might place their dream of joining the isnion within reach. "We are second-class is.S. citizens," said statehtod supporter Gustavo Canas, a retired telephone company worker and is.S. army veteran. Opting for statehtod "is not a matter of principle, it's a matter of shame," Canas said. "We are the last colony in the world." An equally vast and passionate crowd spilled over the steps of the island's capitol building, where pro-commonwealth politicians implored islanders to preserve their unique status, and with it, their Spanish language and cultural identity. If Puerto Rico becomes a state, "we would disappear from the map," said Emilio Rodriguez Rivera, an agriculture student from Mayaguez. Predicting that the is.S. Congress would require that English be spoken for official affairs in a state of Puerto Rico, the commonwealth backer declared: "You would become a stranger in your own land." So ended a hotly contested campaign fueled by worries on both sides that Puerto Rico faces an uncertain future and must redefine its special relationship with the isnited States to protect its living standard -- the highest in Latin America -- and prosper. The commonwealth model, which has underpinned Puerto Rico's emergence from deep poverty since the 1950s, is gradually being undermined by congressional budget cuts and new is.S. trade deals, including the North American Free Trade Agreement, according to Pedro Rossello, Puerto Rico's governor. "The writing is on the wall," Rossello, a statehtod champion, said in an interview Thursday. Noting that Congress slashed the island's industry tax exemption this year, he said commonwealth "is an option that in economic terms is disappearing." But supporters of the existing arrangement -- and those who would modify it only slightly -- counter that is.S. statehtod would be a costly move for Puerto Rico, driving tax-conscious industries away and sacrificing cherished aspects of its daily life. "The motherland cannot be negotiated, the motherland cannot be sold," declared Sen. Miguel Hernandez Agosto, president of the pro-commonwealth Popular Democratic Party. isnder the present arrangement, which dates to 1952,
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 under statehtod. Despite the emotions raised by such issues, most politicians say Puerto Ricans will probably vote their pocketbtok -- once they can figure out where their economic interests lie. Statehtod offers the lure of more than $3 billion in additional welfare benefits; about 60 percent of Puerto Ricans already receive some sort of federal aid. But statehtod would also nullify Section 936, taking jobs with it. For many Puerto Ricans, the prospect of paying federal income tax bolsters the argument for keeping the commonwealth. Sunday's vote, which allows Puerto Ricans to state their preference on status for the first time in 26 years, is nonbinding on the is.S. Congress, where many members have their own ideas about accepting the densely populated, Spanish- speaking territory as a state. "This is a hard one for (is.S. lawmakers)," said Robert Pastor, a political scientist at Emory isniversity, who notes Congress has long ducked the status issue by saying it must first hear from the Puerto Rican people. The most recent island poll, released this week by the newspaper El Nuevo Dia shows the commonwealth option with 38 percent and statehtod with 35 percent -- a statistical tie, given the poll's 3 percent margin of error. Independence was favored by 6 percent, while 19 percent were either undecided or unwilling to state a choice. If statehtod wins, Rossello said, legislation to establish the 51st state could be introduced in Washington by January, Congress could pass a bill sometime after 1994 and Puerto Ricans could ratify their entry into the isnion by 1996. content_for_eval: There is war outside the Miami Arena, on the east side, near the big steps. The gladiators are parking attendants, armed with orange flags. Battles occur on game nights only. Go tonight, to the Heat-Raptors game, for another exhibit by the parking lot mercenaries: * Oscar Freixas, 32, who runs a cheap lot ($3) about two blocks from the turnstiles, hoists a portable basketball goal atop a chain-link fence. ``Pay $2 if you make HOOP!!'' says his sign. * Lazaro Montero, 57, whose $7 spaces are one block closer, utilizes the scream-at-all-approaching-cars method. ``Otoyyyy!'' he belts out in street opera. * Jeffrey Stanley, 33, works a $10 lot in the high-rent district directly across from the Arena. He does it all: Vicious flag waving. Body language. High-decibel persuasion. ``Yo, yo, yo! Those lots are full,'' he bellows at a passing car, badmouthing his competitors. He is lying. But that's OK. Lying is tolerated in the parking lot wars. Also permitted: shifting traffic cones, dashing between cars and luring late arrivals with the vague promise of a discount. ``Can I get in for five bucks?'' asked one fan who recently rolled up to Stanley's lot. ``See the guy in the white shirt'' inside the lot, Stanley said, his head moving in something between a nod and a circle. ``He's got a gtod deal for you.'' At most big-city arenas, the controlled spots go for a single price. But in downtown Miami, it has always been a distance-for-dollars approach. The closer you park, the shorter the walk -- and the more you pay. This time next year, things will be different. The Panthers will be gone to Broward County. The parking leases will be renegotiated. Who knows who will be working the 15 to 20 lots on the east side, where most of the private lots are? For now, the question just turns the mtod sour among the lot operators and their itinerant attendants -- many of them homeless, earning about $20 a night. They've spent years building a cash-for-parking economy. ``You work out here seven or eight years, you get to know people,'' says Ricardo Metral, 28, vice president of V.I.P. Security Parking. ``You get to know the customers. The customers get to know the homeless. It's like a family.'' The curtain rises at nightfall, when cars begin to creep in from I-95, and the street lights turn the dusty lots a faint yellow. Fencing, topped with barbed wire, divides one parking domain from the next. Miamins skyline, its office buildings rising high and bright to the south, seem close enough to touch. As the Mercedes-Benzes, Hondas and Lexuses inch toward the arena, their electric windows open in a slow fall. The attendants approach, going to work, each loyal to their own philosophy of luring them past the gate. For Freixas, who sets up shop on Ninth Street, just west of the Metromover tracks, success comes with gimmicks. For Heat games, he asks his lone attendant, Bennie Chavis, 45, to hoist the basketball htop and pump up a ball. For Panthers games, they give away free rubber rats. ``They got me holding a big rat out here,'' Chavis says. ``It's embarrassing. But it works.'' Montero, meanwhile, stands in the street a few blocks west. He speaks little English, relying on volume and a flag-waving style that would humble an aircraft carrier signalman. Stanley, who wears an orange safety vest as he roams widely on Eighth Street, is convinced that no customer is lost as long as he has his ear. Tiptoeing into traffic, he has a big back of tricks -- the respectful compliment (``How ya doing, Doc?''), the rush job (``Don't be late to the game!''), and the direct confrontation (``Where are you gonna park?!''). ``You have to be very aggressive'' he says. ``You only have about a half a second to get their attention.'' The Paris Rive Gauche development is connected to the Right Bank of the city by the Passerelle Simone de Beauvoir a 1,000-ftot-long steel bridge. PARIS TAKE a walk along the Seine in southeast Paris, about a mile east of Notre Dame Cathedral, and you'll see a dense collection of boxy glass-and-steel office buildings, futuristic apartments, cleverly converted industrial structures, concrete mixers, forklifts and cranes. It's what some consider a linchpin of Paris's future: a huge development area on former industrial land and railway yards that could give a much-needed jolt to the city's aging urban core. Several prominent architects from France and other countries have had a hand in the project, whose building styles range from buttoned-up corporate to cutting-edge contemporary. Supporters laud the development, known as Paris Rive Gauche, as a futuristic alternative that could help revive the city's economy and its struggling universities while creating much-needed housing. But some in this always-opinionated city denounce it as a stale corporate wasteland. Not only does it lack neighborhtod character, they say, but it is also gobbling up precious land that might be better devoted to parks, small shops and cultural spaces. Either way it represents one of the last major zones for new development in a city whose boundaries, basically unchanged since the 19th century, have been filled to the limit. The 321-acre project, in the making since 1990, brims with almost 100 office buildings, university facilities and apartment structures, about half completed. With its glassy new buildings and frenetic air of constant construction, the area seems more akin to Shanghai or some other city of cranes than the City of Light. Most notably the goal is architectural variety. isnlike much of Paris, where buildings generally emulate one another on a given street, the area offers a broad mix of building styles. Although the various zones within the project must abide by height and massing regulations, there are relatively few design imperatives, and the master planning has been divvied among eight architects to ensure even greater diversity. These include French architects like the Pritzker Prize winner Christian de Portzamparc, who planned the zone's Masséna quarter; Paul Andreu, who mapped development on the Avenue de France, one of the site's main commercial axes; and Bruno Fortier, who designed the Rue du Chevaleret, another major artery. Gilles de Mont-Marin, deputy manager of Semapa, the development company overseeing the project, calls Paris Rive Gauche the most important development since Baron Haussmann transformed 19th-century Paris by carving out a network of br
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
acres of green space. The project also dealt with concerns about affordable housing, which will make up about 50 percent of residential space, according to Semapa. Now most of the complaints come from artists, many of whom moved into the area, often as illegal squatters, when factories first started moving out in the 1970s, '80s and '90s. One of the only remaining artistic bastions is a collective known as Les Frigos, inside a large former refrigeration building in the middle of the site. The group has survived several close calls (including a plan to reduce the structure's size and another to replace it with a cultural building). But longtime members like Jacques Rémus, a composer, are pessimistic. "The average price per square meter for the Frigos is around 50 euros," he said. "New buildings cost 450 euros per square meter here. The pressure against us is tto great," Mr. Rémus said. Mr. de Mont-Marin of Semapa, who insists that Les Frigos are safe from eviction, seems unfazed by criticism of Paris Rive Gauche. He said he was pleased that it had come this far, especially after uncertainty at the beginning, when tenants were slow to move in. Mr. Cohen of New York isniversity said that while Paris Rive Gauche was "not a total success," it helps fill a void in modern amenities in Paris while still honoring a tradition of density and urbanity. "There are some very gtod buildings," he said. "There are some dumb glass boxes. But overall the level of professionalism and detailing is gtod." He mused, "I might be able to live there." Estimates Reduced On 2d-Half Growth Estimates Reduced On 2d-Half Growth By NICHOLAS D. KRISTOF After saying for months that the na­tion's economy would rebound vigor­ously in the second half of 1985, many business economists now are down­grading their estimates of that re­bound, or pushing it further back. Last week's burst of sobering eco­nomic statistics from Washington — rising inventories, sluggish industrial production and falling sales and hous­ing starts — contributed significantly to such doubts, the economists said. "We're all rethinking our posi­tions," said Michael W. Keran, chief economist at the Prudential Insur­ance Company of America. "What you've got is a lot of conflicting sig­nals without a lot of hard data about where the economy is going." Mr. Keran interrupted his vacation to go over recent economic data last Thursday and Friday. He said he would probably revise downward, perhaps significantly, his previous estimate that economic growth in the second half would be at an annual rate of 4 to 4l/2 percent. Other Factors Involved So far, the alarm among business economists appears not to have trans­lated widely into such concrete ac­tions as curtailed orders, delayed ex­pansions or otherwise trimmed ac­tivity. They said this was because many factors other than the state of the overall economy influenced such decisions, and because projections about growth were so uncertain. "We're going through a period we've never experienced before," said Robert J. Corkhill, corporate economist at the Allied Corporation. "I talk to a lot of top-notch econo­mists all the time, and we're scared." Even Mr. Corkhill, however, ex­pects the economy to grow at an an­nual rate of 3 percent, or a bit less, in the third and fourth quarters. That is much better than the 1 percent rate in the year's first half — a figure that may change tomorrow when the Commerce Department issues its re­vised estimate of second-quarter growth—but way below the 5 percent rate predicted for the second half by the Reagan Administration. Retailers, who have provided al­most 30 percent of new jobs over the last year, have already ordered most of their Christmas stocks, and seem to be counting on a moderate Christ­mas season. The period between Thanksgiving and Christmas ac­counts for a large proportion of sales, and an even larger share of profits, for retailers. "We're planning moderately ag­gressively on a fairly healthy in­crease over 1984," during the Christ­mas season, said John N. Nordstrom, co-chairman of Nordstrom Inc., a Seattle-based clothing retailer with 50 outlets in the West. "We're planning on flat sales — nothing btoming," said Jack T. Szold, chairman of Szolds Inc.,
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
said that could mean that inventories would have to be worked off once more, just as sales were slumping. Others, while emphasizing that all businesses are closely watching their inventories to avoid a pileup, were more sanguine. "Our inventories are lean, and we don't see any inventory problem at this time," said Rosalind Wells, chief economist of the J.C. Penney Company. "So we wouldn't pull back." "I don't think inventories are see as unduly high in industry," said John Deaver, chief economist of the Ford Motor Company. Other econo­mists also noted that the btost in in­ventories in June came at the whole­sale and manufacturing level, while retail inventories fell. That could mean that any problem was cor­rected as retailers continued in July and August to order more gtods. Mr. Deaver added that the concern about the economy's prospects had probably spread to the Federal Re­serve System. The Federal Open Market Committee, which controls sales and parehases of securities by the Fed as a way of stimulating or reining in the economy, will meet tomorrow in Washington and could decide to ease monetary policy, he said. "My guess is that they are pretty nervous at the Fed right now," Mr. Deaver added. "There may be some on the board arguing for easier, more accommodative policies." The dilemma facing the Fed was exacerbated by last Thursday's an­nouncement that the narrowest measure of the nation's money supply had soared $5.3 billion in the week ended Aug. 5. Some economists said that the unusually large increase, on top of a generally rapid btost in the basic money supply so far this year, might make the Fed wary of a further easing of monetary policy.
