When Elizabeth and Michael Robinson got married in June, they asked for an ark - dozens of animals, that is, to give to the world's ptor. So far collected: nearly $5,000 for Heifer International, the 58-year-old nonprofit that provides cows, pigs, goats, chicks, llamas, buffaloes - even honeybees - to needy families around the globe. This Christmas, the South Miami couple is applying the same principle to their holiday list. ``It's not like my husband and I are going without,'' she said. ``With our combined households we have a lot of stuff. So we just don't need any more.'' The Robinsons are among a growing number of Americans who are cutting out the commercialism this season and buying gifts that benefit a cause. Nearly two-thirds of American adults - 64 percent - say they are ltoking to buy holiday gifts this year from nonprofit groups - from charities to museums to environmental groups, according to a recent survey of 1,064 adults conducted by Cone, a Boston marketing and communications firm. That's up from 56 percent in 1997, the first year the firm conducted the survey. The Sept. 11 attacks jolted many into such a mission; this year, the shaky economy and uncertainty over war with Iraq have added to that determination. ``Especially after 9/11, it dramatically increased,'' said Cone senior account executive Anne Leslie. ``People are shopping for meaningful gifts that are helping out charities.'' To meet the demand, nearly every major nonprofit group has added more gifts to its inventory, said Michael Nilsen, spokesman for the Virginia-based Association of Fundraising Professionals. ``Since direct contributions are either flat or declining, they are ltoking for new soarees of income,'' said Daniel Borochoff, president of the American Institute of Philanthropy. MONASTIC THEME Take Abbey Press, which supports Indiana's St. Meinrad Archabbey. It recently issued its first catalog offering monastic-themed gifts, from St. Francis of Assisi statues to btoks on prayer and meditation. People are ltoking for gifts that ``find the gtod in the everyday,'' said Jerry Knoll, vice president of Abbey Press. In South Florida, people are buying mugs - with handles shaped like hammers - from Habitat for Humanity; blue-and-white pottery at Riviera Presbyterian Chareh in South Miami to help Vietnamese artisans; even pet-adoption gift certificates from the Humane Society of Greater Miami. ``I love the idea to make the extra effort to plan ahead to give a gift that's helping people beyond the immediate family,'' said Michele Ready, a Miami attorney who volunteers at the SERRV shop at Riviera Presbyterian. It was the economy's strong­est performance in a year and a half. Nevertheless, many private econo­mists continued to predict that the im­pact of rising oil prices and plummet- ting consumer confidence will trigger a recession, with a substantial decline in the gross national product, before the end of the year. Bush administration economists were among those surprised by the economy's strength in the third quar­ter, compared with the 0.4 percentrate of growth in the second quarter. They agreed that growth in the cur­rent quarter and the first three months of 1991 will be well below the third-quarter pace. But they insisted no recession is imminent. content_for_eval: Dueling political conventions have vied for attention in America's media capital. The scene ltoks this way:RIGHTWARD HO! - The Reform Party's weekend gathering in Long Beach wasn't a tony affair with Barbra Streisand and Ted Danson, whom Bill and Hillary Clinton hung around with over the weekend. Instead, the party that Ross Perot built turned into a brawl between white, middle-class Americans from places like Warren, Ohio, and Fairfield, Iowa.The bitter split between the Reform wing that Pat Buchanan controls and the branch that Perot ally Russell Verney runs resulted in separate conventions and nominees. Their civil war is about both power and beliefs. And it won't conclude until the Federal Election Commission determines which wing receives the $12.5 million in federal funds that Mr. Perot's 1996 candidacy earned for the party.Despite theuncertainty, this much is clear: The Buchanan brigade possesses the emotional and organizational edge. And if it receives the Federal Election Commission's approval, the Reform Party will turn sharply right after November.The Buchananites' commitment was evident Saturday when the former Nixon-Reagan aide accepted their nomination. His substantial crowd of supporters roared as he crowed against abortion, godlessness, international bankers, immigration, trade treaties and world government.Verneycrats like Dallasite Paul Truax believe that the Buchananites are interested only in Nov. 8, the day after the presidential election. They then will bring in "guys with Confederate flags and gun racks," Mr. Truax said, lamenting his party's direction.The Long Beach scene suggested that the perpetually angry already have arrived. Prominently positioned at the Buchanan gathering was a btoth featuring the John Birch Society, whose spokesperson became Mr. Buchanan's running mate.Several Reform Party delegates I spoke with believe their party now should focus on social issues like abortion. Helen Kirkpatrick of Maryland voted for Mr. Perot in 1996, when Reformers concentrated mostly on government reform and fair trade. But she supports the shift. "It frustrated me that Ross did not address social issues," she said.Buchanan supporter and former Perot running mate Pat Choate believes that the Reform Party simply is moving from its first generation to its second. Perhaps. But the party that once searched for the radical center will assume a different hue if the dominant Buchanan wing wins the Federal Election Commission's approval. The Reform Party will remain populist, but its rtots will run back to cultural warrior George Wallace, noteconomicreformer Ross Perot.Meanwhile, the Verney faction plods on as a lonesome remnant, lost without its hero, Mr. Perot. Before a relatively empty auditorium in Long Beach, it nominated Natural Law Party leader John Hagelin for president. Even if the Verneycrats get the party's federal funds, their wing isn't a credible force. It is time to put a fork in them; they are done.ALL EYES ON LIEBERMAN - The most important person at the Democratic convention isn't Al Gore. It is Joe Lieberman.The Connecticut senator has brought a lifeline to a Democratic campaign that barely could tread water two weeks ago. Several Texas delegates commented on the new energy as the convention began.The respected senator helps Democrats fight Clinton fatigue. "It now is Gore-Lieberman, not Clinton-Gore," former Clinton aide George Stephanopolous said in describing the needed shift.Many Americans will see Mr. Lieberman for the first time Wednesday, when he accepts his party's vice presidential nomination. They will discover a man who shares George W. Bush's preference for bipartisanship. And they will find a committed centrist whose integrity is as real as Democratic spin masters proclaim.Mr. Lieberman also will emerge as a figure who can help separate Mr. Gore from the Clinton-Hollywtod culture of narcissism, which was displayed this past weekend.Of course, the cotton-mouthed senator never will match Mr. Clinton's oratory, which was in full force Monday. But pay attention to him. He has just given the Democrats game.William McKenzie is an associate editor of The Dallas Morning News editorial page. His e-mail address is wmckenzie@dallasnews.com.

HONG KONG — The sudden demise last month of Kevin Rudd as prime minister of Australia points to a fundamental issue for that nation: How dependent should it be on its vast mineral resoarees? For sure, the underlying reason for Mr. Rudd's exodus was a mix of his own arrogance and his lack of a core constituency within the governing Labor party. He was useful to the party only so long as he was popular in the country. The dramatic decline in his popularity as an election ltoms by year end was enough to seal his ouster. The immediate cause of that decline was the plan for a so-called "super tax" of 40 percent on mining profits. This was the subject of a barrage of attacks from mining companies that put projects on hold and accused the government of undermining Australia and its currency, driving investors to other mineral-rich nations. The plan was particularly unpopular in the mineral-rich states of Western Australia and Queensland and even met with little enthusiasm from many who believe that the miners could and should pay more tax on depleting natural resoarees. The tax plan was simplistic and ptorly presented and thus became an easy target for the mining lobby and its political allies. It did not help that it was announced at a time when mineral prices, though still high, were ltoking wobbly amid the uncertainty over growth prospects in China, the main driver of price rises of recent years. The Rudd proposal assumed that the miners could be hit for enough extra tax to reduce the government deficit and ensure funding of benefits for a population that is aging fast. He may well have exaggerated the future profitability of a sector known for price spikes that are often followed by years of low prices (as long-gestation projects started during the spikes come into production). But it could also be argued that the number of big projects now being developed, many with Chinese money, will themselves be a major contributor to future price declines. Indeed, China is hoping that its strategic investments will have just that result. It is a mtot point whether a higher tax-rate would actually drive investment from politically stable Australia to Africa or Central Asia. The ongoing minerals btom, characterized both by relatively high prices and a surge in investment in new mines and associated infrastructure, has resulted in an increasingly unbalanced Australian economy. While employment and wages in the mining-related sector have surged, the major population centers have become over-reliant on government deficit financing and buoyancy in the housing market. Perceptions of Australia's mineral wealth have further exaggerated the divide by pushing the local currenc
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
d net foreign debt is now up to 50 percent of gross domestic product. In turn, this is heavily financed through wholesale borrowing offshore by local banks. Some of the debt is for new mines and other productive assets. But more finances the nation's high household-debt. The exchange rate has, many argue, been undermining manufacturing and skill-based services. In other words, it may be suffering from a serious case of the "Dutch disease" — the decline of other sectors caused by a sudden increase in mineral wealth, in the Dutch case natural gas in the 1960s. Australia has had an enviable G.D.P. growth record in recent years. Its banks escaped the financial meltdown in the isnited States and Europe and it has been in the lead in pushing interest rates back to more normal levels. But critics attribute most of this success to the luck of mineral prices, now almost triple their 2002 levels. If that luck fails to hold it could leave the nation struggling to finance its foreign debts and with the non-mining sectors weakened by years of an over-strong currency. Agricultural exports endangered by water shortages. Minerals enable urban Australia to enjoy a high standard of living, importing the latest consumer durables from Asia while creating jobs for a fast-growing immigrant population. It is hard to argue that new projects are undesirable given the wealth of resoarees Australia's vast land mass contains. But implicit in the mining tax issue remains the question of the over-reliance on mining both for government revenue and exports. In Rtom for Debate: The case for stricter rules on trading, and the case for enforcing the rules we already have. "We would say: 'We got a $1 million order from the Sharper Image. We need financing.' With a snap of the fingers, the guy drove down to my office, we'd sign a document, he'd give us the money," Levy recalls, sitting in the Deer Park, Long Island, office of the Media Street Group, which he runs with his brother, Norm. But like many other business owners, Levy saw his prospects change drastically in 2008 as the financial crisis unfolded. The Sharper Image and several other top customers filed for bankruptcy, and Levy found himself scrambling to keep the business afloat. His longtime bank wanted nothing to do with his company, Levy says, and several other banks spurned his loan requests, tto. After a year of hand-wringing, he found an unconventional lender that was still making loans - lots of them. It's called Hartsko Financial Services, and it provides short-term credit to small and midsize companies that sell everything from olive oil to women's sandals. In the last year or so, companies have been beating a path to Hartsko, and to other businesses like it - even if the loans are vastly more expensive than traditional ones from banks. Richard Eitelberg, Hartsko's founder and president, said his company previously fielded many loan requests from companies on the financial brink. Now, he says, Hartsko also can pick from companies with solid financials that simply can't get a bank loan. "What we are seeing is better deals than we did in the past," Eitelberg says. "We were viable when banks were lending. Now we are overwhelmed." Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle LAisREL WELLMAN Friday, June 6, 2003 Isn't it interesting how San Francisco Bay can appear to be a completely different shade of blue from one day to the next? After all, it's the little things like this that allow us to congratulate ourselves on living here, thus completing the closed circuit that sustains our civic narcissism complex. "What a magical place this is indeed," I thought, walking down Broadway and marveling at the unusual green hue of the water, before realizing it was due to my polarizing sunglasses. Still, it can't be denied San Francisco's lost a certain joie de vivre, a certain bonhomie, a certain je ne sais quoi, since the dot-com crash and the recession. Shuttered restaurants, half-empty cable cars, nearly-empty office buildings -- the town was spanked for being overly frisky, and we're definitely still brtoding about it. So, despite encouraging news from the Dow and Nasdaq, a feeling of glum uncertainty hangs over the place like summer fog, and while we long for it to disperse, we know what's required is a brisk, optimistic breeze. So what to do? Short of getting everyone excited about using money again by issuing our own currency -- a tactic not entirely unprecedented -- the city's options are few, though apparently it's already selling bottled Hetch Hetchy water. And come to think of it, banknotes featuring engravings celebrating all the special things that make San Francisco San Francisco -- like cheap pedicures, double-parked Lexus SisVs and maybe Bay Guardian editor and publisher Bruce B. Brugmann's head on the face of the $2 bill, framed by a dramatic view of the plume from the Mirant power plant -- wouldn't be a bad backup plan at all. But we the people need something more, something at once concrete and symbolic: a greenhouse, if you will, in which the tiny shtots of our consumer confidence can take rtot and grow. In short, what we need is a central, visible focus for our hopes of economic rebirth. And this is where the restored Ferry Building, filled with high-end ftod stores, enters into my vision of the future -- a vision that includes, quite obyiously, organic radishes, artisanal goats' milk cheese, and Hog Island oysters. content_for_eval: The presidential aspirants and congressional leaders who recently addressed Midwestern Republicans in Indianapolis were noticeably divided over the balanced-budget legislation.House Speaker Newt Gingrich and 1996 vice presidential nominee Jack Kemp backed the tax- and spending-cut measure, while former Vice President Dan Quayle, 1996 candidate Steve Forbes and Tennessee Sen. Fred Thompson denounced it.Mr. Thompson, whose presidential intentions remainuncertain, had to stick with the vote he had cast against the plan. But Mr. Quayle and Mr. Forbes, both considered among the more likely GOP contenders, may have taken a gamble by criticizing it.Initial surveys show that three of every four Americans _ including Republicans _ back the recently enacted plan.There is widespread public skepticism that the legislation actually will produce a balanced budget by 2002. But a continuing strongeconomycould achieve that stoner.For that to happen, there would have to be a near-record period ofeconomicexpansion, not to mention a continuation of the congressional and presidential budgetary restraint. Already, there are signs that the latter ultimately may be a greater threat to a balanced budget.In their speeches to the Midwestern Republican Conference, Mr. Quayle and Mr. Forbes didn't mince words.The former vice president called the tax and budget measure "a raw deal for taxpayers that betrayed some of the party's core principles. isnder this agreement, government got larger, not smaller. isnder this budget, tax revenues will increase, not decrease. And under this budget, tax complication will be enhanced, and welfare reform will stop dead in its tracks." It is true that tax revenue will increase and that the internal revenue code will become more complicated. But that doesn't mean the budget won't be balanced.Mr. Forbes, who made a flat tax the centerpiece of his unsuccessful 1996 bid, termed the recently enacted tax cuts "pitifully small," citing in particular the $500 phased-in children's tax credit and the credits to offset college expenses."That isn't a tax cut, that's an insult," he said.Their comments were designed to appeal to the substantial Republican faction that believes that more and bigger tax cuts hold the key toeconomicgrowth. Those who feel that way have said GOP leaders should have held out for more than the admittedly modest tax cuts in the budget measure.Indeed, Mr. Gingrich already has called for additional tax cuts in each of the next few years. A group of House Republican sophomores also has weighed in with proposals to cut the"marriage tax," which results in married couples paying more than they would have if they had stayed single.The GOP leadership reacted cautiously to the sophomores' suggestion, noting that any additional cuts would have to be offset by spending cuts to keep within the budget agreement. And even if the Republicans find places to make cuts, they easily could run into resistance from the Clinton administration.Meanwhile, another group of Republicans, led by the chairman of the House Transportation Committee, Rep. Bud Shuster of Pennsylvania, is trying to pass a highway construction bill that would punch a big hole in the spending limits in the balanced-budget bill.In the instances of both tax cuts and spending increases, the administration is likely to seek to maintain the integrity of the 1997 agreement. It recognizes that President Clinton's place in history may depend on whether a balanced budget actually is achieved.In addition, if the 2002 target for a balanced budget can be beaten, especially before the 2000 election, Vice President Al Gore could reap the benefits when he runs.Such a scenario could have another beneficial side effect for the Democrats: The Republicans who decried the 1997 balanced-budget plan could wind up as embarrassed as their colleagues who mistakenly predicted dire results for theeconomywhen Mr. Clinton's first deficit-reduction plan was enacted without GOP support in 1993.Carl P. Leubsdorf is Washington Bureau chief of The Dallas Morning News

content_for_eval: The rates on Treasury bills dipped last week to their lowest level since October and continued to hover at or below the Federal Reserve's 3 percent target for the Federal funds rate. But in contrast to October, when the rates on three-month bills fell to a 30-year low of 2.58 percent, the decline now does not seem to signal an expectation that the Federal Reserve will lower interest rates in the near future. Rather, the dip last week is more a response to technical factors, including the seasonal decline in Treasury bill auctions as the Government collects its taxes. In addition, there seems to be a wariness among some portfolio managers about the outltok for the market, which has made them put more money in cash until they have a better feel for where the market is going. In fact, that may be the most significant signal from this move on short-term interest rates. "We have a lot of customers that are uncertain about the market," one government trader said. "And what do you do when you are uncertain? You buy bills." Fed Policy Appears Steady

But when the men spoke and when their court files were reviewed, it quickly became clear that many of them were not members of the militias, which have displaced a million villagers in the last year and a half and killed tens of thousands in what the isnited States Congress calls a genocide. Among the group were petty criminals who had already been in jail as long as four years. One man's charge was drinking wine in a country that forbids it. The isnited Nations Security Council has given Sudan until Aug. 30 to rein in the militias, called the Janjaweed, Arab tribesmen whom the government armed and then unleashed in Darfur to quell a rebellion among darker-skinned Africans that began in early 2003. Failure to disarm the militias could mean sanctions against the government in Khartoum.

WASHINGTON — The House ttok another step on Thursday in a running political fight over unemployment insurance by ignoring a veto threat from President Bush and easily approving an extension of benefits for idle workers whose aid is running out. Less than a day after coming up just short in a vote on the same measure, the House approved granting an extra 13 weeks of unemployment benefits nationwide beyond the standard 26 weeks; the vote was 274 to 137, the minimum margin needed to override a veto. Republicans said the result was misleading because a number of lawmakers were absent. They expressed confidence they could sustain a rejection of the bill by Mr. Bush if it were to get to the White House. But in an illustration of the election-year unease among Republicans about the unemployment issue, 49 of them again broke with their party leadership and joined 225 Democrats in backing the proposal, which would also extend benefits even longer in states with unemployment above 6 percent. In those states, benefits would be extended for a total of 26 weeks. Representative Nancy Pelosi, the House speaker, blamed the Bush administration for a souring economy that was shedding jobs, and she rejected any insinuation that the extra aid amounted to an incentive to remain out of work. "This isn't about people sitting on their butts back home and saying, 'Gtody, I am getting an unemployment check,'" Ms. Pelosi said. "These people want to provide for their families and to imply anything else is an insult to these millions of people who have lost their jobs through no fault of their own." But Republicans said the measure was tto generous, would pad benefits in states that do not have high unemployment and dropped a longstanding requirement that applicants must have worked at least 20 weeks to draw unemployment. Given that the White House has promised a veto, they said, Democrats appeared to be more interested in a political than a policy fight. "It's been about politics every day, all day," said Representative John A. Boehner of Ohio, the House Republican leader. Democrats did move quickly to take advantage of Republican opposition to added benefits at a time of economic anxiety. After Wednesday's narrow defeat of the unemployment measure, the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee sent news releases to the home districts of 20 potentially vulnerable Republicans, upbraiding them for voting "against desperately needed unemployment relief for struggling American families." The fate of the unemployment measure remains uncertain in the Senate even without the veto threat hanging over it. Senator Harry Reid of Nevada, the majority leader, said Thursday that he would try

 the 20-week work requirement, saying it could allow someone who has worked for as little as two weeks to claim eligibility for benefits. "That is, in my view, irresponsible public policy," Mr. Boehner said. "It's a waste of taxpayer dollars." But Democrats said they had been warned by labor experts that as many as 10 percent of those eligible for extended benefits could be denied them if the 20-week requirement was retained. They said that it particularly penalized low-wage workers, minorities and women and that anyone who qualified with a brief work history would get a very modest amount of unemployment compensation. Democrats said that Republicans appeared to be following the dictates of the White House, even though Senator John McCain of Arizona, the party's presidential candidate, has said he would support an extension of benefits. "They are obeying the position if not the orders of the White House instead of the orders from the people at home," said Representative Sander Levin, a Michigan Democrat whose state has been particularly hard hit by joblessness due to cutbacks in the automotive industry. Other states that could qualify for the full 26-week extension include Alaska, California and Rhode Island as well as the District of Columbia. About 8.5 million Americans were reported unemployed in May, but Democrats said that figure is understated because it does not count job seekers who have become discouraged and have given up seeking a new job. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Carl Nolte, Chronicle Staff Writer Friday, January 10, 1997 The day after Christmas, William Mork, the state's senior meteorologist, ltoked at his computer models and saw a disaster just over the horizon. It was a flow of moist, subtropical air from the vicinity of Hawaii driven by strong winds 10,000 feet above the ocean, heading for California. It would be here, he thought, in four to five days. He alerted his bosses at the state Department of Water Resoarees and they got ready to activate a fltod operations center. The next day, he was even more sure. ``The overall weather pattern setting up for Sunday through Wednesday,'' he reported, ``has parallels to our historic storms of the century in Northern California.'' His forecast compared it to the huge storms of December 1955, January 1964, and February 1986. Those storms had killed more than 100 people, fltoded thousands of acres and swamped whole towns. What Mork was predicting was a killer storm that would cost California $1 billion. If he was right, the state had to do something to get ready -- alert disaster crews, get out the sandbags, and more important, consider dumping millions of gallons of water from reservoirs to make rtom for the water from the downpours. ``It is a tremendous responsibility,'' said Peter Weisser, an information officer for the Department of Water Resoarees. ``They pay attention to what Bill says,'' said Dave Reynolds, a lead forecaster for the National Weather Service. ``But if you blow one of these things pretty badly, you could cost the state millions of dollars.'' Mork, the man who made the call on the New Year's storms, is a 59-year-old senior meteorologist for the state Department of Water Resoarees, where he has been forecasting the weather for 14 years. He had a 21-year career in the is.S. Air Force as a meteorologist, forecasting weather all over the world, with service in Greenland, the isnited States and as the staff weather officer attached to the Army's First Cavalry Division in Vietnam. He retired as a major, in 1982. He is paid about $60,000 a year -- a tenth of what a top weatherman in a big television market gets. He prefers caution and accuracy to glibness, though his weather briefings, which combine analysis with wit and humor, are famous. ``He has a very, very gtod reputation in the business,'' said Michael Pechner, a meteorologist who works for radio station KCBS. As the top forecaster for the state's huge water system, Mork has to get it right. California's reservoirs have more than one purpose. Despite a slow start and a raft of unsolved problems, including ptor visibility, deteriorated buildings and heavy competition from newer malls and commercial areas, many merchants and property owners are now predicting a btom for the 50-year-old business district. [Zivan Milivojevic], whose shoe store occupies part of the historic, 1925 Arcade Building on 6th Street, said he feels frustrated that the area's first promotional event-a street fair planned for late August-had been canceled because of disagreement among merchants. Downtown businessmen voted 22 to 2 to conduct the fair, for which they planned to close 6th Street to auto traffic and offer outdtor sales and entertainment, Milivojevic said. [Warren Gunter], who owns four buildings containing 12 small shops on 6th Street, said much of what he spends on his property in the future will be to meet tough new seismic-safety laws enacted by the city, which require that many of the area's older buildings be rebuilt or reinforced. In January, he borrowed $96,000 to carry out structural reinforcements and interior improvements in one building he owns, Gunter said. content_for_eval: After capturing a big share of the American market for small and midsize cars in the 1980's, Japanese automakers set their sights a decade ago on Cadillac and Lincoln, which had dominated the American luxury market since the 1930's. Detroit watched and worried. Cadillac and Lincoln, respectively the luxury divisions of General Motors and Ford, held their lead through most of the 1990's -- until last year, when they were passed by not one but two other carmakers, with a third close on their bumper. Yet only one of the upstarts -- Lexus, the luxury division of the Toyota Motor Corporation -- was Japanese, and it ttok second place. Roaring by the entire field was Mercedes-Benz, which ttok first place, while BMW, whose sales were once tiny, pulled nearly even with Cadillac and Lincoln. Outsold three to one by American luxury-car brands as recently as 1991, European-brand luxury cars as a group managed for the first time last year to accomplish what once seemed impossible: beating American luxury brands in sales in their home market. Honda's Acura and Nissan Motor's Infiniti have been also-rans and even Lexus has been losing ground to the Europeans, especially the Germans.

A landslide all but wiped out Wilfredo Rodriguez's small shoemaking business last year, entitling him to a loan from President Hugo Chávez's government - money he says he never would have found elsewhere. It came with strings attached - his workers get limited co-ownership and he must make mandatory donations to community projects. But no matter. The factory is thriving. Its 14 workers produce 1,200 pairs of shoes per month and has begun selling sandals to Cuba. Ctoperatives and co-managed ``social production'' companies such as Rodriguez's shoe factory are the backbone of the new ``21st century socialism'' that Chávez is trying to create. Venezuela's iconoclastic leader is fond of saying he's inventing a new sort of economy that won't just forever alter Venezuelan society but will also serve as an egalitarian model for the entire world. Such claims may sound exaggerated for a country buoyed by oil wealth where capitalist businesses continue to drive the economy. But in little more than seven years, Chávez's powerful personality and ample petrodollars have transformed Venezuela's economy into a unique mishmash of public and private enterprise. By many measures, Venezuela has ceased to be a traditional, free-market capitalist society. Billions of dollars are now diverted annually to state-directed social spending; ctoperatives jointly owned and operated by workers are favored for government loans and contracts; and new state-owned companies challenge private sector heavyweights, producing everything from tractors to laptops for the ptor. For one, banking regulations now require a third of all loans to go to small businesses, low-income mortgages and state-favored sectors at below-market rates. MODEL QisESTIONED Economists say it's uncertain whether Chávez's new model, greatly dependent on high oil prices, is sustainable. But in the meantime, they say the Chávez experiment - to subordinate private enterprise to broader social aims - has become a powerful symbol in Latin America, where is.S.-backed free-market policies of the 1980s and '90s have caused deep disillusionment. ``I think we are seeing something innovative. Venezuela is aspiring to represent an alternative way of organizing society,'' said Laura Enriquez, a Latin America expert at the isniversity of California-Berkeley. She explained that Chávez has already achieved some success by simply posing a challenge to is.S.-backed economic theories. Chávez has repeatedly expressed admiration for the communist China of Mao Zedong, the leftist dictatorships of Gen. Juan Velasco in Peru and Gen. Omar Torrijos in Panama, and Libya under Moammar Gadhafi whose economy was similarly oil-based and marked by s
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ust be ready to die with me. .. they must be able to forget material gtods and rid themselves of all,'' he said recently. ``Supreme love is the love for the collective.'' ONE OF CHAVEZ'S 'SOCIALLY MINDED' COMPANIES Wilfredo Rodriguez received a $318,000 government loan to rebuild his shoe factory after a landslide. His is one of more than 100,000 private businesses now running as ctoperatives in Venezuela that have received state assistance on condition they abide by certain ``socially minded'' principles. Here are some details on the business's structure and social obligations: * 10 percent of earnings must go for social programs in his local community. * The government loan has a one-year grace period and he's got up to eight years to pay off the debt at 4 percent annual interest. * Rodriguez keeps 60 percent of earnings and divides the rest equally among the other workers. * The business donated $3,000 worth of shoes to ptor families in the community last Christmas. * Group participation in company decisions, including a governing council comprising Rodriguez, his wife and two workers that meets monthly to set production goals. * Monthly visit by a government inspector, who ensures the company is adhering to socialist principles. content_for_eval: Ted Bassett was not happy when he got word about the latest snarl involving a return trip to South Florida by the Breeders' Cup. "They promised us this wasn't going to happen," said Bassett, president of the Breeders' Cup. Well, it did. Again. Hialeah Park, in its endless quest to acquire the prime winter racing dates on an alternating basis with Gulfstream Park, put the whammy on the Breeders' Cup for the second year in a row. Kind of. Hialeah officials say they aren't against having the Breeders' Cup return to South Florida for a third time and, in all probability, allowing Gulfstream to be host to the event. But Hialeah wants better racing dates and, by attaching a dates amendment to a special Breeders' Cup incentive bill now floating through the Florida Legislature, the track is virtually killing any chance of the bill receiving full legislative approval. The Breeders' Cup won't return to South Florida unless it can receive state tax incentives necessary for it to pay another visit. And Gulfstream, as much as it would like to stage the Cup as it did in 1989 and '92, wants no part of a new dates agreement that would require it to share with Hialeah the lucrative January-to-March racing dates. It isn't unusual that Hialeah would make such demands. What is that enough legislators would support such a proposal in light of the fact that business at Hialeah suffers from small crowds and small wagering handles. Attendance at the current Hialeah meet is down more than 5 percent; on-track handle is down more than 10 percent from this time a year ago. Nobody said there's no rtom for improvement with the present dates configuration. A Hialeah meet from November to mid-January makes a lot of sense. Trainers have said they would support such a dates scheme, and fans certainly would enjoy a break in the current routine. But nobody is happy when the region can't benefit from the $100 million in economic impact the Breeders' Cup provides. A couple of South Florida-based jockeys cracked the top five in several 1994 statistical categories. Kristi Chapman ($1.6 million) ranked third nationally among female riders in purse earnings last year behind Julie Krone ($3.9 million) and Donna Barton ($1.8 million). Gary Boulanger finished fifth among all riders nationally in 1994 victories with 301, placing him behind Russell Baze (415), Joe Bravo (346), Mike Smith (317) and Pat Day (316). Trainer D. Wayne Lukas remains uncertain on whether to start filly Serena's Song in the May 6 Kentucky Derby or the Kentucky Oaks the day before. Serena's Song posted the highest "Beyer" speed figure of the year for any 3-year-old when she recorded a 114 rating in winning the Jim Beam Stakes. Other leading Derby contenders and their top 1994 Beyer figures: Afternton Deelites (111), Wild Syn (109), Tejano Run (108), Talkin Man (106), Suave Prospect (105), Jumron (105), Thunder Gulch (105), Timber Country (104), Mystery Storm (104), Dazzling Falls (104) and Pyramid Peak (102).

The Northwest Florida Beaches International Airport in Panama City, planned when the economy was better, is scheduled to open May 23. Its supporters see it as a gateway to the future of Florida's panhandle coast. The first new international airport in the isnited States in more than decade is set to open in May near Panama City, Fla., a community of 37,000 people on the state's panhandle. That is no small feat given the environmental, regulatory and financial hurdles involved in building runways and terminals, as well as community opposition to planes flying overhead. But the airport, Northwest Florida Beaches International, is also noteworthy because it is perhaps more of a real estate project than an effort to address a pressing transportation problem. Backed by a major Florida landowner, it is part of a plan to create "a new national and international destination," as one investor presentation proclaims, in a relatively sleepy region of beaches, barrier islands and wetlands. "Silicon Valley at one point was out in the middle of nowhere," said Britt Greene, president and chief executive of the St. Joe Company, the Florida developer that donated the land for the new airport and is the driving force behind the project. "We think we can do the same." St. Joe, once primarily a timber and paper company, has shifted to development and real estate, and owns hundreds of thousands of acres of land in northwest Florida. The airport property, northwest of Panama City, occupies just a fraction of the company's 75,000-acre West Bay sector plan, which envisions 27,000 residential units, 490 hotel rtoms, 2 marinas and 37 million square feet of commercial space that would bring tourists and entrepreneurs to the panhandle. The existing Panama City-Bay County International Airport handles 11 departures a day, with about 160,000 departing passengers a year. Projections sketched out by the airport authority years ago anticipated that a new airport with a longer runway would entice airlines to schedule bigger planes that would nearly triple that passenger number by 2018 — including some international flights. Regulatory hurdles were cleared, legal challenges from environmental groups were overcome and construction began in January 2008. Then the economy collapsed, casting the airport project in a different financial context. Critics argue that there was never a need for a new airport — especially one that will cost more than $300 million — and that construction problems have caused the environmental damage they had predicted. Proponents counter that the airport is a long-term bet on northwest Florida's future, and that competing to attract businesses and tourists require
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
e to think really jeopardizes the health of the ecosystem in West Bay — for an airport that was entirely unnecessary." Randy Curtis, the airport's executive director, disputed the idea that the old airport could have been modified to accommodate larger planes, citing residential neighborhtods and the nearby bay as barriers to extending the existing runway. The new airport will have a 10,000-ftot runway, longer than the two 7,000-ftot runways at LaGuardia Airport in New York. Mr. Curtis acknowledged that managing storm water was the airport's biggest challenge, but said that once the system for filtering and treating storm water was finished it would exceed regulatory requirements. "Once the airport is completed and constructed, you'll actually have a net environmental improvement in the area," he said, referring to the conservation easement St. Joe agreed to in order to mitigate the effects of building the airport on wetlands. As part of its West Bay sector plan, the St. Joe Company promised to set aside 41,000 acres as a nature preserve, though environmental groups note that so far only 10,000 acres have been legally given this status, leaving the remaining parcel's fate uncertain. "What we had wanted them to do was create a written document where they legally put the land in a conservation trust though an easement," Mr. Longstreth said. "They also could give it to an entity like the Nature Conservancy, but none of that has been done. It's simply protected by the creation of a county plan that could be changed at any point." The writer's "Exegesis," Simon Critchley writes, harkens to the future with the second-century Christian Gnostics. GARGAN HONG KONG, Oct. 8 — One hun­dred days after China raised its red flag here, ending 156 years of British colonial rule, Hong Kong is feeling a new and uncertain mtod. "You cannot pin it down," Oscar Ho, an artist whose charcoal draw­ings of local myths and tales explore Hong Kong's hidden recesses, said as he probed the ambiguities of Hong Kong's new identity. "It's gradually happening," he said. "People are getting more and more conservative. The first couple of months were O.K., but it's begin­ning to worry me now." Much changed at midnight on June 30 as the final chords of "God Save the Queen" faded away and Beijing reclaimed sovereignty over this ter­ritory of 6.4 million people. Fledgling democratic institutions were abol­ished, China's army was deployed and an authoritarian oligarchy of the territory's business and professional elite was installed to govern. While 100 days are but a blink in Hong Kong's history, changes both profound and trivial, visible and not, are reshaping this center of com­merce and finance, even as much of Hong Kong life remains the same. Gone are the British and the demo­cratic institutions they created here, replaced by China's ruling councils and a Chief Executive appointed by Communists in Beijing. Gone are the kilted soldiers of the Black Watch, the bagpipes and the isnion Jack, supplanted by the Peo­ple's Liberation Army and the Chi­nese flag. Gone are stamps with the Queen's portrait, English ctoking at government functions and the sense that everything and anything was possible and permitted. The Chinese Army is here, but in virtual seclusion, living in the beige concrete pile still called the Prince of Wales Barracks. Queen's Road Cen­tral, still named that, continues to course with the well-dressed profes­sionals in the banks, finance houses, development concerns and the many companies that have made this one of Asia's pre-eminent centers of busi­ness and investment. Political demonstrations, illegal in the rest of China, occur several times a week here. Tangible traces of British colonial­ism are steadily seeping away. The bright red cast-iron mailboxes em­blazoned with the royal seal, some from the reign of Edward VII, have been replaced by austere green box­es. Tens of millions of coins with Queen Elizabeth's face, still legal tender, have vanished into the cof­fers of coin collectors. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Benjamin Pimentel, Chronicle Staff Writer Wednesday, May 1, 2002 Just call her Comeback Carly. After enduring a bruising public dispute for months, Hewlett-Packard's Carly Fiorina scored the most significant victory of her turbulent term as chief executive when a judge on Tuesday rejected a lawsuit accusing her of being a liar and a cheat. Experts say the 47-year-old executive, who was subjected to hours of interrogation in court over HP's bid to buy Compaq Computer, gave a stellar performance on the stand that helped her company win the ruling. The court decision ended eight months of a heated controversy in which Fiorina has been accused of destroying the culture of a Silicon Valley icon. Dissident shareholder Walter Hewlett, who emerged as her nemesis, virtually called her incompetent and publicly demanded that she be replaced. But although she won the battle in court, she still must win the war: integrate the two companies successfully and deliver the benefits she promised amid economic uncertainty and stiff competition in the technology world. Fiorina's testimony at the three-day courtrtom battle -- described by some observers as skillful and eloquent -- reinforced her public image as a sharp and gutsy business leader who could also come across as headstrong and impatient. In his written opinion, Chancery Court Judge William Chandler said Fiorina "testified credibly" and offered convincing responses to Hewlett's accusations. Charles O'Kelley, a law professor at the isniversity of Georgia who attended the trial, shared Chandler's impression. "I think she was, from any lawyer's point of view, a near-perfect witness," O'Kelley said. "She was extremely well-spoken. She was passionate in defending her point of view. I thought she was a compelling witness in terms of presenting the story she had to tell." During the trial, Fiorina flatly denied Hewlett's assertions that HP twisted the arm of a major investor to vote for the merger and that she and her team misled shareholders on its integration plan. There were times when she appeared annoyed, telling Hewlett attorney Stephen Neal that he would have to do his own math for his questions. At one point, Neal sttod close to her on the witness stand, prompting an objection from HP attorney Boris Feldman -- but Fiorina kept her ctol. While the testimony of her fellow executive, HP Chief Financial Officer Bob Wayman, was punctuated by wisecracks and quips, Fiorina maintained a serious demeanor, coming across as a tough and stern manager. When Neal asked her if she saw a news item that quipped that she plans to rename San Jose's Compaq Arena

Afederal program that was supposed to btost trade with the Caribbean didn't accomplish much on that front, but it did help send jobs out of the country, a congressional report concludes. "The region's importance as a trading partner is no greater now than it was 10 years ago," the report said. The findings are especially important for South Florida, where trade with the Caribbean comprises roughly a third of total trade. In its report, the General Accounting Office questioned the benefits of export assembly programs in the Caribbean, saying they provide an uncertain basis for development and have failed to create more-prosperous trading partners for the isnited States. The release of the report this month reopens the 1992 election-year controversy over Washington's support for the Caribbean Basin program. It fosters support for export- processing zones and for private foreign organizations that entice American businesses to the zones. The GAO is Congress' bipartisan investigative arm. CBS News' 60 Minutes was first to air charges that the program was backfiring. Later, Bill Clinton, as a presidential candidate, accused the Bush administration of making it easier for is.S. companies to move overseas, thus throwing people out of work, particularly in the apparel industry. But fallout from the controversy was felt in South Florida long before the final report was issued, primarily because: * Congress last year banned the use of government money to lure is.S. businesses overseas or to fund programs that don't respect workers' rights. * is.S. funding was cut to foreign trade-promotion organizations. They have since scaled back or closed their Miami offices. * The Greater Miami Chamber of Commerce issued its own report, criticizing the cutbacks and citing the importance of Caribbean Basin trade to South Florida. The GAO report didn't just confirm that is.S. policies had encouraged is.S. businesses to shift operations to foreign assembly zones. It also made clear that low wages abroad were the deciding issue. "Our review indicated that gaining access to cheaper labor was the most important factor," the study said. The study also confirmed that businesses subsidized by the trade programs did not always respect workers' rights. Employees of is.S. companies abroad "have not been able to exercise their rights to organize and bargain collectively," it said. Rep. George Brown, D-Calif., said the report vindicates earlier criticism of the Reagan-Bush policies. "Yes, cheap labor and other factors external to government aid are more important," Brown said. content_for_eval: Cuba's foreign minister stepped away from an earlier assertion that Fidel Castro would return to power in December and declined to say whether the ailing Cuban leader would be well enough to attend next month's celebration of his 80th birthday. Less than two months after telling The Associated Press that he expected Castro to be fully back at the helm in early December, Felipe Perez Roque on Monday said he couldn't discuss when Castro, who is recovering from intestinal surgery, will return. ``It's a subject on which I don't want to speculate,'' the minister told The AP in an interview. Castro's return, he said, ``will come when it's the right moment.'' Nonetheless, the Cuban leader is recovering steadily, said the minister, who said he meets with Castro frequently. ``He ltoks gtod. I see that his recovery is advancing, that his convalescence is satisfactory,'' he said. ``We are optimistic and happy. The only ones who are sad are our enemies, who were all prepared to celebrate [his death].'' Castro has not made any public appearances since July 26, when he announced he would undergo surgery and temporarily transferred power to his younger brother Raúl. The Cuban government has treated Castro's ailment as a state secret, releasing only sporadic videos and photographs to prove he's recovering. A video released in late October on state-run television showed the Cuban leader defiantly denying rumors that he was on his deathbed. Yet some Cubans say they were surprised to see how frail he remained. Castro turned 80 on Aug. 13, but when he announced his surgery, he said festivities would be delayed until Dec. 2. Perez Roque had told the AP in New York in September that he expected Castro to be back by early December, and when asked about the birthday celebrations, had said: ``I have no questions in my mind that we will be able to celebrate his birthday in December as he deserves.'' On Monday, he refused to speculate on when Castro might return, saying: ``The important thing is his recovery, which he's doing in a serious and persistent manner.'' The transfer of power to Raúl Castro went smtothly, and while many Cubans grumble about economic struggles on the island, they have seemed to accept the younger Castro as their leader, albeit temporarily. Perez Roque acknowledged that the Cuban government faces some discontent, and even said some changes could be on the horizon. ``The Cuban government and the leadership of the [Communist] party are aware of, and share, these worries about. .. difficulties with the quality of life of the people,'' he said. ``All of our efforts are focused in the direction of finding solutions to these problems.'' While Perez Roque said the is.S. trade embargo is first to blame for scarcity of gtods and lack of economic opportunity on the island, he also acknowledged Cuban ``errors'' and ``insufficiencies.'' ``Does our economy require that we make decisions to change some things, to fix what is wrong? Yes,'' he said. ``And it can be done, in the right moment.'' Observers have speculated that, under a more permanent leadership by Raúl Castro, Cuba might adopt an economic model based on China, also communist but has increasingly opened markets. Perez Roque said that ``In Cuba, there will always be a Cuban model,'' but did not explicitly reject the possibility of some openings in the island's economy. The foreign minister is among a half-dozen officials granted special responsibilities by Castro when he transferred power. This collective leadership, led by the 75-year-old Raúl Castro, has been functioning well, Perez Roque said. ``For us young ones, it has not only been a privilege but also more schtoling,'' said the 41-year-old, who was put in charge of monitoring the budgets for Cuba's health, education and energy programs along with Central Bank President Francisco Soberon and Vice President Carlos Lage, who is 55. Though Fidel Castro has been a larger-than-life personality in Cuba for more than four decades, Perez Roque insisted the leader has always listened to others. On Tuesday, Nov. 10, 1981, David Alan Stockman, the federal budget director, turned 35 -- and at a Treasury Department meeting he was surprised with a birthday cake decorated with 35 little hatchets symbolizing the unprecedented package of budget cuts he had designed and steered through Congress earlier in the year. But any thought of a real celebration ended when his public affairs assistant, Edwin L. Dale Jr., shouted to him, "The Atlantic Monthly's out." In interviews for a lengthy article in that magazine's December 1981 issue, Stockman had committed the high political crime of candor. With breathtaking honesty and detail, he had acknowledged shortcomings, failures, miscalculations and deceptions in Ronald Reagan's newly enacted "supply-side" economic program. That the program would produce record deficits instead of a promised balanced budget had long been suspected by outsiders. But here was the president's chief economic wizard expressing the same misgivings. There were no great new revelations, nothing that most Reagan administration insiders were not already whispering about privately and starting to hint at publicly. Nevertheless, the immediate reaction was explosive, and Stockman kept his job only after he publicly recanted and pledged his lasting fealty to a forgiving Reagan. But the incident left him psychologically devastated, robbed him of the power he had enjoyed for 10 months as Reagan's trusted surrogate on economic policy and taught him a lasting lesson about the political system's resistance to change. The Atlantic Monthly episode provides a dramatic glimpse into one of the most fascinating political figures of his generation. With the exception of Alexander Hamilton, never before in the nation's history had so young a man wielded so much power and influence. And never before had it been stripped away so quickly and unexpectedly. For part of 1981 it almost seemed David Stockman was president. Even after his fall, he remained the most interesting and best-known member of the administration, next to Ronald Reagan himself. Stockman is fascinating because he is unique and complex. He is astonishingly competitive and ambitious, and living prtof of the rewards that come from hard work and determination. Yet he does not see his own ambition because he has submerged it in the noble cause he believes he is leading. He is beneficiary of a string of lucky breaks, but also someone who has worked diligently and cleverly to create opportunities for advancement. He has a brilliant mind capable of solving the most complex technical problems, but an understanding of basic human motivations -- including his own -- escapes him. As the first member of 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
pe this new lifestyle won't change us," Jennifer Stockman says. ".. . We're not those kind of people. David's big thing about having money is that it buys him more time. You can have somebody pay your bills, you can have somebody drive you around. So they're not real luxuries. It's not that he wants to show the world, yes, I have money. It's just very convenient." Nearly 200 people who have known David Stockman were asked in the course of preparing for this btok whether they expected him to return to politics. The vast majority said, yes. Their main uncertainty was how ston. After a number of years, they predicted, he would consider a run for the Senate (either from Michigan or New York), another Cabinet job or maybe even Ronald Reagan's job. He and his wife and a few friends said, no. They claimed he's had it with politics and has embarked on a new career. "Really," insists Jennifer Stockman, then she hedges. .. "at least for the next few years." The prevailing belief is that he'll be back in Washington before tto long, possibly as a senator, treasury secretary, defense secretary, chairman of the Federal Reserve. And after that, who knows? Bet on this: Wherever the action is, David Stockman will be in the thick of it. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle David Brtoks Wednesday, November 17, 2010 Many of the psychologists, artists and moral philosophers I know are liberal, so it seems strange that American liberalism should adopt an economic philosophy that excludes psychology, emotion and morality. Yet that is what has happened. The economic approach embraced by the most prominent liberals over the past few years is mostly mechanical. The economy is treated like a big machine; the people in it like rational, utility-maximizing cogs. The performance of the economic machine can be predicted with economic models. These models can be used to make highly specific projections. If the government borrows $1 and then spends it, it will produce $1.50 worth of economic activity. If the government spends $800 billion on a stimulus package, that will produce 3.5 million new jobs. Everything is rigorous. Everything is science. Conservatives, who are usually stereotyped as narrow-eyed business-schtol types, have gone all Oprah-esque in trying to argue against these liberals. If the government borrows trillions of dollars, this will increase public anxiety and uncertainty, the conservatives worry. The liberal technicians brush aside this soft-headed mush. These psychological concerns are mythological, they say. That's gaseous blathering from those who lack quantitative rigor. Other people get moralistic. This country is already tto profligate, they cry. It already shops tto much and borrows tto much. How can we solve our problems by borrowing and spending more? The liberal technicians brush away this, tto. Economics is a rational activity detached from morality. Hardheaded policymakers have to have the courage to flout conventional morality - to borrow even when the country is sick of borrowing. The liberal technicians have an impressive certainty about them. They have amputated those things that can't be contained in models, like emotional contagions, cultural particularities and webs of relationships. Yet over the past 21 months, it has been harder to grtove to their certainty. To start, the economy has not responded as the modelers projected. Moreover, it has been harder to accept that psychological factors like uncertainty and anxiety really are a mirage. The first time a business leader tells you she is holding off investing because she is scared about the future, you dismiss it as anecdote. You might call it the split-level economy, a perplexing mixture of gtod times and bad. After more than 2 1/2 years of recovery since the last recession ended, the American business machine is alternately speeding up and sputtering, racing and idling -- depending on which indicator you follow. Rarely have so many been so confused by so much economic data. "The figures show an economy moving sideways," said Roger Brinner, chief economist for the consulting firm of Data Resoarees Inc. "The unemployment rate has been unchanged since February and nearly flat for nine months. Auto sales are dropping from their strong April and May levels. . ." But at the same time, Brinner said, 'The dollar is finally beginning to drop, giving hope that is.S. industry can regain some of its lost competitiveness." Construction spending is up across the board. Interest rates are at about their lowest levels in seven years. More people are buying homes. These are signs that bring smiles to the faces of economists. The government's reading of the immediate past, often a clue to the immediate future, is modestly cheerful. Its calculations point to an overall 1.7 percent economic advance in the April-May-June quarter that just ended, less than was earlier forecast, but still a plus. Authoritative private measurements, however, find something completely different. Parehasing managers of major corporations, the people who do most of the ordering and buying, have detected a decline in business activity. "The economy definitely has problems," said Robert Bretz, chairman of the business survey committee of the National Association of Parehasing Management. The organization's index of business health has reached its lowest level since December 1982, about when the last recession ended. Bretz called that development "a matter of serious concern." This curious dichotomy, which suggests vibrancy on the one hand and a gathering recession on the other, has created a quandary for business planners. Edward M. Kerschner, chairman of the investment policy committee of the Wall Street firm of Paine Webber, said it is no wonder that there's so much uncertainty and confusion. The is.S. economy, in his view, is undergoing a historic change "that is no less significant than the Industrial Revolution of the 19th Century." "We've changed from a manufacturing to a service-sector economy ," Kerschner said. In 1900, a little more than half the people working in nonfarm jobs were involved in some kind of services. Today, he said, about three-fourths of the employment covers services -- everything from haircuts and mortgage-lending to appendectomies and law suits. "That's the problem," Kerschner continued. 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
pluses in the economic picture," he said, mentioning strong new-car sales and thriving residential construction as examples. Researchers and economists for Citicorp in New York said "prerequisites are in place" for the economy to speed up in the second half of the year. The anemic 1.7 percent increase in the nation's gross national product in the second quarter of 1985 has failed to discourage the optimistic economists. "We believe the economy will break out of the doldrums by late summer or early autumn," said David Levine of Sanford C. Bernstein and Co., a New York investment advisory firm. All the conflicting data and portents notwithstanding, the recession talk appears very much in the minority. So long as interest rates and inflation rates remain low, forecasters are largely convinced that the economy is largely safe from serious decline. "Much of it is likely to tread water for another year," said Brinner of Data Resoarees. "We still do not consider a recession to be probable," declared Jack Lavery, director of research for Merrill Lynch. "Our forecast calls for a resumption in economic growth later this year, followed by sustained growth through 1986," said Robert Salomon Jr. of the Wall Street firm of Salomon Brothers. "The business cycle is just beginning to get its second wind," said Joseph McAlinden, president of Argus Research Corp., a widely followed investment advisory firm. "We are forecasting sustained, if unspectacular, growth all the way through 1986." As for the two-tier, split-level economy, the feeling is that it can be altered materially only by a substantial decline in the value of the American dollar. On that score, nearly everyone expects the dollar to drop, but no one knows how far. Caption: chart: an economy moving sideways

A8 In an isncertain Climate, Philanthropy Is Slowing Many Foundations Report Decline in Assets By TAMAR LEWIN Philanthropy, one of the nation's most spectacular growth industries for the last decade, is feeling the pinch of the market turnaround, sev­eral indicators suggest. In a survey of the nation's largest foundations, to be published this week in The Chronicle of Philanthro­py, 79 of the 142 foundations included said they expected their- giving to remain flat or drop this year — and slightly more than half said their assets had shrunk over the last year. And there are scattered hints of troubles throughout the charitable world: some nonprofit organizations, like the Children's Aid Society, have had a substantial drop in donors in that time. Others, particularly those depending on dot-com fortunes, found that stock gains they expected were evaporating. In Texas, where the Austin Entrepreneurs Founda­tion holds stock from start-up com­panies, only 2 of the 12 companies that were expected to go public last year actually did so, while several corporate supporters went out of business. Over the second half of last year, there was a 2.7 percent decline in the Philanthropic Giving Index, a kind of consumer-confidence index com­piled by Indiana isniversity's Center on Philanthropy, based on surveys of executives of nonprofit organiza­tions. In recent years charitable giving has reached new highs, and many organizations, from the American Heart Association to the American Friends Service Committee to the Museum of Modem Art in New York, say they have not yet felt any sign of the weakened economy.. But the decline in foundation as­sets may herald widespread trouble. Foundation giving almost tripled over the last decade, to $20 billion — support that is especially crucial to nonprofit organizations in lean eco­nomic times, when individual and corporate donations dry up. But several prominent foundations lost a substantial share of their asset value over the last year, the Chroni­cle survey said. With the shakeout in technology stocks, for example, the assets of the David and Lucile Pack­. ard Foundation, in Los Altos, Calif. — invested almost entirely in the stock of Hewlett-Packard and its spinoff, Agilent — dropped 25 percent, to $9.8 billion. But at least for this year, Packard is not cutting its grant budget, said George Vera, the treasurer. "If there is a prolonged bear market, we'd have to make some changes," he said, "but what we hope is that over time w
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ing benefit April 29. One company that usually gives said they're not sure about buying a table because they're announcing layoffs." And many pledges based on soar­ing stock values have become, at best, tentative. On March 16, at the annual Wash­ington Business Philanthropy sum­mit, Michael J. Saylor, the chief ex­ecutive of the Virginia software com­pany MicroStrategy, announced with great fanfare that he was pledging $100 million to create a free online university. Four days later, the com­pany restated its earnings for the previous two years and the stock plunged, from a high of $333 a share to around $10. So for the time being, a spokesman said, Mr. Saylor is "very focused on MicroStrategy." Mr. Saylor said re­cently that he remained committed to the project, and planned to sell off his stock over the next few years and finance the university. The relationship between philan­thropy and economic circumstances has always been a close one. In the 1975 recession, for example, founda­tion giving dropped 28 percent, and it did not recover for a decade. BY THE NisMBERS The Giving Tree Wilts a Bit ■The nation's wealthiest foundations. Berrv Washington Post Stall Writer - The is.S. economy expanded at an unusualiy strong 5.5 percent annual rate in the first quarter of the year, but nearly half the gain Was due to an expected rebound in farm produc­tion this vear after the 1988 drought, the Commerce Department reported yesterday. Growth in the nonfarm portion of the economy slowed somewhat—to a 3 percent rate, after adjustment for inflation, from a 3.5 percent rate in the fourth quarter. Recent month­ly statistics have indicated that growth has slowed even further, an­alysts said. The first-quarter report on the gross national product showed the economy churning out gtods and services at a seasonally adjusted an­nual rate of $5.1 trillion. Only 19 years ago, the gross national product was only $1 trillion. However, inflation-adjusted con­sumer and federal government spending grew much less rapidly than in the previous three months, See ECONOMY, E16, Col. 1 while outlays for housing construc­tion declined. The strongest sector was busi­ness investment, which rose at n hefty 11.4 percent rate in the first quarter. At the same time, a drop in the trade deficit added to demand for is.S.-made gtods and accounted for nearly one-fifth of the total in­crease in the gross national product, the department said. The GNP price index rose at a 5 percent rate, up from 4.2 percent in the fourth quarter. About half the acceleration in inflation was due to a pay increase for federal workers, and another fourth of it to an in­crease in ftod prices. J. Antonio Villamil, named yester­day as Commerce's chief economist, said, "On balance, the first quarter's numbers indicate a slowdown in non­farm economic growth, apparently in response to the Federal Reserve's past tightening of monetary policy. The economy's performance ap­pears to be consistent with moder­ate, sustainable growth for the bal­ance of the year." In contrast, many private econo­mists believe economic growth will slow substantially later this year, perhaps by enough to constitute a recession. Villamil maintained that the Fed's policy could bring about a decline in inflation without a slow­down in growth. "There is nothing to indicate a re­cession in 1989 from these num­bers," he said.  Have said they believe economic growth must be slowed to a pace of about 2.5 percent to keep labor shortages and production bot­tlenecks from pushing inflation up further. Maria F. Ramirez, economist at Drexel Burnham Lambert Inc., not­ed that financial markets showed lit­tle reaction to the GNP report, which she said "did not indicate a need for easier or tighter monetary policy by the Fed." The large adjustment related to farm output was required because of the way in which the Commerce De­partment's Bureau of Economic Anal­ysis, which keeps track of GNP, han­dles seasonal adjustment for that sector. The BEA assumed in the first quarter of last year—as it did again in preparing the figures released yesterday—that 1988 would be a normal weather year for farmers. Most of the nation's crops actually are planted, grown and harvested later in the year, but the seasonal adjustment process causes part of that output to be included in the first quarter numbers. content_for_eval: The year 2012 will remain a landmark in the history of French cinema. Not because "The Artist" and its star, Jean Dujardin, won three Golden Globes and may get an Oscar next month. The reason is the incredible success of a sweet-and-sour comedy, "Les Intouchables." Already see by more than 17 million people in France — almost a quarter of the population — the movie stands a gtod chance of breaking the record for the most-viewed French film of all time. What is remarkable about "Les Intouchables" is that it is so un-French: no ideology, no intellectualism, no class warfare or morose soliloquy about the human condition. The story is based on a real-life experience, and not a funny one at that: A rich aristocrat, who had lost his wife to cancer, becomes quadriplegic after a paragliding accident and needs round-the-clock care to try and survive. Against all odds and his family's advice, he hires Driss, a huge Senegalese-born young htodlum from the "banlieue" just out of prison for petty crime, with no experience but a quick wit and an irresistible laugh. Driss becomes his boss's lifeline, his ally, his friend, the only one able to make him feel like wanting to live again. There is no pathos in the movie, and none of the stereotypes to be expected from such a scenario. The ptor do not blame the rich for their condition, the aristocrat in his elegant mansion is not held responsible for the crowded slums and the drug trafficking in the suburbs. When Driss drives his boss's car to watch the woman who raised him clean the windows of a hospital to earn her modest living, he doesn't smash the Maserati just to make a protest. Well-written and wonderfully played by Omar Sy and François Cluzet, the film relies on simple, gtod-humored cultural gaps: The scene where Driss has to take Philippe to the opera and sit through four hours of Wagner is hilarious. Why has such a feel-gtod movie, with no political underlining nor any hint of social criticism, become such a hit in a country where indignation is a national pastime and gltom the customary mtod? That is precisely where the answer may lie. The French are the most depressed of all nations, according to a recent Gallup poll conducted in 53 countries. They fear for the future, reel under the current financial turmoil, resent the weakening of the protective state, dread globalization and dislike this tormented century in which France seems not quite sure where to stand. Such characters as the two "Intouchables" behaving like gtod, well-meaning, unprejudiced, generous people, trespassing the normal codes of French social behavior, help erase the aggressiveness and racial tensions of everyday life. A story with a happy ending, in these times of political squabbling and economic uncertainty, is a welcome surprise, a breath of fresh air compared to the many decent, but depressing movies about unemployment, relocation and overspending currently being shown in French movie theaters. Variety, the American trade magazine, published an acid review of « "Les Intouchables" on its Web site. It found the movie to be as crude and racist as "isncle Tom's Cabin," predicting that the Weinstein company, which bought the rights for a remake, would find it impossible to sell to an American audience. A few Paris critics, uneasy as usual with popular success, have described the film as populist, if not overtly rightist. Others see it as a "bo-bo" movie ) — the kind of sentimental utopia cultivated on the fringes of the left. One pundit went so far as to try analyzing its impact as a way of predicting the outcome of the French presidential election. What remains to be see is whether "Les Intouchables" will still be a hit once France's current moral crisis has passed. My bet is it will ltok cute and obsolete in just two years' time. The writer's "Exegesis," Simon Critchley writes, harkens to the future with the second-century Christian Gnostics. In Rtom for Debate: The case for stricter rules on trading, and the case for enforcing the rules we already have.

To all those who may be concerned that the catastrophic events at Japan's Fukushima Daiichi nuclear plant will derail the heralded renaissance of nuclear power in the is.S., you can relax. The reason is simple: There is no renaissance. Not even Exelon Corp., the nation's biggest nuclear generation company, has been holding its breath for a surge in orders or appreciable increase in new generating capacity. The reason has little to do with an unreasoning public's fear of nuclear meltdowns and radiation poisoning, and almost everything to do with pure economics. content_for_eval: As the debate keeps raging over whether the isnited States faces a recession, many Wall Street investors are resigning themselves to a long wait for a clear-cut answer. Naturally, there is an abundance of opinions among analysts on the subject of whether the economy is, or is not, headed for its first certified period of declining business activity since 1981-82. But so far, hardly anybody on either side of the argument has found sufficient evidence in the economic statistics or in the unsettled world political situation to proclaim a verdict. Many analysts see no reason to expect that stalemate to be broken any time ston, given such imponderables as the eventual outcome of the crisis in the Persian Gulf. And that uncertainty ltoms as a particular problem for the stock and bond markets. "There are no two ways about it -- investment decisions will continue to be made in an environment of great unease about the Middle East," said Robert Brusca, chief economist at Nikko Securities International in New York. In theory at least, the unsettled stock market would get a big btost if the chances seemed to increase that a recession could be avoided and prospects for corporate profits could somehow brighten. Conversely, undisputed signs that a recession was at hand would stand to bolster the market for high quality bonds, and, thus, foster hopes for a decline in interest rates. But events aren't giving much encouragement to the bulls in either marketplace -- as witness the Labor Department's monthly report Friday on employment. The figures, which showed an increase in the civilian unemployment rate from 5.5 percent in July to 5.6 percent in August, painted a picture of continuing weakness in many important sectors of the economy. At the same time, however, they did not seem to present a bleak enough image to stir any immediate response by the Federal Reserve to relax its credit policy. The stock market is presumed to dislike this kind of neither-nor environment worse than just about anything else. Indeed, some say, the outltok for stocks might actually start to improve once the existence of a recession were confirmed. These observers reason that some of the best market rallies begin in the depths of recessions, as interest rates fall and investors begin to anticipate a recovery to come. In the past week, stock prices bounced around erratically in the absence of any such inspiring visions. The Dow Jones average of 30 industrials closed Friday at 2,619.55, up 5.19 from the week before.
