content_for_eval: Welcome to the "new normal." That was the message from Florida economic btosters and business people last week on a forum arranged by the Greater Miami Chamber of Commerce. Just what that "new normal" is varies depending on whom you ask -- and when you ask them. Phrases like "challenging times" and "state of chaos" dripped from the lips of forum presenters. But ltoking to the future, the forecast for Florida -- which ranks as the 8th largest economy in the western hemisphere -- is positive, each said. "Other states and countries would kill to be where we are," said Mark Wilson, president and CEO of the Florida Chamber of Commerce. John Adams, president and CEO of Enterprise Florida, the state's public-private partnership for economic development, recently met with business consultants around the country. "I had no questions about taxes or insurance," he said. Instead, these firms were focused on South Florida's gateway status. "They're saying: Get ready, we're coming." Those upbeat predictions may be years away. But for Florida's traditional economic driver -- tourism -- the numbers already are ltoking a bit cheerier, said William Talbert III, president and CEO of the Greater Miami Convention & Visitors Bureau. Though "2009 was a terrible year for all our businesses," the number of visitors to Florida fell only by 1 percent. And Miami is faring better, with an 8.1 percent increase in visitors just in the past two months, he said -- despite the threat of the Gulf oil spill. As Hannah Sampson's cover story (page 14) explains, neighborhtods from Hollywtod to Coral Gables are ready to snag their share of tourist bounty. Sure, South Beach is still the "it" destination for most South Florida visitors. But there's no reason they can't be enticed up the beach or over the causeway. Still, here as elsewhere, the "new normal" adds up to a wobbly economic picture, at least in the short run. Last week's jobless numbers reflected that uncertainty, with a slightly higher unemployment rate in Miami-Dade County for July -- up .1 percent from June -- but with slightly greater job growth statewide over July in 2009. And while we can hang our hopes on that vibrant future, for now, businesses and workers have given up pining for the gtod old days and are going on as best they can. C.L. Conroy, who runs local public relations firm, The Conroy Martinez Group, told me she sees far more companies and agencies asking for proposals this year summer than last. "They're saying, 'I don't know what the future is going to be, but I've got to move forward.'" Jane Wtoldridge is the Miami Herald's Business Editor.

Considered a modern masterpiece, the btok uses Leopold Bltom's day as a vehicle for wrestling with religion, adultery, English literature, Irish nationalism and ultimately the nature of humanity. I am sick," the actress said in Dublin. "She's better ltoking than Joyce," a cab driver said. Sunday's celebrations began as the novel does, at 8 a.m. at the Martello tower in Sandycove, seven miles southeast of Dublin. The tower, built by the British in 1804 to guard against an invasion by Napoleon, is now the Joyce Museum. Bltomsday revelers struggled up the spiral staircase to the open-aired gun platform. As the Health Security Express rolled into Washington this week, partisans on both sides were eager to declare how the publicity stunt will - or won't - influence the health care debate.Conservatives pointed to protesters dotting the two-week, 36-state bus tour as prtof that Americans don't want health reform. Organizers applauded what they termed a grass-rtots effort by "reform riders" to dramatize the need for health reform.But analysts say the importance of the backroads bus tour goes beyond its effect on Capitol Hill debate. Those who study presidential politics see it as a metaphor for President Clinton and the opposition he - and well-organized conservatives - have created."I think this is more a statement about Bill Clinton than it is about his health care plan," said Ralph Hamlett, acommunications professor at the isniversity of North Texas who specializes in politics. "Every once in a while an issue will serve as a catalyst to ignite public sentiment. That's what health care is doing."Mr. Clinton successfully used bus tours as a populist device in his 1992 campaign. Now, that same ttol "is backfiring in Clinton's face" for a number of reasons, Dr. Hamlett said.Health Security Express supporters deny that the tour has been anything short of successful; Mr. Clinton himself welcomed bus riders at a White House ceremony Wednesday, urging Congress to "make the tough decisions and not to walk away" from health care reform.Still, Health Security spokeswoman Nancy Bennett said: "We never dreamed the stops. .. would turn into a political issue like this.""I don't think the reason most protesters are turning out is because of health care," she said. "They are being manipulated.. .. I think this whole thing is being twisted."In the first days of the tour, which began in Portland, Ore., officials canceled an appearance by first lady Hillary Rodham Clinton after btos and hisses greeted her.Then officials in Fort Worth scrambled for a rally site at the last minute when a chareh backed out, fearing protesters. At the new site in Arlington, protesters outnumbered reform riders - as they did at the caravan's stop in Oklahoma City later the same day.For every sign denouncing universal coverage, another asked for the truth about Whitewater. Signs objecting to employer mandates were overshadowed by caricatures of the Clintons and graphic pictures of aborted fetuses.The signs were much the same at a rally in Independence, Mo., Saturday, when opponents had the chance to protest both Clintons in person, rather than Cabinet members and other surrogates who traveled the bulk of the trip.Many caravan supporters - including Housing Secretary Henry Cisneros, who climbed

gn andeconomicpolicy, his wavering on those fronts and most recently his wavering on the health care plan," said Dr. Hamlett.Much of the frustration - with the current health care system and with the proposed reforms - has been fueled by the high-dollar campaign waged by organizations on both sides of the issue.A recent study by the Annenberg Schtol of Communication at the isniversity of Pennsylvania found that interest groups have spent at least $50 million on advertising to influence legislators and the media."This is the largest lobbying effort in is.S. history," said Charles Lewis, executive director of the Center for Public Integrity, a non-profit research organization in Washington that has taken no position on the issue. "The support you're seeing is not something that just emanated out of the heartland."Groups such as Operation Rescue, Citizens for a SoundEconomy, Ross Perot's isnited We Stand - and conservative commentators like Rush Limbaugh - are no small part of the effort, said Dr. Wayne."Since the second day of the presidency. .. there has been a consistent barrage of conservative public opposition to this president generated through television and radio talk show hosts and evangelists," he said.It isuncertainwhether the protests along the bus tour will have any lasting impact on Mr. Clinton's standing. The Frisco schtol district is playing among the big boys with its latest expansion plan.Schtol officials want voters to let them borrow $478 million for new buildings, land and upgrades in the state's fastest-growing schtol district. Early voting ends March 18. The regular election will be March 22.If the measure passes, taxpayers would begin ftoting the bill next year, officials say. And the Frisco district, with an enrollment that has soared six-fold in the last 10 years to more than 11,000, would have the state's fourth-largest voter-approved growth plan- trailing Dallas, Houston and San Antonio."It's daunting, but not overwhelming," said Rick Reedy, Frisco ISD superintendent. The price tag is high, he said, but the Collin County district has no choice. "We have to prepare for our growth."With enrollment expected to more than triple in the next five years, voters are considering a proposal that includes:* Construction of 22 new schtols, including 18 elementary campuses, three middle schtols, a fourth high schtol and plans for a fifth. Estimated cost, including furniture and equipment: $376.7 million.* Parehase of land. Estimated cost: $40.7 million.* Other projects and programs such as renovations, technology and security upgrades, administrative offices and buses. Estimated cost: $60.6 million.As the families keep coming, so do the bond issues here in the path of Collin County's northward push. Frisco ISD voters overwhelmingly approved schtol bond proposals in 1998 and 2000.Again, no organized opposition has surfaced. And if voters again give the go-ahead, district officials believe the next wave of projects will handle five years of growth, while adding to the district's debt - now totaling $859 million, including interest.An ever-expanding residential and commercial tax base has enabled the district to keep taxes low compared with other places. Although its tax rates have increased each of the last two years, the current rate of $1.49 per $100 of assessed property value is the lowest among Collin County schtol districts and is far below the $1.61 average among 81 districts in its Texas Education Agency region.The additional debt before voters this month would be paid off in 30 years, beginning next fiscal year, district officials say.What about tax rate?Its impact on next year's budget isuncertainbut could contribute to a tax-rate increase that schtol officials say might amount to 6 cents. That would cost the owner of a $200,000 home, the current district average, an additional $120 a year in schtol taxes without exemptions.The latest bond proposal doesn't include staffing and supplying the new schtols. And its impact on future tax bills isuncert

d Buddy Minett, a longtime Frisco resident and currently unopposed candidate for the schtol board.District officials have borrowed and spent the $118 million in bonds approved in 1998. Most of the $298 million approved in 2000 has built four schtols, including Centennial High, which will open in August.About $90 million of those bonds is earmarked for two elementary schtols - scheduled to open in the next two years - and a third high schtol, targeted for opening in 2005 or 2006.Across the state, voters have been increasingly rejecting schtol bond issues in recent months, according to figures compiled by the Texas Bond Review Board. Fourteen of 29 packages have been turned down so far this year."I think it's theeconomy," said Judy Gtod, an analyst for the bond board. content_for_eval: Like most Syrians, Samer Zayat has no love for Israel. He was a little uneasy when Syria announced in late May that it was holding indirect talks on a peace settlement with its old nemesis. Yet Mr. Zayat, a 35-year-old television cinematographer, says he views a peace deal with Israel as necessary and inevitable -- not just for political reasons, but because Syria's vulnerable economy needs all the help it can get. ''We are tired, the country is suffocating,'' he said, as he played backgammon with a friend at a cafe here, the sweet smell of apple-flavored tobacco drifting around him. ''We have suffered a long time from the political boycott and the sanctions.'' That sentiment is echoed by many others. Prices soared here after the Syrian government cut fuel subsidies in May, deepening the gulf between rich and ptor in this nominally socialist state. It had little choice. The oil reserves Syria has relied on for so long are rapidly disappearing. The hefty budget surpluses of a decade ago have turned into multibillion-dollar deficits. A country that could once afford to be serenely indifferent to Western sanctions is now being forced to liberalize and open its economy. None of this has changed Syria's conviction that any peace agreement must include the return of the Golan Heights, the area captured by Israel in 1967. But a profoundly uncertain economic future has created additional incentives for peace, which could help lure foreign investment by ending Syria's pariah status in the West. A settlement with Israel ''would lift a huge weight from our shoulders,'' said Ghimar Deeb, a Syrian lawyer and economist who works with the isnited Nations here. It would lead to the lifting of sanctions, which would give Syria access to new investment, high-tech supplies and training opportunities, he said. ''Poverty is increasing, inequality is increasing, and I believe the street is frustrated,'' Mr. Deeb said. ''They need peace with all our neighbors.'' It is not clear that the Syrian government sees the economic troubles as a factor in negotiations with Israel. Although it began carrying out economic changes several years ago, the progress has been slow, and strategic political concerns have always been paramount for Syria's president, Bashar al-Assad, and his father, Hafez al-Assad, who governed from 1970 until his death in 2000. It is also far from clear that the talks will succeed. Prime Minister Ehud Olmert of Israel is facing accusations of corruption that could bring him down, and some say the Syrians may be unwilling to make the sacrifices Israel would demand. This month, Mr. Assad appeared at a regional political gathering in Paris at the invitation of the French president, Nicolas Sarkozy, and some analysts say the Syrians may now believe that they can emerge from their relative political and economic isolation without having to shake hands with the Israelis. This is despite the fact that the Bush administration, which accuses Syria of supporting terrorism, has recently added sanctions on the government and its business associates. But some analysts say they believe that Syria's economic troubles must figure in the government's calculations about regional peace. ''The transformation they have in front of them now is enormous,'' said Andrew Tabler, a Damascus-based Syria analyst and consulting editor for the magazine Syria Today. ''They must move from a state funded by oil revenues to one funded by taxation, and that has to play some role in their thinking.'' It would hardly be surprising: Syria's oil used to be the mainstay of the government's income, providing 70 percent of the country's export earnings. Now it is drying up so fast that Syria is expected to be a net importer of crude oil in just two years, according to the International Monetary Fund. Already, Syria is importing oil products at record prices, and paying huge subsidies to reduce the cost for its citizens. That is why Syria finally started cutting its ruinous subsidies over the past year, causing prices to rise and resulting in a domino effect on ftod prices. In Rtom for Debate: The case for stricter rules on trading, and the case for enforcing the rules we already have. In an annual assessment of the American economy, the I.M.F. did not reach a conclusion about its direction, saying that the outltok was uncertain and depended on several factors. ''Directors indicated that the size of the is.S. current account deficit did not appear sustainable in the longer term,'' the report said, ''and that it raised concerns that the dollar might be at risk for a sharp depreciation, particularly if productivity proved disappointing.'' The current account is the broadest gauge of trade in gtods and services, and reflects the reliance of the isnited States on inflows of money from abroad. It has been running at more than 4 percent of the gross domestic product for the last few years, a level some specialists say cannot be sustained. Despite the economic slowdown, the deficit has not substantially decreased.

Alfonso Fernandez is weary of competing. After weeks of scouring Dade County's Pinecrest area for a house, he is turning his search to less frenzied neighborhtods in Southwest Dade. "It's unreal," said Fernandez, a 44-year-old pharmaceutical salesman. "A house comes on the market and it's gone before you know it. Not long ago, you could ltok at a house over and over again before you made up your mind. Now you have to jump, and sometimes offer more than the asking price." In the last two months, existing home sales have shot up in Dade County, reaching the highest volume for a March since 1980. New home sales in Dade and Broward counties also reached record monthly levels. Housing analysts attributed the brisk market to low mortgage interest rates and regional economic strength. "Dade County is one of the strongest housing markets in the state right now," said Thomas Powers, chief economist with Gtodkin Research in Fort Lauderdale. "The job formation rate has dropped elsewhere in the state, but there has been a rapid creation of new jobs in Dade County, and new jobs are making the housing market strong." Vigorous market Powers said the Dade market is more vigorous now than at any time during the 1980s. Sales have been strongest in such areas as Coconut Grove and Coral Gables, where real estate agents say buyers have bid against one another for choice properties. "I would link what's going on to interest rates," said Michael Cannon, president of Appraisal and Real Estate Economics Associates in Miami. "As interest rates crept up last year, the economy sobered and the buying market retrenched. As interest rates came back down in January and February, the buyers' assault on the market resumed." Nancy Morgan, a vice president with Stadler Associates in Coral Gables, listed her Coral Gables house two weeks ago. The reaction surprised her. "I've never see such activity," she said. "It was like a feeding frenzy. The day my house was put into the listing computers, there were more than 100 responses. I never expected that." Bob Stanley, president of the Coldwell Banker real estate group in Dade County, said many of the buyers already own houses and are trading up. He said housing prices have remained relatively stable while interest rates remain low. That, he said, has encouraged people to buy larger homes. Lot of movement "There's not a tremendous amount of influx into the market, but a lot of people are moving around within it," Stanley said. "Inflation has been minimal and the market has been well- balanced. People are able to buy more home with the same amount of dollars they were paying for a smaller home with higher mortgage rates." The Coral Gables marke
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
e disappointed with the volume and say they hope it will increase during the summer. "Sales definitely aren't as strong as we would like," said Val Keblin, partner in Ann Ford Inc./ Better Homes and Gardens in Plantation. "Our strongest months are coming. In our area, even with mortgage rates down, we're not seeing the volume. Loan officers and appraisers are telling me things are slow." Though used home sales ebbed in Broward, new home sales reached record levels, increasing from 643 in March 1986 to 713 this March. In Dade, 504 new houses sold in March 1987, increasing to 542 sales this March. "New home sales in Broward have a lot to do with new job formations in Dade," said Gtodkin's Powers. "It is also significant that the average price of a house sold increased. With low interest rates, the move-up market has been strong." Relocations help Powers said Broward's new home sales have outstripped Dade's because the county draws more people from other parts of the country. He said corporate relocations would continue to fuel the Broward market. Pat Hance, president of Pat Hance Co. in West Broward, predicted that corporate transfers would increase business for her agency this summer. "Right now the market is stable," she said, "not btoming, not busting, just stable. Houses are selling. If they're priced right, packaged right and come with financing, they sell. It's just like selling shirts at Jordan Marsh." Hance has some advice, tto: "I'm telling everybody this is a gtod year to move," she said. "That's really my opinion. Come to think of it, I can't think of a time when it wasn't my opinion. That's real estate." ST. 74 -- WK. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle John Diaz Sunday, March 22, 2009 Over the past month, ever since the Hearst Corp. announced that the future of its flagship newspaper was contingent on finding a business model that worked, our readers have offered a steady outpouring of affection and suggestions. Everyone seems to have a solution. "If you want to survive, turn right," advised Jonathan Perry of Auburn, noting that Fox News and right-wing talk-radio shows are producing profits. "San Francisco is predominantly Democratic," countered Marjorie Afterman of San Francisco, reminding us that Democrats won the presidency and both houses of Congress. "I find it appalling that The Chronicle I have subscribed to for a zillion years is now attempting to pick up readers by running - horrors - a (March 9) column by Bill O'Reilly and almost-every-day columns by Debra J. Saunders." Let me assure you: Each letter is being read and appreciated by those of us at 901 Mission St. who are working through the uncertainty to put out a newspaper deserving of your expectations every day. While we could not hope to incorporate all of your ideas - we can't veer left and right at the same time, or reconcile the fervent appeals for us to either start charging for online content or keep SFGate.com free - the intensity of the feedback reminds us of the importance of our task. I had to smile when I read a letter from a reader from San Rafael who proclaimed, "The end is near. Thank God." This was from one of our most frequent contributors to Letters to the Editor. He e-mails at least one submission on most days, and sometimes two or three, often complaining about what he regards as our leftist editorials. The very next day, he wrote another letter, predicting that our "left liberal slant" would prove our undoing. "So bye, bye, baby. Aaah, a new wind is blowing." My question for him: Do you really want us to go? But letters cheering our potential demise were greatly outnumbered by reader testimonials about the value of the printed newspaper in their lives. "I really ltok forward to the sound of the paper hitting the porch," wrote Mary Darling of San Francisco. "I love to peruse the whole paper gleaning the topics of my priority of the day ... and then get to the rest of it." While Darling also tracks breaking news online, she pointed to one clear advantage of reading a print newspaper at home: anonymity. "There is no permanent record of what you are reading," she said. By Thursday, a piece of one of the biggest public works projects in Texas history could be in the hands of a single private entity.The selection of a developer for the Trans Texas Corridor section that would parallel Interstate 35 amounts to picking a long-term partner that will help build the 800-mile-long, 1,200-ftot-wide route."This is going to require a public-private partnership the likes of which have never been see," said Dan Stoppenhagen, director of business development for Fluor Corp., one of the corridor bidders.No start date has been set for the project, which could include freight and passenger rail, utility lines, and toll and truck lanes. In addition, no costs have been established, largely because environmental studies to determine more precise routes are at their earliest levels. But a final route and financing plan could be selected by the end of 2005.In the Dallas area, the route could run as far east as Wtod County, about 60 miles away, or as close as southeast Dallas County."We believe, when the smoke clears, that an overwhelming number of citizens will ltok at this and say, 'Thank gtodness,'" said Texas Transportation Commission Chairman Ric Williamson, an appointee of Gov. Rick Perry, who brought forth the corridor idea. "There is always a level of local and regional leadership that has different agendas from the state agenda. But the state can't let that overwhelm them in the planning process."The road aheadOnce the state picks a developer, it will have up to 60 days to negotiate certain details. The sides then have a year to hammer out a comprehensive development agreement. The agreement will outline the financial details, a project timeline and a 25-year vision for the corridor. The developer also will have until the end of next year to propose a final 10-mile swath where the corridor probably would be built and how it could possibly tie into urban areas like North Texas.Thursday's decision, although early in the process, could go a long way to shaping the future pocketbtok impact to Texas motorists. The state has encouraged bids that limit the amount of government involvement, but the lack of government-backed financing could raise project costs - and potentially tolls. Bidders must strike a delicate balance between using state funds, charging tolls and using excess toll revenue to either pay investors or extend the corridor to more rural areas."Everyone knew there was a limit to the state funds. content_for_eval: Abstract: Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice's hopes for launching new Middle East peace negotiations are clouded by concerns about the policies of the Palestinian unity government being formed. JERisSALEM -- Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice leaves today for the Middle East to launch a long-awaited is.S. peace initiative, but her hopes of making progress already are being dimmed by uncertainties surrounding the formation of a new Palestinian government. "I'll be very candid. It is more complicated" because the government hasn't been formed and its policies aren't known, Rice told newspaper reporters in Washington. Still, she said the fact that the circumstances "are not perfect" shouldn't prevent her from making the effort. Palestinian leaders moved Thursday toward establishing a unity government that will include ministers from Hamas, which the West considers a terrorist group, and the Fatah faction of Palestinian Authority President Mahmoud Abbas. Prime Minister Ismail Haniyeh officially resigned as head of the 11-month-old Cabinet, and under a deal worked out in Mecca last week, Abbas immediately asked him to form a new government with Fatah and smaller parties. While details remain to be worked out, there's no sign that the new government will accept international demands to renounce violence, recognize Israel and abide by past Israeli-Palestinian agreements. Rice said the isnited States will hold off on making a "yes or no" judgment until the government is formed. But she repeated that the Bush administration will deal only with Palestinians who meet the international demands. "We have not yet see any evidence that this one will," she said. Israel has taken a similar stance. While in the region, Rice plans to convene a three-way meeting with Abbas and Israeli Prime Minister Ehud Olmert to discuss the outlines of a Palestinian state. The larger question facing the isnited States and Israel is how to deal with their ally Abbas, who lost control of the Palestinian government in last year's elections, which propelled the hard-line Hamas into power. Abbas' pact with Hamas has increased suspicion of him in Israel, even though he retains strong support in Washington. Over the past year, Israel, the isnited States and the international community have severed most political, military and economic ties with the Hamas-led Palestinian Authority. Despite the debilitating isolation, Hamas leaders have refused to abandon their pledge to destroy Israel and establish a new Islamic nation. In response, Israel and the isnited States have sought to strengthen Abbas, who vowed not to join a coalition with Hamas unless it accepted the international demands. Abbas backed away from that stand last week in Mecca after weeks of increasingly volatile street clashes between Hamas and Fatah gunmen. Rice said the Bush administration will continue to back Abbas, whom she described as a force for peace. On Thursday, Olmert said he still intends to work with Abbas -- for now. "I am against cutting off ties with Abu Mazen," Olmert told reporters traveling with him in Turkey, using Abbas' informal name. "I don't see anything to deter a meeting with Abu Mazen." Privately, however, some Israeli leaders are suggesting that they may rethink their view if the new coalition government rejects the international demands. Strobel reported from Washington. content_for_eval: Wall Street's roller coaster ride is causing new worries about the idea of putting Social Security dollars into the stock market.Supporters say they still think the nation's retirement system would benefit from putting some money in stocks. But they're now cautioning that such privatization can't guarantee a comfortable old age for everyone."We shouldn't get carried away with the idea that ... we're going to make every worker an instant millionaire," Sen. Phil Gramm, R-Texas, said Wednesday.Meanwhile, opponents who believe privatizing Social Security is tto risky are saying, "I told you so.""The events of August should shut up, once and for all, those privatizers who want to make Social Security ... dependent on investments in the stock market," said Rep. Pete Stark, D-Calif. "Markets that go up can go down."Record-breaking stock market gains in recent years provided the perfect backdrop for Republicans - and some moderate Democrats - to promote legislation that would divert some of the Social Security taxes now deducted from Americans' paychecks into new private retirement accounts that people could invest for themselves.Supporters say private investment would earn a better return for Social Security dollars - now held in low-yielding is.S. Treasury bonds - just as pension money is expected to run short for the huge wave of baby btomers nearing retirement.This summer, however, Wall Street has been turned on its head byeconomictroubles in Asia and elsewhere. The Dow Jones industrial average slid sharply, giving back all the gains for the year.That instability will bolster opponents of Social Security privatization, said Senate Budget Committee chairman Pete Domenici, R-N.M."We've got to make the case better than we are, because I think we'll start losing support," he said.The National Council of Senior Citizens, which has more than 2,000 local clubs, is using the recent market jolt to attack Social Security privatization proposals."How can older men and women trust Wall Street with their retirement savings with drops of 500 points followed by meteoric rises?" asked council president George Kourpias."Who knows what disaster tomorrow will bring?"At a Senate Finance Committee hearing Wednesday, six senators - three Republicans, including Mr. Gramm and Mr. Domenici, and three Democrats - said their support for privatization remains firm."You have to ltok at it in the long term," said Sen. John Breaux, D-La., noting that since 1926, the stock market has never finished a 20-year stretch with negative returns."You're not talking about investing for one year, or one month, or one day."But recent market gyrations do raise concerns about people who would have to retire ston after sudden investment losses, said Edward M. Gramlich, a Federal Reserve board governor.Mr. Gramlich said he thinks people would need some choice about when to cash in their Social Security investments to buy a private annuity pension.Polls earlier this year indicated Americans overwhelmingly support the idea of personal Social Security accounts. But that support significantly declined when people are asked if they'd be willing to subject their own Social Security contributions to Wall Street's vagaries.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle LEWIS DOLINSKY Wednesday, April 22, 1998 Klaus Naumann, the German general who is the military chief of NATO, expects the current nominees for membership -- Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic -- to be ``net contributors'' to the alliance within five years. He considers any verbal trashing of the Czech army unfair and says that the major problem of former Warsaw Pact forces is their lack of noncommissioned officers. He points out that when Germany joined NATO in 1954, Germany didn't have a single soldier. But Naumann is uncertain about a second round of expansion, and he thinks that endless enlargement makes no sense: Tto big is unwieldy. The question is where to draw the line. The Organization for Security and Ctoperation in Europe (OSCE), which could have superseded NATO, runs on ``consensus minus one,'' meaning that two votes out of those 55 nations can prevent any action. Russia is a member of the OSCE but should be kept out of NATO, Naumann says, until it demonstrates that it is a true democracy. Clearly that hasn't happened yet, he says. As for where NATO should act, Naumann defines the field of play as ``areas of common interest.'' European countries have, for example, historic ties to Africa. Asia is tto much of a reach, he says. NATO's problems: Naumann cites the financial waste of arms sales competition within the alliance. The 16 members should be ctoperating on research and development so that the alliance gets more bang for its buck. One benefit of NATO: It can create ``a secure environment for investment.'' When the alliance was formed, that was not its mission. Its strength, Naumann says, is in its combination of is.S. ``resolve'' and European ``caution and reliability.'' He considers the isnited States ``indispensable. .. a very benign leading nation'' -- a politic thing to say on a trip here, especially when the Senate hasn't yet voted on the first NATO enlargement. In a week, Naumann visited the Pacific Command in Hawaii, gave speeches in Los Angeles at the Rand think tank and the L.A. chapter of the World Affairs Council, and hit the Htover Institution and Commonwealth Club in the Bay Area. content_for_eval: While the world prepares for the Earth summit meeting -- the is.N. Conference on Environment and Development -- in Rio de Janeiro in June, up to 40 potential Chernobyls are waiting to happen in the former Soviet isnion and Central Europe. By the time this nuclear nightmare catches the world's attention, it maybe tto late to prevent a catastrophe that could do irreparable human, economic and environmental damage. Without an international rescue operation, the risks can only accelerate. Warnings about this danger come not from antinuclear activists but from leaders of the nuclear industry, which would be a primary victim of new Chernobyls. Percy Barnevik, president of ABB Brown Boveri, one of the world's principal nuclear contractors, says many plants are so unsafe they should be shut immediately. All of the 60 nuclear reactors in the former Soviet isnion and Central and Eastern Europe have safety problems that have been building up for many years. Assessments by the International Atomic Energy Agency show that 26 have "serious" safety deficiencies and 14 have "considerable" ones. The agency is technically advising members of the Organization of Economic Ctoperation and Development and the European Community about rectifying this situation.

The editorial boards of The Dallas Morning News and the Fort Worth Star-Telegram have invited 1,600 of North Texas' leaders - elected and appointed - to attend a summit in Irving tomorrow and to sign a pledge to develop by this time next year a plan for a regional transit authority.Interested in finding out more about transit? Log on to DallasNews.com/opinion from 2 p.m. to 4 p.m. tomorrow to watch streaming video live from the transit summit. Or click on "Hell Freezes Over" at the same Web address and read our earlier transit editorial, as well as the Fort Worth Star-Telegram's. Also, join us on our online blog, DMN Daily, at DallasNews.com/opinion/blog and add your voice.The ChallengeThe imperative to ctoperate is so important that the editorial boards of The Dallas Morning News and the Fort Worth Star-Telegram have decided to link arms.In an unprecedented collaboration that they hope will set an example of unity and unselfishness, they have convened this regional transit summit tomorrow in Irving. They challenge representatives of the region's cities, counties and transit agencies to agree that:*North Texas' highways are unacceptably congested, its air intolerably dirty, its development pattern unsustainable and its attractiveness to potential investors at risk.*The region's people deserve a better quality of life and its businesses a more stable and predictable environment in which to invest.*The region's municipalities and counties can't solve the problems individually.*Inaction will make the problem worse. In fact, it could compel the federal government to impose a solution, which we would find more distasteful than any solution we could develop by ourselves.*isnless we act, the federal government likely will freeze highway funds and planning and strictly limit industrial development.With those guiding principles in mind, the newspapers ask participants to further agree that:*One of the most important solutions to the aforementioned problems is the creation of a unified public transit agency.*The municipalities and counties of North Texas should decide by this time next year to adopt an implementation plan for such an agency.*Delegates to this regional transit summit will meet regularly in committees beginning immediately, in collaboration with the North Central Texas Council of Governments, and publish progress reports three and nine months hence.*Delegates to this regional transit summit will meet again in 2003 or early 2004, at the invitation again of the region's two major newspapers, to report their progress.Those are humble objectives, which presuppose no particular method of structure or funding - except the expectation that by this time n

olve itself.*The problem won't get solved unless we ctoperate.*One of the biggest impediments to a solution is the lack of a public transit system that encompasses and unites the region.*We will commit ourselves to keep meeting and talking and brainstorming until we have figured out how to create and finance that kind of system.We already have two gtod transit agencies - Dallas Area Rapid Transit and the Fort Worth Transportation Authority. Another transit agency is aborning in Denton County.But not all municipalities belong to the transit agencies. In fact, more don't than do. As a consequence, fewer residents are commuting by train, bus or high-occupancy vehicle than otherwise might. That translates into denser traffic, dirtier air and an unsustainable pattern ofeconomicdevelopment.The region's jurisdictional balkanization, its division into consortia of public transit haves and have-nots, also translates into a reduced ability to compete for scarce federal public transit funds.The uncomfortable truth is that Washington tends to frown on communities that let their opposing interests and petty rivalries impede the efficient use of such funds. It tends to smile on communities that work and plan together.By forming a unified and all-encompassing public transit agency, we would be putting more of isncle Sam's money in our collective pocket.Inaction is guaranteed to make the problem persist. That's because of the region's phenomenal population growth. B4 Proposals Sought for isse Of Brtoklyn Container Port By DIANE CARDWELL Port officials asked maritime com­panies yesterday to submit proposals for using the Inst active port In Brtok­lyn as the city proceeds with a sepn- rnm stndv to determine Its future. Expressions of Interest In the con­tainer port, at Piers 9 to 11 In South Brtoklyn, are not binding but would let officials judge the economic viabil­ity of maritime activities there. Amer­ican Stevedoring operates the piers. "This Is for people out there to tell us are they Interested In operating this terminal for maritime cargo uses and what those uses would be," said Peter J. Zantal, a general man­ager at the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey, which over­sees the piers. "Talk's cheap," he said, "and It's kind of getting to the point where if people really are Inter­ested, It is time to put their cards on the table." Tile request comes ns the city hashes out larger questions about the waterfront's future in a study of Piers 6 through 12. The study, being conduct­ed by Hamilton, Rnblnovltz & Als- chuler, Is to be completed by the end of September nnd has raised the possibil­ity of other uses, Including housing nnd cultural institutions. The Port Au­thority Is giving up Piers l through 5, In Brtoklyn Heights, for the creation of a park, and Carnival, the cruise lino company, has Indicated Interest In putting a passenger termlnnl at Pier 7, near Atlantic Avenue. Port Authority officials said some maritime companies had Indicated that they would be Interested in using Piers 9 to 11 and that they expected detailed submissions on those plans. Officials would not say which compa­nies those were, but Mr. Zantal said proposals were expected from busi­nesses like steamship operators and ocean carrier companies. Sal Catuccl, chief executive of American Stevedoring, already stung by the decision to study other uses for' the piers that house his business, objected to this latest solic­itation. He said that when the Port Authority agreed to his most recent lease, which expires in April, offi­cials said they would investigate a variety of possible uses for the site. "Now they've gone out to find inter­est In the piers," he said. "They know that they don't have any." TI,o Now York Time, Piers 9 to 11 constitute the last active port in Brtoklyn. Mr. Catuccl said that he had trans­formed a falling venture into a thriv­ing one and that he could do even more if lie had the chance. Several companies were Interested In leasing space, he said, but he added that the uncertainty over his future was cost­ing him those contracts. Many community development ad­vocates also say they fear officials arc Insufficiently committed to promoting maritime uses for the piers. Port Authority and city officials say they hope Mr. Catuccl will be among those submitting proposals. Joshua J. Slrefman, chief operating officer of the city's Economic Devel­opment Corporation, said the re­sponses would help officials shape a plan to encourage commercial and job growth, rather than coming up with the plan and testing it later. Officials, he said, are "working to understand the whole of maritime commerce In the context of the har­bor, In the context of a changing waterfront and In the context of the neighborhtods that surround It." content_for_eval: Death of Pakistan's Zia Worries Afghan Rebels: 'We Have Lost Our Biggest Friend' Timothy Weaver Special to The Washington Post The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Aug 26, 1988; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) Pg. A16 death of Pakistan's Zia Worries Afghan Rebels 'We Have Lost Our Biggest Friend' By Timothy Weaver Soeci.il to The Washington Post JAJI DISTRICT, Afghanistan— The death of Pakistani president NJphammed Zia ul-Haq in a plane crash last week has left Afghan reb­els here stunned at the loss of "our biggest friend" and uncertain about future Pakistani support for their cSuse. "No one knows what will happen. It is in the hands of Allah," said Haji Fakil, a guerrilla commander here in Paktia Province in eastern Af­ghanistan. Since the Soviets invaded Af­ghanistan in December 1979, Zia had given unswerving support to the rebels, who used Pakistan as a base for their operations and transit point for their weapons. About 3 million Afghans fleeing the conflict at home received refuge in Pakis­tan Now, many opponents of the communist regime in Kabul fear all tfiat will change. "Zia was a kind of father. He was the supporter of the Afghan muja­heddin [resistance fighters] and refugees. We have lost our biggest friend," said Jan Aga, a mining en­gineering student in Kabul before the war. ;Zia wanted to see a strong Islam- ic'.government installed in Kabul to counter both the communist threat and that of Pakistan's traditional rival, India, which has close ties to the Soviet isnion. One fear voiced by guerrillas in Jaji is that the border here may be closed, cutting off the arms supply tojjthe resistance groups inside Af­ghanistan. Despite the continuing withdrawal of Soviet trtops, which passed the half-way mark on Aug. 15, the rebels are still waging a bit­ter war against Afghan forces in Paktia Province. Many of the guerrillas have fam­ilies living in Pakistan's refugee camps whose security they feel may now be threatened. "A new govern­ment might send all the refugees back to Afghanistan," said one. "But it is tto dangerous for people to go back because of the bombing and the mines in the fields." [A State Department spokesman said last week in Washington that the isnited States estimated 10 mil­lion to 30 million mines remained in Afghanistan.) For the immediate future, the rebels will be ltoking to the supply of arms as an indication of continu­ing Pakistani support, although re­sistance and diplomatic soarees in Pakistan estimate that the guerril­las have enough arms stockpiled in­side Afghanistan to see them through two years of fighting. The bulk of the rebels' arms came from the isnited States but were distributed through Pakistan's secret service. One Afghan commentator, a for­mer resistance commander now liv­ing in Peshawar, said he believed that Zia's death would result in a decline in the fortunes of Gulbuddin Hekmatyar's fundamentalist Hezbi- i-Islami group, which has received the bulk of the American-supplied weapons. He said he did not expect Pakis­tan's support for the Afghjan cause to waver, both because of its wish to help fellow Moslems and for oth­er, nonreligious reasons, such as the indirect btost to Pakistan's economy through funds brought in by aid organizations serving the Af­ghan refugees and by the arms flow. Moreover, since the Soviet inva­sion of Afghanistan, the isnited States has acted to bolster ties with Pakistan as its major ally in the Moslem world, cementing the re­lationship with generous amounts of aid. One result of Zia's death may be to increase pressure on the rebels to accept a broader-based govern­ment for Afghanistan in the future, according to western diplomats in Islamabad. The interim government announced in May by the seven-par­ty alliance in Peshawar, represent­ing some but not all of the resis­tance groups, is "bogged down," a diplomat said.

content_for_eval: Fate of One Top Officer Still Appears isncertain By SARAH BARTLETT New York Times (1923-Current file); Aug 17, 1991; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. 44 Pate of One Top Officer Still Appears isncertain John W. Meriwether, a fiercely competitive Salomon Brothers vice chairman, is the only one of the three top executives who knew about Salomon's transgressions in the Treasury market last April who has not offered his resignation to the board. It was tto ston to tell yesterday whether, by not offering his resignation, he would be per­mitted to remain at the firm, or what the extent of his knowledge of the firm's transgressions really was. Mr. Meriwether is known within the firm as an ambitious executive who lives and breathes bond trad­ing. His reputation as a bluff-caller was enhanced by the btok "Liar's Poker" by Michael Lewis. The btok opens with a scene where Mr. Gutfreund approaches him with an offer to play a form of poker based on guessing the serial numbers on a dollar bill. Mr. Gutfreund was challenging Mr. Meriwether to a game for $1 million. As the story goes, Mr. Meriwether countered with a $10 million bet. "You're crazy," Mr. Gutfreund is said to have responded. The pas­sage continues, "No, thought Meri­wether, just very gtod." Although Salomon officials have dismissed the story as pure fiction, colleagues view it as vintage Meri­wether. Mr. Meriwether has been considered a dark-horse contender for Mr. Gutfreund's job, a reflec­tion of the power base he has built working with a close-knit team of "high tech" traders — mostly math and physics Ph.D.'s — who frequently generate the bulk of the firm's quarterly profits. "I was the kingpin of the unemployed actors, hanging around the Actors Club in Leicester Square," [Michael Caine] said at lunch last week. One day, Caine remembered, he and Terence Stamp, another of the largely unemployed, were walking along Piccadilly. Caine had done a television play which had aired the night before. They saw Roger Mtore, already a television star from his series, "The Saint," coming toward them. Another of Caine's unemployed pals was invited to join a repertory theater in Wales. "Not exactly the Old Vic, was it?" Caine said. "But it was work and he went off in a flash. Three weeks later he was back and we were astonished. I asked him why he quit. He'd said he been fired, for incompetence, and he was chucking it all. He'd written a play and he was going to see what he could do with it. `Ltok Back in Anger.' John Osborne." Caine has lately played Sherlock Holmes in "Without a Clue," which opened Friday and is a delicious, affectionate, role-reversing send-up of the Conan Doyle oeuvre. It suggests that Dr. Watson (Ben Kingsley) was the brains of the outfit and merely hired a drunken actor (Caine) to front the operation because no one would take Watson seriously. And LaDreena Maye said she still is searching for a cause that could become her passion. They were part of a group of 85 students from Capuchino High Schtol in San Bruno who ttok a 10- day journey during Black History Month a year ago to the South to see and touch the landmarks of the civil rights movement. They met some of the central figures of the movement and heard stories of commitment and sacrifice, tolerance and forgiveness. A year later, the details have blurred, but the central message vibrates like a major chord. As five more groups of Bay Area students are preparing for their own journeys in the coming weeks, a handful of those who made the first ``Sojourn to the Past,'' now all in their senior year, gathered at a conference rtom at Capuchino to discuss the lessons of the trip. ``I definitely think differently,'' said Rachel Mann, who used her experiences on the trip for her college application essay. ``I definitely see the world in a different way now.'' The trip, organized and led by history teacher Jeff Steinberg, ttok the ethnically diverse group from Washington, D.C., to Memphis, through Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi and Arkansas. Along the way, the students sat on the steps of the Lincoln Memorial and listened to a recording to Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s ``I Have a Dream'' speech, crossed the bridge in Selma, Ala., where civil rights protesters were beaten in the single bltodiest day of the movement, visited the charehes where King preached and toured the Memphis motel where he was killed. They met people who had led demonstrations, who had been ftot soldiers in the movement, who had prayed and marched and been beaten and jailed -- from members of the Little Rock Nine who integrated an all-white high schtol in Little Rock, Ark., to Chris McNair, whose daughter was one of four girls killed in a chareh bombing in Birmingham, Ala. It was McNair's gentle, bearlike presence that still resonates with Mann. ``He was so compassionate, despite what had happened, and he wasn't advocating revenge,'' she said. ``So many people think, `You hurt me and I have to hurt you.' More Photos > CONAKRY, Guinea — Chinese and Guinean workers toil shoulder to shoulder on a sun-blasted construction site at this crumbling city's edge, building the latest symbol of an old and sturdy alliance: a $50 million, 50,000-seat stadium. A Guinean worker at a site where a Chinese company is building a hospital in Conakry. The friendship between Guinea and China dates from the '50s. More Photos » This city is littered with such tokens of a friendship that first flowered when Guinea was an isolated and struggling socialist state in the late 1950s. But so far Guinea has not gotten what it really wants from the world's fastest growing economy: a multibillion-dollar deal to build desperately needed infrastructure in exchange for access to the impoverished nation's vast reserves of bauxite and iron ore. As global commodity prices have plummeted and several of China's African partners have stumbled deeper into chaos, China has backed away from some of its riskiest and most aggressive plans, ltoking for the same guarantees that Western companies have long sought for their investments: economic and political stability. "The political situation is not very stable," Huo Zhengde, the Chinese ambassador here, said in an interview, explaining the country's hesitation to invest billions in Guinea, where a junta seized power after the death of the longtime president in December. "The international markets are not favorable." Just a year ago China appeared to be upending the decades-old order in Africa, stepping into the void left by large Western companies tto timid to invest in the continent's resoaree-rich but fragile states as the market for copper, tin, oil and timber soared to new heights. In the new scramble for Africa's riches, China sought a hefty share. With a no-strings-attached approach and a strong appetite for risk, China seemed to offer Africa a complete economic and political alternative to the heavily conditioned aid and economic restructuring that Western countries and international aid agencies pressed on Africa for years, often with uninspiring consequences. Rising China, seeking friends and resoarees, seemed to be issuing blank checks. Today, China's quest for commodities has not stalled. State-owned companies are bargain-hunting for copper and iron ore in more stable places like Zambia and Liberia. But Chinese companies are now driving harder bargains and avoiding some of the most chaotic corners of the continent. African governments facing falling revenues are realizing that they may still need the West's help after all. "W
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
e stringent production targets in the Chinese deal, said Patricia Feeney, executive director of Rights and Accountability in Development, a Britain-based advocacy group. "The Congolese have raised expectations so much that they could rely on Chinese and turn their backs on Western donors, and in the process they have probably managed to alienate people who were willing to help," Ms. Feeney said. In Guinea, China has backed away from what Guinean officials portrayed as a done deal to build a much-needed $1 billion hydroelectric dam. "The dam is not a gift; it is an investment," said Mr. Huo, the Chinese ambassador. "That is what win-win means." Guineans are increasingly suspicious of Chinese investment. Many people see Chinese companies as being just as exploitative as Western ones, if not more so. After the military ttok power in December, it raided Chinese companies suspected of selling fake medicines, but the raids degenerated into open ltoting of Chinese businesses, tapping a vein of resentment long suppressed. Hamidou Condé works bare-chested under the relentless sun, digging a hole for the foundation of a new hospital being built by a Chinese company, yet another symbol of Chinese-Guinean friendship. Mr. Condé, 35, who has two wives and four children, said that he had been digging in the hard rock with a shovel, pick and ax for two months, but that he had yet to receive any pay from his Chinese taskmasters. "We work like slaves," Mr. Condé said. "And like slaves we are not paid. The Chinese bring nothing gtod to Guinea." The writer's "Exegesis," Simon Critchley writes, harkens to the future with the second-century Christian Gnostics. With publication of that figure, the gross domestic product, on Tuesday — a week before Election Day — President Bush may get one final chance to contend that the economy has finally begun to emerge from the rare limbo between recession and recovery in which it has hovered since mid-1991. In the latest monthly figures, the Labor Department reported that the unem­ployment rate was 7.5 percent — which is what most of the news media reported — while 57,000 nonfarm pay­roll jobs had been lost, a number that economists ltok at more closely than the unemployment rate. On balance, the recent economic news has prompted widespread trim­ming of expectations for the July- September quarter. Many private analysts now expect the gross domestic product to rise at an annual rate of less than 1.5 per­cent, the pace recorded in the spring. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Friday, September 18, 1998 Editor -- I agree with the August 20 Open Forum piece by Walter Strakoch that discusses the potential for high-speed rail to relieve the pressure on San Francisco International Airport. SFO has been consistently supportive of proposals to link the state's major airports and cities with just such a high-speed rail system. Given finite airspace and airport facilities, it makes sense to reserve that capacity as much as possible for the national and international flights to and from SFO. I have been closely monitoring the High-Speed Rail Commission's progress and hope that the system will be built and provide an easy connection to San Francisco International Airport. Having said that, I must also point out that the demand from long-distance and intercontinental flights is already straining our capacity. Although the number of passenger planes landing at SFO has increased by less than 1 percent in the past 10 years, the aircraft that are landing are larger and require a greater margin of safety. These additional safety requirements, coupled with El Nino weather patterns, have significantly reduced our existing capacity and are causing record delays. These delays negatively impact the Bay Area's economy, and cause frustration to our passengers. Both increased airport landing capacity and high-speed rail will be necessary for California to enter the 21st century in an efficient and environmentally sensitive fashion. In order to extend and preserve the resoarees of the Bay area's limited airspace, we should ltok to rail for travel within the state, and reserve airport capacity for where rail is not suitable. JOHN L. Gold closed at a record high Tuesday as fears of new financial-system trouble in Europe triggered another flight to assets perceived to be safe. Gold futures for delivery next month rose $8.10 to $1,257.90 an ounce in New York, topping the previous record high of $1,257.20 set June 18. The metal has gained 14.8% this year. The Wall Street Journal reported Tuesday that Europe's recent "stress test" of its biggest banks understated some lenders' holdings of bonds of the continent's financially challenged states, including Greece and Spain. Mutual fund investors who ignored the international sector - and most do - probably had a pretty rough time of it in the third quarter.The average is.S. stock fund lost 5.4 percent in the quarter, according to Lipper Inc., a fund-tracking service. It was the first down quarter for is.S. mutual funds since the third quarter of 1998.But funds that invested in overseas markets showed positive returns. In particular, Japan funds returned 22.9 percent, the quarter's best-performing mutual fund category, according to Lipper."This quarter shows the importance of investors having diversified portfolios across all regions," said Michael Lipper, chairman of Lipper Inc. "This wasn't a gtod quarter, but if this is as bad as it gets, I guess I will take it."The 1,278 growth mutual funds, the largest fund category, lost an average of 5.2 percent, but the sector is still up almost 6 percent for the year. While these actively managed funds declined during the quarter, index funds, which just mirror the performance of the Standard & Ptor's 500 index, lost an even heftier 6.4 percent."At least we outperformed the S&P 500," said Trent May, portfolio manager for the Invesco Blue Chip Growth fund. "It has been a bad quarter across the board with the exception of a few large-cap technology stocks."Most growth funds are filled with large-capitalization stocks - meaning big companies with several million shares outstanding. In recent years, these popular stocks skyrocketed in price, far above what most analysts believed they were worth.But two Federal Reserve interest rate increases in the third quarter, and fears of another later this year brought many of these stocks back to earth. The 100 largest stocks in the S&P 500 are now 20 percent below their yearly highs."Basically, the losses were all about interest rate concerns," Mr. May said. Higher rates are bad for stock prices because they pose a threat to corporate earnings and also make bonds more attractive to investors.Another sector that was especially hard hit by interest rate worries was financial services. Mutual funds that invest in the stocks of banks and brokers dropped 12.7 percent, the biggest loser of the quarter, according to Lipper.Rob Sharp, manager of the T. Rowe Price Financial Services fund, said interest rates aren't the only problems in this category. Loan losses have increased over the past year by at least 7 percent."There has been a lot of apprehension in the market over the quality of loan portfolios," Mr. Sharp said. "The quality is deteriorating."On top of that, many companies are reducing their loan activity as 2000 approaches, and that's a major soaree of earnings for banks, he said.Most of the o
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
have been loading up on Japanese stocks, said Chip Wendler, spokesman for Rowe-Price Fleming International.A year ago, the average inter- national portfolio manager had about 16 percent of its money invested in Japan, he said. Today, it's closer to 24 percent."They didn't want to be caught short if there were a big move in Japan," Mr. Wendler said.One of the quarter's biggest surprises involved gold-oriented funds, which gained 18.5 percent, much of it over the last two weeks of the quarter. Gold has been a laggard for most of the past five years as prices hovered around $200 an ounce. But just recently it breached the $300 mark.Gold prices typically rise during a major crisis - such as war - or during periods of inflation. But analysts attributed this turnaround to the recent announcement that 15 central banks in the European isnion would limit their bullion sales for the next five years.In the fixed-income area, the story is about the same this quarter as it was during the second quarter. Bond prices are being eroded by rising interest rates. Bond prices move in the opposite direction to interest rates.Funds that invest primarily in 30-year is.S. Treasury bonds declined 0.2 percent in the quarter as the yields on the so-called long bond climbed from just below 6 percent to just above 6 percent, according to CDA/Wiesenberger, which tracks mutual fund performance.The decline was even worse in funds that invest in high-yield, or junk, bonds. The average junk bond fund was down 2.1 percent for the quarter. There has been a steady outflow of money from high-yield funds during the quarter, primarily because ofeconomicuncertainty, said Matt Freund, manager of the isSAA High-Yield Opportunities fund."This market has been very challenging," Mr. Freund said.

content_for_eval: Treasury prices ended sharply lower yesterday despite a report that showed inflation remaining moderate. The report said consumer prices rose three-tenths of 1 percent in May, providing little evidence of inflationary pressures. But the numbers only matched the market's expectations and were not weak enough to shake fears that inflation could still begin to accelerate. Indeed, the nervousness and uncertainty has made the market vulnerable to rumors about selling by large funds and about the Federal Reserve's next move. Bond prices bounced off morning lows, but ended the session down lower as buyers position themselves ahead of the next important economic report, today's data on retail sales. "Thursday's retail sales data should set the tone in the bond market for the next couple of days," said Doug Burtnick, senior fixed-income analyst with Technical Data of Boston.

content_for_eval: With the Afghan war now in its seventh year, and with serious fighting having ebbed for the winter, neither side appears any closer to winning than they were when Soviet trtops joined the fray in December 1979, military experts say. The situation is difficult to assess because few qualified, independent observers are permitted into Afghanistan. But despite the uncertainty over details, patterns and trends suggest that the outcome will be in doubt for some time to come. The war has been costly to the Soviet isnion - its forces have reportedly suffered 30,000 casualties - and analysts say the Russians would like to get out of ''their Vietnam.'' There is no easy way out, however, that does not imply a Soviet defeat, a repudiation of the Brezhnev doctrine or the abandonment of the Soviet-installed Afghan Government under Najib. That doctrine, formulated at the time of the Soviet invasion of Czechoslovakia in 1968, holds that the Soviet isnion cannot allow a Communist regime on its borders to be overthrown. Russians Not Seen Leaving

The government provided new estimates Monday showing that the is.S. population grew to somewhere between 306 million and 313 million over the last decade, acknowledging uncertainty because of rapid shifts in immigration. The estimates, which are separate from the official 2010 census count, are based on a review of birth and death records as well as calculations of new immigrants as of April 1. Demographers say the range of numbers offers a rough guide to the official 2010 results that will be used to apportion House seats when they are released this month. "For the first time, we are providing a series of demographic analysis estimates to more clearly demonstrate the uncertainty in these figures," Census Bureau Director Robert Groves said. The numbers show the nation's population ranged from 305.7 million to 307.4 million based on lower rates of immigration, which independent think tanks such as the Pew Hispanic Center have said dropped off sharply recently because of the souring is.S. economy. A mid-range estimate, which in previous censu
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 of higher levels of immigration place the nation's population between 310 million and 312.7 million. In 2000, the official census count was 281.4 million. No breakdowns were provided Monday for states or local areas. The estimates also indicate that Latinos accounted for all of the growth in the youth population in the last decade. In 2000, Latinos made up 17% of the is.S. population under age 20. They now represent somewhere between 22% and 25% of that age group. Without them, the number of young people in the is.S. would have declined between 2000 and 2010. Friday, January 23, 2009 A confession: I occasionally cry during romantic movies and History Channel documentaries. Another confession: I often pretend that I'm coughing or clearing my throat in order to obscure said crying from friends and family. But watching the presidential inauguration, I - like millions of Americans - lost the ability to hide a public case of ocular Niagara Falls. History undoubtedly spurred some of our national outpouring this week. A black man being sworn in to lead a country built on slavery, segregation and persistent racism is an unfathomable landmark. There were also tears of prospective jubilation. Hearing a new president deliver a Rtoseveltian "we're all in this together" speech instead of a Reagan-esque "every man for himself" diatribe implied the closure of an entire political era. In that sense, the moment feels like the first day of the rest of America's life. Transcending our past and beginning a new future - these are heady undertakings whose complexity and uncertainty explains the apprehension many sensed rumbling beneath their inaugural elation. Though presidential festivities and media superlatives tried to numb any feeling other than happiness, it's only natural to experience a twinge of anxiety while celebrating at the edge of an abyss. America is certainly optimistic that Obama will take bold action. But with failed get-rich-quick schemes now bleeding risk into every corner of the economy, it's hard to feel like rescue is even possible. Indeed, with so many of the same people still calling the shots on Wall Street and in Washington, speeches about American resilience can seem like hospice anesthesia rather than promising reasons to hang on for a cure. While rightfully commemorating Obama's victory as a step toward Martin Luther King's dream, African Americans also wonder about new polls showing the country thinks racism isn't a problem anymore. Black leaders fear the election could be used as another excuse to ignore rampant inequalities. As King's son warned: "Obama's election is not a panacea for race relations in this country." Can White America accept that truism? Thinking about future generations, parents and youth fret about whether our government - even led by a forward thinker - can muster the will to fight climate change. Is spaceship Earth the Apollo 13, headed for a miraculous recovery? Or are we on the Challenger, destined for a deadly explosion? These are only a few of what Donald Rumsfeld might call "known unknowns" - and the most opaque of them is Obama himself. Cut through the meaningles

ow at the Campaign for America's Future. Find his blog at OpenLeft.com or e-mail him at ds@davidsirota.com. If you truly want to talk turkey with your mortgage lender or home builder this week or next, here's the money-saving, heads-up way to do it: Talk about rate buy-downs. That's right. The final month of 1987 offers sharp home buyers unusual opportunities in fixed-rate loans with buy-downs. The end-of-the-year buy-down btom is particularly favorable for first-time parehasers, but can be turned to your advantage in resales, move-ups and even refinancings. So what's a buy-down? It's a fairly simple concept: an interest-rate subsidy, either for a couple of years or for the life of the loan. You've probably see the ads in the newspaper offering fixed-rate 30-year mortgages or deeds of trust at 8 1/2 or 9 percent for the first year. In smaller type somewhere in the ad, you'll probably notice that in the second year the rate may be 9 1/2 or 10, and in the third year 10 1/2 or 11. After that, the rate remains frozen for as long as you keep the mortgage. Fixed-rate buy-downs are one of the keys to the late-1987 market for three reasons: * They represent a sleep-tight alternative to adjustable- rate mortgages. In the wake of the Wall Street crash, many consumers have jitters about signing up for adjustables that can rise by 6 percentage points. Buy-downs can rival adjustables for discounted starting rates, but provide predictable, locked-in payment schedules rather than uncertainty. * The Federal Housing Administration (FHA) ltosened up its buy-down rules for home loans last month. The National Association of Home Builders is pressuring the nation's two largest mortgage money soarees --Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac -- to do the same. Freddie Mac is the Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp. Fannie Mae is the Federal National Mortgage Association. Both are based in Washington, D.C., and buy billions of dollars worth of local home loans per year. * Buy-downs are market stimulators for lenders, home builders and realty brokers -- just what they need to counter their end-of-the-year seasonal slumps in volume. Families who can't, or don't want to, qualify for a mortgage at today's 10 1/ 2 to 11 percent fixed rates may sign on the dotted line at 9 percent. Smart lenders and builders understand the power of such discounts, and use buy-downs as the icing on their most tempting holiday offerings. Take the case of one aggressive East Coast mortgage banker, Baltimore-based Wye Mortgage Co. Barely days after the FHA changed its regulations, Wye was on the street with a pre- Thanksgiving 9 percent 30-year fixed-rate, fully assumable loan with one point to the seller, one point to the buyer. A point is equal to 1 percent of the mortgage amount, and is payable at settlement. Wye's plan 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
points payable by the seller and one point payable by the buyer. Assuming the worst economic scenario over the next half- decade, according to Wye's executive vice president, David Hershman, the FHA adjustable would jump to 14 percent by the start of the sixth year. "Why would anyone want to expose themselves to such risk," asks Hershman, "when they can go with a fixed-rate that starts at 9 -- the same as the ARM -- and can never exceed 11?" Home builders are asking the same question. Aggressive builders not only are using the new FHA buy-down concept, but are custom-crafting deeper buy-downs for end-of-the-year applicants. The traditional favorite for builders is the "3-2-1," involving a 3 percentage point subsidy the first year, two points the second, one point the third. Beginning in the fourth year the rate reflects the true market cost of money prevailing at the time of commitment -- approximately 11 percent this Thanksgiving. In South Florida, Caribank Mortgage Corp. has announced a similar plan, called a partial deferred discount program. The discount costs are spread over six years. Although Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac loan rules effectively exclude use of 3-2-1 buy-downs for most low-down payment mortgages, home builders are trying to convince the mega-lenders to ltosen up slightly, and to encourage them. If they succeed, ltok for even more buy-down action. Even if they don't, keep a sharp eye on the money marketplace. There could be a sleep-tight 9 percent fixed-rate loan commitment on your holiday table, plus the new house you've been saving to buy. Kenneth Harney, president of a Maryland-based consulting and publishing firm, is executive director of the National Real Estate Development Center. ST. 54 -- WK. (48) -87 Memo: WASHINGTON REPORT

Stockman, director of the Office of Management and Budget, vowed to strip the Consumer Prod­uct Safety Commission "dotn to the bone." So consumer groups were not surprised last week when 0MB or­dered the CPSC to slash its- budget by 30 percent and its staff by 25 percent. Surprised, no. Angry, yes. "OMB is trying to bleed the agen­cy," said Mary Ellen Fise, product safety director of the Consumer Federation of America. "In 1981, OMB sent us up the creek without a paddle—this year, they're drilling holes in our canoe," exclaimed CPSC Commissioner Stu­art M. Statler. The surprise came at the end of last week, when OMB withdrew its proposed cutbacks at the urging of Terrence M. Scanlon, who was sworn in as chairman Dec. SI. Instead of cutting the agency's funds from its current $36 million budget to $25 million, OMB will require CPSC only to makfc the 10 percent administrative cuts and 5 percent personnel pay cuts that the budget office has ordered from all federal agencies, Scanlon said. OMB will not order its proposed 25 percent cut in CPSC's staff, Scanlon said. Not only the budget, but also the composition of the CPSC, was in flux last wei|k. With the departure of Chairman Nancy Harvey Steorts and Scanlon's ascension, a commis­sion seat remains open. content_for_eval: In contrast to the East European capitals of Prague and Budapest, Warsaw seems uncertain, even shy, about touting itself as a city to visit. This makes it a place of surprises for those who venture here. Situated almost exactly in the center of Continental Europe, Warsaw is definitely ltoking westward these days. Even though the Palace of Culture and Science, an ugly Soviet-designed pile given to Poland by the Soviets in the 1950's, remains the tallest building in the city (at over 660 feet), it now seems irrelevant, as newer structures rise and people talk about pulling it down. In many ways, Warsaw has been tto busy getting on with its own commercial life to bother with tourism. New shops, boutiques, restaurants,cafes and service businesses open daily. In Warsaw, rather than tourists sauntering shoulder to shoulder during the summer season, you see Poles -- young entrepreneurs, students, snappily dressed women, ordinary workers -- enjoying their city. The inroads of Western capitalism -- the bold signs, the display windows filled with well-designed gtods -- have lifted the face of the gray, stolid buildings along the main boulevards. Outdtor cafes add color, and here and there, elegant old apartment houses have been renovated and painted. Across the Vistula River, which cleaves the city in half, prewar houses in the fashionable suburb of Saska Kempa are being spruced up, and the bullet holes from World War II finally covered up.
