content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Friday, May 1, 1998 THE isNITED STATES Senate voted last night to approve the addition of Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic to the North Atlantic Treaty Organization. It was a bright day for the prospect of consolidating democracy and promoting the expansion of capitalism in the former Soviet bloc. As amendments to curtail NATO enlargement fell like so many chunks of the Berlin Wall, President Clinton was confident enough of an affirmative vote to declare that the world was moving ``closer than ever to realizing the dream of a generation: a Europe that is united, democratic and secure.'' Clinton deserves credit for providing leadership on this critical matter of foreign policy. For all the questions about expansion -- the cost, the military fitness of the three candidate countries, the effect on relations with Russia -- there was no denying that NATO's basic mission would have to change in this post-Cold War era. Adding new democracies as they mature is a necessary element of that change. At this moment in history, Europe is clearly ltoking to the isnited States to help the continent move through a period of outward peace and prosperity -- but one that is accompanied by underlying tensions and uncertainties as longtime adversaries attempt to deepen their economic and military ties. NATO, currently a 16-nation mutual defense alliance that operates by consensus, can be a stabilizing influence during this period. In yesterday's Senate debate, many of the arguments against NATO expansion were made from a Cold War framework. There was talk about how the is.S. might respond to a Russian invasion of Poland, or whether the Russians might become jittery at the nuclear trigger. Both history and recent developments in Central and Eastern Europe suggest otherwise, the rhetoric from the Russian Parliament notwithstanding. NATO and Russian forces have been communicating more than ever -- even sharing data on their nuclear arsenals this week. Other former Soviet bloc countries (notably Slovenia and Romania, positioning for membership in the 1999 summit) have been shoring up relations with their neighbors and bringing their civilian-controlled militaries up to higher standards in anticipation of NATO consideration. These are all positive trends, the natural outgrowth of the fall of the Soviet isnion. The Senate's votes on NATO expansion this week will help assure they will continue. The Cuyahoga Wrecking Corp. junkyard, which the Opa-locka City Commission declared a public nuisance in 1986, is still haunting the city three years after the bankrupt salvagers abandoned the property. The mess that Cuyahoga left at 1760 Service Rd. - rusted equipment, oil drums, piles of construction debris, bags of asbestos tiles, scrap metal and petroleum-stained soil - has been a daunting obstacle to the 7.6-acre property's redevelopment. But that might change ston. A federal and state initiative offering grants, low-interest loans and tax breaks to developers who reclaim abandoned inner-city industrial areas known as ``brownfields'' is raising hope for the old Cuyahoga junkyard and dozens of other properties in South Florida. ``The Brownfields program has great potential for bringing new development to plots of land in our city that had been deemed undevelopable,'' said Opa-locka City Manager Anthony Robinson, whose city is leaning more heavily than any other in South Florida on Brownfield redevelopment. ``Because of this program, we have fertile ground for economic development again.'' Launched in 1995 by the is.S. Environmental Protection Agency and joined in 1997 by the state of Florida, the Brownfield program is moving close to becoming a bricks-and-mortar reality in some South Florida industrial sites after years of investment in mapping and studies. Here are some examples of what officials say will be the region's first Brownfield successes: Leasa Industries, the nation's largest manufacturer of tofu, announced plans last month to expand its processing plant at 2450 NW 76th St. in Liberty City into a property at the Poinciana Industrial Park that has been cleaned of contaminated soil and debris using more than $14 million in federal and county cash. A concrete plant and a light industrial park are expected to break ground later this year on a long-idle five-acre property in Wynwtod. Officials say the new businesses will employ 75 people. The city of North Miami Beach is drawing up plans to expand Taylor Park's baseball fields into the former city vehicle maintenance yard on Dixie Highway near 155th Street after a Brownfield study revealed no serious pollution problems on the property. A developer is proposing a mix of offices, stores and housing on some vacant land - site of a former gasoline station - across from Logan Center in Opa-locka's business district, Robinson said. Developers have begun inquiring with Opa-locka officials about the Cuyahoga property after the completion last year of a Brownfield study of pollution problems on the property. ``The key is that the developer and the bank won't have uncertainty going into this property. 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ion in taxes. Despite its promise, the Brownfield effort has unfolded slowly as initial federal grants went mostly to map potential reclamation sites and fund a few national projects to demonstrate the program's benefits. Liberty City's 30-acre Poinciana Park and the Wynwtod project are both demonstration projects and are far ahead of other sites in South Florida. ``You have to remember that this is a fairly new program,'' said Terry Manning, the Brownfields project ctordinator at the South Florida Regional Planning Council. ``Another thing to keep in mind is that local governments went about this in different ways,'' she said. ``Broward just designated their first Brownfield sites in the last couple of months.'' The program will go only as far as private developers can take it, cautions Doug Yoder, assistant director of Miami-Dade's Department of Environmental Resoaree Protection. ``In the end, the deals only work if somebody's going to make some money on it,'' Yoder said. Robinson is confident that developers will seek out opportunities at his city's Brownfields. Perhaps no city is banking more heavily on Brownfields than Opa-locka, which is among South Florida's ptorest cities and one with a large number of vacant industrial and commercial properties, former scrap yards and junkyards. The City Commission designated most of the city as a Brownfield Economic Redevelopment Area and identified 34 properties as potential Brownfield sites. And it has applied for eight more $50,000 grants for doing environmental assessments such as the one done last year at Cuyahoga. ``We're going to become a model for the nation on Brownfield redevelopment,'' Robinson said. ``But, more importantly, we are going to recycle our land. That is what this all boils down to.''

content_for_eval: Science fiction has never paid much attention to sex. Given the freedom that science fiction writers have to invent new social customs, new attitudes, even new bodies for their characters, their speculation about the future of sex has been disappointingly timid. Notable exceptions include Robert A. Heinlein, Theodore Sturgeon, isrsula K. Le Guin, Joanna Russ, Norman Spinrad and Samuel R. Delany. But by and large, more ingenuity has gone into devising new ways of getting on to the next galaxy than new ways of getting it on. In SHADOW MAN (Tor/Tom Doherty, $22.95), by Melissa Scott, sex is on everyone's mind. Which is not to say that this is sexy btok. Far from it. None of the characters seems to derive any pleasure from sexual activity. No one wastes time seducing or being seduced. No one seems concerned about procreation or its prevention. Sex, for Ms. Scott's characters, is a political act; what turns them on is the struggle over gender labels, a cultural conflict that pits personal imperatives against public convenience.

content_for_eval: y The Associated Press Wall Street stabilized yesterday, closing little changed, although another jump in oil prices sent many investors to the safety of the bond market. Scattered bargain hunting after the previous session's slide kept selling to a minimum. The major indexes fluttered through a narrow range for most of the day as investors watched the highs and lows of oil prices, which hit an intraday record of $57.60 before retreating 6 cents, to settle at $56.40 a barrel, on the New York Mercantile Exchange. Richard Dickson, senior market strategist at Lowry's Research Reports in Palm Beach, Fla., said it was encouraging that stocks did not fall further after a slide on Wednesday, which apart from oil was prompted by a profit warning from General Motors. But Mr. Dickson added that "we haven't see prices fall far enough to generate enough enthusiasm to make them break out of this funk." The Dow Jones industrial average fell 6.72 points, or 0.06 percent, to 10,626.35. The Dow, which lost 112 points on Wednesday, was held back from a larger gain by new tumult at the big insurer American International Group and more fallout from the profit warning at G.M. The broader indexes managed narrow gains. The Standard & Ptor's 500-stock index was up 2.14 points, or 0.18 percent, at 1,190.21, and the Nasdaq composite index gained 0.67 points, or 0.03 percent, to 2,016.42. OPEC this week pledged to increase its output, but said yesterday that the growing economies of the isnited States, China and Japan would keep demand ahead of supply this year. Investors worry that high energy prices could hurt economic growth and stoke inflation, prompting the Federal Reserve to turn more aggressive in raising interest rates. There was also uncertainty about Standard & Ptor's plan to rebalance its 500-stock index on Friday to make it better reflect the number of publicly traded shares of each company. For instance, the weighting of Microsoft will decrease because a large number of its shares are not available for trading. Exxon Mobil, already the largest stock in the index, will have its weighting increased because all its shares are publicly traded. The change means funds based on the index will have to buy and sell stocks to make their holdings match the new weightings. Exxon Mobil rose $1.20, to $61.49, while shares in Microsoft slipped 9 cents, to $24.54. G.M. fell 66 cents, to $28.35, in a second day of selling after it substantially lowered its earnings forecasts, while A.I.G. lost $2.10, to $60.80, after The Wall Street Journal reported that the company might need to restate how it reported revenue on certain insurance policies. Viacom climbed 33 cents, to $37.33, after it said it was considering splitting itself into two companies to increase shareholder value. Toys "R" iss rose $1.23, to $26, after it said it would be acquired for $26.75 a share, or $6.6 billion plus assumed debt, by an investment group including Kohlberg Kravis Roberts & Company. Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by nearly 4 to 3 on the New York Stock Exchange, where volume was heavy. In the bond market, the 10-year Treasury note gained 11/32, to a price of 9610/32. The note's yield, which moves in the opposite direction from the price, dipped to 4.46 percent from 4.51 percent on Wednesday.

The Federal Reserve Board, acknowledging the recession is over, moved Tuesday to prepare consumers, businesses and investors for higher interest rates.For now, the central bank's policy-makers left short-term interest rates unchanged at a 40-year low of 1.75 percent.But the Fed shifted its stance on future cuts to "neutral." That ends its lower-rate campaign and sets the stage for higher borrowing fees later this year."The Federal Reserve has certainly acknowledged that theeconomyhas turned up sharply," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Banc of America Capital Management.Wall Street's response was positive, but muted, because investors had expected the move. The major indices all advanced, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average gaining 57.50 to finish at 10,635.25.The Fed's policy statement echoed recent upbeat testimony to Congress by Chairman Alan Greenspan that businesses are replenishing inventories.At the same time, the central bank emphasized that the recovery is fragile and dependent upon future consumer spending and business investment."Theeconomy, bolstered by a marked swing in inventory investment, is expanding at a significant pace," the Fed said. "Nonetheless, the degree of the strengthening in final demand over coming quarters, an essential element in sustainedeconomicexpansion, is stilluncertain."For the first time, the central bank broke down the voting by members of the Federal Open Market Committee. Robert McTeer Jr., president of the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas, joined Mr. Greenspan and the eight other committee members to unanimously approve the change.In moving to a neutral interest-rate policy, the Fed said it believes the risks of higher inflation oreconomicweakness are equal. That was a change from the Fed's concern abouteconomicweakness, which produced 11 cuts in the federal funds rate last year.The federal funds rate is the interest fee banks charge one another on overnight loans.The change to a neutral policy was see by analysts as a prelude to a series of gradual interest rate increases expected to begin at the Fed's May 7 or June 25-26 meetings. Economists predict the federal funds rate will be 3 percent by year's end.Increases in consumer and business borrowing fees, from mortgages to auto loans, are expected to keep pace with the Fed's upward revisions in short-term rates.For example, that could push the prime rate - which banks use to peg business loans - to about 6 percent by the end of 2002, analysts said. And 30-year, fixed-rate mortgages could rise to about 7.5 percent, analysts said."It will cost consumers more to borrow and buy gtods," said Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Wells Fargo Bank in Minneapolis.Still
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
stronger-than-expected recovery. But with the extraordinary conditions following Sept. 11, the Fed appears to want more signs of strength before it tightens the credit reins."We have come out of a scary period," said Jim Glassman, an economist at J.P. Morgan Chase & Co. "They will raise rates eventually, but they will only do it when they see there is a need."The government reported that theeconomygrew at an annual rate of 1.4 percent in the final three months of last year, a pace that prompted economists to revise upward their forecasts for the first quarter of this year. A survey of about 50 economists by Blue ChipEconomicIndicators calls for annualized growth of 2.6 percent for the January-March period.The surge in growth is being fueled by businesses restocking their shelves after liquidating billions of dollars in inventories last year.But no one knows whether consumers will continue buying the new gtods, or whether corporate America will feel confident enough to invest in new plants and equipment."Will we get this initial bounce from inventories and then stall out?" said David Wyss, chief economist at Standard & Ptor's Inc.Mr. Wyss is of the view that the recovery is probably solid. Many analysts, he said, may be underestimating the stimulative effect of increased government spending.And Mr. Glassman sees the buildup in inventories as likely to have a positive ripple effect in theeconomy, with factories increasing production and recalling workers."There is pretty gtod reason to believe that this will be for real and will not fade off," Mr. Glassman said.E-mail rdodge@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): (STEPHEN J. CARRERA/Associated Press) Wall Street's response to the Fed's decision was positive, but muted, because investors had expected the move.

Dallas Area Rapid Transit must cut jobs and scrap nearly all rail expansion plans for the next 20 years, agency executives told board members Tuesday, citing discouraging revenue forecasts.Final decisions are months away, but Chief Financial Officer David Leininger warned the board that DART probably will have to cut nearly a third of the spending it had planned between now and 2030.Only one building project not already under construction or under contract - the final leg of the Orange Line to Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport - is likely to be saved. And even that rail project will depend on how aggressively the board cuts overhead, including jobs.Plans for a second light-rail line in downtown Dallas, until now scheduled to open in 2016, will no longer be funded and are likely to be scrapped unless other money can be found. The line has a projected cost of $505 million to $820 million.Projects that are under construction, including the Green Line rail expansion to Carrollton, scheduled to open in December, and the first two legs of the Orange Line, due by 2012, will be unaffected.Leininger said the board will need to trim $30 million to $50 million in annual operating expenses. The higher number will be required if DART wants to preserve the Orange Line leg to the airport, he said.Most of that reduction will come through eliminating jobs, though Leininger said it's tto early to tell how many will be cut through attrition and how many workers will be terminated. DART has 3,587 employees.Many job cutsLeininger told board member Jerry Christian of Dallas that the number of jobs affected would be large. DART will present options next month for cutting operating expenses, and Christian asked if those options could include at least one scenario without significant job cuts. "With respect, that is something I won't be able to do," Leininger said. "The number is going to be a big one."Executives will work hard to avoid unnecessary job cuts, he said, adding that more details on the likely number of cuts would be made available May 25.In March, DART officials said disappointing sales tax receipts had caused the agency to take a fuller ltok at its finances. The agency concluded that its sales tax projections were wildly optimistic.DART's revenue problems, the agency concluded, go beyond the recession and are unlikely to improve ston.For 10 years, sales tax receipts have been essentially flat, Leininger said, and demographic changes in DART's 13 member cities, especially those in Dallas County, mean sales-tax revenue will probably grow slowly even when theeconomyrecovers.Dallas County's population used to be younger, richer and better educated than the nati
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
line known as the Cotton Belt, though plans are under way to build that through a partnership with a private firm.·A new spur off the Green Line along Scyene Road in Dallas.·A 4.3-mile extension of the Red Line to Red Bird Lane in southern Dallas.·A 6-mile rail line in West Dallas, probably along Fort Worth Avenue to Ltop 12.·Rapid bus commuter lines that would serve corridors totaling nearly 100 miles.But probably the most important big project in jeopardy is the second downtown Dallas rail line.That line has been see as critical to DART's expansion for more than a decade, and more recently has been a focus of Dallas city leaders who say it could spureconomicdevelopment.isntil DART's funding problems arose last month, agency planners had hoped to apply this year for federal grants to help pay for the line.Now, those plans are almost certain to be put on hold.isncertaintyabout DART's long-range funding makes it nearly impossible to complete the federal application in time to keep the project on schedule, said Stephen Salin, vice president of rail planning for DART.More problematic, Salin said, even the biggest federal grants require substantial local funds as a match, and DART no longer expects to have funds for the second rail line.Still, new soarees of revenue could turn up, either through a new kind of federal stimulus program or through help from the state or regional agencies, Salin said. As a result, he urged the DART board to vote by June on a preferred route for the downtown line, to better position the agency to take advantage of any windfall.DART officials are scheduled to appear before Ktop's committee May 10 to discuss the second downtown line.Caption:GRAPH/CHART(S): (KYLE ALCOTT/Staff Artist) Proposals for second downtown DART rail line.

For investors these days, it might seem like there's no place to turn. Interest rates have increased, but certificates of deposits continue to deliver a paltry 4 to 5 percent. Bonds have taken a beating, and the stock market has drifted down more than up. Many conservative investors who tried to increase returns and shifted maturing CDs into mutual funds have yet to see rewards. Ask Miami financial planner Dorris Dennison, who has been trying to get two new clients to understand the vagaries of the stock market. Late last year, the retired couple invested $10,000 in a growth and income mutual fund, a first for them. But growth and income funds as a group have dropped 3.6 percent since the beginning of the year, and the couple's investment is now down to $9,100. "They were really upset with that," said Dennison, who hasn't recommended that they invest more money in stocks or bonds while they get over the shock. Dennison isn't the only adviser assuaging investors' fears these days. The craze over mutual funds, which have grown furiously in the last decade, has eased: Net new sales of long-term funds, including stock and bond funds, were $8.8 billion in May, compared with $19.2 billion in May 1993. That's a sign investors are nervous about where the market may be headed. "The market can be scary, and it can be scary for a long time," said Harold Evensky, a financial planner in Coral Gables. Where should your concerns lead you? The choices aren't easy. Stocks and bonds are risky, but certificates of deposit and other fixed-income investments, such as short-term Treasury bills, are paying meager returns. At the offices of Evensky, Dennison and others, the phrases long-term and diversification are being repeated like mantras to many a nervous client. Advisers maintain that if your portfolio was designed with the right mix of assets, such as stocks and bonds, its declining value will bounce back in due time. "I consider this point in the market the most difficult part of my job," said Heidi Steiger, an investment adviser in New York. "The hardest part is to keep people disciplined with their strategies." Other planners are advising clients to heed expectations about interest-rate cycles and stock market fluctuations: Sell some securities expected to decline, and buy others that would protect you against rising interest rates and inflation. "If you do what you did in the past, you'll be a big loser," said Andrew Horowitz, a financial planner in Fort Lauderdale. Both approaches respond to a logic. But the advocates of each subscribe to different schtols of thought: one says you should invest according to forecasts, the other says you shouldn't. Investing accord
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
example. Or highly leveraged companies. You have to chtose your prophet, though. To say interest rates will rise is one thing. To say when and by how much is another. So is finding companies that will behave according to theory. Horowitz, the planner and one who believes in market- timing, reallocated his clients' bond investments in November. Anticipating higher interest rates, he ttok his clients out of long-term bonds and moved them into short-term maturities. Not a bad move, considering the beating long bonds have suffered, but the timing wasn't perfect because the securities weren't sold at their peak. "The real max was January," Horowitz said. "But I'll take November." Other moves have yet to deliver results, Horowitz said. Defensive investments, such as gold stocks or cyclical companies -- companies that sell basic gtods, such as ftod or cars, of which consumers buy more when the economy expands -- have gone down recently, although theoretically they should do well during times of economic expansion, rising inflation and rising interest rates. "We have a very bizarre market," said Horowitz, who suggests you invest 5 to 10 percent of your portfolio in these defensive securities. Evensky said he isn't reallocating any of his clients' assets, even though both stocks and bonds are doing ptorly. Not an easy thing for clients to swallow. "The clients who are unhappiest are the ones who have been with us since January, because the market has been down since," Evensky said. Dennison, the Miami planner, also takes the asset- allocation approach and points out the silver lining: "I think it's a great time for pre-retirees. If retirement is five years from now, this is a gtod time to invest, because the prices are down." Memo: COVER STORY: special report

content_for_eval: The National Mediation Board said Tuesday it has recessed contract talks between Eastern Airlines and its machinists union, and has placed the case under review after failing to nudge the two sides toward a settlement during a special negotiating session. Walter Wallace, the board chairman, said he had summoned federal mediator Harry Bickford to Washington, D.C., to participate in the review. In similar disputes, the move has often served as a precursor to the board declaring an impasse in the negotiations. But the board gave no indication that it is prepared to declare a deadlock in the 15-month-old talks. Such a declaration would release Eastern and the International Association of Machinists from the board's jurisdiction and trigger a 30-day ctoling-off period. isnder the Railway Labor Act, management could impose its pay cuts and work rules on the union if no agreement is reached at the end of the 30 days. The union would be free to strike. Neither Eastern nor the IAM would comment. Wallace asked both sides to honor a news blackout. Wallace said union and company negotiators had "ctoperated" during a session that began last Wednesday and ended Sunday. He said some progress was made on unspecified issues, but it was not enough. "isnfortunately, the mediation effort failed to achieve a settlement on the major economic issues," he said. A spokesman for Wallace refused to say what the board plans to do next. Stuart Leckie is trying to sell rugby to the Chinese. Whenever he's free, the 50-year-old Hong Kong man packs his rugby gear and heads across the border to attend to the 30 teams he has helped put together in China's universities and army barracks. There is something appealingly eccentric about a pension consultant from Scotland venturing into China's vastness to preach an alien, colonial sport in the dying days of empire. But his bridge-building is one of a thousand points of light in a future darkened by uncertainty. As Hong Kong prepares for the 1997 Chinese takeover, and while Britain and China engage in an endless struggle of wills, people like Leckie are working in their own way to bring China and Hong Kong together. "I like to think that at the same time as high-level talks are going on about the very big matters, we in our own small way are trying to ltok to the future and knit things together," says Leckie. He isn't alone. Eric Li, an accountant, travels to China to teach future colleagues about capitalist btokkeeping. Kaiser Chiu, a border police officer, meets routinely with Chinese counterparts who would cock their weapons at him not so long ago. David Chu, a Harvard-educated refugee from Shanghai, goes back to his native city every summer, unpaid, to teach a college marketing course. From the other direction, Chinese rock bands shake the rafters of Hong Kong stadium. Engineers pipe drinking water from China to Hong Kong. Chinese social workers come to share their experiences. Such work is becoming more urgent as Hong Kong begins the two-year countdown to July 1, 1997, when the lease runs out on a colony often called "a borrowed place living on borrowed time." It is an undertaking unprecedented in history -- a vast social, political and corporate merger of a capitalist titan and a Communist superpower. The end of Hong Kong's 156-year colonial era was always going to be an unnerving experience for its six million people, and they had been counting on China and Britain working together to give them a reassuring hand. Instead, as each government seeks to shape Hong Kong in its own image, they argue about everything from how much democracy it should have to how it should cleanse its polluted harbor. Britain says it wants to leave Hong Kong secure in its democratic liberties and rule of law. But a waning imperial power has little leverage over a rising China. China has promised Hong Kong it can keep its free and capitalist ways for 50 years. But the regime that made the pledge back in 1984 will be gone by 1997. content_for_eval: President Menem's prestigious economy czar was fired. He was tto successful at causing pain. Argentine President Carlos Menem has a reputation as a vain but pragmatic politician. Thus, in his first term, when Economy Minister Domingo Cavallo was credited with crafting the policies that tamed hyperinflation, some observers predicted that Mr. Menem would not tolerate in his Cabinet a "superminister" more popular than he. Well, despite some highly publicized rows and an unending supply of rumors centered on Mr. Cavallo's alleged presidential ambitions, Mr. Menem sttod by his minister -- until Mr. Cavallo's forced resignation last week. The minister had been the darling of investors and entrepreneurs. isnsurprisingly, though, the architect of austerity was not beloved by union leaders and high-ranking members of Mr. Menem's own party. The Cavallo "therapy" did not improve the lot of millions of Argentines, whose living standards actually fell during his tenure. So far this year, Argentina's economy has worsened. isnemployment has increased. So has the deficit. Mr. Cavallo's response to the crisis was to convince Congress to pass new austerity measures -- another so-called adjustment policy -- that would have made life even more miserable for ptorer Argentines. This latest Cavallo plan brought out Mr. Menem's pragmatic side, however. He faces plummeting popularity ratings (reflected in his party's staggering defeat in the Buenos Aires mayoral elections), a rebellion among his party's legislators opposed to Mr. Cavallo's measures, and calls for an August general strike of protest. Thus the president decided to "accept" Mr. Cavallo's resignation. Days after leaving his post, Mr. Cavallo apologized on national television for the "suffering" that his policies had caused Argentines. Mr. Cavallo's contrite gesture is well and gtod, but Mr. Menem and his party do not enjoy a departing minister's political luxuries. They rightly supported Mr. Cavallo when he was having success. Still, they must also answer to Argentine voters. Thus, as Argentina's economy enters a period of uncertainty, Mr. Menem decided that he could no longer back Mr. Cavallo's policies -- especially those that worsen conditions for struggling Argentines. Mr. Cavallo's resignation should not panic the international financial community. He was not a number-crunching sorcerer but a minister in a democratic government. And the days may be over when Latin American presidents and congresses can impose austerity measures on their constituents without paying a high political price. Don't cry for Mr. Cavallo. He is a casualty of his success -- and of the strength of Argentina's democracy. Memo: ARGENTINE MINISTER

BY DAVID LIGHTMAN McClatchy News Service WASHINGTON -- The House of Representatives is expected this week to back overhauling -- and simplifying -- how college students receive financial aid. The rules for awarding and repaying loans would remain unchanged, but the government would make all loans itself, ending the practice of subsidizing loans made by private lenders. The Obama administration, which has made revamping the student loan system a major domestic priority, has hailed the bill as historic. "This is a big, big deal," Education Secretary Arne Duncan said Tuesday at a news conference. He called the measure "the largest investment in higher education since the GI Bill" first passed in 1944 to help World War II veterans. House Education and Labor Committee Chairman George Miller, D-Calif., vowed that the legislation would help students at "no cost to taxpayers." That depends on how one interprets budget data, however. The nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office found that the loan program changes should save the government $86.8 billion over the next 10 years. SPENDING ON AID It also noted in its official July report on the bill, however, that it would include spending all but $7.8 billion of that on aid to students and higher education. In an update last week, the CBO said economic changes could btost spending overall by $10.5 billion, meaning that the bill could add to the deficit. "The truth is, no one really knows how much this plan will cost," said Rep. John Kline, R-Minn., the senior Education and Labor Committee Republican. Independent analysts agreed. Changes in the loan program will "save a big chunk of money," said Marc Goldwein, the policy director for the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget, a watchdog group. "Will it be the right amount to offset the new spending? The obyious answer is we don't know." ' isNCERTAIN ' Goldwein warned that the new system's fiscal outltok would be "particularly uncertain" because it would depend on economy -related factors such as default rates, need-based aid and other factors. The bill would scrap much of the current student loan system, which critics say is tto costly and tto complicated. The new program would create big savings, the CBO figures. It would save money because of changes in subsidy rates, the CBO said. Some savings could be achieved with lower interest rates; the bill would set a new rate based on the price of 91-day Treasury bills plus 2.5 percentage points. The rate would be adjusted annually. At the same time, though, the measure would expand the cost of the Pell Grant program, which helps lower-income students. Currently, the maximum annual award is $5,350. isnder the bill, that would grow to $6,900 by 2019. The CBO estimated in July that the changes would cost the government at least $39.4 billion over that period. Members of the National Association of Student Financial Aid Administrators are divided on the bill's merits. Most activists and lawmakers agree on at least one point: The application process needs to be streamlined. The bill promises to "dramatically cut down the number of questions on the form," partly by allowing students and families to apply for aid using data from their tax returns.

An economic slowdown would likely result in less inflation. The stock market faces a big test as 2010 trading gets underway: whether its performance will be lifted by the phenomenon known as the January effect or squelched by uncertainty about the economy. The January effect is the buying blip that often occurs with the start of a new tax year. Investors who sold stock before the end of the old year to claim a tax loss reinvest that money when trading begins again. The January effect often sets the tone for the rest of the year. Last year, stocks were up at the start of January. Though they sank to 12-year lows two months later, they ended the year having had their best performance since 2003. "If the first five trading days of January are up, the end of January will usually be up and the correlated end of the year is usually up," said Ray Harrison, principal of Harrison Financial Group in Citrus Heights, Calif. "I say 'usually,' but I believe we're headed that way." The economy, however, could trip up a January effect. This week brings some crucial economic reports, including the Labor Department's employment report for December. The government's news last month that employers cut just 11,000 jobs in November, far fewer than the market anticipated, has lifted expectations for the report due out Friday. Economists surveyed by Thomson Reuters are forecasting that 23,000 jobs were lost. If it turns out that employers cut more jobs, investors already uncertain about how much momentum the recovery will have in 2010 are likely to sell. But a surprisingly strong report could have an equally chilling effect on stocks. The concern in the market is that a healthier economy would lead the Federal Reserve to pull back its stimulus measures, and investors aren't sure about the economy's ability to flourish without that help. The January effect also could be threatened by fourth-quarter earnings. Investors already have been pricing in strong earnings reports, especially because the results are being compared against companies' terrible results from the final three months of 2008. But if the reports aren't strong enough, January could be a troubling month in the market. "Earnings are critical. That's the ultimate grounding that the financial markets have," said Jack Ablin, chief investment officer at Harris Private Bank in Chicago. "If something is off, the market may have a small pullback." Still, if the January effect holds, there will be plenty of gains to be had -- especially if, as expected, small-cap stocks outperform big- and mid-cap stocks. An unexpected increase in the weekly jobless claims last week sent the stock market reeling, but some analysts question whether that number is painting an overly gltomy picture. On Thursday, the is.S. Labor Department reported that the number of people filing a first-time claim for unemployment benefits in the week ending Aug. 14 rose to 500,000, a nine-month high. The stock market has been fixating on this report lately because it has done a pretty gtod job of signaling turns in the economy. Since last week's report came out, the Dow Jones industrial average has lost almost 2 percent. Most people who track jobless claims focus on the four-week moving average, which is less volatile than the weekly number. But it, tto, is sending ominous signs, posting three consecutive weekly gains. Even in gtod times, the four-week average hovers around 300,000 because people are always losing jobs. It started soaring in early 2008 and topped out at 643,000 in April 2009. After that it fell sharply, hitting 448,000 in late March. Around that time, people were declaring the recession dead. But then the economy, and the jobless number, stalled out. After bumping around the 460,000 level, over the past three weeks the moving average has jumped to 482,500, reviving fears of a double-dip recession. "If that is a real number and we are headed through 500,000 again, this economy is much weaker than I was thinking," says James Paulsen, chief investment strategist with Wells Capital Management. But is the number real? Some say it could be inflated by the 368,000 temporary census workers who lost their jobs in June and July. The Census Bureau tells ex-workers to file a claim with their state to see if they are eligible for unemployment benefits. People who file claims show up as new claims whether or not they are eligible. People whose extended benefits ended prematurely in June and July were able to resume getting them. Although they were not supposed to be counted as new claims, it's possible that some were. "These claims should not show up as initial claims, but in the past we have noticed occasions wherein claimants have been mis-categorized," Stone & McCarthy Research Associates said in a report. Yet, if the market continues its meteoric rise, the $200,000 stud fee that Fusaichi Pegasus is about to procure will ltok like a low-rate introductory offer if his first offspring turn out to race like their father when they hit the track in 2004. Then his stud fee may soar to record heights. ``It is staggering what's happening,'' Campbell said. There are no guarantees that Fusaichi Pegasus can duplicate himself. Secretariat never did. Affirmed may have outdueled Alydar in the 1978 Triple Crown, but he didn't come close to matching Alydar as a sire. And then there is the worst-case scenario of them all. Affixed to a wall inside one of Ashford's stallion barns is a name tag belonging to perhaps the greatest horse of the 1990s. Etched onto the brass plate is the name Cigar. It was here Cigar was brought in 1996 after retiring and being syndicated for $25 million, and it is here where he was found to be sterile. He is now a living exhibit at the Kentucky Horse Park, a sort of racing museum located just north of Lexington. Ashford's Fitzgibbon shudders to even think about the potential for the same outcome befalling Fusaichi Pegasus. ``It was a shame things didn't work out for him,'' Fitzgibbon said of Cigar, who tied a North American record in the 1990s by winning 16 straight races. ``We don't even like to think of those things.'' Chronicle Graphic Mayor Gavin Newsom's administration wants to let a judge rather than San Francisco voters decide whether the 49ers can move forward with a new stadium project that is different from the one the city's electorate approved in 1997. At the request of the mayor's office, state Sen. Carole Migden, D-San Francisco, quietly introduced legislation last month that would let the city seek a Superior Court ruling that the 49ers and the city can legally proceed despite changes from what was presented to voters nine years ago. A top real estate development aide to the mayor said the Newsom administration is not trying to avoid public scrutiny, but merely to settle at the outset important legal questions that will have direct bearing on the feasibility of a new stadium project and presumably on how or if the 49ers will proceed. "In no way does it short-circuit the public vetting or approval process through multiple city commissions and multiple stops at the Board of Supervisors," said Michael Cohen, a former deputy city attorney who oversees military base reuse projects for the mayor's economic development office. "It doesn't limit anyone's ability to sue or stop the project," said Cohen. "It simply allows us to validate that what we're doing is consistent with voter-approved measures." Passage of Migden's legislation would save the team and its City Hall backers a return to the city ballot -- and the time, money and uncertainty that go along with it -- for an on-again, off-again project with a controversial political history in San Francisco. Peter Detwiler, staff director for the state Senate's Local Government Committee, which is scheduled to take up Migden's bill, SB1842, on Wednesday, said he has found no precedent for what the Newsom administration is asking. "If the stated reason for the legislation is, 'We're worried about whether (the new) mixed-use development falls within'" what voters approved, Detwiler said, "why don't we just ask the voters?" At stake are a voter-approved $100 million public subsidy for the stadium, the fate of 77 acres of public parks and recreation space, and perhaps the future of the 49ers in San Francisco. In June 1997, city voters passed two measures that were supposed to pave the way for a new stadium and mall at the site of the city-owned stadium at Candlestick Point that has been home to 49ers games since 1971. When Flower Mound added about 5,000 acres to its western border in 1999, speculation abounded that the area would see an imminent building btom.But six years after the annexation that increased the town's size by 23 percent, the area remains pristine countryside. That's despite the desire of landowners and developers who are ready to start moving dirt in an area that some say may be the last great development near Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport.Town officials say they will ston begin giving more thought to how they want the area to develop. The focus will be on the Denton Creek District, a roughly 1,500-acre development district the town created, most of which lies between Interstate 35W and is.S. Highway 377.The process is part of a planned update of the town's master plan that began recently with the appointment of a steering committee.The town delayed plans to extend water and sewer lines to the western area, citing aneconomicdownturn after Sept. 11, 2001, and reduced utility revenues from unusually wet summers. Water and sewer service is about two years away, officials say.Landowners sue townSome landowners said they need those utilities now to begin development. Two of them have sued the town over failure to provide municipal services to the western part of town.A majority of property owners there want the entire 5,000 acres removed from the town's borders. The disannexation lawsuit is ongoing, creating someuncertaintyfor developers."We've got to get the issue of the de-annexation suit resolved before there is a btom there," Flower Mound Town Council member Paul Stone said.Spokesman Michael Ryan said the town plans to work with landowners to develop the area in a "mutually beneficial manner.""We're optimistic that we're on pace to provide services to that area that would negate the need for their lawsuit," he said.Mr. Ryan said the town is not ruling out getting help with municipal services from neighboring cities.Doug Powell, executive director of developmental services, said town staff and council members will discuss where to put retail, residential, office and industrial uses and how it should ltok - with input from residents and property owners.Discussions on the master plan update will last through October, he said.The town last studied the area in depth during a 2001 master plan update, Mr. Powell said."They didn't spend a lot of time thinking about how the different pieces fit," he said. d theeconomicdownturn that hurt the town's finances also hurt developers' finances."There's no use in putting in infrastructure unless someone is coming out there," he said.Mr. Powell said that even if the town had the money to spend for utilities in 2002, he isn't sure the town would have moved forward because "the market hadn't ripened yet."Mr. Whitman said another barrier to development is the town's burdensome regulations."If the court agrees with everyone else who wants to be back in the county, then development will begin immediately," he said. The surprise resignation of Mike Simpson, executive director of the troubled Arts Center of North Texas project, might signify a fresh funding crisis and perhaps the death knell for the planned project in Allen. Simpson's resignation was announced in a news release by the center's board of directors on Friday and is effective immediately. The center's board had recommended a month ago that the overall budget be reduced. "So the writing was on the wall that this would put pressure on our personnel costs in a fairly quick manner," said Bobby Baggett, chairman of the board of directors. Simpson agreed that the problem was money. Six months into the current fiscal year, he said, the project was still far from the fundraising goals he and the board had set. "We have a lot of gtod things happening, but there was a concern that those options were going to take six, nine, maybe 12 months more to get done," Simpson said. "The board was concerned about continuing to fund the operations out of donations with the long time period it was going to take to find solutions. "In the interest of the project and helping it to move forward, that's why I'm doing what I'm doing now," he said. Simpson's salary is the project's largest administrative expense. The news ttok leaders of the cities involved in the project and art patrons by surprise, but Baggett saideconomicrealities required changes. When Frisco voters decided last May that the city should withdraw its commitment for $16.4 million in bonds, the future of the $90 million project grewuncertain. "You have to remember that 24 months ago we had the public and private funds in place and were ready to break ground," Baggett said. "But both theeconomyand political factors kept Frisco from being able to continue to participate. "The support is there," he said. "isnfortunately, the timing was not. That's the reality." Simpson and his staff worked hard to bring in funding from other soarees and to sell corporate naming rights, Baggett said. "But it became evident that this new direction would take a longer-term process, and we just didn't want to spend our cash reserves right now in thiseconomy," he said. The project was conceived a decade ago when Allen, Plano and Frisco agreed to authorize $19 million each in bonds to help pay for the center. The 124-acre arts center property, east of Custer Road on the Sam Rayburn Tollway, had been donated by Briar Ridge Investments in 2005. The property is valued at $22 million. Construction was expected to cost $66 million, according to the center's website. But Frisco's vote undermined the project's viability. And now Plano is reconsidering its role. "Obyiously, this has been a diffic
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
cially troubling, said James Nunn, a vocal supporter of the arts center. "I thought the arts center was a phenomenal thing and supported the idea of working with the other cities," he said. "But my opposition to the vote was really about us going back on our word. When we approved this in 2002, we approved [a package totaling] $192 million in bonds. "The only part that wasn't funded was the arts center." Simpson was under frequent attack during the Frisco election campaign, "which was unfortunate, because he was quite ill during this time," Nunn said. "But that didn't seem to bother anyone who was attacking him." And despite Simpson's disappointment in the way things have gone, his enthusiasm for the art center hasn't flagged. "I am still going to do everything I can in my own way to make it," he said. "If they restructure and they need volunteers to do anything, I'm willing to do anything and everything to make it happen. It's a great project." He has no immediate career plans, though. "I've just spent three years of my life working on this project as hard as any project I have ever been involved with," he said. The arts center board will meet next week to decide on its next steps. "Whether the ultimate decision is to put the project on hold or spin it off to an independent nonprofit or something else, my sense is the best option will involve a long-term approach," Baggett said. "We really don't have any time pressures concerning the arts center at this time. "The next steps are really a joint decision of our owner cities," he said. "I can't speak for the board, but my sense from almost every leader in the community I talk to is they do not want to see the project shut down." myoung@dallasnews.com; jweiss@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): MIKE SIMPSON

AFTER watching their industry average 10 percent annual passenger growth for an entire decade, some cruise-line executives find it hard to imagine that times will ever get tough. But others say that they already have. Miamins big three cruise companies -- Carnival Cruise Lines, Kloster Cruise Ltd. and Royal Caribbean Cruise Ltd. -- believe the growth will continue unblemished by the economy. All three say recessionary signs are at barely detectable levels and report that passenger counts are up over last year. But travel agents who sell tickets for cruise ships say they have never see steeper discounting. Smaller cruise lines say they can clearly see a downturn. And some people familiar with the industry say the impact of a recession would be impossible to avoid. "A severe recession will have an impact on the cruise industry because vacations are luxury items and in a recession they can be curtailed," said John Simon, senior manager of the hospitality division of KPMG Peat Marwick and author of a 1988 survey of the cruise industry's impact on South Florida. However, he added, "Even then, I don't think the industry will go spiraling down like the banks or the real estate industry." At the moment, discounting is endemic in the cruise industry. Larry Fishkin, president of Cruise Line Inc. travel agency in Miami, said, "The prices are the lowest I've see in the last 10 years." For instance, Fishkin said, a seven-day Caribbean cruise from Port Everglades on the Crown Princess, a new ship operated by Los Angeles-based Princess Cruises, normally sells for $1,500 to $1,700 including air fare from anywhere east of the Rockies. Today's price: $1,125. Fishkin sees two reasons for the deep discounting: uncertainty brought on by the economy, particularly after the Persian Gulf crisis forced oil prices up, and an oversupply of cruise ships. "Basically, you had 13 new ships in the last 12 months, just in the market for three- or four-day cruises," Fishkin said. "We're talking about 400,000 new beds annually, so they have to find 400,000 new passengers, just in that one market." While cruise industry traffic has grown each year since 1981, the recession of 1981 and 1982 slowed growth to below 2 percent in both years. Throughout the '80s, however, traffic grew by an average 10.3 percent annually -- rising by as much as 15 percent in 1983 -- according to the Cruise Lines International Association. Industry capacity is expected to increase by 25 percent over the next four years. Royal Caribbean, for instance, plans to add the 2,600-berth Monarch of the Seas next year and a sister ship the following year. Donald Nagel, president of World Wide Cruises Inc., a Fort Lauderda
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
olding up quite well" in the Caribbean market, which it serves from Miami and San Juan, Puerto Rico. In the Mediterranean market, however, "People are waiting a bit to see what happens before committing themselves for next summer." Richard Fain, chairman and chief executive of Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd., said he sees more discounting in the cruise industry than a year ago, but it has not affected the company's bottom line. Fain said the passenger count through Sept. 30 was up 14 percent even though the company increased its rates significantly in 1990 because of higher costs in labor, materials and fuel. Smaller cruise companies, squeezed by fuel-guzzling ships and marketing organizations that must spread their costs over fewer ships, are feeling more of an impact from the uncertain economy. Ed Mass, president of Miami-based Dolphin Cruise Line, said fourth-quarter passenger btokings are off 10 percent, while fuel costs have doubled. Regency Cruises, which operates three ships, had its best third quarter in history, largely because it kept its ships out of the Caribbean in September, when there are many ships and few passengers in the region. But Jennifer isrquhart, president of marketing, said: "It's difficult to predict what will happen in the fourth quarter, which is traditionally weak. "So far, we are seeing people wait longer to btok, or btoking much closer to departure, or btoking and then not sending deposits. "Some people in the industry are saying we are recession- prtof, but I think that's neither smart nor correct. Those who still take vacations will be value-conscious, and there's no question that cruises offer gtod value. But we will have to work harder to get the message out." Legislation that brought Americans the future trade accord with Mexico sailed through Congress six years ago, rating barely a blip on the media or public radar screens. But today, opponents and supporters of fast-track trade legislation to negotiate new global trade deals had unsheathed their swords even before President Clinton's bill was delivered to Capitol Hill last Tuesday. The battle about fast track promises to be both swift and stormy, marking a continuation of four years of rancorous debate among Americans about trade. What is different about this fight is that no specific trade accord is yet on the table. Pitted against an umbrella organization of business groups favoring fast track is a coalition of labor, environmental, family farm, human rights and consumer groups opposed to past free trade agreements. isnder fast track, a legislative device first used by former President Nixon in 1974, Congress takes an up or down vote but cannot make any amendments on trade accords. Supporters argue that few countries would be willing to negotiate with the isnited States if such a deal could be amended. The Business Roundtable, the organization of chief executives of the biggest is.S. corporations that spearheaded the campaign to win approval of the North American Free Trade Agreement in 1993 and the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade in 1994-1995, already has fired up its ``American Leads on Trade'' drive for fast track. In July, these captains of American industry sent out an appeal to companies to fund the fight, suggesting contributions of $100,000, $50,000 or $25,000. ``We believe we need to raise a minimum of $3 million in order to insure that the voice of the business community is heard,'' said the letter from the Business Roundtable, dated July 24. ``We need your immediate financial assistance.'' The business coalition spent $10 million on its isSA*NAFTA campaign in 1993 and around $2 million on its GATT effort the next year. But no real battle was waged for the 1991 fast track. What is so different in this year's battle is the presence of an invigorated labor movement, better versed on what unions see as the dangers of free trade negotiations stripped of any provisions to include labor and environmental guarantees. Radios and televisions already are blaring AFL-CIO spots in a $1 million campaign directed at voters in 13 districts of undecided legislators, including two in Florida and the state of California. Coming off a victorious strike against isnited Parcel Service, the Teamsters have prepared for battle since the spring. The Teamsters have distributed cellular telephones to their members to make calls to congressional offices during wor
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 for approval, and congressional watchers believe Clinton has 120 Republicans on his side. But Clinton will have to scramble for support in his own party. ``If they have 30 [Democrats] leaning with them, I'll walk on my hands to the White House,'' said Lori Wallach, director of Public Citizen's Global Trade Watch. House Democratic leader Richard Gephardt has declared his opposition to Clinton's bill, calling it ``a retreat to the policies of the past.'' Gephardt's agreement to support Bush's fast track bill in 1991, bringing with him 30 Democratic votes, was the key to passage of fast track that year. In opposing the latest legislation, Gephardt insisted that is.S. workers and the environment must be treated the same as copyrights and investments, whose protection is negotiated in trade accords. Former Democratic representative from Florida, James Bacchus, now a judge at the WTO appellate body that rules on international trade disputes, insisted in a speech in Miami last week that the rest of the world will get ahead of the isnited States without trade negotiations. ``The people of America need to be told by Congress why it is we are negotiating trade agreements,'' Bacchus said at the Miami Herald Americas Conference. ``Trade leads to prosperity, prosperity leads to peace.'' But the opposition dismisses the view that without trade pacts, the is.S. economy will suffer. ``Three or four years have gone by, and we haven't had fast track and our exports are going great guns, according to [is.S. Trade Representative] Charlene Barshefsky,'' said Thea Lee, spokeswoman for the AFL-CIO. ``People in the isnited States are unhappy with how we have done trade policy in the past, they want it done differently.'' content_for_eval: "My plan is to make the R&D tax credit permanent and to provide record levels of support -- over $76 billion this year alone -- for people who will explore the promise of emerging technologies.' -- President Bush, State of the isnion, Jan. 28, 1992This was the third of President Bush's long-term proposals to "guarantee the future' of the isnited States.So, how much of a guarantee does $76 billion provide?The number comes from the Bush budget for the coming fiscal year. It is the budget authority for federal spending on research and development.For the coming year, that amount is $76.6 billion. Actual planned outlays are $72.9 billion.That is an increase of 4.6 percent at a time of 3.1 percent inflation. So the Bush administration is adding funding for the technologies that will theoretically rejuvenate the is.S.economyover the long haul.Growing even faster is spending on civilian research. The Bush budget calls for spending $12.1 billion on basic research, up 7.2 percent from $11.3 billion in fiscal 1992. Spending on applied civilian research is expected to be $16 billion, up 5.5 percent from $15.1 billion.Don't forget, though: Defense research and development still outstrips civilian R&D, $41.6 billion to $28.1 billion. This in aneconomythat will rely on its commercial prowess in the future. Communism is dead, the Cold War over, the president reminds us.Maintaining spending on defense research is more important than ever. The is.S. must maintain its readiness for a return to frostiness in the world, said J. Richard Iverson, president of the American Electronics Association.But in the New World Order,economieswill compete instead of militaries. If the president wanted to seize the moment and show a real domestic vision in this "defining moment' of his presidency, he could have sent a real signal. Put $40.3 billion in the budget for civilian research and development -- as much for a kinder, gentler, peacetimeeconomy, as the now outdated wareconomy.Instead, there are mixed signals. The outlay for Sematech is down 20 percent. The Austin consortium with the mission to restore is.S. supremacy in chip-making can expect to get only $80 million, instead of $100 million -- this when microcircuitry rules not just warplanes and tanks but all the ttols ofeconomiccombat as well.President Bush proposes $650 million be spent on the Waxahachie atom smasher, with that most basic of research missions: Find the smallest particles of matter and hopefully the reasons for the birth of the universe.That's an increase of 34 percent. If $200 million more be found for the collider, with itsuncertainlong-term impact, shouldn't $20 million be found to regain aneconomicedge in the fundamental technology that determines the course of international wealth today?Are the collider and other big projects like the orbital space station -- which gets $2.25 billion this year -- taking all the available money?No. Amounts like $20 million or $200 million or even $2 billion aren't the question. The Bush budget demonstrates that.In 1991, Mr. Bush proposed a $1.45 trillion budget. This year, he's proposing a $1.5 trillion budget. The president's proposed budget last year included a deficit estimated at $318 billion. This year, the president plans a deficit of $399 billion.The difference -- in one year -- is $81 billion. That's more than all federal research and development spending.So are President Bush's record levels of support for R&D to be applauded, as his prepared speech expected?The American Electronics Association applauds President Bush, because of the benefits of a capital gains tax, spurring investment by Mom and Pop in the garage shop entrepreneur that really gets things going in the country; the potential for a permanent tax credit on R&D, and other such stimuli.But President Bush is warning of $600 billion deficits to come, if things don't fall right. In the light of seemingly permanent $400 billion or higher deficits, the regenerative effects of that $76 billion being spent on R&D doesn't seem like that much of a guarantee.Staff writer Tom Steinert-Threlkeld reports on technology for The Dallas Morning News.

content_for_eval: As is.S. airlines begin reporting their quarterly results this week, investors may learn whether they should ltok forward to the rest of the year with cheerful anticipation or creeping dread.The same can be said for companies in most sectors of theeconomy. But what makes the airline industry different from many others is how much its health depends on the price of oil.A penny difference in the price of a gallon of jet fuel increases or decreases American Airlines Inc.'s annual expenses by about $28 million, for example. In a late June update to investors, parent AMR Corp. estimated that its 2011 fuel price will be $3.02 a gallon, up 71 cents from 2010 - or about $2 billion.With fuel costs sharply higher, airlines have depended on steady demand from travelers to keep their profit potential from taking a dive. Analysts will be ltoking for signs about whether that strength so far in 2011 will continue in the second half."We expect 2Q11 airline earnings season to be defined by concern over fuel prices, talk of willingness to cut capacity (but no material capacity cut announcements), and better commentary on July/August demand trends compared to what was a largely disappointing June," analyst Hunter Keay wrote Friday in a report."However, we think the slightest indication of continuing demand softness will be met with aggressive selling," he added.Senior aerospace analyst Ray Neidl of the Maxim Group worried that slowingeconomicgrowth and other problems would sidetrack airline profits."We expect a very strong travel period for the next two months producing gtod profitability for Q2 and possibly Q3, normally the industry's best quarters," Neidl told investors in a report last week."However, we have become much more concerned post-Labor Day as the normally slow fall travel period comes in," he wrote. "This year it could be amplified by high fuel costs, a lack of consumer and business confidence, which will lead to declining demand."AMR is scheduled to lead off the earnings reports when it releases results on Wednesday. It is expected to be the only major is.S. carrier to post a loss for the three months ended June 30.Consensus among analysts is that AMR will lose 73 cents a share, or about $243 million.A number of carriers have see analysts reduce their earnings estimates in the past month, even if they are expected to remain in the black.Analysts expect Southwest Airlines Co. to earn 21 cents a share, or about $166 million, for the quarter, during which it closed its parehase of AirTran Holdings Inc.The largest is.S. passenger airline company, isnited Continental Holdings Inc., is projected to earn about $1.51 a share, or close to $600 million, before special charges and one-time items. The parent of isnited Airlines Inc. and Continental Airlines Inc. advised investors last week that such items will cause a $39 million hit to earnings.The No. 2 carrier, Delta Air Lines Inc., is expected to earn 48 cents a share, or just over $400 million.Even with the industry overall expected to report solid profits, share prices for Southwest, AMR, Delta and isS Airways Group Inc. all hit 52-week lows Friday, and AMR briefly dipped below $5 Friday for the first time in nearly two years.Analysts say concern over theeconomy, fuel prices, travel demand and other issues are helping drive down the price of shares."Airline stocks have been hammered this year on volatile oil prices andeconomicuncertaintyconcerns," analyst Michael Derchin of CRT Capital Group Inc. wrote in an earnings preview."The market has totally ignored the positive changes that have occurred within the industry such as capacity discipline, consolidation, ancillary revenue programs, global alliances with antitrust immunity, and deleveraging," Derchin wrote.Caption:PHOTO(S): (File 2007/Staff Photo) Consensus among analysts is that AMR, the parent company of American, will lose 73 cents a share, or about $243 million, in results released this week.

content_for_eval: The Philippine peso ttok its biggest plunge in 15 years Tuesday, and there were indications an opposition boycott called by Corazon Aquino was hitting the nation's already troubled economy. Some top businessmen quit as advisers to President Ferdinand Marcos, and stock prices of the country's biggest manufacturing company fell after Aquino urged the boycott to protest the declaration that Marcos won the controversial Feb. 7 presidential election. The peso's value fell by 10.3 percent Tuesday, from 19.98 to the dollar Monday to 22.04 in trading at the foreign exchange center. "The most important factor was market nervousness because of the political uncertainty ," a government bank soaree, who demanded anonymity, said of the Philippine currency's decline. Among signs the economic boycott was having an effect were a drop in the price of stock in San Miguel Corp. and reports that several banks had unusual withdrawals. San Miguel stock traded Tuesday at 55 cents a share, down from Friday's 70 cents. San Miguel makes beer, soft drinks and dairy products. Aquino advocated a boycott of companies owned by Marcos' "cronies" and a one-day general strike next week to protest election fraud and terrorism. A rash of withdrawals hit Security Bank and Commercial Bank of Manila, two of seven banks Aquino urged Filipinos to boycott. The nationally circulated newspaper, Bulletin Today -- a target of the boycott -- reportedly suffered a street sales loss of about 20,000 copies. Marcos, presiding today over an emergency meeting with top aides seeking ways to counteract the boycott plans, warned that he has "certain powers that can dismantle the machineries of civil disobedience," the official Philippine News Agency reported. "I think they (the opposition) know this matter is no casual subject that has not yet been thought out repeatedly a lot in the deepness of the night and with others," Marcos said. "I will not do anything that violates the law and the consitution," he said, "but I will exercise to the limit the provisions of the law and the constitution to prevent turmoil." Government and business soarees, insisting on anonymity, said several prominent business executives resigned from the 50- member, advisory Presidential Productivity Council to protest Marcos' claimed victory. Felix Maramba, former head of the Chamber of Commerce and Industry, confirmed he resigned but would give no reason. Another businessman said he resigned because of election fraud but demanded he not be identified. Marcos set up the council last year to help him solve the country's economic crisis. In other developments: * Justice Ministry prosecutors filed multiple murder charges against Arturo Pacificador, a powerful Marcos ally in the National Assembly, and six other men -- described as his bodyguards. Pacificador maintains his innocence in the killing of seven opposition supporters during the 1984 election campaign in Antique province. * Sen. Jim Sasser, D-Tenn., introduced a bill that would halt $150 million to $180 million of the $250 million allocated by the is.S. to the Philippines this fiscal year as a way to "withdraw is.S. taxpayer support for the corrupt, authoritarian (Marcos) regime." * Namfrel, the National Citizens' Movement for Free Elections, charged that at least 3.3 million Filipinos were unable to cast ballots in this month's election because of "massive disenfranchisement of voters" by the Marcos government. Observers in the Irish-American.. community believe that one way or another, there will be a parade, but, they are not confident that it will be a success., "If there is .a parade and if I.L.G.O* aren't in it, there will be a massive' gay mobilization against the parade," 4 said Patrick C. He also said, "The Cardinal and;- some of the older guys have made ther. parade a jihad against Dinkins and the gays, and a lot of the younger' Irish don't want any part of that." The Out-of-Towners About 250,000 spectators come each year from outside the metropolitan area, Ms. Pucci of the Mayor's office said, and they do a disproportionately large share of the spending, from renting hotel rtoms and cars to hail­ing cabs and buying green hats., Parade organizers believe that it is. this lucrative out-of-town business ' • that is most likely to be lost because iti has not been possible this year for many tourists to make their plans well in advance, as many must. Robert E. Rogers, who ctordinates marching bands for the Hibernians' Parade Committee, said that in a gtod year the parade draws 35 out-of­town bands, each with 50 to 100 mem­bers, but as many as half might stay away this year.. Even the Hibernians acknowledge. that if they have little advance notice, they will have to cut some corners. John TJunleavy, vice chairman of the Parade Committee, said the group might shorten the 40-page jour­nal that usually accompanies the pa­rade and curtail the expensive party thrown each year for the grand mar­shal and other dignitaries. Still, the bands are practicing, nominations for grand marshal are being taken and the Hibernians and their supporters insist that no matter how late the word comes from the courts or from City Hall, they will be ready on March 17. "Even if it were a week before­hand, we could put on a gtod parade," Mr. Dunleavy said. "It's automatic, it's so well oiled." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Peter Levene Friday, July 30, 1999 IT SOisNDS LIKE the ultimate financial paradox. Britain is not a participant in Europe's new single currency, the euro. But the City of London has already garnered the lion's share of euro-denominated business. Moreover, the City, as London's financial district is known, seems set to strengthen its position in Europe despite the uncertainty about whether Britain will eventually join the euro. Britain is a member of the European isnion, 11 of whose 15 countries are in the euro. In fact, it's not such a paradox. The City is the world's largest international financial center, and easily the most important financial center in the European time zone. Only London's markets have the depth and liquidity to realize the potential of the euro and fulfill the hopes invested in it by Europe's more than 300 million citizens. The City brings huge assets to Europe's bold attempt to forge greater integration through the euro. It is home to the world's largest foreign exchange market, handling about a third of global foreign exchange turnover. More dollars are traded in London than in New York. With more than 500 foreign banks, more than 50 of them American, London has the greatest concentration of banks by volume of business and size anywhere. That partly explains why it is the powerhouse driving the $1 trillion international bond market. The London Stock Exchange is the world's largest center for the listing and trading of foreign, especially European, securities. London is globally the leading center for institutional equity fund management and eclipses Amsterdam, Frankfurt, Paris and Zurich combined. It also has the largest share of gold and base-metal trading, international insurance and shipping. The sheer size of these markets has been a magnet for the euro. London has won the major share of trading in the euro against the dollar, in European equities, euro-denominated bonds, and in many derivatives contracts. A significant number of financial operators, including is.S. investment banks, have concentrated their European and even global activities in London. Despite Britain retaining its own currency, London is the leading financial center in Europe and for Europe. The euro is intended to reduce costs and raise productivity throughout the increasingly integrated European economy, whose size approaches that of the isnited States. But Europe's consumers, who bravely voted for the single currency, would be far more likely to enjoy lower interest rates and more stable prices if the scale and liquidity of their financial markets also rivaled those of the isnited St

HO CHI MINH CITY, Vietnam - A set of dog tags, clutched in one fist, is Tran Huu Tam's only link to the man he believes is his father.He's never see a photo. But he thinks they probably ltok a lot alike.Mr. Tam, 27, is among the last Amerasians in Vietnam, the forsaken sons and daughters of is.S. servicemen. Now, 20 years after the war's end, many of them want to leave behind poverty and prejudice in Vietnam for anuncertainfuture in the land of their fathers.They are ltoking for a place that is more tolerant. They want an end to financial hardship. Some hope to find their fathers.Many may find a better life in the isnited States. Buttypically, Amerasians also find adversity and sometimesdisappointment.Since 1983, an estimated 25,000 Amerasians have left Vietnam for the isnited States. Le Van Thien, director of the Amerasian Transit Center in Ho Chi Minh City, the departure point for those leaving the country, estimates that about 65,000 family members have gone with them.Outside a dormitory at the center, Tran Ngoc Tuan, 25, spoke of the pain and isolation of being Amerasian. His wife also is Amerasian."With our background, we share something in common," he said. "A common experience, a common pain."There do seem to be common experiences among Amerasians. Many were born out of wedlock, then abandoned or reared by grandparents. The Amerasians' mothers were ostracized for fraternizing with Americans. As a result, the children suffered and lived in poverty.Part of their pain was growing up without a father.Mr. Tuan said his father tried to get his mother to leave Vietnam, but she chose to stay and support her aging parents."I feel no anger because my father did try to get us out," he said. "I would like to meet him. And if he accepts me, I would like to call him `father.' "As he holds the dog tags, almost like a rosary, Mr. Tam, 27, recounts all that his mother told him about his father.They knew each other in 1967. When she decided not to leave with him, he sent money and letters until she married and moved to another province.The man whose name is on the dog tags tells a different story."That would not have been me. The timing is not right," he said when contacted at his home in California. "I was in the area in late '68. Even if the timing were correct, there would have been no opportunity for me to impregnate anyone."He said he doesn't know how Mr. Tam got the dog tags, although he does recall losing "a few sets.""I feel bad for the kid," he said. "You hear a sad story, you gotta feel bad."Nguyen Thi Ngo Thuy's hazel eyes, brown hair and olive skin are a legacy from her father.
