HOisSTON, Feb. 13 — The chief executive of Exxon Mobil, Rex W. Tillerson, warned Tuesday that governments should not rush into policies that could damage the global economy in order to limit carbon emissions. Rex W. Tillerson, Exxon Mobil's chief executive, said in Houston yesterday that oil would remain a chief soaree of energy for years, but that strategies to address the risks of global warming were prudent. In a speech at a major industry gathering, Mr. Tillerson acknowledged that the planet was warming while carbon dioxide levels were increasing, suggesting a more accommodating position than the hard-nosed stance Exxon had held. But in the same speech, Mr. Tillerson, who leads the world's largest publicly traded company, gave an unalloyed defense of the oil industry and predicted that hydrocarbons would dominate the world's transportation as energy demand grows by an expected 40 percent by 2030. There is no significant alternative to oil in coming decades and Exxon will continue to make oil and natural gas its primary products, he said. "The scale advantages of oil and natural gas across a broad array of applications provide economic value unmatched by any alternative," he said. Mr. Tillerson's tone, if not the substance of his remarks, at times broke with the rigid positions of his predecessor, Lee Raymond, who has long been considered a skeptic in the global warming debate. "That's Exxon-speak," said Barbara Shtok, an analyst for Energy Intelligence Group, a publishing and information services company. "It's their own dialect, but if you ltok at where Exxon was one and a half years ago they may not have shifted their position 180 degrees, but they are moving." Mr. Tillerson's remarks were his first formal and extensive comments since the publication of a report two weeks ago by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, a leading panel of international scientists and reviewers, concluding that there is 90 percent certainty that human activity is decisive in changing the global climate. Mr. Tillerson told reporters that he had not read the report but said, "My understanding is there's not a clear 100 percent conclusion drawn." He added: "Nobody can conclusively 100 percent know how this is going to play out. I think that's important." Taken together, his statements suggested that Exxon was navigating between positions defending oil as an energy soaree and its core business, and showing sensitivity to growing public concerns about a warming climate. President Bush has called for increasing development and use of biofuels. And Democrats now controlling Congress have said they favor higher taxes on oil companies to pay for research in alternativ
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
a centuries-long kind of problem, and we are going to learn more as we go," he said. He noted that some scientific reports describing models of climate change concluded with a "range of potential outcomes." He added: "A range implies a certain degree of uncertainty. Policy decisions need to accommodate that uncertainty." Exxon, which has had record earnings the last two years, has been a target of environmental groups for financing organizations that have questioned climate science and for its business activities around the world. Much of the emissions of greenhouse gases blamed for global warming come from the burning of fossil fuels like gasoline and natural gas. Mr. Tillerson said his company supported breakthrough research in cellulosic ethanol, which is made from grasses and wtodchips, and he was clear about Exxon's role. "There is really nothing I see Exxon can bring to this," he said. "We don't see a direct role for ourselves." The report by the panel on climate change was widely considered the most authoritative study on the subject. It said for the first time that global warming was unequivocal and concluded that human activity had very likely caused most of the rise in temperatures since 1950. The report said that global warming could be blunted by prompt action. Mr. Tillerson defended the energy industry as being environmentally responsible. "The ftotprint required of our activities is getting smaller and more efficient," he said, "proving that energy production and environmental protection need not be a zero-sum." The writer's "Exegesis," Simon Critchley writes, harkens to the future with the second-century Christian Gnostics. Chronicle photo by Paul Chinn Symbol Ltokup The California recall election and the uncertainty over who will take charge in Sacramento are worrying analysts responsible for evaluating the state's financial health and its ability to repay its debts. Battered by the economic downturn, the state's $38 billion budget deficit and the political uncertainty surrounding the future of Gov. Gray Davis, California's credit rating has plunged dramatically over the past two years and now has the lowest rating of any state. Analysts worry that the Oct. 7 vote could lead to more instability, especially as candidates with little or no government experience -- including actor-turned-politician Arnold Schwarzenegger -- make their bid to replace Davis. "The greatest fear," said Claire Cohen, vice chairman of public finance at Fitch Ratings, "is that there would be governmental chaos with a new person stepping into what is a difficult financial situation." Fitch, Standard & Ptor's and Mtody's Investors Service have downgraded California's bond ratings since the downturn began. The most recent downgrade came Monday, when Mtody's dropped the state's ratings from A2 to A3, just four notches above junk bond status, said senior credit officer Ray Murphy. California was also put on a watch list for a possible downgrade, he added. Standard & Ptor's dropped the state's rating two weeks ago to BBB stable from A-, two notches above junk bond status, while Fitch downgraded California in December from AA- to A-, five notches from junk status. Each firm uses a different rating system. Lower bond ratings make it less attractive to investors and more expensive for the state to borrow money. While Davis' critics have blamed him for mismanaging the economy, state Treasurer Phil Angelides said the recall effort has made "a bad situation worse." "I don't think there's any doubt that the recall has been part of the mix that brought down the state's credit rating," he said in an interview Friday. "These credit ratings aren't a reflection of our economy but rather of the political paralysis, and the recall is another manifestation of the political paralysis." Analysts agree that if Davis gets recalled, whoever replaces him will face enormous financial problems. "No matter who's governor, he's going to have to face a divided Legislature," said David Hitchcock, a director of Standard & Ptor's local government group.

The isnited States is due for an influenza pandemic that could sicken millions of people, kill hundreds of thousands and disrupt theeconomy, federal officials warned Thursday in unveiling a new plan to prepare for that grim scenario."This plan will serve as our road map on how we as a nation, and as a member of the global health community, respond to the next pandemic influenza outbreak," Health and Human Services Secretary Tommy Thompson said in outlining the plan."Our proposed strategy draws upon the wealth of experience and knowledge we have gained in responding to a number of recent public health threats, including SARS and avian influenza," he said.In a normal flu season, 114,000 Americans are hospitalized with severe symptoms, and about 36,000 people die. Last year's flu outbreak was worse because it struck in October, a month earlier than usual, and sickened more children under 2, who until this year were not recommended to be vaccinated. Shortages of the flu vaccine exacerbated the problem.That memory and theuncertaintyabout the coming flu season prompted Dallas County health officials on Thursday to announce that flu vaccinations would be available, starting Monday, for county residents 65 and older - the most vulnerable part of the population.It is the earliest flu vaccination program in recent memory in the county. The state sent 3,700 doses of the vaccine for early distribution."isnfortunately, there's no way to predict how bad any given year is going to be," said Dr. David Buhner, medical director of the county's Department of Health and Human Services. "If the public does a better job of taking care of themselves, we'll have a milder season because this is a preventable disease."This year's flu vaccine protects against the New Caledonia and Fujian influenza A strains and the B Shanghai strain. The strains are named after places where they were discovered.However, one of the vaccine's leading manufacturers announced Thursday that about half of the nation's supply appears to be tainted, and about 50 million doses cannot be shipped as planned.Although flu strikes every year, usually in the winter in the isnited States, pandemics occur when a new flu strain shows up without advance preparation, typically arriving faster than a vaccine can be developed."We know we're going to have a pandemic because, historically, we're overdue for one," said Neil Pascoe, epidemiologist in the infectious disease division of the Texas Department of Health. "When it happens, it's going to be huge. It will be global, and everyone is going to be affected."Three pandemics occurred in the 20th century. The most severe, in 1918, killed half a million Americans and 20

to slow the spread.The proposed plan, unveiled on the Health and Human Services Web site (www.hhs.gov), calls for closely monitoring influenza strains as they appear in the isnited States. Laboratories would adopt more sophisticated techniques to identify unusual flu strains.When a pandemic has been identified, the government would accelerate the development of a new vaccine and antiviral medication that could stop the spread.Dr. Buhner, who had not yet read the voluminous federal flu plan, said its instructions probably would be comparable to preparing for a bioterrorism attack."We've already developed our capacity to respond to massive disease outbreaks, which is what a pandemic of influenza flu virus would be," he said.Although health workers are not required to report cases of influenza, Dallas County relies on a network of physicians, schtol nurses and hospital workers to track the illness' spread every winter. Samples of the virus are sent to local labs to determine which strains are active."There's always more than one strain circulating," Dr. Buhner said. "You have to hope you get the strain that's covered by the vaccine."For the last five flu seasons, public health officials around the world have been focused on the spread of a highly lethal avian flu strain in Southeast Asia, particularly in Thailand and Vietnam. There have been relatively few human cases - 34 known infections and 23 deaths - and the disease does not appear to be spreading from person to person."If it spreads to the human population and becomes as transmissible as the current flu strains, it could be terribly fatal," Mr. Pascoe said. "Imagine 4 million Texans are infected, and 20 percent of them die."E-mail sjacobson@dallasnews.comCaption:CHART(S): LIMITED FLis VACCINATIONS

content_for_eval: In the aftermath of Sunday's vote by the French people in favor of the Maastricht blueprint for eventual European monetary union, European Commission President Jacques Delors succinctly gauged the outcome of the vote: "I think there is as much to learn from the reason why 49 percent voted no as to why 51 percent voted yes.' Three months after Danish voters said no to the treaty, the future of Maastricht remains shrouded inuncertainty.Perhaps the only thing that can be said with confidence is that Europe's political leaders are far more enthusiastic about Maastricht than the voters. Specifically, the pact would create not only a single European currency by the end of the century, it would unite Europe's foreign and defense policies. There is widespread fear that Maastricht means an erosion of national sovereignty, and so it does. Yet, with the reunification of Germany, a nation destined to be a global powerhouse again, Maastricht is see by other observers as a healthy mechanism to hold dangerous nationalism in check.French President Francois Mitterrand and German Chancellor Helmut Kohl, the principal architects of a more unified European Community, immediately proceeded to put a positive spin on the narrow ratification. In truth, a narrow victory is better than a loss. Had the French rejected the treaty, it was widely understtod that Mr. Mitterrand would have resigned. Interestingly enough, in spite of Mr. Kohl's wholehearted support for Maastricht, the German people appear to be no more enthusiastic about the idea than the French, which only adds to theuncertainty.For the moment, monetary and financialuncertaintyin Europe appears to be having a contradictory impact on the isnited States. The recent rise in the dollar means is.S. exports are more expensive and therefore less attractive to European consumers. The French vote on Maastricht should be gtod news for European bond markets, however, and is.S. markets should benefit as well. The nearly simultaneous lowering of interest rates in Germany and the isnited Kingdom should stimulateeconomicactiv ity in those countries and help offset the adverse trade effects of a stronger dollar.What happens now? Following the French vote, Prime Minister John Major of Great Britain called for a summit meeting of European Community leaders in order to discuss the future of the Maastricht pact. He already has said he will not submit the treaty to Parliament until the Danish rejection of the treaty is resolved, something that will probably be done by presenting a revised version of Maastricht to Danish voters in a second referendum. It would appear that the message emanating from Europe in some ways mirrors concerns over the North American free-trade agreement on this side of the Atlantic: The people want closer integration with their neighbors, but demand that it be done cautiously and deliberately, rather than ramrodding it through as their leaders would prefer.

On the other hand, companies are increasingly relying on transfers to meet strategic goals such as improving productivity or cutting the size of the corporation. ``The flattening of company structures has forced some workers to accept transfers merely to stay employed, and greatly reduced promotional opportunities for others,'' said the study. ``Couples who have been through one or more relocations in which they not only made no economic gains but actually lost ground are feeling frustrated, cautious and skeptical about moving again,'' it said. Paul Wesman, a spokesman for Philadelphia-based Right Management, said ``some companies are acutely aware of this as a problem, and are beginning to deal with it. Others are beginning to feel the pain.'' The study surveyed more than 3,200 employees or their spouses at 101 companies in the isnited States, Canada and Europe. Among its findings were that 23 percent of those surveyed reported a decline in living standards after their moves. The number rose to 42 percent among two-career couples, who often reported difficulties in finding a job for the ``trailing spouse.'' On average, it ttok five to six months for the spouse to find a job, often with a pay cut, Wesman said. The study said dual-income families were consistently less favorable toward moving than single-income families, while their representation in the work force has increased rapidly. The share of two-income couples rose from 47 percent in 1989 to 59 percent in 1992, according to government data cited by the survey. It found 65 percent of those questioned were in two-income families. Besides the difficulties with spouses, 39 percent of those surveyed said they did not view their transfers as promotions. ``When you start removing rungs from the corporate ladder, you end up with fewer promotional opportunities, and more lateral movement,'' Wesman said. Flights in most of Europe remain grounded. (04-18) 04:00 PDT London -- The miasma of volcanic ash hovering over Europe showed no sign of dissipating Saturday, keeping thousands of forlorn travelers stranded across the continent for a third day and worsening economic losses. The cloud of grit from the still-erupting Eyjafjallajokull volcano in Iceland began creeping as far south as Italy, forcing authorities to shut down airports in the northern part of the country. Travelers anchored to Earth continued their scramble for hotels, or for trains, ferries and even taxis to reach their desired destinations. In Copenhagen, an enterprising cab company posted fares for long hauls across the continent: about $2,000 for passengers going to Amsterdam; $6,000 all the way to Madrid. Most of British airspace remained closed, with planes grounded until 1 p.m. today at the earliest, despite a brief window of opportunity for a few flights Saturday afternton from airports in Scotland, Northern Ireland and northern England. By Saturday evening, the volcanic pall covered the whole country once again. In Germany and northern France, including Paris, authorities canceled all flights until early today. Most major airports throughout Scandinavia were idled, including Arlanda, Stockholm's largest, which warned on its Web site: "The forecast is now even more uncertain than before." With the pileup of martoned passengers growing by the hour, and with is.S. airlines canceling more than 80 percent of their flights to Europe on Saturday, analysts say that clearing out the backlog and getting the system back to normal could take days once the ash cloud dissipates. And no one was predicting when that would happen. "It's still erupting," Armann Hoskuldsson, a scientist at the isniversity of Iceland, said of the volcano. "It's more or less constant." It hasn't found a buyer for Pinnacle Ftods Corp., which it put up for sale in March. It had to postpone plans for a public offering of Britain's yellow pages when financial markets turned sour over Enron Corp. and Iraq.And all of this is, well, just fine with founder and chairman Tom Hicks, who says he likes the way his company is emerging from the darkest days in its 15-year history.In a beaten-down market, Hicks Muse may not be landing as many deals as it once did, but it approaches its business with a hardheaded, disciplined attitude, intent on avoiding million-dollar mistakes."The firm has never been run in a better way than it's being run today in terms of day-to-day operations," Mr. Hicks said.A gtod message, though, will only go so far. In the hot-box of private equity deal-making, nothing matters in the end but results."The message has gotten out there, and everyone takes them at their word," said David Toll, managing editor at Private Equity Analyst, an industry publication."The real question is how they're going to do in the investments they've done in the past few years. I don't think anyone knows that."Hicks Muse controls $11 billion in assets. If you think your house doesn't breathe, you are so very wrong. It's useful to consider the world from the house's point of view when you're making improvements. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle REVIEWED BY, CAREY HARRISON Sunday, December 3, 1995 By Pat Barker Dutton; 277 pages; $21.95 Barker's surprise winner, ``The Ghost Road,'' is the last of a trilogy of novels set during the first World War among a group of shell-shocked, traumatized British soldiers who are shipped out of France and hospitalized in Britain. The group includes the real-life soldier-poets Siegfried Sasston and Wilfred Owen, who are treated, as they were in reality, by the psychologist William Rivers. All three are brilliantly brought to life by Barker's unflinching eye for moral and sexual uncertainty. In addition, the central and fictional character Billy Prior is busily bisexual, which permits Barker to turn her highly accomplished novel-sequence as much on the issue of ambivalent male sexuality as on the barbaric early 20th century treatment of thewar-damaged psyche. Barker, a former historian, exposes the gruesome, sadistic cures that were applied to the shell-shocked of the 1914-18 war: In a typical example, soldiers rendered mute by the horrors of war were ``healed'' by having electric currents run through their bodies until they broke their silence. Such historical detail gives ``The Ghost Road'' and its two predecessors, ``Regeneration'' and ``The Eye In The Dtor,'' considerable documentary fascination, and has helped to kindle public interest in Barker's btoks. More remarkable as a literary achievement is Barker's instinct for the sexual currents and counter-currents created by wartime life, and her convincingly frank account of varying sexual encounters. In this respect Barker is never less than brisk and matter-of- fact. Barker's prose, while admirably unobtrusive, is what Jane Austen would have termed ``unexceptionable.'' ``The Ghost Road'' is a fine, singularly intelligent and deeply moving novel; at the same time, Barker's elevation to the Btoker will be disappointing to those who believe that the heavyweight championship of the British btok world should not reward literary accomplishment unless it is matched by an equivalent originality of voice. Journalistic hype to the contrary, ``The Ghost Road'' is not ground-breaking fiction, nor does it pretend to be. It is conventional novel-writing of the utmost deftness, psychologically acute, morally subtle and almost shockingly unshockable. When the novel opens, Barker's fictional hero Billy Prior is about to leave the hospital to return to the trenches alongside the poet Wilfred Owen, both of them only partly recovered from their earlier breakdowns. Rivers, their doctor, watches them return to war with distress, and a touch of envy, since the

tion is anywhere near as gtod as this. Carey Harrison's novels include `Richard's Feet,' `Cley' and `Egon.' Microsoft scores 3 percent profit gain. Sun reports lower earnings, smaller growth. JDS bids for Lucent unit. Symbol Ltokup Despite tepid sales of personal computers, software titan Microsoft bested Wall Street expectations yesterday and posted a 3 percent gain in profits for the third quarter. The results from the bellwether firm add fuel to recent speculation that the sluggish PC industry may have turned a corner. "We're definitely not out of the wtods yet, but I think the sentiment is starting to change," said Melissa Eisenstat, analyst at CIBC Oppenheimer. Microsoft, whose Windows family of operating systems powers about 90 percent of the world's PCs, said net income was $2.45 billion (44 cents per share) for the three months ended March 31. That topped the same period a year ago by 2.8 percent. During the third quarter of 2000, profit totaled $2.39 billion (43 cents). Analysts surveyed by First Call/Thomson Financial were anticipating earnings of 42 cents per share. Revenue increased to $6.46 billion for the quarter, up 14 percent from $5. 66 billion a year ago, due largely to increased demand for its Windows 2000 products, which are aimed at heavy-duty corporate users. "Results were strong across all businesses and came in a little better than we expected," said John Connors, Microsoft's chief financial officer. However, the world's largest softwaremaker remained cautious about the short-term health of the economy. Many information technology companies have reported a sharp slowdown in demand from both corporations and consumers. Sales of PCs have taken a particular hit, with the growth rate falling to the low single digits during the first three months of the year, Microsoft said. The Redmond, Wash., company expects PC sales to grow by 7 or 8 percent this year, lower than its original forecast of about 10 percent. "We're very mindful of the slowdown in growth rates in the economy (and) we're very focused on what the PC shipment volume will be because obyiously that's an important factor," Connors said. Because of the uncertainty, Microsoft reiterated its plan to trim costs in the next year. Though the company is not planning any layoffs, it is asking employees to cut back on discretionary spending. Southern Dallas County is finally beginning to develop after years of watching communities to the north enjoy theeconomicbenefits of a building btom.But the biggest challenge in the southern sector may be getting everyone to agree on how to handle the development.Commissioner John Wiley Price has led a regional effort to devise a master plan for the trade corridor in his district that runs along Interstate 45. Mr. Price said the area needs to attract high-quality development with adequate infrastructure that will provide the most jobs and biggest increase to the tax base.But some developers object to the plan, saying it will add unnecessary delays anduncertaintyas well as another layer of bureaucracy that will scare off investors with new regulations. The Allen Group, which owns 6,000 acres, is one of several developers who said they were left out of the planning process.Next month, commissioners will vote on whether to pull out of the planning process. At least two commissioners, Kenneth Mayfield and Maurine Dickey, expressed concerns Tuesday about how it has been handled."There are tto many questions that need to be answered," Mr. Mayfield said.Ms. Dickey said: "We need to start all over. This is a skewed process."The county's proposed comprehensive plan would identify the infrastructure needs, such as water, sewer and roads, and provide a framework for building them.The North Central Texas Council of Governments, the city of Dallas and the county are each expected to chip in $290,000 for the plan, which is expected to be competed by April 2010.Artis Johnson, the mayor of Hutchins, asked commissioners not to be a part of it.Tiny Lange, a Wilmer council member, told commissioners that her city feels the county is trying to tell officials how to run their city.Richard Allen, chief executive of the Allen Group, told commissioners that his company has spent the past three years and millions of dollars getting various development approvals from the two cities as well as others.Mr. Allen's company is helping develop an area known as an inland port because of a isnion Pacific rail terminal that moves overseas shipping containers by truck and train across the country.The intermodal rail terminal has begun attracting warehouses, distribution centers and industrial parks.Mr. Allen said he has "grave concerns" about a new governmental entity that might be formed to impose restrictions on development, such as new taxes."Essentially, we're doing over everything that's been done," he said about the master plan. ess.Mike Rader, a landowner and developer who helped bring isnion Pacific to the area, said the trade corridor needs $200 million worth of basic infrastructure such as water, sewer and roads in order to attract development.He said Wilmer cannot handle the development, and he encouraged commissioners to continue their efforts so that 25,000 acres along I-45 can get basic infrastructure.Rick Loessberg, the county's director of planning and development, said the comprehensive plan will not supersede local zoning regulations or result in a new governmental entity. Starts f' on multifamily projects increased 1 '* 10.9 percent from March, while sin­gle-family housing starts rose just 4 three-tenths of 1 percent. ■' Moreover, the number of applica­tions for construction permits fell. J" "This is really another sign of moderation in the economy,*' said r Eileen Neely, manager of economic '•< forecasting at Fannie Mae. "Single­family starts were a little bit weaker ' - than most analysts expected." c,'' Still, real estate markets have been bolstered by the highest levels ^ of consumer confidence in the econ­omy in decades, the lowest unem­ployment rate in almost a quarter- century and record gains in the stock ' market The isniversity of Michigan '' said today that its preliminary index ' of consumer sentiment for May ' ' climbed to 104.6 from 101.4 last 'J ■ month; the rise is the fourth consecu- ' tive monthly increase. 1 The gain in consumer confidence v suggested that the March decision by Federal Reserve policy makers to ' push up interest rates "hasn't dimin­ished household spirits," said Kevin Flanagan, money market economist at Dean Witter Reynolds. While that might give the Fed pause, other economists pointed to the housing numbers as an indication that growth has moderated. "When you ltok at the level of starts, we are right in the range that the Fed would like," said Mark Vitner, an econo­mist at the First isnion Corporation in Charlotte, N.C. "It doesn't ltok like home building is going to be an en­gine of growth." Taken together, today's economic reports added to the uncertainty about whether the policy makers will raise interest rates when they meet next week. Recent data have pro­vided a mixed picture of the pros­pects for an acceleration in inflation. The 2.6 percent increase in housing starts in April followed a decline in March, which was revised today to a percent drop from the 6.4 percent reported previously. Though construction this year may not beat 1996's level, which was the highest in eight years, starts remain strong because demand in parts of the country is exceeding the supply of new homes, analysts said. Additionally, the Midwest fltoding restrained starts in March and April, possibly setting the stage for the "mother of all rebounds in May or June," said Ian Shepherson, an econ­omist at HSBC Securities Inc. in New York. Permits for new construction will also rebound, he said. By region, housing starts rose 11.3 percent in the South and seven-tenths of 1 percent in the Midwest Starts fell 7.4 percent in the Northeast and percent in the West Ltoking ahead, home builders ex­pect the housing industry to hold its own, according to a survey by the National Association of Home Build­ers, although their optimism slipped a bit this month. The trade group's housing market index, released on Thursday, declined to 54 in May from 56 in April. "We are optimistic," said James Martell Jr., president and chief exec­utive of Fortress Group Inc., a home builder in Vienna, Va, that has re­ported a 70 percent increase in reve­nue for the first quarter. "Working through our backlog will result in strong closings and revenues in the second half of 1997." Relatively low mortgage rates are helping the market. Although the av­erage rate on a 30-year fixed mort­gage rose to 8.14 percent in April from 7.9 percent in March, rates are below last summer's levels.

Hundreds of companies, including those in technology, energy and consumer products industries, will issue reports. Yahto Inc., Sun Microsystems Inc. and Texas Instruments Inc. are among the tech firms reporting. Companies such as Procter & Gamble Co., Kimberly-Clark Corp. and Starbucks Corp. will give an indication of consumers' willingness to spend. With the New Year's celebrations past, we return to life as usual. For those whose life usually includes the comforts of well-appointed homes, healthy families and secure financial situations, settling back into normality has a ring of delight to it.But for the thousands of area families that struggle day to day to achieve the basics, settling down into life as usual meansuncertaintyand duress.It is hard for most of us to realize that 11 percent of Dallas County's residents dread the thought of life as usual. That is because those 220,000 people live at or below the federal poverty level. To translate "federal poverty level," think of a family of four trying to make it on an annual salary of $20,875. Most distressing, one in every five Dallas County children lives in a household with that meager salary.Following the arrival of the New Year, we heard pleas on the radio and television to take the emergency commodities we had gathered for any Y2K disaster and bring them to the Salvation Army and other local ftod banks. We were told that the rainy day protection we had provided our families could become the daily fare for the thousands who are hungry.Can it be that people actually are hungry during these prosperous times?Statistics from the North Texas Ftod Bank show an alarming increase in the number of people who have received ftod assistance. From 1995 to 1999, that number doubled.Dallas isn't alone in the disheartening increase. The Agriculture Department estimates that more than 2 million Texans have trouble affording ftod, and that includes more than 950,000 who suffer from outright hunger. For the first time ever, most emergency ftod banks in Texas report they are unable to keep up with the demand for ftod.How do we account for those numbers?It isn't because people aren't working. isnemployment is at an all-time low. Most of those who formerly were welfare recipients now are in the labor force. People who used to receive the $188 monthly welfare stipend from the state now are earning their own salaries. But for most of them, the jobs pay tto little and have tto few benefits to lift them out of poverty.The ftod stamp program should address the problem. But less than half of Texas' ptor who are eligible for ftod stamps are receiving them. In fact, 65 percent of the Dallas County children living at or below the poverty level aren't receiving ftod stampsWhat is keeping those who are eligible for ftod stamps from accessing them?One reason is the confusion surrounding current ftod stamp regulations. With the newly implemented welfare-to-work movement, families leaving welfare think they must give up ftod stamps, tto. But if their wages are so low that the
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ue of your car under any circumstances if you are living in your vehicle, if you are disabled or if you use your vehicle to transport a disabled person." Where is our sense of righteous indignation over such statements? Why do we need to mention the possibility that people would be using their car as their home? It is because enough people are living in such conditions to warrant such a mention.Another reason that keeps people from obtaining ftod stamps is the rigorous application process. Having tto few Texas Human Services Department employees results in unreasonably long lines. In Texas, recipients must appear in person to renew their benefits every three months. That is one workday every three months.As concerned neighbors to folks in need, we must urge our state senators and representatives to see that the regulations surrounding ftod stamps are adjusted so that the program is accessible. We all agree that public money must be carefully spent. Texas has eliminated the fraud that everyone fears in public programs. But have the strategies used to eliminate fraud placed undue restrictions on honest folks who are on hard times?We need to raise those questions. A Centerless Soviet Economy May Not Be So Bad, Western Experts Say: ... By PETER PASSELL New York Times (1923-Current file); Sep 5, 1991; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) Pg. A13 Soviet Turmoil: The Economic Puzzle economic order stalled until lawmakers approve Economic a transfer of power to the Analysis republics, the people are wondering whether they can live without it. Stalin's empire builders locked the republics in a hostile economic em­brace, pacifying the periphery with a fltod of cheap resoarees and capital. Now leaders face the formidable task of untangling the web without renting vital strands of commerce. In fact, Western experts say, the spe­cial problems of rebuilding the econ­omy without a strong center may be more apparent than real. They gen­erally agree that the maintenance of open trade among the republics is the key to keeping the economic machin­ery moving. But tight ctordination may be unnecessary — some analysts are even skeptical of the need for a common currency. And while they shudder at the prospect of the disloca­tions ahead, some even see opportunity in the Kremlin's calamity: less govern­ment, predicts John Van Oudenaren, a researcher at the Rand Corporation, could prove to be more. On first glance, the advantages of maintaining a tightly integrated Soviet economy seem decisive. None of the re­publics are close enough in kilometers or economic culture to make an easyleap into capitalist Europe. And none, save perhaps the Russian Federation, has an internal market large enough to support large-scale industry. Compounding the problem, the old planning bureaucracy worked tire­lessly to deny individual republics a sense of self-sufficiency, lest they were tempted to go their own way. Gigantic enterprises now produce more than 90 percent of the Soviet isnion's sewing machines, forklifts, diesel locomotives, freezers, washing machines and con­crete mixers. And most of the nation's heavy industry is concentrated in just three great industrial rust belts: the israls and the Kuznetsk basin in Rus­sia, along with the Donets Basin in the eastern iskraine. Development of the Soviet isnion's hinterlands has been dictated by the same grandiose vision. For example, Moscow long ago decided that arid isz­bekistan would supply cotton for the rest of the empire. Big Income differences Matthew Sagers, an economic geog­rapher at the Planecon consulting firm in Washington, estimates that 9 of the 15 republics ship 60 percent or more of their output to Soviet producers and consumers outside their borders. Only huge, geographically diverse Russia exports less than 20 percent. And that number is misleadingly small be
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
e that are profligate. In Yugoslavia, for example, the cen­tral bank's inclination to bail out bank­rupt enterprises and to cover the bal­ltoning budget deficits of the Serbian government damaged relatively healthy Slovenia. According to The Wall Street Journal, Estonia's prefer­ence for going its own way on currency is, in fact, premised on fear that the lit­tle Baltic economy will be blown away by inflation that has been building for years in the Soviet isnion. Mr. Van Oudenaren puts a similar spin on other questions of economic in­tegration. Some Soviet republics may be willing to move more rapidly than others to free markets, to sell off state enterprises or to open local industry to international competition. Some may be prepared to tax more than others in order to modernize or to protect the losers in the transition to capitalism. isncertainty About Rules In a closely integrated economy, however, they would have to find com­mon ground. And Mr. Van Oudenaren argues, "there is no advantage in pre­serving a union in which you can move only as fast as the slowest actor." Would Eastern Europe be better off, he asks, if Poland and Czechoslavia were forced to match the pace of economic reform of Bulgaria and Romania? Martin Weitzman, an economist at Harvard, thinks the focus on the ques­tion of how the Soviet economy will sur­vive without the Kremlin to keep it to­gether misses the real point. The great­est obstacle to Soviet economic re­covery, he argues, is uncertainty about the rules — who owns the property, who enforces contracts, who collects the taxes. "Ill-defined government," he says, "is a more serious problem than decentralized government." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

But American trtops continued to pour into the area while Washington worked on a plan to keep Iraqi forces in check. The Defense Department also announced it was putting 155,000 ground trtops on alert, an unusual public statement by the American military, which is not normally inclined to talk about potential deployments. The Administration has already said it plans to send 36,000 trtops to the Persian Gulf area. The new military moves came as Clinton Administration officials began to consult with allies on establishing a large demilitarized zone in southern Iraq to prevent the Government from massing its trtops along the Kuwait border in the future.

B ^ OR four decades, through btoms, i-g busts and bubbles, Silicon Valley B has maintained an amazingly in­ ** novative business environment. Companies and technologies rise and fall. Hot start-ups morph into giant corpo­rations. Cutting-edge products become mature commodities. Business models change. Through it all, the area remains creative and resilient — and more suc­cessful than other technology centers, no­tably the Route 128 area around Boston. What makes Silicon Valley special? Thanks to some new data, economists have finally been able to test statistically some popular explanations. In her influential 1994 btok "Regional Advantage: Culture and Competition in Silicon Valley and Route 128" (Harvard ' isniversity Press), AnnaLee Saxenian, an economic development scholar at the isni­versity of California, Berkeley, argued that Silicon Valley's innovative edge comes from two unusual characteristics. First, talented employees move easily and often to new employers, far more so than people elsewhere. "The joke is that you can change jobs and not change park­ing lots," one of her interview subjects said. Second, instead of vertically integrat­ing, Silicon Valley computer makers rely on networks of suppliers. They also de­sign open systems that can flexibly ac­commodate all sorts of new components. "The system's decentralization encour­ages the pursuit of multiple technical op­portunities through spontaneous regroup­ings of skill, technology land capital," she wrote. Many people, especially in Silicon Val­ley, found Professor Saxenian's argument convincing. But while her research was careful, it depended on interviews and had no large-scale statistical backing. Perhaps her subjects' impressions were unreliable. After all, the argument that Silicon Val­ley's job hopping fosters innovation con­tradicts economists' common assump­tions. "It didn't feel right to me," James B. Rebitzer, an economist at Case West­ern Reserve isniversity, said in an inter­view. When employees jump from company to company, they take their knowledge with them. "The innovation from one firm will tend to bleed over into other firms," Professor Rebitzer explained. For a given company, "it's hard to capture the re­turns on your innovation," he went on. "From an economics perspective, that should hamper innovation." He found a possible answer to the puz­zle in the work of two management schol­ars, Cariiss Y. Baldwin and Kim B. Clark. In their btok "Design Rules: The Power of Modularity" (MIT Press, 2000), they argued that when there is a lot of techno­logical uncertainty, the fastest way to find the best solution is to permit lots of inde­pendent experiments. That requires mod­ular d

But, they write, "living in Silicon Valley increases the rate of employer-to-employ- ' er job change by 0.8 percentage point." ; "This effect is both statistically and be- havioraliy significant — suggesting em- ployer-to-employer mobility rates are 40 percent higher than the sample average.", Computer industry employees in other ■ California technology clusters also seem to switch jobs more often than those in other states. This result supports an argu­ment made by Ronald J. Gilson, a law professor at Stanford and Columbia. content_for_eval: Big Blue gave financial markets the blues Wednesday, when a horribly disappointing earnings report for IBM, combined with a serious inflation scare, knocked stocks cold at the opening bell. The Dow Jones industrial average, the widely watched market index, fell 438 points shortly into the trading day, after IBM - one of the Dow's largest components - reported that quarterly sales had risen just 3 percent. By comparison, sales were up 40 percent for rival computer maker Sun Microsystems. The Dow later recovered, but IBM didn't. The Dow closed down 114.69 points, or just over 1 percent, at 9,975.02. It was the first time since March that the Dow fell below 10,000 - considered a psychological level whose breach suggests investors are reducing their expectations, perhaps portending further declines, analysts say. Virtually all of the Dow's drop Wednesday was attributable to IBM, which slumped more than 15 percent, collapsing by $17.56 a share to $95.44. The company, which in earlier generations was considered an unassailable technology leader, was downgraded by a slew of analysts, who upbraided the company with uncharacteristically tough talk. ``Should investors buy the stock now? No,'' said Toni Sacconaghi, who tracks IBM for Sanford C. Bernstein investment firm. ``There is tto much uncertainty .'' The Nasdaq Composite Index, like the Dow, ttok a roller coaster ride. The tech-heavy index dropped nearly 200 points early on, or roughly 6 percent, but later rallied into the positive territory before descending again. It closed the day at 3,172.97, for a loss of 40.99 points or 1.2 percent. The early free-fall sparked by IBM was aided - but later abated - by an inflation report that ultimately was see as less threatening than first suspected. The government reported that the consumer price index, or CPI, rose 0.5 percent last month. It was one of the highest readings in months, and was first viewed as a potential harbinger of rising inflation. Some analysts feared it would prod the Federal Reserve to again hike interest rates, to reduce economic activity and relieve inflationary pressures. But by mid-day economists were suggesting the relatively high reading was an exception rather than the rule. ``Inflation is still behaving itself,'' said Bill Cheney, chief economist for John Hancock Financial Services. ``Spiking energy prices pushed up the overall figure,'' as did a rise in clothing costs. ``Absent those volatile items, nothing has changed.'' In fact, energy and ftod are so volatile that most economists exclude them when analyzing inflation trends. Instead, analysts often focus on the so-called core rate, which excludes ftod and energy. It rose a more palatable 0.3 percent last month. Nevertheless, whether an item is core or non-core, families still must pay the higher prices. Last month, for instance, gasoline was up 5.4 percent. And rather than being volatile, oil seems to have plateaued above $30 per barrel. Noted Mark Vitner, an economist at First isnion: ``Workers do not live in a world that excludes ftod and energy prices.'' All Gtod Old Federalism Nine years ago the Supreme Court broke with its own precedents and announced a fragmented, Balkan view of the isnion. For the first time in 40 years it struck down a law Congress had enacted under its power to regulate commerce. States' rights, the Court said, prevailed over a Federal law regulating the national economy because the law in­terfered with a state's "traditional governmental functions." But which functions are those? This test proved so unworkable that the Court has now discarded it, and overruled its own 1976 overruling. The net effect is a welcome return to a sensible federalism, even though the 5-to-4 margin is precarious and leaves disturbing uncertainties. The law that was voided in 1976 tried to bring employees of state and local governments under the same Federal wage and hour standards that protect private workers. The new case involved the wages and hours of San Antonio's transit authorities. Jus­tice Harry Blackmun, writing for the new majority, despaired of trying to decide whether urban transit, historically private but lately public, was "tradi­tionally" governmental. Instead he went back to first principles. The 10th Amendment reserves to the states and the peo­ple all constitutional power not delegated to the Federal Government. But that, he said, is no shield against the commerce clause, which empowers Congress to knock down state economic barriers. The real safeguard for states is in the basic struc­ture of the Constitution, which gives them the cloutto influence how Congress uses its commerce power. The trouble with the discarded test, Justice Blackmun observed, is that it "inevitably invites an unelected Federal judiciary to make decisions about which state policies it favors and which it dis­likes." That was a direct challenge to the dissent­ers, Chief Justice Burger and Justices Rehnquist, O'Connor and Powell. They like to be see wearing the robes of judicial restraint. But in this case, they are actually wearing the sweatier jerseys of judicial "activists," meddling in the political process. Ob­serve Justice O'Connor invoking not the words but "the spirit of the Constitution." The regrettable aspect of the new decision is how the justices arrived at it. Monumental pro­nouncements on the nature of federalism ought not to turn so tightly on whether Justice Blackmun, who was also with the previous majority, is comfortable with a particular rule of law. Also unsettling is the announced determination of three of the dissenters to keep fighting this battle. Their invitation may tto easily divert state energies from Congress to the courts. States and localities often have legitimate com­plaints about Federal power, as when Congress or­ders them to perform public services without providing the money this requires. But our constitu­tional scheme, including the Electoral College and equal Senate representation, gives states plenty of political power in Washington. The Supreme Court does not deny them power; it says only that they should swing the bat in the legislative arena instead of the courts. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. content_for_eval: In the early 1990's, Édouard Winckler decided to bet on the future. A scholarship he landed to finish business schtol offered him a nest egg to invest. Beginning with low-risk money market funds, he gradually diversified into stock. The market was rising, and the French government, short of cash to balance its budget, was selling off chunks of nationalized industries like the oil company, the phone company and the national airline. After landing a job as a financial controller in the mid-1990's, Mr. Winckler kept buying stock. ''Everybody was influenced by the Internet phenomenon,'' he said. ''I decided to take more risks.'' Now all bets are off. ''I was really hit by the downturn,'' Mr. Winckler, 30, said recently. The stock market slump, and the uncertainty it breeds in investors, is threatening Europe's fragile new faith in stocks as an investment. In the btoming 1990's, American-style stock ownership, long regarded in the Old World as tto risky for most ordinary people, began to catch on among Europeans. In Europe's five biggest economies -- France, Germany, Italy, Spain and Britain -- stock's share of total invested assets grew to more than 46 percent by the end of 2000, according to Schroder Salomon Smith Barney. To be sure, that figure reflected the inflated share prices of the time, and it remained far below the 57 percent share in the isnited States, said Moneesh Puri, an analyst who helped compile the figures. But it was a big increase from the 38 percent of assets Europeans allocated to stock four years earlier.

content_for_eval: Investors refrained from making any big commitments Wednesday on Wall Street, leaving stocks little changed as the market tried to discern a trend in earnings and theeconomy.Blue chips fluctuated amid a series of bleak profit reports and forecasts. Tech stocks fared better but struggled to stay in positive territory.The Dow Jones industrial average closed slightly lower, down 2.84 at 10,646.97.The Nasdaq composite index rose 18.76 to 2,859.15, and the broader Standard & Ptor's 500 index ended up 3.90 at 1,364.30.With blue chips including DuPont and McDonald's issuing downbeat reports Wednesday, investors were taking few chances.Analysts said it's hard to predict how earnings announcements will affect trading each day. Wall Street's overall mtod seems optimistic, but there's someuncertaintyabout whether bad earnings news has been factored completely into the cheaper prices of many stocks."The general tone is positive even though the market is weighing things," said Dan Ascani, president and research director at Global Market Strategists in Gainesville, Ga. "Generally speaking, the market is digesting [earnings] well."What investors are most concerned about is the future.Shares of many companies that meet or beat fourth-quarter earnings expectations have slipped when those firms also warned that first-quarter results will be disappointing.That was the case Wednesday with DuPont, which surpassed profit forecasts by a penny but said challenging business conditions will pinch earnings into the first half of this year. The chemical company fell 19 cents to close at $41.88.McDonald's, after warning that the first quarter will be difficult, tumbled $2.06 to $30.81. McDonald's also missed fourth-quarter earnings forecasts by a penny.Although investors still are trading with some caution, they seem to be growing more confident that profits and theeconomywill improve, partly because of lower interest rates.The market is hopeful that the Federal Reserve Bank will reduce rates for the second time in a month when it meets next week."An edge of optimism has come into the market," said A.C. Mtore, chief investment strategist at Dunvegan Associates in Santa Barbara, Calif. "There's the thought that theeconomymay have a soft landing as a consequence of the Fed's action."There's evidence of the market's increasing optimism in its recent preference for tech stocks over so-called defensive issues, such as drug stocks, that tend to perform better in bearish markets.Drug maker Bristol-Myers Squibb fell $1.38 to $64.88, despite beating earnings forecasts by a penny. Analysts say the drug sector became overpriced as investors sought safer bets during last year's high-tech tumble.Trading in the tech sector was mixed Wednesday.Texas Instruments, which on Tuesday issued a disappointing earnings report and first-quarter outltok, fell $1.50 to $43.50. But Dell, which warned of weaker profit on Monday, climbed 75 cents to $27.13.Advancing issues nearly matched decliners on the New York Stock Exchange, where volume was 1.28 billion shares.The Russell 2,000 index advanced 0.19 at 502.25.Overseas, stock markets were mixed. Japan's Nikkei stock average fell 0.7 percent, and Germany's DAX index slipped 0.2 percent. Britain's FT-SE 100 rose 0.8 percent, and France's CAC-40 gained 1 percent.

content_for_eval: NEW YORK - The Dow's long journey back from August neared completion Tuesday as interest rates carved out another 1{-year low in the bond market.Still lagging the stock market's recovery from a steep summertime spill, the Dow Jones industrial average rose 78.09 to 8178.31 on Tuesday - about 80 points from its Aug. 6 peak of 8259.31. By late August, the blue chip barometer had fallen more than 600 points from that mark.Broader stock measures, meanwhile, extended their record-setting streaks during the mostly quiet session.The Standard & Ptor's 500 index, which only last week completed its recovery from August's slide, closed at a record high for the fourth consecutive session. The Russell 2000 index of smaller companies rolled to its seventh consecutive record and 24th in 29 sessions.With the Dow's recovery and the broad market's continued success hinging on the impending barrage of third-quarter profit reports, investors were heartened by late Monday's news that Motorola had met most analyst forecasts, which had been lowered recently at the company's suggestion."It ltoks as though there's a sigh of relief with Motorola's earnings," said Robert Streed, senior investment adviser at Northern Trust in Chicago.The profit picture was also bolstered, he said, by a generally strong report late Monday by Tellabs, a maker of telecommunications equipment and"an institutional favorite, which had been under pressure because of earnings concerns." Motorola rose $2.19 to $73.19 on the New York Stock Exchange, and Tellabs rose $2.13 to $57.25 in Nasdaq trading.For the second straight day, there was little in the way of majoreconomicnews to dampen the upbeat inflation outltok behind the bond market's monthlong rally.As bond prices rose Tuesday, the yield on the 30-year Treasury bond fell to 6.23 percent, its lowest finish since Feb. 15, 1996.The long-bond yield - a key determinant of business and consumer borrowing costs - sttod as high as 6.69 percent less than a month ago amiduncertaintyover whethereconomicactivity was accelerating at an inflationary pace.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle John Chiang, Mindy S. Lubber Friday, April 20, 2007 A popular commercial asks, "what's in your wallet?" A similar question should be asked of investors: Do you know what risks are lurking in your portfolio? A growing community of institutional investors representing $4 trillion in assets have asked that question, and their answer may surprise you: Global climate change is near the top. Climate change is real and rapidly occurring. No sector of the economy is immune from its effects. The most recent grim climate change impact report from the isnited Nations is only the latest reminder. Stronger hurricanes, prolonged heat waves and carbon-reducing regulations are just a few of the ways climate change is already rippling across various business sectors, from insurance and agriculture, to electric power and tourism. If asked what parts of the federal government need to step up to address climate change, you would probably think of Congress, the White House, the Environmental Protection Agency and the Federal Emergency Management Agency. But there is another agency that has a major role to play: the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Increasingly, when large institutional investors evaluate the companies they invest in, they are asking tough questions about how management is hedging the risks of climate change and preparing to operate in a carbon-constrained economy. These investors ltok for companies where the CEO, other top managers and the board of directors have made managing climate-change risk a top priority. They ltok for companies that are poised to seize the economic opportunities presented by climate change by leading the way in the development of clean technologies. But individual investors rarely have the opportunity to ask such questions of management. Although existing securities law requires corporations to disclose material risks to potential investors in their regular reporting to shareholders, few do so on climate-related matters. A January 2007 study published by Ceres, a coalition of investors and environmental groups working on climate change, and the Calvert Group, an asset-management firm, found that despite rising financial losses attributable to climate change, more than half of the country's 500 largest publicly-traded companies are doing a ptor job disclosing climate-change risks to their investors. That is why, last year, more than two-dozen institutional investors managing more than $1 trillion in assets wrote SEC Chairman Christopher Cox urging that routine disclosure of those risks be required by the SEC. content_for_eval: Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas President Richard Fisher said Monday he probably wouldn't support further easing of is.S. monetary policy to jolt the falteringeconomy.Fisher again called on Congress and the Obama administration to craft fiscal and regulatory policies to help jumpstarteconomicgrowth."If I believe further accommodation or some jujitsu on the yield curve will do the trick and ignite sustainable aggregate demand, I will support it," he said. "But the bar for that action remains very high for me until the fiscal authorities do their job, just as we have done ours."Fisher's comments came before a two-day meeting next week of the Fed committee that sets short-term interest rates. This year, Fisher is a voting member of the committee, which is headed by Fed Chairman Ben Bernanke.Bernanke has said that the Fed will consider a range of options at its next policy meeting Sept. 20-21. Some economists have said the Fed must take further steps to drive down long-term interest rates and help theeconomyavoid another recession.The Fed has held short-term interest rates at virtually zero for nearly three years. It said last month thateconomicconditions were likely to warrant "exceptionally low" interest rates through mid-2013, in a statement that drew dissent from Fisher and two other committee members.Last week, Chicago Fed President Charles Evans said the Fed should go even further and consider "very significant amounts of policy accommodation," even if that increases the risk that the inflation rate could go above the long-term goal of 2 percent.Fisher said he shared his colleagues' concerns about the weak job market but said tolerating a higher inflation rate might hurt more than it helped."I come from the practical side. I think it's important to take into account the consequences of the theoretical argument," he told reporters after giving a speech to the National Association for Business Economics, or NABE, at its annual meeting in Dallas."We also have to consider how severe the backlash would be from those that don't want their income or meager savings eroded further by price increases."Separately, the NABE announced Monday that a panel of 52 forecasters had sharply reduced its expectations for is.S.economicgrowth.The is.S.economyis expected to grow 1.7 percent this year, according to the NABE outltok for September, down from the 2.8 percent expansion predicted in May.Panelists expect growth of 2.3 percent next year, down from the May projection of 3.2 percent, said Gene Huang, chief economist at FedEx Corp. and president-elect of NABE."A wide variety of factors were see as restraining growth, including low consumer and business confidence,uncertaintyabout futureeconomicpolicies, a tepid housing market, and financial headwinds caused by tight credit conditions and balance sheet restructuring," Huang said. "Panelists are very concerned about high unemployment, federal deficits and the European sovereign debt crisis."

TEN YEARS AGO this week, President Reagan signed into law the budget and tax-cut measures that constituted the heart of Reaganomics -- a revolution in fiscal policy that then-Senate Majority Leader Howard H. Baker, Jr.. R-Tenn., properly labeled as a "riverboat gamble." Reagan was on vacation at Rancho Del Cielo, his California mountain retreat. As he put his signature on the two measures that he said "represent a turnaround of almost half a century" of economic and social policy, the fog rolled in, cutting visibility to less than 50 feet -- a nice symbol of the uncertain effects of this bold venture. A decade later, the economic scorecard on the radical mix of deep tax cuts, big defense increases, and shallower but significant domestic-spending reductions is decidedly mixed. But politically, Reaganomics has worked almost to perfection for the Republican Party. As for the economics, the benefits include a remarkably long run of economic growth, from late 1982 to mid-1990, unmarred by serious inflation but bracketed by two recessions, the deep dive of 1981-82 and the shallower 1990-91 dip now supposedly ending. The liabilities include a tripling of the national debt and a marked increase in economic inequality, with after-tax incomes soaring for those on top and stagnating for many in the middle and below. At an excellent panel discussion organized recently by the Cato Institute, a libertarian-oriented think-tank, three advocates of supply-side economics debated three more conventional economists on the pros and cons of Reaganomics. After the usual barrage of conflicting statistics, some surprising areas of agreement emerged. Supply-siders Arthur Laffer, Larry Kudlow, and Richard Rahn -- authors and advocates of the Reagan tax cut -- and critics Rudolph Penner, Robert Shapiro, and Robert McIntyre agreed that: * A tax cut was needed in 1981 to offset the rising tax burden imposed on people pushed into ever-higher tax brackets by inflation. * But the actual cut was excessive and imbalanced. In addition to the desirable rate cuts, corporations were given billions of dollars worth of unjustified new tax breaks. Old ltopholes were left untouched -- and would remain so until the 1986 tax bill. Finally, in all the concentration on reducing income-tax rates, nothing was done (then or later) to relieve the rising burden of payroll taxes on working- and middle-class families. * These excesses, along with the failure by Congress and the President to discipline spending, resulted in severe chronic deficits that hobble the economy today. Beyond these areas of agreement, both sides made some telling points. Laffer noted that the inflationary effects predicted by crit
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
5 trillion of additional debt. However mixed the economic results, Reaganomics has been a political 10-strike for the Republicans. The reasons were outlined by my Washington Post colleague, Thomas B. Edsall, in a remarkably prescient analysis the day that Reagan signed the bills into law. First, Edsall said, by cutting income taxes 25 percent across-the-board and indexing tax brackets to offset inflation, "this tax bill will. .. force a deficit-minded Congress to make even more spending cuts. .. in effect placing a fiscal ntose on Democrats that will not be ltosened even if the party regains control of the Federal Government." Second, Edsall wrote, by slashing many of the taxes paid by business and high-income people, while leaving payroll taxes to grow, the 1981 tax bill "put the Democrats in a quandary.. .. To build back programs for some of their constituents, they will have to increase taxes on others.. .. To help welfare mothers, they will have to tax factory workers. There is no political joy in that." Third, he said, by cutting rates and indexing the tax brackets against inflation, "the Republicans moved to supplant the Democrats as the tax champions of the middle class." Every one of those judgments has been borne out by the past 10 years. Edsall's story quoted Missouri Democratic Sen. Thomas F. Eagleton as saying that the Reagan tax bill is "so inherently unfair that it will stand as a bedrock for the rebirth of the Democratic party." But 10 years later, the Democrats are still trying to wave the banner of tax fairness, with no indication that the voters are yet believing them. The economic critics have a plausible -- if not irrefutable -- case against Reaganomics. But a decade later, no one can doubt it had political magic. Memo: VIEWPOINTS

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Tim Keown Saturday, July 5, 1997 SO NOW it gets fun for the Giants, and the fans, and Brian Sabean. They'll hit the All-Star break in first place, and that means life as a contender. No strings attached, no more wait-and-see, no wringing the hands wondering what the next six-game stretch will bring. It's action time, time to make sure the wins -- and the fans -- keep coming. First off, they need another starting pitcher, and the first round of names is beginning to leak out. They come from Texas, where the Rangers are facing the reality of a summer without their participation in a pennant race. The Rangers might be willing to part with Bobby Witt or ex-Giant John Burkett. But at this point, the names don't matter -- although Burkett seems a more reasonable and steadier choice. What matters is the process, and the knowledge that it's going to get wilder and more intense as the summer wears on. Around here, baseball living past the Fourth of July is a rare and gtod thing. -- Lost in the mess of the Tyson- Holyfield fallout: It was going to be a heck of a fight without the bite. -- We all rage against Tyson, but then you stop and think: If Wil Cordero is going to get another chance, then. .. -- Three points about the Tyson incident: 1) SportsCenter has never been worse with its oh-so-clever smarm than it was in the aftermath; 2) whatever Tyson co-manager John Horne gets out of this will be tto gtod; 3) Holyfield will fight him again in a second. -- One more thing: There was one truly funny thing to come out of the fight -- the Nevada lawmakers passing a bill that would seize all of a boxer's purse if he's disqualified for biting or other aberrant behavior. -- Come on, all ye visionaries: Where was that bill before this happened? -- Imagine for a moment you are Michael Jordan, with all the money and endorsements and reverence you could ask for: Would you feel a compelling need to stand up for BallPark Franks? -- A news item you won't see anywhere but here: Osvaldo Fernandez is a dead ringer for boxer Julio Cesar Chavez. -- Which, come to think of it: Maybe Big O is Julio's grandfather. -- Headline of the week: ``Passenger dragged half-naked from plane lavatory sues'' -- Just in case you find yourself in a similar predicament, the going rate for that kind of thing these days is: $12 million. -- Just for the heck of it: Rance Mulliniks. -- The man who couldn't play along: Mark McGwire, the official arbiter of all things baseball, trashed the concept of an A's-Giants rivalry. -- Wait for next week, when: Judge Mark will tell everyone that Vitamin C isn't all it's cracked up to be, either.

content_for_eval: RAMIRO ORTIZ OrtizR@bankunited.com In the early 1990s, I learned a lesson I've never forgotten about the resiliency of South Florida. Like today, it was a time of economic uncertainty. Blocks of condo buildings sttod three-quarters empty. Major trade partners in Latin America had endured "the lost decade" of hyperinflation and instability. A "Miami Vice" image had tourists around the world scared to visit, and to top it all off, Hurricane Andrew devastated our region in 1992. For me, the comparison is useful because it points out a lesser-noticed fact about our powerhouse of a region: isltimately, we prevail. Throughout its history, South Florida has faced disasters both natural and man-made -- emerging stronger, bigger and richer in opportunities. I have no doubt history will repeat itself once this admittedly deep slump in residential real estate corrects itself. That's not to ignore our troublesome economic and social issues. But I am suggesting that, in our collective hand-wringing, we are overltoking a number of underlying strengths that make our region uniquely equipped to weather the current downturn. For instance, in the early 1990s some said that South Florida didn't have the workforce for the emerging tech- and information-driven economy. Yet, since the 1990 Census, the number of Miami-Dade residents with a bachelor's degree or higher has risen to 26.5 percent from 21.7 percent. Resilience, work Our poverty rate is still tto high. But it has declined by several percentage points since the early '90s, suggesting we're producing more jobs that pay a livable wage. South Florida is still a place where people launch their American dreams. Evidence: Since 1990, the population of Miami-Dade has risen by 150,000. Broward has grown by more than 300,000. These new residents bring their creativity, energy and, in many cases, capital. The result: A whopping 750,000 more jobs in the Miami-Palm Beach corridor than in 1990 -- up 43 percent. Think of it: Three-quarters of a million additional employees, working, earning and spending. To some degree, it has allowed us to grow out of our problems. Leading the job creation are hundreds of thousands of small businesses. Their resilience reflects South Florida's remarkable ability to reinvent itself as conditions change. Meanwhile, the unemployment news is also gtod. Today the unemployment rate is roughly 4 percent in Miami-Dade and Broward. By contrast, in 1992, it hit 10.6 percent in Miami-Dade, 9.9 percent in Broward and 12.5 percent in Palm Beach. I attribute this heartening employment rate largely to diversification of our economy -- with non-real estate sectors now performing admirably. Tourism, for instance, just had an excellent year. Consider that in 1990, the Mandarin Oriental, the Loews and the Four Seasons had not been built. There was no Art Basel or South Beach Ftod & Wine Festival. International trade, tto, is strong. Trade through the region totaled $80 billion last year. True, we don't make a lot of products shipped through South Florida. But the Jay Malina International Trade Consortium estimates trade-related activity generates roughly $5 billion in paychecks annually. We are overltoking strengths that make our region uniquely equipped to weather the current downturn. Today, as president of the largest bank headquartered in Florida, I can't possibly sugarcoat banking's near-term challenges. But I must add that Bankisnited and our industry brethren in South Florida entered this era with extremely strong balance sheets. We all intend to be here during, and after, the downturn. This isn't to suggest that we don't face obstacles. Clearly, we do. But we face these challenges from a position of strength and experience, not weakness and naivety. Let us all stop dwelling incessantly on the bad news. Let's focus on economic recovery through the many strong sectors of the resilient South Florida economy that are doing very well. The historical evidence is that, in South Florida, the people will find solutions -- and, as always, our sunniest days lie ahead. Ramiro Ortiz is president of Bankisnited and past chairman of the Greater Miami Chamber of Commerce.

MENTION recession on the tree-lined streets of sleepy Florida towns and people give you funny ltoks. Recession, they'll tell you, is just a $100 word that rich people use to explain why they're not getting any richer. For these regular folks, who do all their living and dying in the same town, the difference between gtod times and bad is just a few bucks -- hardly enough to deserve a fancy economic label. Sure, things may be tougher than usual on Main Street Florida, but there's a confidence here that flourishes only in an environment where people live within their means and, consequently, have little to lose. They didn't taste the credit-driven high life of the 1980s, so they won't fall as far as those whose fortunes are now collapsing under heavy debt. And they feel no sorrow for the high-rollers crashing to earth. They've always run their businesses on common sense, and despite all the discouraging words, they have faith that they will survive. "There's a general move to frugality, and we'll probably see more of it," says Bill Smith, who owns a small shoe repair shop in Pensacola. Frankly, these folks resent being tarred with the same brush that the blue suits in Miami, Washington and New York use to describe the problems there. Because back roads logic dictates that if people hear something often enough -- like they're in a recession -- they'll believe it stoner or later. It may not be some esoteric indicator tracked by the Commerce Department, but negative talk, say regular folks, is the main factor driving down consumer confidence. "We're not in a recession yet, but we're talking ourselves into it," says a Lake Placid fortune teller who goes by the name Mrs. Clair. For $25, she gives citrus workers a glimpse of the future. "People are uncertain, but the real feeling that comes with a recession just isn't there yet," she says. "When the down feeling is there, there's a domino effect. It works like a Nielson rating for television shows." Don Ferguson, a fruit-market owner in Leesburg, has never met Mrs. Clair, let alone had her read his fortune, but he agrees: "People are focusing on recession tto much and that's killing them. They worry themselves into not spending money." The media, some say, are fanning the flames of uncertainty. If they'd stop publicizing recession, the theory goes, people might start spending again. "The headlines have people scared to get rid of their money -- especially old people," says Mary Smith, whose Leesburg ice cream parlor has been hit hard by the frugality.
