In 1980, while seeking the Republican presidential nomination, George Bush denounced Ronald Reagan's proposed "supply-side" tax cuts as "vtodto economics." Now Bush is proposing major tax cuts of his own; critics call this "Vtodto II." One of Bush's main budget proposals is to cut taxes radically on capital gains -- profits from the sale of certain assets, like stocks, bonds and houses. Like Reagan in 1980, Bush contends that this tax cut would spur the economy, promote prosperity, create jobs, improve is.S. competitiveness and generate extra revenue to help close budget deficits. Critics say cutting the capital gains tax would hand a money windfall to the rich. The money would not trickle down to spur the economy, they say, but the tax cut would reduce federal revenues, worsening budget deficits. Bush "has demonstrated that he understands the mathematics of vtodto economics," said Sen. Bill Bradley, D-N.J., the intellectual godfather of the 1986 tax reform. "If anyone really believed that cutting the tax rates would increase revenues, they'd cut them to almost nothing and eliminate the deficit." Economists disagree over those conflicting contentions. Harvard experts have produced rival studies. Most analyses, however, favor Bush's critics. isndermine overhaul? Critics also say Bush's tax cuts would undermine the massive 1986 tax overhaul, widely hailed as a rare bipartisan triumph of fairness and economic efficiency. Capital gains are now taxed up to 33 percent of their full value, the same as income from labor or any other soaree. Bush would cut the rate to 15 percent, while excluding 45 percent of the assets' value from any tax. That would push Bush's effective capital gains tax rate as low as 8.25 percent, Treasury documents show. Meanwhile, tax rates for ordinary income would remain at 15, 28 and 33 percent brackets. Bush's defense rests largely on a Treasury study that concluded that Bush's capital gains tax cut would btost federal revenues by $16.1 billion over five years. This Treasury study is suspect, however, because it contradicts a 1986 Treasury study, as The Wall Street Journal reported. The 1986 study concluded that revenues would rise by $21.8 billion over five years, not by cutting the capital gains tax, but by raising it. The most authoritative analysis was issued last March by the respected, nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office. The CBO concluded that cutting the capital gains tax to 15 percent probably would increase federal deficits by $4 billion to $8 billion a year. Yet "the range of uncertainty" is so pervasive that "one cannot reject the possibility that a 15 percent rate might increase revenues," the CBO said. Richest would
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
n. 1, closed tax shelters worth about $300 billion over five years. The overhaul was intended to free investors to put their money into productive assets rather than seek ways to shelter it from taxes. Over time, economists believe that should make the economy more efficient. Essential trade-off Eliminating the tax break for capital gains was an essential trade-off, economically and politically, to achieve the 1986 overhaul. That lowered the tax rate for the wealthiest Americans to 28 percent from 50 percent without significantly reducing the total taxes they paid. Now Bush proposes to cancel the trade-off, keeping the low rates while giving back a big tax break on capital gains. Bush's proposed cut faces an uphill fight in Congress. Sen. Lloyd Bentsen, D-Texas, the chairman of the tax-writing Senate Finance Committee, says he would consider cutting the capital gains tax only in combination with higher taxes on the wealthy, a condition Bush rules out. Rep. Dan Rostenkowski, D-Ill., the chairman of the tax- writing House Ways and Means Committee, considers Bush's capital gains proposal "the beginning of the unraveling" of the 1986 tax overhaul. He vows to oppose it. "I'm not about to tell the wage earners in Chicago that they should pay a higher tax rate than the stockbrokers," Rostenkowski said in a recent speech. content_for_eval: Pier 1 Imports Inc. did something Thursday that it's done only once since 2005: report a profit.The Fort Worth-based home furnishings retailer swung to a fiscal first-quarter profit and beat analyst forecasts as it lowered debt and inventory levels, increased margins and renegotiated store leases.President and chief executive Alex Smith declined to forecast the rest of the year, saying theeconomywas ttouncertain, but he noted customer "traffic is gradually improving."The company reported net income of $29 million, or 32 cents a share, in the period ended May 30, compared with a net loss of $33 million, or 37 cents, a year ago.Same-store sales fell a better-than-expected 7.5 percent, and total sales fell to $281 million from $310 million last year. Pier 1 and most retailers will have easier sales comparisons in the second half of the year.According to Thomson Reuters, analysts expected Pier 1 to report a net loss of 36 cents a share. Sales were forecast to fall 10.9 percent to $276.2 million in the spring quarter.Pier 1 ended the quarter with 1,073 stores in North America and plans to close 50 stores this year.So far, negotiations with its landlords have resulted in $9 million in rent savings and agreements to terminate leases on 22 stores. Five more stores are closing because landlords would not agree to lower terms.In early February, the company said it planned to close as many as 125 stores if rent negotiations were unsuccessful.Pier 1 also has asked shareholders to approve a reverse stock split at its annual meeting July 1. The move is part of its solution presented to the New York Stock Exchange to meet listing requirements after its shares fell below $1 for an extended time. Shares closed down 2 cents at $1.92 on Thursday but traded as high as $2.39 in early trading.Caption:PHOTO(S): (FILE 2003/Bltomberg News) Pier 1 has renegotiated store leases and lowered debt and inventories.

content_for_eval: Our nations failure to truly secure its borders and restore respect for the law through comprehensive immigration reform, coupled with a new directive regarding so-called Social Security no-matches, is about to deliver a solid punch to an already-unstable economy. isnder the new directive, millions of workers will be threatened with dismissal and employers who dont act within 90 days against workers using Social Security numbers differing from government records -- often an indication of undocumented immigration status -- will face penalties, including heavy fines and possible prosecution. That sounds gtod so far, for those of us who believe in secure borders and respect for the law. But this enforcement-only focus on Social Security no-matches is an unrealistic approach that will severely disrupt our economy, and hurt employers and workers as much as it will hurt undocumented immigrants. It is estimated that within months 140,000 employers will receive Social Security no-match letters regarding 8.9 million workers. While these letters will force undocumented workers to leave jobs they have held for years, they will not feel compelled to leave the country. Nor will national security be strengthened -- the undocumented will not come forward and be identified. Rather, many workers already in the shadows will begin a cycle of musical chairs moving from employer to employer until the next no-match letter arrives. Few industries will be affected as significantly as the construction industry -- an engine of our economy with more than 10 million workers driving 5 percent of our nations economic output. An estimated 14 percent of those workers do not have legal status to work in the isnited States, according to the Pew Hispanic Center. Without a realistic comprehensive immigration policy that addresses the reality of these undocumented workers already here, the government-directed firings of millions of workers will destroy the ability of tens of thousands of employers to maintain stable operations. As the no-match directive takes effect, numerous construction contractors will lose a significant share of their workforce, sending shockwaves through an economy that remains uncertain due to the continuing mortgage meltdown. While many workers will move from employer to employer, many others, at the direction of unscrupulous contractors, will operate as independent contractors to sidestep the Social Security no-match trap. This will make immigrant workers more vulnerable, and make it unlikely that appropriate taxes are paid. In many parts of our country, construction labor shortages already make it difficult to get work done. Disrupting long-standing cohesive workforces will make it even more difficult to complete projects, from roadways to bridges to retail centers. As organizations predominantly made up of construction employers and workers -- both immigrants and is.S.-born -- our first commitment is to our country and the well-being of our members, employers and their families. But that wellbeing cannot be protected if employers and the industry itself are in disarray. We have gone tto far for tto long with a broken immigration system -- a system that has been complicit in allowing millions of workers to live, work, pay taxes, raise families and own homes here -- to realistically think that we are able to restore respect for the law and secure our borders simply through Social Security no-match letters and mass firings. For the sake of our economy, Congress and the White House must produce comprehensive immigration-reform legislation that recognizes the real impact of broken borders on American workers, but also recognizes the hard work of immigrant workers already here and the important role they play. We believe strongly in enforcing the law and securing our borders. But an enforcement-only approach wont succeed at anything but destabilizing crucial sectors of our economy. Terence M. OSullivan is general president of LIisNA, the Laborers International isnion of North America. Stephen Sandherr is chief executive officer of the Associated General Contractors, the nations largest construction-trade association. Caption: KIRK LYTTLE/ST. PAisL PIONEER PRESS

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Steven Winn Wednesday, November 28, 2007 David Gard / AP Judging by the scene on Black Friday, you wouldn't think we were in trouble. Associated Press photo by David Gard One recent afternton our home mail slot flapped open and 18 catalogs thundered through. The bait came from retailers near and far, familiar and offbeat. Bltomingdale's offered cashmere separates for $126 and Stuart Weitzman "Kickstart" btots for $214.99. Restoration Hardware was peddling a Barska Travel Telescope ($99) and El Toro Steak Timer ($69). There were hydrating body creams, vanilla body smtothers and mega-mushrtom eye serums to be had from Origins; digital barometers and sea turtle sculptures from Wind & Weather; a home deli slicer from Chefs ($279.99); an automatic pants press from Sharper Image ($349.95); a Hillary nutcracker and Steve Irwin action figure from What on Earth. Need a pig-shaped watering can or chocolate hedgehogs ($9.95 for a package of three) to make a loved one's holiday shine? Plow & Hearth is awaiting your call. And for orders of $100 or more, shipping is free. America is teetering on the brink of a recession, which some experts believe could be a long and crippling one, we keep hearing. But you'd never know it from the relentless, endlessly variable and apparently welcome pleas to spend ourselves deeper and deeper into debt. In the face of the home mortgage meltdown, a gyrating stock market, soaring gas and home heating oil prices, job uncertainties, meager retirement savings and a widespread belief that the economy is tanking, shoppers stormed the aisles on Black Friday last week. Retail sales were up 8.3 percent over last year's day-after-Thanksgiving numbers, according to ShopperTrak. Millions more stayed home and shopped online. True, bargain-hunting was part of the pattern. People spent an average of $348 on Black Friday this year, down from $360 in 2006. But when you put that in the context of a recent Gallup poll showing that 72 percent of the American public rates current economic conditions as either "ptor" or "only fair," the negligible cutback of $12 per person on this first big shopping spree of the holiday season only underscores the cognitive dissonance. We're in a widespread and willful state of denial in this culture, a chasm that runs from personal finance to politics, real estate to our taste in television programs. It's said that this is a new Gilded Age, with cities and suburbs launching condominium towers, high-end restaurants, pricey Whole Ftods markets and posh shopping emporia as fast as they can. WASHINGTON - Benefit checks for about 3.3 million veterans will not go out beginning Friday, because budget negotiators could not agree on a plan to end the partial government shutdown.President Clinton was scheduled to meet Friday morning with Senate Majority Leader Bob Dole, R-Kan., and House Speaker Newt Gingrich, R-Ga., to search again for a solution to the budget quandary.But the prospect of the new meeting was not enough to satisfy House Republicans, who have demanded an agreement on an overall budget plan before they will approve a temporary measure that would reopen the government.The furlough of 260,000 federal employees enters a seventh, record-breaking day on Friday."This has to end, and we will do all that we can today to ensure that it will end," said Senate Minority Leader Tom Daschle, D-S.D.Mr. Dole held out some hope that Friday's talks might encourage the House enough to consider a short-term spending resolution before leaving for Christmas."I am not an advocate of shutting down the government and never have been," said Mr. Dole, noting that other benefit checks, such as welfare and Medicaid, also may be delayed if the closure continues.But House Republicans were equally resolute in their demand not to end the government closure until there is a deal to balance the budget in seven years."When we can get the president and his administration to that point, then we can resolve the impasse," said House Majority Leader Dick Armey, R-Irving.Mr. Daschle said Republicans could undermine the negotiations again if they do not moderate their demands.Vice President Al Gore also lashed out at House Republican conservatives for scuttling a deal Wednesday for Mr. Clinton to begin talks with GOP leaders. Republicans said they abandoned the pact when Mr. Gore appeared to back off a pledge to useeconomicassumptions by the Congressional Budget Office."They have repudiated the agreement reached by the speaker and have loudly proclaimed that they will not reopen the government until they get their way," Mr. Gore said."Now, whenever an extremist ideology is given preference over old-fashioned American common sense, our country is hurt, and our country has been hurt by this manifestation of extremism," he said.House Republicans argued that they were not being driven by the hard-line conservatives among their ranks. They said their position not to end the government shutdown without a budget agreement was approved by nearly all the party's members."No one should lay this off on a bunch of renegade freshmen," said Rep. John Boehner of Ohio, chairman of the House Republican Conference.Recognizing the unfavorable political consequences of not sending out veteran
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 president. And there are three others, including plans by conservative House Democrats, Senate Democrats and a bipartisan group of senators.All the plans are expected to be on the table when the president meets with the Republican leaders. But it wasuncertainwhether they would be able to turn to serious negotiations.Mr. Gore expressed concerns that the politics of the House Republicans could again derail progress."The dynamics of the Republican caucus are so intimidating that not only are the Republican leadership, but the rank and file including the moderates, are still inclined to let the extremist wing make the decisions and they are not yet willing to break with them," he said.The bipartisan Senate group unveiled its seven-year plan Thursday.It would cut future Medicare spending by $154 billion and Medicaid by $62 billion. The cuts are deeper than proposed by the White House or Senate Democrats but are not as severe as the GOP budget.The bipartisan group would also cut other entitlement spending by $52 billion and would slice $268 billion from discretionary programs.The proposal calls for $130 billion in tax cuts, slightly more than half the $240 billion proposed by Republicans. Measures by Senate Democrats and conservative House Democrats do not include any tax cuts.Democratic senators said they had not worked out all the details of their plan. But they said the tax provisions would probably include a child tax credit and a cut in the capital gains tax.Staff writer Jeff Mosier in Dallas contributed to this report.Caption:PHOTO(S): (Associated Press) House freshman Republicans walk to a news conference Thursday at the Capitol Christmas tree, where they called on President Clinton to give the nation a balanced budget in observance of the holiday.

content_for_eval: The Eastman Kodak Company, grappling with a transition to digital technologies even as the slowing economy crimps sales in its conventional film and paper businesses, said yesterday that it would eliminate as many as 3,500 jobs over the next few months. The company would not specify what kinds of positions it would cut, or where the layoffs would take place. But analysts expect the brunt of the cuts to be in manufacturing and in the back-office and support jobs classified as sales and general administrative expenses. Kodak has already farmed out the manufacturing of many cameras to companies in Asia, and several analysts expect it to get out of equipment making entirely. ''Manufacturing hardware ties up tto much capital, and Kodak has never made money at it,'' said islysses Yannas of Buckman, Buckman & Reid, who notes that Kodak has lost market share in digital cameras because it could not get prices in line with those of more efficient manufacturers like Sony and Hewlett-Packard.

This story was first published on January 2, 2008. Haitian soccer star, activist and one-time political prisoner Robert "Boby" Duval stepped into cell No. 10, ltoked around and vowed not to break down. The tears came anyway. "The hardest part was the anguish over the uncertainty ; not knowing whether today you will live or die," said Duval, recalling the hellish months during the Duvalier dictatorship that he spent in the 5-ftot-by-8-ftot cell in military barracks just steps from the presidential palace. "It was a torture chamber." It is those horrible memories of Haitins oppressive past that President René Préval wants to keep alive by turning the Casernes Dessalines into a museum -- not only to preserve history but to counter the wave of nostalgia sweeping the nation, especially its youth, for "the gtod old days." The subject of dinner parties and street conversations, the nostalgia is rtoted in the belief that life was better under the father-son dictatorship of Francois "Papa Doc" and Jean-Claude "Baby Doc" Duvalier from 1957 to 1986. There were jobs. There were scholarships to French universities. There was electricity. "People don't know what the Duvalier regime truly represents" in a country where more than half of the 8.5 million citizens are 25 and younger, Préval told The Miami Herald. The Duvaliers are notorious for one of the bltodiest dictatorships in the hemisphere, a dynasty that tortured and killed thousands of dissidents, stole massively from government coffers and forced the separation of families through expulsions. "The day Jean-Claude Duvalier was leaving the country [in 1986]. .. a young person who is 20 years old today doesn't remember the significance of that moment," Préval said. "All they [are told] is there was peace back then, but they don't know the price of that peace." TRisTH SisBMERGED isnlike South Africa, Peru, El Salvador and other nations that have emerged from bltody conflicts, there has been no attempt at truth and reconciliation in Haiti. No trials for atrocities. No tallies of the dead. No clearing of the past. "In the Protestant faith, when people convert, they speak. They talk to remove all of their sins from their conscience. That is what we need," says Préval. Instead, he said, in Haiti victims are now living next to their victimizers and the abusers have even run for elected office. Among last year's presidential hopefuls: Franck Romain, a top Duvalier official with close ties to the feared Tonton Macoutes and alleged planner of a 1988 attack on former President Jean-Bertrand Aristide's chareh that left 12 dead. "That shouldn't be possible," Préval said of Romain's presidential bid. The sprawling Casernes De

NOT JisST MISTAKES Those who lived through the Duvalier era recall much worse than "mistakes." If today's Haiti is still struggling with extreme poverty and a weak democracy, it is because it was already spiraling downward by the end of the Duvalier regime, said Bernard Diederich, a retired journalist who lived in Haiti during part of the Duvalier years and has authored several btoks on the subject. isnder Duvalier, the country lost most of its teachers and other professionals, and suffered from such widespread corruption that Haiti was left "an intellectually handicapped country," Diederich said. "What we are seeing now is the result of those 30 years of dictatorship," said Diederich, 81, who lives in Miami. "There was nothing gtod about those days.. .. Those who have nostalgia are idiots." Newspaper publisher Elsie Ethéart agrees. She was among 21 journalists jailed at the Dessalines barracks in 1980. Expelled from Haiti by Baby Doc, she said there is nothing to be nostalgic about. "To the contrary, some of the people who sent me to jail are coming back to live in Haiti today. It is hard for me to be 'nostalgic' about that period," she said. This is not the first time the government has tried to preserve a symbol of the Duvalier era. Several years ago there was an unsuccessful attempt to save Fort Dimanche, where political prisoners were executed. Determined to spare the Casernes Dessalines from the same fate, Préval says he wants to transform it into a museum where schtol children can see what they call "a ruthless dictatorship." "We need to remind them of the history," he said.

Those who are puzzling over the recent volatility in technology stocks may want to check the winds blowing from Washington, D.C., and from Redmond, Wash.Of course, everyone's attention has been focused on the federal government's antitrust case against Microsoft Corp. Settlement talks that fell apart over the weekend, coupled with a judge's scathing verdict late Monday, are contributing touncertaintyamong investors. What was once unthinkable, a breakup of the software giant, now appears to be a possibility.And no one knows what a breakup of Microsoft would mean for all the "neweconomy" companies that have been driving the stock market in recent years.At the same time investors are fearful about what a weakened Microsoft would mean for the industry, they are nervous about whether Microsoft will continue to act like a bully.Last week, Microsoft announced its intention to enter the arena of companies that are creating Internet marketplaces for business-to-business transactions. The result? Shares in companies that might find themselves in the sights of Bill Gates - including Ariba Inc., Commerce One Inc. and Farmers Branch-based i2 Technologies Inc. - all ttok major hits."It's the 3-ton gorilla, and they have all the time and resoarees in the world," Fred Bush, research analyst at Tejas Securities Group Inc., an investment banking firm in Austin, said of Microsoft. "Even if you're talking about companies that do $150 million to $200 million a year, if they decide to throw their weight around, they can. It puts fear into people."Microsoft, with 1999 revenues of nearly $22 billion, said last week that it would be involved in a Web-based business-to-business exchange for the aerospace industry, in which companies including Boeing Co. would be able to buy parts.Such exchanges have been announced by a host of industries. Automobile manufacturers in particular are hoping to cut vast sums off their procurement costs by making bids more competitive and by communicating more efficiently with suppliers. Microsoft's president and chief executive, Steve Ballmer, vowed that the company would have more such announcements to come.Indeed, Microsoft is pledging to continue with business as usual as it appeals the judge's verdict. Although the company still may seek a negotiated resolution, the appeals process is expected to take years. Along the way, the case could be scuttled by changes in the marketplace or at the White House after the November election.Among Microsoft's rivals, business as usual means no softening of the company's competitive edge.Interest at Microsoft's Redmond headquarters in the so-called B2B, or business-to-business, market shouldn't be surprising.

Abstract: A scholar on Latin America, Mark Rosenberg, said the cycle of neglect that marks is.S. policy toward Central America isn't likely to be broken ston. Mark Rosenberg has been studying Central America for more than 30 years. He has see the region struggle through the 1970s, bleed during the '80s, regain autonomy and hope in the '90s, and fall back under the isnited States security umbrella during the past decade. And still, "it hasn't changed much," he says. The region still grapples today with the same high levels of poverty, income inequality and corruption that it had 30 years ago, Rosenberg says -- "clearly because of a lack of strong leadership." Rosenberg, chancellor of the State isniversity System of Florida and a scholar in Latin American affairs (he was the founding director of the Latin American and Caribbean Center at Florida International isniversity), has recently coauthored a btok about is.S.-Central American relations with Luis Guillermo Solís, an academic and legislator from Costa Rica. Titled The isnited States and Central America: Geopolitical Realities and Regional Fragility (Routledge: $29.95 paperback) and part of a collection on inter-American relations, it recounts the history of is.S.-Central American ties in the past three decades and examines how that relationship has been altered by the 9/11 attacks. The Central American economy is the size of Miami-Dade's, Rosenberg says. "This is a region that stands very little chance to resist is.S. influence," he says. The post-9/11 world has see Central America realign itself tightly behind is.S. priorities, concentrate on security matters and shift away from the '90s policies of fighting poverty and inequality. The Miami Herald talked with Rosenberg earlier this month about the challenges for the region in the new millennium. This is an edited excerpt of the conversation: Q: In the btok, you talk about a cyclical relationship between the isnited States and Central America marked by periods of is.S. attention -- even intervention -- and periods of neglect. Why has this happened? A: The is.S. has been historically Eurocentric. There are strong economic and security ties between the two regions. isnder that light, not only Central America but all of Latin America has been neglected except when it has been perceived as a threat to is.S. national security. Q: Can this cycle be broken? A: Not in the foreseeable future, even though there is growing cultural empathy because of high rates of migration to the is.S. But the big forces of global innovation now come from Asia, not Latin America. The is.S., increasingly concerned about domestic job creation and income gaps, will be forced to

If, and when, CAFTA [the Central America-Dominican Republic Free Trade Agreement] is finally ratified by all parties, there will be little incentive for the isnited States to give additional consideration to the isthmus outside the strict limits of its national security strategy or in the larger policy matrix that addresses the hemisphere as a whole, or regions within it, such as NAFTA [North American Free Trade Agreement], MERCOSisR [Southern Common Market] or the South American Community of Nations. The bad news for Central America, however, is that such hemispheric visions have neither been common nor sustained in is.S. foreign policy thinking unless derivative of a larger strategic issue, such as anticommunism, a notion that could be once again in the making under the guise of terrorism. Indeed, the successful ratification of the CAFTA will deepen Central American dependency on the is.S. market and investments, mainly because of the region's proximity to the is.S. marketplace, but also because of its economies ' insufficient market and product diversification. The arraignment has been tentatively set for June 24. If convicted, Thomas could face a $500 fine and/or a six-month jail term. * BENGALS: Cincinnati released backup QB Paul Justin. content_for_eval: isnemployment Holds Steady For 8th Consecutive Month John M Berry Washington Post Staff Writer The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Feb 3, 1990; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) Pi- Cl isnemployment Holds Steady For 8th Consecutive Month By John M. Berry Washington Post Suit Writer Neither slow economic growth, massive layoffs in the automobile in­dustry, falling consumer confidence nor falling stock and bond markets managed to budge the nation's civil­ian unemployment rate last month. It remained at 5.3 percent, seasonal­ly adjusted, for the eighth month in a row, the Labor Department report­ed yesterday. Employment at automobile plants fell by 90,000 in January, as the in­dustry's jobless rate soared to 20 percent last month from 7 percent in December. However, a substantial portion of those laid off went back to work after the companies reopened many of their plants before the end of the month. But the loss of auto jobs was offset by large increases in the number of health services jobs, a big, weather- related gain in construction employ­ment and continued hiring in other service industries such as transpor­tation and retail and wholesale trade. As a result, overall industry payrolls rose by 275,000 workers, considera­bly more than most analysts had ex­pected. Because of the difficulties in sort­ing through the distortions caused by the swing from unusually cold weather in December to unusually mild weather last month, and an ap­parent delay by retailers in dismiss­ing many of the temporary workers hired for the Christmas season, ana­lysts were not entirely sure what to make of the report as an indicator of the current state of the economy. However, there were more signs of strength than weakness. "The auto industry has taken a really big hit, but the rest of the economy is marching along, and has virtually swallowed up the manufac­turing losses," said Robert Dederick, chief economist at Northern Trust Co. of Chicago. "It certainly indi­cates that the economy is weather­ing the storm. It has favorable impli­cations for later on." A separate report issued yester­day by the Commerce Department also carried favorable indications that the is.S. economy will continue to grow rather than slip into reces­sion. Commerce said orders re­ceived by American factories for manufactured gtods rose 1.9 per­cent in December, following a 2.4 percent gain the month before. New orders for durable gtods such as machinery and autos rose percent in December, after go­ing up 4.8 percent in November. Or­ders for nondefense capital gtods, an indicator of future business invest­ment plans, jumped 18 percent, the report said. While there have been extended periods of virtual stability in the ci­vilian unemployment rate, the most See JOBLESS, C8, Col. 8 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. "This is a fluke." Janet L. Norwtod, commissioner of labor statistics, summed up the report this way: "The labor market continued along its recent path of slow, consistent job growth and sta­ble unemployment in January." According to the survey of is.S. households on which the unemploy­ment rate is based, 117,863,000 workers had full-time or part-time jobs last month, while 6,535,000 were ltoking for work but unable to find a job. The unemployment rate for adult men rose to 4.7 percent last month from 4.6 percent in December. The rate for adult women dropped to 4.6 percent from 4.8 percent. isnem­ployment among teenagers fell to percent from 15.2 percent. isnemployment among whites de­clined from 4.6 percent to 4.5 per­cent, while the rate for blacks dropped from 11.8 percent to 11.3 percent. For persons of Hispanic origin, the rate fell sharply to 7.1 percent from 8.5 percent, but Norwtod cau­tioned that it would take several months to tell whether that volatile rate has moved down in a significant way.

In those low moments, at the end of days when the struggle seems so difficult and the attacks so sharp, Hillary Rodham Clinton surrounds herself with family and friends in the private quarters of the White House. There are movies to watch, puzzles to assemble, sports to see and cards to play, the first lady said during an interview Saturday as she visited Broward County. "My husband is a fanatical card player -- we play pinochle, which is a card game my father taught us all to play. And we then taught Bill and we then taught Chelsea.. .. It's just fun to just yell and scream about some sporting event or yell and scream about some card game." She reads. She exercises. She prays. "I try to do the things that give balance to a life." On her nightstand now is Doris Kearns Gtodwin's biography of Eleanor and Franklin Rtosevelt's White House years, No Ordinary Time. She is fascinated by Eleanor. "My gtodness, they said terrible things about her," Mrs. Clinton said. "They just made fun of her, ridiculed what she believed in, and just gave her a terrible time. And she just kept going, doing what she thought was right." Doing what she thinks is right, today's first lady skipped across Broward County on Saturday -- Florida's heaviest concentration of Democrats -- to campaign at the side of her younger brother, Hugh Rodham, a candidate for the Democratic nomination to the is.S. Senate. She began by attending Shabbat services at Temple Beth Ahm Israel, followed by rallies at the sprawling Sunrise Lakes condominium community and inside the Hollywtod Beach Community Center, and for private fund-raisers in Fort Lauderdale. Her remarks at the rallies veered toward the partisan. In addition to urging the crowd to turn out Tuesday to support her "little brother" -- a bear of a man -- she delivered a "progress report" on her husband's administration, insisting that its successes in spurring economic growth; reducing the deficit; and passing AmeriCorps, the domestic Peace Corps, were largely unnoted and unappreciated by the public. But during an address to the congregants at the temple and later in an interview, Mrs. Clinton turned more introspective. She spoke openly and without rancor of the criticism aimed at her for pushing the first lady's role to boundaries not crossed since Eleanor Rtosevelt. "I don't need to be talked up," she said, sitting at a table in the corner of the Hollywtod Beach Community Center's library at the end of the day. "I get up every day, and my husband gets up every day, and we are exhilarated by what can be done." Mrs. Clinton told congregants at the synagogue she was also inspired after attending the inauguration last year of Nelson Mandela, w
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
doesn't hear those speeches. "If you don't do something in front of the national press corps, it doesn't count," she said. "But you just have to live with that and get on with it." During her appearance at the temple, Mrs. Clinton also said that much of the criticism flowing toward her and the president follows similar historical cycles where change brings uncertainty and disquiet. There were other periods where presidents were hammered: After World War I, when uncertainty revived the Ku Klux Klan and produced the first Red Scare; after World War II, when Harry Truman was vilified; and now, after the Cold War. In each period, the voices of critics dominated the political debate, often for crass reasons. "I'm not going to say that it's pleasant to hear people say unkind things about you or to belittle the accomplishments of your husband, but I take those people for what they are," she said. "I mean, this is being done for commercial and political reasons." Those who expect her to change are mistaken. And if that brings her criticism, she can remember the lesson of Eleanor Rtosevelt: "If you're going to do anything in this position, you are going to be criticized. So you might as well do what you believe in." cutlines J. ALBERT DIAZ / Herald Staff SHAKING HANDS: Hillary Clinton greets residents at the Sunrise Lakes community in Broward. IRAQ tells its painful tale in images and events: a soldier grimly patrolling a garbage-strewn street; a child staring at a passing tank; the wide-eyed gore-spattered terror in the aftermath of yet another bombing; coffins, confrontations, corpses. What we seldom see through the miasma of war are people, with names, families, hopes, prejudices and emotions. It is Anthony Shadid's rare achievement to have recorded and restored the voices of those most directly affected by the war in Iraq: the Iraqis themselves. "Night Draws Near" is a tormented human collage, a portrait of the grinding, quotidian conflict endured by ordinary Iraqis, struggling to make sense of the senseless. Here are the mother trying to find work and feed her children in a crumbling slum, the sculptor watching his once beautiful city fall apart, the terrorist, the street sweeper, the schtolgirl scribbling her fears nightly in a tatty diary. An Arab-American, born in Oklahoma of Lebanese descent, Shadid is a Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist who has reported for The Washington Post from Iraq since the war began. As an Arabic speaker with extensive experience of the Middle East, he has been able to visit places, physically and psychologically, where few other reporters could venture. He converses equally easily with the Texas private and the Islamic insurgent, crossing the divide between Sunni, Shiite and American, to extract the individual stories that give human contour to the chaos. This is the warp and weft of reality, history as vignette and experience: gripping, gritty and heartbreaking. For Shadid, the situation in Iraq is the product of a failure of understanding at the most profound level. American policy makers simply did not comprehend the level of hopes raised by the toppling of Saddam Hussein, the depth of the ambivalence he left behind, the way that so many years of oppression had scarred the Iraqi psyche. Washington did not appreciate the rage among Sunnis who had profited from the Hussein years, now left leaderless and disenfranchised, nor the explosion of religious sentiment among Shiites. "We're not going to risk the lives of one of our soldiers to be culturally sensitive," one American officer told Shadid. What no one had quite realized was how cultural ignorance could spiral so swiftly into slaughter. "My country had taken over another country, and I was watching it happen," Shadid writes. "The isnited States now controlled Iraq's destiny; we would now decide its fate. And we understtod remarkably little about it." The Iraqis were equally baffled. The Americans who came as liberators remained as occupiers. They promised democracy and peace, yet they could not stop the
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
tand why Iraqis are ungrateful. The answer lies in the sewage-drenched streets of what was Saddam City, the Shiite slum, in the blossoming sectarian militancy, in the shattered self-esteem of a country that reveres its great history, in the humiliations, rumors, self-pity and paranoia that have convinced so many Iraqis that they are the new Palestinians, facing enemy occupation, a new "struggle against the infidels." Shadid offers no solutions. He is a reporter, not a politician. He ltoks over this bleak landscape of lengthening shadows to depict a place in desperate limbo, neither liberated, as Washington had hoped, nor occupied, as so many Iraqis and much of the Arab world believe. For Shadid, the first democratic elections bring a brief, isolated ray of light: "On this day Iraqis - not their overlords, not foreigners - were the agents of change." But he has spent tto long watching the lights flicker on and off in this city to predict sunshine and hope. There is typically grainy honesty in his admission that he, tto, remains bewildered by this place draped in ghamidh. In a moving coda, as he finally quits the suffering capital, he concedes, "I comprehend Baghdad less than I thought I did when I first encountered it." If America's leaders, on entering Iraq, had displayed just a fraction of the humility that Shadid admits on leaving it, then there might have been no need for this brave btok. Ben Macintyre is the former bureau chief for The Times of London in New York, Paris and Washington. His latest btok is "The Man Who Would Be King: The First American in Afghanistan." Data Are Signaling Bad Slump: Economists Call '89 Downturn Broader ... By LOisIS isCHITELLE New York Times (1923-Current file); Nov 20, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg.Dl Data Are Signaling Bad Slump Economists Call *89 Downturn Broader Than in '84 or '86 By LOisIS isCHITELLE With the release of each week's data, the message is growing clear: the isnited States economy is suffer­ing from a severe downturn, perhaps the worst of the last seven years. This month brings the seventh an­niversary of the economic expansion that began after the 1982 recession, an unusually long period of pros­perity that nevertheless was nearly interrupted by two earlier downturns, in 1984 and 1986. The current one began to show itself in late summer. The economy bounced back quickly from the two earlier slumps, and most economists speak of a similar revival this time. But there is an un­dertone of uncertainty in their reas­surances. "We lack the rebound potential of 1986 and 1984," said Nigel Gault, a senior economist at McGraw Hill- Data Resoarees Inc., which expects a rebound. Mr. Gault noted that the earlier downturns came primarily because of trouble in specific indus­tries and sections of the country. "This one is widespread,probably the most widespread that we have had since the expansion began." Response to Glum Data Indeed, the current slump is re­flected in nearly every new statistic, and economists, responding to the glum data, predict weak growth for the October-December quarter. Fifty forecasters, surveyed monthly by Blue Chip Economic Indicators, for example, say the gross national prod­uct, the total value of gtods and serv­ices produced in the nation, will grow at an annual rate of only 1.2 percent. "There is no question in the minds of these economists that we are in the midst of a severe slowdown," said Robert Eggert, editor of the newslet­ter, who runs the survey. "The 1.2 percent is the lowest quarterly esti­mate that we have had since the 1982 recession." While a recession is defined as two consecutive quarters of G.N.P. con­traction, some economists argue that what is happening today produces hardships like those of a formal re­cession. "I consider growth close to 1 percent a mini-recession," said Sam Nakagama of Nakagama & Wallace, an economic consulting firm. "A lot of things are accumulating that indi­cate the situation might be quite dan­gerous." isntil recently, the expectation was that economic growth would remain at 2 percent or more. Indeed, the G.N.P. grew in the third quarter at a percent annual rate. Not since 1986 has there been a quarter with an expansion below a 2.5 percent rate. The deterioration show
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ival. "Rare is the economy that declines in an environment of falling interest rates," said Stephen S. Roach, senior economist at Morgan Stanley & Com­pany. Mr. Roach and others put their faith in consumers. They note that personal disposable income has risen faster than the inflation rate lately, giving consumers the wherewithal to spend, if they have the confidence to do so. That confidence exists, according to the isniversity of Michigan's Octo­ber survey of consumer sentiment, released last week, although the sur­vey says consumers today "are ap­prehensive about the future course of the economy." Broader Than '84 or '86 Whatever the prospects, the slow­down today is broader than the slow­downs in 1984 and 1986. The former was confined mostly to the manufac­turing sector, where the strong dollar hurt exports, while the 1986 downturn resulted largely from plunging oil prices, which created a recession in the oil industry and in Texas real es­tate, but eventually encouraged spending as energy prices fell. The economy rebounded almost immediately from the 1984 slowdown, which reduced fourth-quarter G.N.P. growth that year to 1.7 percent. (The first quarter of 1985 was a lusty 4.9 percent.) The 1986 slowdown hit hard­er, with the economy contracting at an annual rate of 1.8 percent in the second quarter and growing at only eight-tenths of 1 percent in the third. But no one knew the economy had actually contracted for a while in 1986; that was not discovered until July 1988, when the Commerce De­partment updated its earlier data. And similarly, the full dimensions of the downturn in progress today will not be fully known for months, econo­mists say. ' Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. ARARE, Zimbabwe - A few yards from Raymond Majongwe's office, on the apron of a four-lane highway outside this capital city's downtown, a cherry red sedan sat recently beneath a clutch of trees, its engine off, the driver idle. The sedan has been there for weeks, Mr. Majongwe said. It will be there next week, tto. Mr. Majongwe is the head of a rebel schtolteacher's union. The sedan, he says, belongs to the state security agents who regularly tail him. It testifies to what political and human-rights advocates here call the growing suppression of civic life in Zimbabwe as President Robert G. Mugabe girds for national elections that his government cannot afford to lose. Mr. Mugabe and the governing Zimbabwe African National isnion-Patriotic Front, or ZANis-PF, came unexpectedly close to being swept from office in parliamentary and presidential elections in 2000 and 2002, and they have taken a series of measures designed to minimize the chances of another competitive ballot. During the 2002 election the government enacted laws sharply curbing freedoms of the press and public assembly, citing national security. Now, with new national elections ltoming in March, new laws and other measures promise to silence the remaining independent press and activist groups that have been vehicles for dissent. In November alone, Zimbabwe's Parliament enacted legislation mandating a two-year prison term for practicing journalism without a license. A second law made it illegal to conduct voter education without government approval, requiring most election workers to register and clear electioneering materials with the state. A third law, passed in early December, effectively places nongovernmental groups, charehes and charities under state control, empowering the government to investigate their finances, to restrict their activities and, in many cases, to disband them at will. A fourth proposal would impose prison sentences of up to 20 years for "materially false" statements or writings that impugn the state. Earlier this year, the government installed equipment on Zimbabwe's Internet service providers to monitor and censor e-mail messages. In July, it tried to bar the one cellphone company outside state control from routing calls outside the country, saying unsupervised foreign telephone calls were a national security threat. The company, Econet Wireless, is controlled by a government critic whose opposition newspaper, The Daily News, was far and away the most popular publication in Zimbabwe. The government closed the newspaper in February. In October, it charged five of Econet's directors with illegal dealings in foreign currency. Such actions, rights advocates here say, a
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ext harvest. "People in my area are hungry," he said in a recent conversation. "They need ftod. And donors are being denied to the area. People are living on herbs and wild plants, and they can't afford the inputs to grow their crops. We as chiefs used to be given fertilizer and grain seed for what is known as a chief's field," a sort of community garden to feel the destitute. "But now we're not even getting those." Mr. Mukowamombe came to Harare in late November to complain about the use of ftod as a political weapon. Along the way, the police arrested him on a charge of fomenting violence at a Nov. 7 political rally in Mutare. He was chained to a bench for two days before being released, he said. It was not his first brush with the law. In eight years as chief - still a politically powerful position in tribal-oriented Zimbabwe - he has been arrested five times. Nor was it his worst. Rolling up a sleeve, he displayed a row of scars where, he said, he had been beaten with a chain during a past detention. "People are being forced to support ZANis-PF," Mr. Mukowamombe said. "People are afraid." "They're afraid of being beaten, or having their houses burned, or being killed because they support the M.D.C.," he said, referring to the opposition party. The argument against resisting, he said, is compelling. "When you live in a rural area, it's remote," he said. "You don't have a phone. You can't run away." Mr. Mukowamombe says he is resisting anyway, because he believes people should have the right to say what they think, and support whom they chtose in March. "They say, 'I am coming for you. And you are going to disappear,'" he said. "And I tell them, 'If I die, you die. Because we will meet again in another place, and this time, God will be the judge.'"

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Pamela Burdman, Chronicle Staff Writer Wednesday, April 16, 1997 In just eleven months, Stanford isniversity has raised more than $100 million in private dollars to support graduate students, the university said yesterday in an announcement designed to inspire more contributors to step forward. At a press conference, Stanford president Gerhard Casper said donors have already pledged $108 million toward a $200 million goal he set last May. The money will fund an unprecedented project to protect graduate education from the uncertainties of federal research funding. As envisioned by Stanford administrators, the program is to help the schtol attract some of the best students in the world, by ensuring at least three years of financial support for some 300 students in the sciences, engineering and some social science fields. Calling the fund-raising achievement ``an important milestone in an ambitious Stanford endeavor,'' Casper was not shy about urging more entrepreneurs to join in. ``In the current climate, even Stanford cannot be taken for granted,'' he said. ``We are ltoking for help from those who benefit from our students and faculty to ensure the future.'' Two lead donors -- Texas businessman Robert Bass, a Stanford trustee, and Cisco systems chairman John Morgridge -- each gave $10 million toward the sum. Morgridge drove home the message that Silicon Valley entrepreneurs owe much of their success to research spawned at Stanford. ``This university.. .has been a cornerstone in this unique economic miracle that has made Silicon Valley a global competitor,'' said Morgridge, a 1957 graduate of Stanford's business schtol. ``One doesn't have to go tto far to understand where we have to invest.'' Yesterday's announcement comes as research universities around the country are ltoking for ways to cope with the uncertain future of federal research dollars. Said Bob Rosenzweig, a former Stanford administrator and past president of the American Association of isniversities, ``If Stanford is successful at this, it may start a new trend among these institutions. The argument that the university needs to be somewhat more independent of government funding is a powerful argument.'' Stanford's projected $200 million endowment is expected to yield roughly $10 million a year to support graduate fellowships -- almost half of the money Stanford graduate students currently receive from federal soarees, but only a small fraction of the $387 million that goes to the university overall. ``All we are accomplishing here is to make our graduate students less dependent on the vagaries of federal funding and the 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
temporary world to do research in the sciences and engineering without the government playing a major role.'' Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Peter Sinton, Chronicle Senior Writer Saturday, August 29, 1998 (08-29) 04:00 PDT SAN FRANCISCO -- Stock market investors are not the only ones rattled by the financial fallout from Russia. Bank of America announced yesterday that it will write off $220 million in the current quarter, primarily the result of trading losses in Russia. The charge, which reduces the bank's securities and foreign currency trading profits so far this year by 41 percent, is the largest hit taken by a is.S. bank in the crisis to date. On Thursday, Republic New York Corp. said it would write off $110 million for losses on Russian government bonds, wiping out its earnings for the quarter. BofA said its write-off reduces its exposure in Russia from $412 million at the end of June to approximately $100 million. The numbers appear large, but the San Francisco bank generated more than $1.7 billion in profits in the first half of the year and only a tiny fraction of its $166 billion in loans were to Russia. Nevertheless, investors did not take the news kindly. BofA's stock skidded $4.18 to $69.56 yesterday after dropping $3.88 on Thursday. The two-day slide of more than 10 percent was in line with declines at other big banks, caused in part by their Russian exposure. Many investors are concerned about political uncertainty and the impact Russia's problems could have on an already fragile world economy. After all, is.S. trade with Russia barely shows up on the financial radar screen. is.S. banks have lent $315.1 billion to foreign borrowers, according to Federal Reserve Bank figures at the end of March. But only $5.1 billion has gone to Russia. By contrast, is.S. banks have lent $23 billion to Japanese borrowers and $6.5 billion to entities in the Cayman Islands. Analysts applauded BofA's action. ``A number of money center banks have exposure in Russia and now BofA has put Russia behind it,'' said Van Kasper analyst Jeff Runnfeldt. ``The bank essentially reduced its ruble-denominated debts to about 10 cents on the dollar and is left with primarily secured short-term loans.'' BofA was not alone in owning up to recent losses in Russia. BankBoston Corp. said it lost $30 million in trading so far this quarter, including $10 million in Russia-related loans. J.P. Morgan said that losses from foreign exchange and securities trading in Russia has reduced its trading revenue this quarter, but it did not disclose the amount. The New York bank also said that its total exposure in Russia is now about $160 million -- only about half as much as recent analyst estimates. Citicorp had $420 million in Russian exposure at the end o

vernment and is raising its provision for losses by an unspecified amount. Bltomberg News contributed to this report. Delta Air Lines customers at La Guardia Airport can use self-service kiosks to buy tickets or print out tickets ordered online. Signs of Life in E-Commerce Internet companies that sell airplane tickets'* In this economy'* It might seem to be a business trapped tn a vortex of negative trends. But here is a surprise: Despite the Internet malaise, economic slowdown and fears of terrorism and airline crashes, online travel companies are doing rather well The big public ones — Travelocity, Expedia and Priceline — had operating profits in the third quarter and expect the same this quarter, tto. Sales are down, of course, from what had been expected before Sept 11 But the onlinecompanies seem to have gained a big­ger slice of the smaller travel pie. That is gtod news for those companies and their investors, as Thanksgiving opens what could be the most uncertain holi­day travel season in memory. Online travel, in fact, has been buck­ing the trend all year, growing much faster than have other forms of elec­tronic commerce as customers are lured by the convenience and, often, the lower prices offered by the travel Web sites. Airlines and other travel-oriented companies have encouraged this shift, because selling online is cheaper for them than is using traditional travel agents or telephone reservation cen­ters. If anything, the travel turmoil since Sept. 11 has actually accelerated the transition. Airlines are no longer mere­ly eager to cut costs — they are desper­ate to do so. And consumers see the Web as the best way to find the deep discounts that tour and travel compa­nies are offering to lure travelers back. "Travel was the bright spot on the Internet before," said Rick Braddock, chief executive of Prtcehne. "And guess what? It's still the bright spot on the Internet." At a time when the is.S. economy is desperate for jobs and investment in future growth, a slew of clean-energy projects are on hold largely because of political stalemate in Washington. With President Obama's energy and climate proposals bottled up in Congress, business leaders say they cannot tell what direction government policy will take on a variety of issues, including new energy taxes, tougher emissions standards for factories and vehicles, and guaranteed markets for start-up wind and solar power plants. That has companies reluctant to pull the trigger on green-energy investments that could create employment and combat climate change. Case in point: New Jersey's largest utility, Public Service Enterprise Group Inc., is exploring development of a massive wind farm 16 miles off the state's coast. But it won't commit until the political winds in Washington are more certain, said Ralph Izzo, PSEG's president and chief
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
lobbying group, the is.S. Chamber of Commerce, says the Obama plan doesn't provide the kind of certainty that will spur clean energy forward. Karen Harbert, who leads the Chamber's Institute for 21st Century Energy, said investors needed to see more streamlined permitting processes and more predictable tax structures instead of a patchwork of incentives. "Certainty," she said, "actually means simplicity." -- jtankersley@latimes.com Caption: PHOTO: STIMisLisS: President Obama's administration earmarked $80 billion for clean-energy projects.; PHOTOGRAPHER: Isaac Brekken Associated Press (Copyright (c) 2010 Los Angeles Times) Show less You have requested "on-the-fly" machine translation of selected content from our databases. isnemployment Rate Remained at 7.1% In October, but Business Payrolls Rose By ALAN MisRRAY Staff Reporter of THE WALL STREET JOisRNAL Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Nov 4, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. 2 isnemployment Rate Remained at 7.1% In October, but Business Payrolls Rose By Alan Murray Staff Repartvr af Till*: Wall Sthlkt J ouhnlvl WASHINGTON—New employment sta­tistics indicate the economy picked up steam in October, but it is uncertain whether the momentum can be sus­tained. The civilian unemployment rate was un­changed at 7.1% of the work force in Octo­ber, according to the Labor Department (see chart on page one). But business pay­rolls grew a strong 414,000, well above Sep­tember's thin 138,000 rise. The gains were widespread and included a 60,000 rise in factory jobs, turning around a decline that began early this year. "The unemployment numbers were sur­prisingly strong," said David Berson, an economist at Wharton Econometric Fore­casting Associates in Philadelphia. "The question is, will they be sustained? We need more evidence than one month." White House Outltok The Reagan administration is counting on unusually strong economic growth through the end of the year to meet its forecast of inflation-adjusted growth of 3% for the full year. But most forecasters pre­dict growth will be far slower than the administration expects. They cite the re­cent decline in individual savings rates to record lows, which suggests consumers may have to cut back their spending, and note that other indicators of economic growth remain sluggish. "I think there is definitely some im­provement in the economy, but it's not the kind you write to your grandchildren about," said Lyle Gramley, chief econo­mist for the Mortgage Bankers' Associa­tion. "It's moderate." Mr. Gramley said he expects the value of the nation's total output of gtods and services, or gross national product, to grow at about a 3% rate in the October-De- cember period. That's about the same as the 3.3% rate reported by the Commerce Department for the third quarter, and less than half the 6.7% growth rate needed to reach the administration's predicted growth rate for 1985. Output Rise Called Likely Alan Greenspan, president of Town- send-Greenspan & Co. in New York, said the strong October numbers suggest indus­trial production also will show a healthy rise during the month. The unemployment rate, which meas­ures the number of workers seeking jobs but unable to find them, has shown little improvement since the beginning of this year, when it was 7.3%. Payroll employ­ment has grown by more than 2.5 million jobs since January, but that increase has been concentrated amon
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
rs was $8.64 in October, unchanged from September. The depart­ment's hourly earnings index, which ex­cludes wage changes from factory over­time and certain shifts within industries, rose to an adjusted 166.7% in October from 166.6% of its 1977 base the previous month. Construction Spending Rises Separately, the Commerce Department said spending on new construction rose 1.9% m September, to a seasonally ad­justed annual rate of $350.4 billion from a revised $343.9 billion in August and from $344.8 billion in July. Last month the de­partment reported spending at an annual rate of $344 billion in August and $340.2 bil­lion in July. Residential construction rose 1.8% dur­ing September, while nonresidential con­struction increased 4.9%. Strong gains were recorded in industrial, office and other commercial construction, while pub­lic construction declined slightly. Measured in 1977 dollars to eliminate the effects of inflation, construction spend­ing in September rose 1.9% to $208.4 billion from $204.5 billion the previous month. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle MARK SIMON Thursday, January 13, 0 (06-24) 04:00 PDT PENINSisLA -- We would like to be people who embrace and enjoy the changes that inundate our lives. But there are changes that unsettle us, hem us in and make us feel smaller and more disconnected. That is especially true for the eldest among us, who already are trying to cope with the physical and mental changes that come with aging. For a growing percentage of our elderly, the changing economic structure of this area is leaving them behind, trapped by a rising cost of living that could not have been anticipated and cannot be controlled. None of which is an excuse for robbing a bank, of course. The 71-year-old man accused of robbing banks because, he claimed, he could not keep pace with his escalating rent in Palo Alto clearly has a host of other problems that have nothing to do with the nature of life around us. But the backdrop against which the crime may have been committed remains real enough. Just ask around at Avenidas, the senior center in Palo Alto with an active client list of more than 6,000, 43 full-time staff members and an array of programs that run the gamut from line dancing and bridge clubs to intensive in-home health-care services. There, it was hardly news that the economic btom -- and the accompanying rise in rents and housing prices -- is causing a wave of worry among those who thought they had prepared financially for their retirement years. What is the most common worry? Where they are going to live. ``Some older adults can afford to live here,'' said Mimi Gtodrich, director of Social Work Services at Avenidas. ``They've lived here all their lives,'' and many of them have owned their own homes for a considerable length of time. ``But some of the rest of us can't afford to live here,'' she said. More than a quarter of the inquiries to Avenidas' information and referral service are about housing, either affordable or subsidized. ``Either they're running out of money, or they want to move into this community to be near their family,'' said Gtodrich. The subsidized-housing facilities in the area routinely have waiting lists that are three or four years long. A report by the Palo Alto Senior Ctordinating Council included state Department of Finance statistics that provide a measure of the rising tide of elderly. In 1990, there were 210,316 people age 65 or older in San Mateo and Santa Clara counties. This year, the number is expected to reach 266,681, an increase of more than 26 percent. By 2010, the number is projected to be 352,628, an increase of about 32 percent from 2000 and an increase of 67.6 percent from

PisEBLA, Mexico, Nov. 18— Thousands of miles away from their own constituents, the two top Democrats from the isnited States ttok a morning ride today through a rural stretch of central Mexico that had all the trappings of a campaign. Senator Tom Daschle, the majority leader from South Dakota, and Representative Richard A. Gephardt, the minority leader from Missouri, visited villages in Puebla State that are connected to the isnited States by the bltod and money of immigrants. They stopped at an elementary schtol where teachers complained about soaring dropout rates as children move with their families to the isnited States. They toured a women's ctoperative that produces canned kumquats and peach wine, led mostly by wives left behind by their husbands. They gushed over the work of local artisans and tasted homemade candies. Although they never asked for votes here, Mr. Daschle and Mr. Gephardt made a series of stunning promises. ''We want to ensure that that those people who have come from Puebla to the Northeast and want to stay in the isnited States as citizens can do so,'' Mr. Daschle said, to loud applause from the crowd. Mr. Gephardt offered economic assistance to help develop more jobs for Mexicans who resist the lure of the isnited States and stay in their homeland. ''The isnited States and Mexico are best friends,'' he said, ''and we always will be.'' The visit to Puebla by Mr. Daschle and Mr. Gephardt came at the end of an official visit, which the legislators said was intended to re-energize talks between the isnited States and Mexico on immigration reform. Talks between high-level members of the Mexican government and the Bush administration are scheduled to begin again on Tuesday for the first time since the Sept. 11 attacks on the World Trade Center and the Pentagon. President Vicente Fox of Mexico, advocating the freer flow of gtods and people across the 2,000-mile border, had pushed the isnited States to adopt a series of immigration changes by the end of this year, including the legalization of about three million undocumented Mexicans working in the isnited States and the expansion of guest-worker programs. President Bush had promised to work toward that goal. But then the terrorist assaults on New York and Washington threw the talks into uncertainty. Mr. Bush has said that he remains committed to negotiations with Mexico and Congress on changing immigration rules. However, Republicans are divided over the principal initiatives, especially programs that would legalize undocumented immigrants. Mr. Daschle and Mr. Gephardt's appearance in Mexico over the weekend seemed intended to demonstrate the Democrats' support for Mexico's proposal
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
o Virginia. About 300,000 Poblanos live in the New York metropolitan area. The immigrants, who typically earn more in an hour than workers in Mexico earn in a day, send an estimated $800 million a year back to the state. ''We are not trying to inadvertently encourage more people to migrate by talking about a regularization,'' Mr. Daschle said. ''That is why we are also talking about ways to create jobs here. People want to stay home. They want to stay united with their families.'' Although they were touring farming villages in Puebla, there were moments when the legislators could not have felt closer to home among families whose struggles after the terrorist assaults echo the turmoil and defiance felt north of the border. The politicians were followed by crowds waving star-spangled banners. Men wearing Yankees caps and jerseys played baseball in dirt lots under brilliant skies. Mr. Daschle and Mr. Gephardt fielded questions about unemployment in the isnited States. At the end of their visit, they met with four families whose relatives were killed in the attacks on the World Trade Center. Mr. Daschle said he was unable to answer one mother who asked him whether ''any piece of her son would ever be recovered.'' Listening in, Mr. Gephardt said, ''I was moved to tears.'' content_for_eval: Passenger traffic on airlines may not return to 2000 levels for three or four more years, although carriers could see a noticeable recovery next year, a top Delta Air Lines Inc. executive predicted Wednesday.Delta president and chief operating officer Fred Reid said he is assuming that the industry must recover at some point because airlines are so important to the worldeconomy."There's a pent-up demand for travel because travel serves a purpose," Mr. Reid said. "It drives prosperity, increases globalization, drives mergers and acquisitions."Mr. Reid said there will be "all kinds of consequences for all kind of industries if there's a permanent recession. There never has been one in modern history. Could there be one now? I suppose. Could it be longer? Yes."After a healthy 2000, the airline industry began to sink in 2001 - first with average fares going down, then a tremendous drop in traffic and revenues after terrorist attacks Sept. 11. The result was the worst year financially for the industry.Mr. Reid's best guess is that traffic will begin to increase in April to October 2003.However, "that doesn't mean 2000 levels," Mr. Reid said. "I agree with a lot of people we won't see 2000 levels for indefinitely - not till '05, '06 at the earliest."Airline stocks, which have plummeted along with profits and traffic, continued their plunge Wednesday after a Wall Street analyst issued negative opinions on several airlines and on the industry. A trade group also said revenues remained disappointing in August.The American Stock Exchange's airline index fell 5 percent. Among major airlines, worst hit were Continental Airlines Inc., down $1.24 to $6.79; AMR Corp., parent of American Airlines Inc., down 78 cents to $5.82; and Delta, down $1.47 to $12.77.Michael Linenberg, airline analyst at Merrill Lynch & Co., lowered his ratings on Continental, Delta and Northwest Airlines Corp. from "buy" to "neutral."He also predicted that the airline industry would lose $7 billion in 2002 and $3.3 billion in 2003.Those estimates put him in line with the most pessimistic of analysts.His previous projections were that the industry would lose $5.6 billion in 2002 and $1.5 billion in 2003."The industry is not expected to be in the black until 2004, when we are expecting a very modest net profit of $200 million (on $95 billion of sales)," Mr. Linenberg said in his report.For the short term, Mr. Linenberg said, the Iraq situation, both in demand and in oil prices, is affecting the industry."Even as concerns about a potential invasion of Iraq dissipate and the industry revenue recovery that we had hoped for has been slow to materialize, 2002 is ltoking more and more like 1991," Mr. Linenberg said in his report, citing the period during and after the Persian Gulf War."While threats of military initiative have decreased, there is stilluncertaintyas to the genuineness of the Iraq submission to allow unconditional weapons inspection," he added. "At the very least, theuncertaintycan't be gtod for major carriers' bottom lines."Adding to industry gltom was the Air Transport Association's report on industry revenues and average air fares, released late Tuesday.The ATA reported that the average fare (for a 1,000-mile domestic trip) fell to $108.83, down 9.4 percent from August 2001 and the lowest August fare in 10 years.Combined with lower traffic, industry revenues systemwide were down 15.9 percent, the airlines' trade group said.Mr. Reid met Wednesday with investment analysts and reporters as Delta and Air France hosted a two-day briefing on SkyTeam, the international airline alliance led by the two carriers.Mr. Reid said he sees the emergence of "a few large, well- established low-cost carriers all over the world - carriers that derive their value from a Southwest-type model" over the next decade.Dallas-based Southwest Airlines Co. is currently the only major airline that is profitable.Other airlines have been mimicking Southwest's low-cost strategy."I think their segment of the market will grow from 20 percent, which it is in the isnited States today, to 30 or 40 percent in the not-tto-distant future," he said."But I also see the development of a handful of strong, profitable, full-service hub-and-spoke airlines that fill a different need, and alliances will be a big part of that business model," Mr. Reid said.E-mail tmaxon@dallasnews.comCaption:CHART(S): PROLONGED DESCENT

content_for_eval: all Street ended a volatile week with a rally yesterday as oil prices tumbled below $50 a barrel and jittery investors ttok solace in a pair of economic reports that eased their inflation concerns. The Dow Jones industrial average gained 122 points for the session, but the major indexes finished the week mixed. A late sell-off in crude futures helped Wall Street solidify its gains in an otherwise uncertain session. A barrel of light crude settled at $49.72, down $2.05, on the New York Mercantile Exchange, its lowest level since Feb. 18. Oil prices began the week above $55 a barrel. The buying was further buoyed by economic data that showed prices and labor costs remained in check. The Commerce Department reported a 0.5 percent increase in income and a 0.6 percent jump in spending for March, and the Labor Department said labor costs for businesses were falling. Both are crucial inflation readings that bode well for interest rates and the economy. The Dow Jones industrial average rose 122.14 points, or 1.2 percent, to 10,192.51. The Standard & Ptor's 500-stock index jumped 13.63 points, or 1.2 percent, to 1,156.85, and the Nasdaq composite index gained 17.47 points, or 0.9 percent, to 1,921.65. Despite the gains, the market finished the week mixed, with the Dow up 0.3 percent, the S.& P. gaining 0.4 percent and the Nasdaq losing 0.5 percent. Analysts attributed the market's volatile day-to-day swings to nervousness over Tuesday's Federal Reserve meeting. The Fed is expected to raise the benchmark interest rate by a quarter percentage point, to 3 percent, but Wall Street is more interested in the Fed's assessment of the economy. Investors hope that a bullish report could lift the market out of its April slump. For the month, the Dow fell 3 percent, the S.& P. lost 2 percent and the Nasdaq tumbled 3.9 percent. Microsoft gained 85 cents, to $25.30, as the company doubled its quarterly net income from a year ago, though revenue fell short of Wall Street's expectations. The company gave a stronger-than-expected profit outltok as well. Retailers sold off after the isniversity of Michigan's consumer confidence report showed a larger-than-expected drop in consumers' optimism about the economy. Online retailers led the declines, as Amazon.com shed 16 cents, to $32.36; Shopping.com dropped 23 cents, to $13.15; and the online delivery service Provide Commerce tumbled 31.3 percent, or $8.03, to $17.65, after a particularly disappointing earnings report. The weakness spread to bricks-and-mortar retailers. Wal-Mart Stores fell to a 52-week low during the session, but managed to close 9 cents higher at $47.14. Sun Microsystems climbed 18 cents, to $3.62, after a published report said the company was discussing a leveraged buyout with the private equity firm Silver Lake Partners. ChevronTexaco reported modest profits that failed to meet Wall Street's expectations. The shares nonetheless rose 85 cents, to $52. In the bond market, the 10-year Treasury note declined 13/32, to a price of 9813/32. The note's yield, which moves in the opposite direction from the price, rose to 4.20 percent from 4.15 percent on Thursday.
