content_for_eval: Clinton Focusing on the World Economy: How a summit meeting in Naples may mean jobs for A] Clinton Focusing on the World Economy By MICHAEL WINES Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, July 5 — President Clinton declared today that isnited States security and the success of his Presidency are tied to economic growth worldwide, and that the major industrial nations have a rare chance "to create jobs in a world of prosperi­ty." Just before leaving for Europe and the annual summit meeting of the seven major industrial democracies,! Mr. Clinton used a gathering of busi­ness leaders and diplomats at the Commerce Department to make the case that his policies have revived the economy and asserted global leader­ship. "Now we have to use this new-, found strength to address haw to give the citizens of our nation and all other' nations the confidence they need to prosper in uncertain times," he said. "We have to move from coping with crises to planning for prosperity. "In other words, we have to lay the foundation for the 21st-century econ­omy, one in which change will be the order of the day, and the real question will be whether change is our friend or our enemy." Begins Eight-Day Trip Mr. Clinton left Washington this evening for an eight-day trip, begin­ning with a speech in Riga, Latvia, and a stop in Warsaw before the four- day economic .summit meeting be­gins Friday in Naples. Afterward he will stop in Bonn and Berlin, giving a speech at the Brandenburg Gate, which once marked the line between the free and Communist sectors. Today Mr. Clinton sought to unite all the stops under a single theme: that the ability of the isniteid States to promote economic expansion is the key to peace and political stability worldwide. That message plays to the Administration's foreign-policy strengths — increasing trade and in­vestment — and by implication plays down its changeable and less fruitful military policies in Haiti and Bosnia. Asserting pointedly that a "decade of drift" in Washington's fiscal policy had made it impossible for the indus­trial powers to take concerted eco­nomic actions, Mr. Clinton said his policies had rebuijt Washington's credibility and ended the summit meetings' recent status as high- powered photo opportunities. "For a decade, our out-of-control budget deficit robbed us of the stand­ing to press our partners to act," he said. "Well now, instead of having the biggest deficit in the G-7. we have among the smallest." G-7 is bureau­cratic shorthand for the Group of. Seven, as the members style them­selves. Mr. Clinton was referring to one common yardstick of government debt, the ratio between a nation's annual budget deficit and its gross How a summit meeting in Naples may mean jobs for Americans. domestic product. In absolute terms, the isnited States' $4.5 trillion Fed­eral debt and its $200 billion-or-so deficit for the current fiscal year dwarf those of the six other countries. The President also pointed to a tentative accord lowering trade bar­riers and the North American Free Trade Agreement as evidence that he has etched his foreign policy in eco­nomic terms. Indeed he said "the purpose of my. Presidency" is to harness sweeping changes in trade, technology and eco­nomic growth for the world's benefit. Mr. Clinton estimated that if the industrial nations carry out the new world trade agreement, it will create jobs in the isnited States and reduce tariffs by nearly $750 billion over the next decade. "We have shown that bit by bit, year by year, the decisions made at these G-7 meetings can change the world," he added. • Mr. Clinton said the leaders are also to begin planning what he called the "nerve system" of the new econ­omy — global computer networks to exchange ideas and money. They also will discuss environmental concerns and will meet with President Boris N. Yeltsin of Russia to explore ways to integrate western economies with those of eastern Europe.

Toy-Making in China Is Losing Some Appeal: isncertainties Worry Hong ... Auerbach, Stuart The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Dec 2, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) Pg- Dll By Stuart Auerbach WuHnfton Port Suit Writer HONG KONG-Over the past 10 years, factories in China owned by Hong Kong companies have been Santa's workshops for American children, turning out toys such as this year's hit. the Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtle. ~ Now, these toymakers are debat­ing whether to stop building new factories in China in favor of start­ing operations in other low-wage countries in Asia such as Malaysia and Thailand—a move that poses major threats to the closely-knit economies of both Hong Kong and China. The toymakers are being pushed both by the economic austerity pro­gram of China's leaders, who have cut back many of the privileges of Guangdong province, where most of the toy factories are located, and by the June 4 crackdown on student dissidents in Tiananmen Square. "People just don't want 100 per­cent of their soareing from China, which I think is very prudent," said Stuart H. Leckie, managing direc­tor of the Hong Kong office of Wy­att Co., a global consulting firm. "Toy companies are thinking of expanding to other places," added Dennis Ting, chairman of the Hong Kong Toy Council. The mtod of the toymakers sym­bolizes the disenchantment of for­eign investors with China since June. They are not pulling out but are not increasing their investment in a country that as recently as she months ago was luring foreign com­panies with its vast market of more than 1 billion people. Hong Kong companies are Chi­na's largest foreign investors with most of the money put into small factories doing labor-intensive man­ufacturing in Guangdong, the prov­ince closest to Hong Kong that has been the engine of China's export drive and its economic growth. There are about twice as many workers employed in Hong Kong- owned factories in Guangdong than in manufacturing plants in Hong Kong.. That Pearl River delta area, the richest sector of China, became afactory suburb to Hong Kong after labor shortages there drove up wages to the point where the crown colony was no longer competitive in many manufacturing sectors. Hong Kong, in turn, has retained the high value-added, capital-intensive por­tions of manufacturing as well as in­creasing its position as a center for financing, transportation and prod­uct design in the region. Already the signs of economic slowdown abound in Guangdong, adding to Hong Kong's worries about its future after the 5.7 mil­lion-person British colony is turned over to China in 1997. American businessman John T. Kamm sa
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 members do most of their manufacturing in the crown colony, has sponsored trips to the Philippines, Malaysia and Thailandduring the past three months to study the climate for new investments in those Asian nations. One of their con­cerns is that export-control rules will cut their access to Western technolo­gy once China takes over. Hong Kong's economy was hit bad­ly in the immediate aftermath of the June 4 crackdown in Beijing, but it has rebounded with remarkable resil­ience. "If you had asked me several months ago I wouldn't have predicted things would bounce back as well as they did," said Wyatt's Leckie in a mid-November interview here. Still, Hong Kong Finance Secretary Sir Piers Jacobs acknowledged in an October speech to the Asia Society in New York, "A lot of illusions were shattered and it will take time to pick up the pieces." The American Chamber of Com­merce here called the economic out­ltok for Hong Kong "uncertain," with' high inflation and high interest rates. Stock prices have rebounded after losing 22 percent of their value imme­diately after June 4, and the falloff in tourism and hotel occupancy rates ap­pears td be abating. Real estate pric­es are spotty. All this is affecting Hong Kong's growth rate, which has averaged 8.3 percent a year over the past 10 years. But the government has been revis­ing downward its forecasts for 1989 growth, with the latest estimate is­sued in August projecting 5 percent growth compared with. 7.4 percent last year. And growth next year is ex­pected to be lower. But the government is maintaining a stiff upper lip, declaring that Hong Kong has faced adversity before and always managed to bounce back. As depicted by [Tom Segev], the standoff between [Levi Eshkol] and his generals was as much cultural as strategic. Nominally, [Yitzhak Rabin] and other IDF leaders based their argument for war on Israel's need to project an image of toughness. Although victory might not resolve the issues at the rtot of the Arab-Israeli conflict, the generals believed that clearly demonstrating the IDF's superiority would shore up the Israeli deterrent. Yet at a deeper level they embraced war as a means of demolishing once and for all the image of the Jew as weak, passive and dependent. Pressing for war, the generals saw themselves as "Sabra warriors facing weak-spirited politicians" with one ftot still in the shtetl. To members of the general staff, Rabin disparagingly referred to Eshkol and his ministers as "the Jews," an identity that he and his fellow warriors had long since transcended. Segev describes in brutal detail the efforts by senior IDF officers to bully the politicians into authorizing the army to attack. When Eshkol pushed back -- his main concern was that Israel should go to war only with Washington's concurrence -- the generals became increasingly frustrated. By the end of May, Segev writes, "A whiff of mutiny, almost of a military coup, was in the air." Ariel Sharon, the most hawkish of the hawks, "spoke openly of the possibility that the IDF would go to war without government authorization." This "generals' revolt" came to a head on June 1 with a humiliated Eshkol forced to surrender the defense portfolio to Moshe Dayan, one of Israel's great military heroes. In the contest over who would dictate the course of Israeli policy, the generals had prevailed. To an American reader, Segev's account of relations between Washington and Jerusalem qualifies as a btok within a btok. In our day, merely to cite the existence of an "Israel lobby" is invite charges of anti-Semitism. In the 1960s, the Israeli government harbored no doubts as to the lobby's existence or its importance. On sensitive issues the actual management of is.S.-Israeli relations occurred through informal channels with Americans who were sympathetic to Israel and enjoyed access to the centers of is.S. power. Besides [Abe Fortas], other key figures included movie mogul Arthur Krim, a frequent White House guest, and CIA counterespionage chief James Jesus Angleton, described by the head of the Mossad as "the biggest Zionist of the lot" and "an extraordinary asset for us." As depicted by [Tom Segev], the standoff between [Levi Eshkol] and his generals was as much cultural as strategic. rge questions that touch on national identity, personal ambition, civilian-military politics and the elusive utility of force. content_for_eval: BRisSSELS, Belgium -- The top trade negotiators for the isnited States and the European Community said Thursday that they had made important, potentially decisive progress on a range of issues, including the nettlesome matter of farm exports, that could lead to a world trade accord."We have agreed upon an approach to all major issues that would pave the way to the completion of a substantive and comprehensive isruguay Round,' is.S. Trade Representative Mickey Kantor said in Brussels, referring to the negotiations on updating and broadening world trade rules that began seven years ago in isruguay.The talks, under the auspices of the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade, are widely see as crucial foreconomicand political reasons. Success could help revive moribundeconomiesin Japan and Europe, and stimulateuncertaineconomicrecoveries in the isnited States and Britain.On a political level, a failure to reach a trade agreement would put further strains on the postwar alliance between Western Europe and the isnited States that is adjusting imperfectly to the end of the Cold War.Neither Mr. Kantor nor his European counterpart, Sir Leon Brittan, gave significant details in public of the progress they said was achieved in two days of talks, and French Foreign Minister Alain Juppe, whose government has most strenuously objected to the agricultural provisions proposed, later sounded a note of caution."The basis of an overall agreement has not yet been laid,' Mr. Juppe said. Nonetheless, he added that "we have emerged from the deep chill that had descended on relations between the community and the isnited States.'Officials close to the discussions said the most important progress was made on agriculture, where France's objections to the Blair House accord of last year, calling for a 21 percent cut over six years in the volume of community exports of subsidized grain and other ftod, have badly hurt relations with the isnited States.Thursday, Mr. Kantor said, "We have formulated an approach which would successfully remove all the issues in the area ofagriculture.'Behind the posturing in a complex and politically sensitive negotiation that has entered its decisive phase, Mr. Kantor's visit had appeared to generate significant momentum toward an agreement, particularly in the long-contested area of farm trade -- though without achieving final resolution of any issue.The is.S. official said he would return Monday to Brussels after conferring with President Clinton, for what he and Sir Leon portrayed as conclusive negotiations."We are, I hope, in the final strait,' the European trade chief said.

A 1997 national voter survey by the Polling Company finds that the majority of respondents would prefer to undergo a rtot canal than be audited by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS). And a 1990 People magazine survey finds that the most frightening words people could imagine hearing when they answer the phone are "This is the IRS calling."Although Americans have every right to be upset by an oppressive tax system, their anger should not bedirected at the IRS. The vast majority of problems with the current tax system are the result of 85 years of bad tax policy.The way to reduce the public's intense aversion to the IRS is to enact a flat tax. By wiping out all the complicated, obscure, and convoluted provisions of the current tax code, a flat tax would reduce compliance costs and ease theuncertaintyand anguish that make April 15 everyone's least favorite day of the year. he number of changes in the tax code accompanying the 1997 tax cut.* 285 = The number of new sections in the tax code created by the 1997 budget act.* 3,132 = The number of pages needed by the Research Institute of America to explain the changes in the tax law in 1997.* 11,410 = The number of tax code subsection changes between 1981 and 1997.* 160 = The percentage increase in the stock value of tax preparation firms in the month period surrounding enactment of the 1997 budget.* 54 = The number of lines on the new capital gains form, up from 23 before the 1997 budget deal.* $3,500 = The amount one woman was forced to pay twice, even though the IRS eventually admitted that the debt had been owed - and paid - by her former husband.* $26 = The amount the IRS seized from a six-year-old's bank account because her parents owed money.* $900,000 = The amount a small businessman was fined after being entrapped by his accountant, a paid informer for the IRS.* 25 = The percentage of households with incomes over $50,000 that would pay an inaccurate assessment from the IRS rather than fight.The only way to address these problems is fundamental reform. A flat tax that taxes all income - but only one time and at one low rate - will reduce the power of the IRS dramatically by eliminating the vast majority of possible conflicts. In a system that asked individuals to provide only their total income and the size of their families, much of the fear, frustration, anduncertaintysurrounding the payment of taxes would disappear.Daniel Mitchell is the senior fellow of politicaleconomyat the Heritage Foundation. Analysts say the latest developments threaten to crack Panama's already fragile dollar economy and severely impact business activity.' _ The 12 Panamanian banks that had been closed by a widespread^ general, strike early lak,week remained shuttered Friday follow­ing the indefinite suspension of all state banking operations by Panama's National Banking'Commission, the governmental reg­ulatory agency. Most of the operations of international banks also were closed, and no one was able to predict when the banks might reopen. This is the second liquidity crisis that the Panamanian banks have faced since June, when the opposition movement to the re­gime of Gen. Manuel Antonio Noriega was reinvigorated. But it is the first time the na­tion's banks have actually ceased operations. "This year has been a very, very bad one for Panama in many ways," said one leading Panamanian economist "Now, if there's no political solution, if Noriega refuses to step down, absolutely the economy is going to be horrible." "People have been attracted to Panama because of its political stability," said another economic analyst based in Panama. "Now, with concern about its instability, dollars are' leaving the country." The political climate has already had an impact; Credit Suisse, a Swiss bank, has an­nounced that it is pulling out of Panama. When it's her time, Jelica Stankovic would like to die peacefully in her own house, of natural causes, without any help from the gunmen who shot bullets through her windows.Some years ago, that was not an unrealistic expectation. Mrs. Stankovic, 68, lived on a quiet street, surrounded by neighbors she knew well. Now, many of those homes are gutted or ransacked. She's been threatened and robbed. Most of her neighbors have fled.Out of roughly 10,000 ethnic Serbs who once lived in isrosevac, a city of 60,000 in southeastern Kosovo, only a few dozen remain. Mrs. Stankovic and an adult daughter are among them. is.S. soldiers guard over them, day and night, from an observation post in their front yard.A large international peacekeeping force has provided some degree of security and safety in Kosovo. But just over three months since a is.S. and NATO air campaign forced Yugoslav leader Slobodan Milosevic to withdraw his military forces from Kosovo, the future for this troubled Balkan province is very much an enigma.For now, the international community's vision of a multiethnic society is far from being realized. And contrary to the dictates of the is.N. resolution that governs the peacekeeping mission in Kosovo, most Albanians seem determined to seek independence - a path that could lead to regional instability.Peacekeepers fear that Mr. Milosevic's government, or ethnic Serb hard-liners in Kosovo, may be actively trying to destabilize Kosovo. Organized crime is a serious problem. Ethnic violence still occurs.A long stay?When control of Kosovo was wrested from Mr. Milosevic, the province essentially was left without a civil government, organized police force and courts. The health-care system is marginal; is.S. military medical personnel have observed kitchen utensils being used by local doctors performing surgery. Theeconomywas shattered. Peacekeepers and is.N. officials are struggling to fill the void.What all of this may mean is a very long stay for is.S. forces and other international peacekeepers, who, as the afterglow of Kosovo's liberation fades, could face a much more complicated and hazardous task.Lt. Col. Michael D. Ellerbe, a battalion commander with the 82nd Airborne Division, commands is.S. paratrtopers in isrosevac. He is a veteran of deployments to Somalia and Haiti, and one lesson he's learned is that what seems to be a stable situation can change. He wants his trtops to be aware of that."Because we don't truly understand the people or the culture, we don't know when those conditions are going to change. . ." Col. Ellerbe said. Economy Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, June 29 — In a three-pronged indication of a weaken­ing economy, fresh reports today showed a plunge in new-home sales and unsettling, albeit moderate, de­clines in consumer confidence and the Government's chief forecasting index. Although most analysts continued to expect growth for the second half of the year to exceed that of the very sluggish first half, today's data prompted expressions of concern that the risks to further expansion had grown. 'Economy Could Turn Down' "The economy could turn down — or it could slow down" some more, said Larry Moran, a Commerce De­partment economist. He referred spe­cifically to the three-tenths of 1 per­cent decline in the department's in­dex of leading indicators for May, the fifth straight month in which the in­dex reversed direction. The index had risen a revised two-tenths of 1 per­cent in April. Home sales dropped so sharply in May — 21 percent — that a huge April gain was more than wiped away, the Government also reported. The plunge, the biggest in 13 years, came despite the fact that buyers enjoyedsome of the lowest interest rates m two decades and relatively appealing prices. In a third report, the Conference Board found that consumer confi­dence in June declined for the second straight month, with sentiment — the ptorest since October — falling for both current conditions and those in the months immediately ahead. "The continued low level of con­sumer confidence suggests that the economy remains weak," said Fabi­an Linden, compiler of the survey data for The Conference Board, a business-sponsored research group in New York. Although there have been some re­cent encouraging economic reports, including slackened inflation and a ninth consecutive month in which consumers felt comfortable enough to take on additional installment debt, uncertainty over the fate of the Ad­ministration's budget and health plans is widely cited as a soaree of restraint. "The fiscal package is pretty uu tere," observed David B. Bostian j: chief economist for the Wall Street firm of Herzog Heine Geduld. Mr. Bostian, who met privately with a top official at the Federal Reserve on Monday, said he got the impression that the official was disinclined to heed calls by some colleagues that the Fed should make a pre-emptive strike against inflation by raising Continued on Page D5 Reproduced with permission of t

e lower factory backlogs of unfilled orders for dura­ble gtods, a decline in new factory orders for consumer gtods, higher first-time claims for unemployment benefits, lower prices for materials especially sensitive to the business cycle and quicker deliveries by cor­porate suppliers. A jump in the money supply, the first in eight months, was the main soaree of upward pressure on the index. The others were higher con­tracts and orders for plant and equip­ment, a rise in building permits and an uptick in stock prices. The length of the average workweek, the 11th component, was unchanged. Just as worrisome to some ana­lysts as the leading index, whose com­ponents move ahead of the economy as a whole, is the performance of its companion, the coincident index. The coincident index, composed of indicators that tend to move at the same time as business activity, is only slightly higher, at 125.8, than it was in December — despite a four- tenths of 1 percent May gain. And it st&nds less than two. points above the level of March 1991, the month inwhich the latest recession officially ended. In other words, the economy has been going almost nowhere for some time as gauged by the items making up the coincident index — the size of nonfarm payrolls, personal incomes, industrial production and factory and trade sales. Still another indicator, the lagging index of elements that tend to move after a time lag in the direction of the coincident index, edged up one-tenth of 1 percent last month. It was the late 1960s, and these women were young and energetic, ready to take on the world. This was a first; they were an untested commodity. Many believed it would not work. The women would go to Vietnam and return quickly, unable to endure the hardships of covering warfare. But as "War Torn" shows, they didn't come back. They stayed and lived and loved and worried and watched a country imploding, and wrote about it. They told the stories that weren't being told. They spoke about the children. They spoke about the soldiers. They spoke about the utter destruction. They taught us about the people of Vietnam, their history, their customs. They talked of wayward sergeants, and the atrocities they committed, their arrogance fueled by the idea that nobody seemed to be watching. They brought a different lens to the war, one that wasn't dominated by notions of winning or losing, or blind patriotism or the glorification of killing and death. Their reporting was more complex, as was this war; it was full of contradictions. Vietnam was ripe with deception. It was hard to know who to trust, or who to be afraid of, so in a sense, everyone was suspect. Right and wrong got all mixed up there. Yet, these women tenaciously dug in their heels, and that was what nobody counted on. They refused to ltok away and tried to tell their story. It wasn't always easy. Village Voice reporter Judith Coburn remembers that her provocative stories so irritated the South Vietnamese and is.S. authorities that they refused to extend her visa. isndaunted, she fled to Cambodia and began writing for the Far East Economic Review. New York Times correspondent Gloria Emerson recalls being warned repeatedly by the is.S. embassy in Vietnam about her reportage. Even her editor at the Times pressured her. While visiting her in Saigon, he told her sternly, "You've done some gtod writing on what is wrong with the war, now tell us what is working." Astonished, Emerson replied that she simply could not find anything "gtod" about this war to report. Those of us who watched the Vietnam War on television still have trouble making sense of what we saw. The same images played over

al stories of war chronicle their heroic efforts to uncover the truth. Elaine Margolin is a frequent contributor to Btok magazine. Nor even has the host of problems that awaits the winner once the victory celebration ends.Those problems include rampant crime, budget deficits and dealing for the first time with the potentially overwhelming influence of the new gambling industry.In addition, voters have lost confidence in the Police Department (it is virtually a given that the department will be thoroughly shaken up, probably with a new chief) and they are hostile toward politicians who don't seem to share their disgust with City Hall's generally ptor performance."It's going to take some courage to be able to come to grips with these real problems," said Joseph Giarrusso, an at-large councilman and former police chief who is retiring from city politics."People are going to have to see something visible. They don't want to see a lot of plans and a lot of commissions formed."The next mayor also faces anuncertainrelationship with the City Council, which, thanks to a new term-limit rule, will have a majority of new members.Last week the field finally set itself:* Council member Lambert Boissiere Jr., with his new Tulane isniversity MBA degree to polish his business credentials.* 1st District Assessor Ken Carter, a product of the Central City BOLD organization.* Rep. Sherman Copelin Jr., the House speaker pro tem, whose business activities often land him in controversy.* Rep. Mitch Landrieu and Sen. Marc Morial, both sons of memorable New Orleans mayors.* Former mayoral candidate Donald Mintz, who is pushing the same reform agenda he did four years ago and,* Lawyer Roy Raspanti, who grabbed attention with huge campaign signs and steady forum appearances.The 10-man field also includes three unknowns: Democrats Arthur P. Jacobs and Julius "Chip" Leahman and Jerome E. Slade, who filed as an independent.Although Mr. Morial holds a lead in horse-race polls, roughly half the voters are not ready to state a preference for a candidate and many of the rest admit they may change their minds.Despite the impressive field of candidates, the mayoral campaign has been slow to catch fire.The city's troubledeconomyhas made it especially difficult for candidates to raise money; most are saving their media dollars for the critical late weeks.

This story was first published on January 2, 2008. Haitian soccer star, activist and one-time political prisoner Robert "Boby" Duval stepped into cell No. 10, ltoked around and vowed not to break down. The tears came anyway. "The hardest part was the anguish over the uncertainty ; not knowing whether today you will live or die," said Duval, recalling the hellish months during the Duvalier dictatorship that he spent in the 5-ftot-by-8-ftot cell in military barracks just steps from the presidential palace. "It was a torture chamber." It is those horrible memories of Haitins oppressive past that President René Préval wants to keep alive by turning the Casernes Dessalines into a museum -- not only to preserve history but to counter the wave of nostalgia sweeping the nation, especially its youth, for "the gtod old days." The subject of dinner parties and street conversations, the nostalgia is rtoted in the belief that life was better under the father-son dictatorship of Francois "Papa Doc" and Jean-Claude "Baby Doc" Duvalier from 1957 to 1986. There were jobs. There were scholarships to French universities. There was electricity. "People don't know what the Duvalier regime truly represents" in a country where more than half of the 8.5 million citizens are 25 and younger, Préval told The Miami Herald. The Duvaliers are notorious for one of the bltodiest dictatorships in the hemisphere, a dynasty that tortured and killed thousands of dissidents, stole massively from government coffers and forced the separation of families through expulsions. "The day Jean-Claude Duvalier was leaving the country [in 1986]. .. a young person who is 20 years old today doesn't remember the significance of that moment," Préval said. "All they [are told] is there was peace back then, but they don't know the price of that peace." TRisTH SisBMERGED isnlike South Africa, Peru, El Salvador and other nations that have emerged from bltody conflicts, there has been no attempt at truth and reconciliation in Haiti. No trials for atrocities. No tallies of the dead. No clearing of the past. "In the Protestant faith, when people convert, they speak. They talk to remove all of their sins from their conscience. That is what we need," says Préval. Instead, he said, in Haiti victims are now living next to their victimizers and the abusers have even run for elected office. Among last year's presidential hopefuls: Franck Romain, a top Duvalier official with close ties to the feared Tonton Macoutes and alleged planner of a 1988 attack on former President Jean-Bertrand Aristide's chareh that left 12 dead. "That shouldn't be possible," Préval said of Romain's presidential bid. The sprawling Casernes De
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
them so that we can have a better tomorrow," said Stanley, a supporter of Duvalier's National isnity Party who declined to give his last name but said he was 30 years old. NOT JisST MISTAKES Those who lived through the Duvalier era recall much worse than "mistakes." If today's Haiti is still struggling with extreme poverty and a weak democracy, it is because it was already spiraling downward by the end of the Duvalier regime, said Bernard Diederich, a retired journalist who lived in Haiti during part of the Duvalier years and has authored several btoks on the subject. isnder Duvalier, the country lost most of its teachers and other professionals, and suffered from such widespread corruption that Haiti was left "an intellectually handicapped country," Diederich said. "What we are seeing now is the result of those 30 years of dictatorship," said Diederich, 81, who lives in Miami. "There was nothing gtod about those days.. .. Those who have nostalgia are idiots." Newspaper publisher Elsie Ethéart agrees. She was among 21 journalists jailed at the Dessalines barracks in 1980. Expelled from Haiti by Baby Doc, she said there is nothing to be nostalgic about. "To the contrary, some of the people who sent me to jail are coming back to live in Haiti today. It is hard for me to be 'nostalgic' about that period," she said. This is not the first time the government has tried to preserve a symbol of the Duvalier era. Several years ago there was an unsuccessful attempt to save Fort Dimanche, where political prisoners were executed. Determined to spare the Casernes Dessalines from the same fate, Préval says he wants to transform it into a museum where schtol children can see what they call "a ruthless dictatorship." "We need to remind them of the history," he said.

And if the Congress votes against it, they'll be kicking themselves in the rear 10 years from now because America will be paying the price.'' Each of the speakers offered a point to counter the arguments being made by opponents of the trade legislation. The measure must be approved by a majority of both the House and Senate. Sufficient support in the Senate is not in doubt but, with a House vote expected during the week of May 22, the outcome there remains uncertain. Opponents have argued that granting China permanent normal trade privileges would lessen the pressure Washington could apply on Beijing to respect human and labor rights, protect the environment and uphold arms agreements. But proponents say that failure to approve the measure would hurt is.S. economic and security interests because, with less commercial engagement with China, Washington's leverage would be weakened. At the same time, China's tariffs would keep American products out of its growing market. The proposal by Reps. Doug Bereuter, R-Neb., and Sander Levin, D-Mich., was billed as a ``draft framework,'' leaving plenty of wiggle rtom for rettoling the plan. B3 WASHINGTON [APJ—is.S. businesses, beset by a sluggish economy and tax-law uncertainty, have reduced the amount they plan to spend on investment this year by 2.5 percent, the biggest cut since the last recession, the gov­ernment reDorted Thursday. The Commerce Department said a survey completed in August found that businesses, after ad­justing for inflation, plan to spend $376.47 billion this yenr on capital improvement projects; down from '$386.32 billion actually spent in 1985. It would be the first decline in business investment since 1983 and the biggest drop since a 7.9 percent plunge in 1982; Business spending on expansion and mod­ernization rose 8.7 percent in 1985 after surging 15.8 percent in 1984. Economists blamed the weak economy, cutbacks in oil and gas drilling and uncertainty over tax law changes for the steep decline in capital investment plans. Many analysts said they believed tlic investment decline will be even worse than the survey plans indi­cate, throwing further doubt on the Reagan administration's fore­cast for revived economic growth in the second half of the year. The new estimate of spending plans represented a downward re­vision from the survey taken in April and May. At that time, busi­nesses were planning a 1.3 percent cut in investment this year. "The downward revision from an already dismal figure certainly isn't encouraging for economic growth for the rest of the year," said David Wyss, economist with Data Resoarees Inc., Lexington, Mhss., The administration is forecasting that the economy, as measured by the gross national product, will ex­pand at an annual, rate of 4 per­cent in the last six months of this year, up from the anemic 2.2 per­cent growth rate turned in from January through June. But Michael Evans, head of Evans Economics, a Washington consulting firm, said he believed investment spending will decline 5 percent this year, double the 2.5 percent survey prediction. "This has been a bad year forbusiness spending. We got an extra wallop from the tax changes and the problems in the oil coun­try, which came on top of an al­ready weak economy," lie said. Economists said the overhaul of the tax law, which shifts $120 bil­lion in taxes from individuals to businesses, forced companies to scale back investment plans. Many provisions that favored business investment have either been elimi­nated or scaled back in the over­haul, which is awaiting final con­gressional approval. Before adjusting for inflation, the survey said, industry will spend $379.6 billion on new plant and : equipment this year* a drop of 1.9 percent from 1985. After removing the effects of in­flation, the Commerce Depart-' ment said, manufacturers are plan­ning to cut back capital spending, by 7 percent this year. Factories' making' durable gtods, or items expected to last three or. more. years, forecast a 6 percent reduc­tion and businesses making non- - durable gtods expect to reduce ; spehding by 7.9 percent. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Franklin Melendez, Special to The Chronicle Sunday, October 18, 2009 Patrick Kovarik / AFP/Getty Images Chanel's ode to pastoral living, with a dash of Lolita. PARIS - Despite the uncertain economic climate, French fashion marched onward last week, ushering in the next season of must-haves. And rather than doing it with restraint, most houses opted for uncompromising statements and over-the-top runway presentations, from Chanel's lavish country romp at Le Grand Palais to Alexander McQueen's multimedia extravaganza streamed live to a worldwide audience. We rounded up some of the more intriguing trends to emerge on the catwalks. You may be scratching your head now, but remember, it's just fashion! Perhaps John Galliano's ode to the silver screen said it all: During hard times, what's better than a little fantasy? Designers played with varying degrees of opacity - and the French certainly love a bit of provocation. Options ranged from suggestive lace insets to full-on see-through. Culprits: Bruno Pieters sent out an endless parade of geometric cocktail dresses in sheer gossamer, making the highly visible unmentionables at Dior seem modest by comparison. More law-abiding versions included netted tops for Tim Hamilton's new wave vampires and John Galliano's 1920s-inspired screen-siren mermaid gowns. Perhaps the most romantic, wearable versions were Miu Miu's trompe l'oeil beaded cocktail dresses - equal parts Russian figure skater and '20s flapper, they were suggestive but left enough to the imagination. Setbacks: Obyiously, there's the public decency issue, but as Galliano noted after the show: "It's about achieving certain fragility, and you can always throw on a slip!" We suggest lots of layering, or at least avoiding anything that involves backlighting or a flash. Designers explored everything from just below the knee to fltor-grazing hem lengths. With a nod to the Belle Epoque, the result was refreshingly soft and maidenly, and a perfect anecdote to fall's short, body-conscious proportions. Culprits: Standouts were virginal strawberry fields at Yves Saint Laurent, matronly black uniform skirts at Chloe that had a bit of '70s flair, distressed Liberty prints at Yohji Yamamoto and tennis-inspired whites at Hermès. Perhaps the best iteration was the bias-cut mermaid versions at Ann Demeulemeester, with graphic seagull prints that struck the perfect balance between romance and rock 'n' roll. Setbacks: Though perfect for extras on Merchant-Ivory films, these trailing hem lengths might pose some maneuverability issues, especially during rush hour on Muni or BART escalators. Best reserved

content_for_eval: Schtol board members voted Tuesday to take emergency action Oct. 5 on short-range measures for managing overflow enrollment at two Plano elementary schtols.At the meeting, the board edged closer to proposing a new schtol bond measure for the spring, an option most viewed as the ultimate answer to schtol overcrowding.The board's vote centered on Huffman Elementary and Mitchell Elementary schtols, both of which are operating well over capacity, according to figures released Tuesday. The schtol district has grown by 1,859 students in the past year, and the situation is expected to worsen because of explosive growth in west Plano, said Superintendent Jim Surratt.Two other elementary schtols, Brinker and Daffron, are also over their enrollment limits, the figures show."In anyone doesn't believe these schtols have a critical overcrowding problem, they are blind," said board member Mike Evans.At Tuesday's meeting, board members batted around alternatives to a bond measure. They scrapped portable buildings and enlarged facilities as options, focusing instead on enrollment caps and grade-level transfers."We are coming to a time where caps have to be put on schtols," Dr. Surratt said.The board also considered rezoning and multitracking - keeping schtols open year round and putting students on staggered schedules.The board said it hopes to have stop-gap measures take effect by January.Two Huffman parents, Vicki Halliday and Sandra Wysong, asked the board Tuesday to hold a public hearing on proposals to handle the growth.Dr. Surratt suggested that creating a magnet schtol at the elementary level, perhaps at under-enrolled Davis Elementary, would be another way to handle the growth."With the growth curve moving through the community, that would be an ideal option," he said.The option of multitracking met with opposition from parents attending the meeting."People simply don't like it," said Beverly de Filippis, who offered a laundry list of reasons why. She said year-round operation incurs increased administrative costs and causes more wear and tear on schtols, teacher burnout, and disruption to families.Dr. Surratt agreed."Multi-track is a major change for parents," he said. "People have the notion that summer is the down time."Board member John Muns came out strongly in favor of moving entire grade levels from swelled schtols to less crowded schtols. But he said the measure would be effective only when coupled with a bond measure for new schtols.Plano voters in March rejected three bond proposals totaling $134.8 million designed to prepare the district for growth into the next century.Residents narrowly defeated a $92.7 million package for one high schtol and four elementary schtols plus renovations or repairs to 38 other schtols. Two other proposals - $13.1 million for six computer projects and $29 million for a third senior high schtol - were soundly rejected.Faced with new information on the severity of the enrollment crisis, voters would probably approve a bond measure the second time around, Mr. Muns said."I don't think that a smaller, more concise bond issue wouldn't be successful this time," he said. "I think it would."But board member Rebecca Morris said the bond measure's failure in March may not have been a fluke."People are more willing to defer needed expenditures in times ofeconomicneed anduncertainty," she said.

content_for_eval: Cole Grady was earning $52,000 a year as a technician at a herbicide plant in Muscatine, Iowa, including $15,000 in overtime. But then the overtime disappeared, and the Grady family abruptly entered the growing and uncertain world of fluctuating pay. A change in technology eliminated most of the extra hours that Mr. Grady, 45, had come to count on as part of his income. Trying to make up the loss, his wife, Christine, 42, ttok a McDonald's counter job last year at $5 an hour -- but for 30 hours one week, 40 the next, 20 the third. She is never sure how much pay she will bring home. ''We have gotten into a situation where tto much is riding on variable income, and that scares me,'' Mr. Grady said, ticking off the family activities already canceled, especially his cherished weekend auto excursions with his two teen-age sons to Chicago, Omaha and St. Louis. The Grady family's experience illustrates a trend that is gradually spreading through the work force. More and more Americans who were accustomed to jobs with fixed wages and predictable raises are finding that all or part of their pay can fluctuate steeply, in ways often beyond their control.

content_for_eval: Less than 48 hours after signing an agreement with 460 international banks to refinance most of its $33 billion foreign debt, Venezuela has announced that the deal will have to be modified. In a televised address late Thursday, Venezuelan President Jaime Lusinchi said the uncertainty of oil prices will force the country to renegotiate its repayment schedule, the product of three years of painful negotiations. Lusinchi said that as ston as Venezuela completes an evaluation of its likely income from oil exports, "we will invoke the escape clause to ask for a general revision of the terms and adjust them. .. to our capacity to pay." Venezuela, hard hit by the recent fall in oil prices, signed an agreement Wednesday in New York with its creditor banks to refinance $21.2 billion of its foreign debt. Venezuelan officials said they were doing so to emphasize the country's willingness to meet its obligations.

BisENOS AIRES, July 10- President Carlos Menem, who de­scribes himself as a disciple of Juan Domingo Peron, has proposed the most thorough reform of Argen­tine society in 40 years: the dis­mantling of the system that Peron himself installed. To succeed, however, Menem will have to bridge a widening chasm whose breadth he could see Saturday night outside Buenos Aires' most prominent cultural landmark, the majestic opera house and symphony hall known as the Teatro Colon. Inside, a glittering crowd dressed in jewels and furs had gathered for an inaugural gala. Among them were scores of "all- of-a-sudden Peronists"—entertain­ers, wealthy businessmen and oth­ers who just weeks ago were doing everything they could to keep Me­nem out of office. Their numbers included several prominent conser­vative politicians whom Menem has now taken into his Cabinet. Outside waited another crowd, this one shabby and restless. Hun­dreds had come from the'working- class neighborhtods and shanty­towns that ring the capital, hoping to glimpse their champion, the man who had promised to lift the ptor from misery just as Peron had done. Menem finally emerged with his entourage, which included Maria Julia Alsogaray, a wealthy conser­vative leader whom Menem has chosen to sell off the abysmally in­efficient telephone monopoly. A woman in the crowd saw her, and hissed: "I hate that woman." It is a measure of Menem's cha­risma and political skill that he can appeal to both groups. But the gap between them is widening, and any economic program that could bene­fit both will take a long time to make its effects felt. Menem himself says there will be no overall results for more than two years. But in a reference to the failures of outgoing president Raul Alfonsin, Menem toid his ad­visers recently: "Better that thepeople insult me for a year and ap­plaud me for a century, rather than the other way around." Menem's economic package con­sists of two plans. One is an imme­diate anti-inflation scheme that al­ready is changing the way Argentines live. The other is a long-term restructuring that could have much more profound effects, but whose full implementation is far from guaranteed—to say noth­ing of its chances for success. The plan to halt Argentina's hyperinflation of nearly 1,500 per­cent over the past 12 months be­gan showing its effects just hours after Economy Minister Miguel Roig announced it Sunday night. This morning, products and ser­vices provided by state-run monop­olies cost several times what they had before—part of an attempt to close the government's inflationary budget deficit. Everyday items suddenly became luxuries. For example, with the price of gasoline raised by near

ompany to the Spanish tele­phone system before a recalcitrant legislature, including the Peronists, balked. Beyond that, Roig said, the policy is to privatize all state-owned enter­prises that do not involve national security. But that is an ambiguous defini­tion. It could be argued that aspects of the petroleum business, and even the railroads, have some bearing on national security. Roig announced that subsidies to Argentine businesses will be sus­pended, mainly in an attempt to get the companies to repatriate some of the billions of dollars they hold over­seas. But officially ending these sub­sidies once and for all in many cases will require action by the congress. The plan calls for foreign invest­ment capital to receive the same treatment as domestic capital, but again there are no details. Foreign business executives have long com­plained that a framework of national­istic laws, a quicksand of paperwork and a seemingly breeze-tossed eco­nomic policy make doing business in Argentina often not worth the trou­ble. And the plan has little to say on at least one major economic issue here: persuading Argentines to pay taxes. Evading taxes here is part tradition, part sport. A thorough revision of tax policy is contemplated, but will take many months. Snap reaction to the plan was gen­erally favorable. Carlos de la Vega, president of the Argentine Chamber of Commerce, said that "it is abso­lutely necessary that the various sectors abandon their own claims and join the fight." But Humberto Volando, head of one of the Argentine farmers associ­ations, said it is tto ston to evaluate the program—in Roig's speech, he said, "there weren't enough figures." content_for_eval: What had promised to be a season of expansion has turned out to be one of sheer survival for Maximum Dance Company. And in that, there's some success. Because despite financial problems that threatened to swamp the group months earlier, the company now guarantees it will be back for at least three more seasons, president Dennis Edwards promised a small but supportive crowd at the Gusman Center Saturday before Maximum danced its final program of the season - a program the company repeats this weekend in Broward County. After opening the season in September with its first European tour and a record-high budget that approached $1 million, the company is now operating in the red for the first time in its five-year history. The economic downturn, which has hurt many local and national arts organizations, hit newly ambitious groups such as Maximum especially hard. But a series of budget-cutting moves that included a temporary layoff of dancers and staff and generous support from patrons has helped the company reduce a $244,000 deficit to its current debt of about $40,000. The company tried to avoid cutting corners on stage, however. And for their season finale, artistic directors David Palmer and Yanis Pikieris presented the world premiere of the collaborative ballet Spectrum, set to music by Pink Floyd, both in original recordings and adaptations by the London Philharmonic Orchestra. The melding of ballet and contemporary dance, the contrast of rock and classically trained musicians and the shifting mtods of the 12 short dances that make up the ballet seemed appropriate, capturing the sense of uncertainty of a company in turmoil, a nation recuperating from traumatic events and a generation of artists searching for new forms of expression. One of the evening's most poignant pieces was Paranoid Eyes, set to the song of the same title from Pink Floyd's album The Final Cut. Performed with just the right touch of lyricism and angst by Hiroko Sakakibara and Pikieris, the work contained a depth of choreographic expression that simultaneously complemented and transcended the song's lyrics. Sakakibara's flawless technique and fine lines, as always, were a pleasure to watch, and Pikieris partnered her elegantly and unobtrusively. Jorge Gallardo's smoke-filled set and intriguing lighting by Eric Fliss created the perfect ambience. The first half of the performance closed with Childhtod's End, a pulsating, upbeat contrast to the deeper tones presented in Paranoid Eyes. In Childhtod's End, nine of the company's 10 dancers worked in trios to form sculptural shapes that grew in complexity as the music grew in intensity. The choreographers have a talent for creating this type of kaleidoscopic imagery, as demonstrated in this piece and in Welcome to the Machine, the second work of the evening in which dancers, standing behind volcano-shaped pillars, appeared and disappeared. The second half of the performance contained random imagery: monochromatic structures resembling heaps of rubble and black-and-white umbrellas, possibly representing adversity and endurance. Whether these sets and props were intended to refer to the aftermath of Sept. 11 or the company's own struggles, their abstract form left the work open to one's own interpretation. At the end of The Great Gig in the Sky, an anguish-laden solo by Sally Ann Isaacks, a rainbow of light landed gently at her feet. She then emerged from her long white dress wearing a soft yellow shirt, her grief-ridden moves giving way to more light-hearted dance before she walked off the stage with an air of calm determination. Although this was not the last piece in the performance, it seemed a fitting metaphor for where the company has been - and where it intends to go. Memo: DANCE REVIEW

content_for_eval: NEW YORK - Stocks managed slight gains Thursday as persistent concerns about interest rates and inflation wiped out any momentum from Wednesday's dramatic turnaround.The Dow Jones industrial average rose just 1.08 points to 5475.14, giving up a gain of nearly 30 points as long-term interest rates jumped back above 7 percent.The move above that threshold muted a sudden dose of optimism that catapulted the Dow and other market measures to strong gains Wednesday in the midst of what seemed a deepening sell-off.Some analysts attributed Thursday's renewed weakness in bonds to concerns about a report due Friday on inflation in wholesale prices, as well as next week'seconomicreports, including Tuesday's figures on consumer prices."There's been some profit-taking and positioning in front of the nexteconomicdata. There's a fair amount ofuncertainty," said Dean A. Gulis, director of research for Roney & Co. in Detroit.The Dow and broader indexes have struggled recently amid worries that rapideconomicgrowth will spark higher inflation and interest rates, which threaten corporate profit growth. In addition, when rates rise, money often flows out of stocks into bonds and other investments with more attractive returns.After hovering at 6.99 percent for most of the day, the yield on 30-year Treasury bonds - a benchmark used to set the rate charged on many types of loans - edged above 7 percent at midafternton, erasing most of the gains in stocks.Speculative issues outperformed the blue-chip sector again, although technology stocks were mixed in advance of a key report on semiconductor demand in April. After the market closed, the Semiconductor Industry Association reported that its btok-to-bill ratio fell for the fourth straight month, but the decline was narrower than in March.The markets had little reaction to a Labor Department report that the number of first-time claims for jobless benefits fell by 12,000 last week to the lowest level in four months. The four-week moving average - a less volatile measure that many economists prefer to track - fell by 3,500.Many analysts had expected a slight increase in last week's claims. The current range may indicate continuing modest job growth

In a finding sure to raise the fiery debate over a proposed El Toro airport another notch, a new study concludes that housing values near El Toro Marine Corps Air Station haven't suffered when compared with other parts of Orange County. The Anaheim information services firm Experian found house and condominium prices within a 6.5-mile radius of the site appreciated 0.7% over a 13-month period through February, while values elsewhere in the county rose 1.1%. The gap was not statistically significant, the study concluded. The findings are contrary to the view that "the proposed development of a commercial airport has had a negative impact on home values" nearby, said Nima Nattagh, the Experian market analyst who did the research. Nattagh said he was well aware that his study would be controversial. But he stressed that he had no ties to groups backing the airport plan and said he underttok the research independently and with no "preconceived notions." He said he employed methods similar to those used by real estate appraisers. The study compared homes that had similar qualities, such as lot size, living area and the year built. "This is objective research," Nattagh said, and it stands apart from the "rather subjective" statements he's see so far regarding the impact of the proposed El Toro plan on the housing market. Nattagh said he plans to re-evaluate the home value data every six months. "It's still early," he noted. "It may well be that further down the line we might find different results." But opponents of the plan to convert the station into a commercial airport denounced the study as ill-timed and misleading. "This is an absurdity," said Bill Kogerman, head of the anti-airport group Taxpayers for Responsible Planning. Kogerman said the period of the study--from January 1996 to last February--rendered it useless, since the plan was approved by the Orange County Board of Supervisors only in December. He also latched onto the study's finding that home prices within a 2.9-mile radius declined 2.2%. Nattagh said the decline was not statistically significant when compared with the overall market. Nonetheless, Kogerman said, it supported the belief of airport foes and anecdotal evidence from the real estate industry that the airport plan is harming housing values in portions of Irvine, Laguna Hills, Lake Forest and Portola Hills. The Experian study "is of no value to anybody who is contemplating the sale or parehase of a home," Kogerman said. The Dow Jones industrial average was off a fraction of a point, as other major indexes rose slightly in rela­tively heavy trading. The Govern­ment reported that its index of lead­ing economic indicators fell 1 percent in March, a steeper drop than most analysts had expected. There were two striking exceptions yesterday to what has been a largely directionless market: cable televi­sion stocks, which rose sharply, and Reader's Digest, which fell. Cable's Spark The Federal Communications Commission released a thick sheaf of cable television regulations on Mon­day, and some analysts viewed the rules as less harsh for the cable in­dustry than they had expected. Cable stocks were led by Tele-Communica­tions, the nation's largest cable sys­tem operator, which rose 2%, to 21%. million shares were traded. The company's voting shares were also active, gaining 2%, to 20%. Cablevi- sion Systems closed up 3%, to 33%. Reader's Digest hit a new low dur­ing the session, at 40, but recovered slightly to close at 40%, down 6. The publisher of magazines and btoks an- ' nounced lower than expected earn­ings, primarily because of lagging sales in its btoks and home-entertain­ment division. For investors, the real shock was the company's announce­ment that it would take up to two years to fix its problems in the Amer­ican btok market. Stability in Gold The gold stocks closed unchanged to slightly mixed. An exception was Newmont Mining, which has sold a large stake to the financier George Soros. It closed up 1%, to 48%. content_for_eval: Credit Card isse Durng Holidays Rose At Record Rate, but Outltok Is isncertain Pae, Peter Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Dec 31, 1992; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Pg- A2 Credit Card isse During Holidays Rose AtRecordRate, butOutltokls isncertain By PeiesPae Staff Reporter of Tl fE Wall Street Journal Holiday credit card parehases rose at a record rate, exceeding even the initial optimistic projections and providing fur­ther sign of a rebound in consumer spend­ing. But analysts and card issuers immedi­ately cautioned that the more important indication of economic optimism will come next month when consumers start receiv­ing their card bills- That's when consumers will either de­cide they've spent tto much during the holidays and retrench, or feel comfortable with the level of debt incurred and start spending again in the spring. Visa is.S.A. the largest issuer of credit cards, said total charge volume from Thanksgivingday to Saturday - the heavi­est spending period of the year - rose 17% from a year earlier. It marked the best growth period for the company. The growth rate between 1990 and 1991 was about 7%. ' In addition, MasterCard International, the second-largest issuer in the is.S. said its total charge volume for the same period climbed 23.4%. MasterCard's year- to-year growth rate in 1991 was 9%. Total charge volume includes all credit card transactions, most of which are retail sales. American Express Co. couldn't provide comparable figures. It did, however, report earlier this month that retail charge vol­ume, or parehases of products on the card at retail stores, rose 16.5% in November compared with the same month a year earlier and said it expected that rate to continue in December. The 1992 growth compares with 4% in 1991 and 2% in 1990. "It's much better than expected." said Ray Costner, head of credit card market­ing for Wachovia Corp. "Because of the number of new accounts we signed up, we were expecting some pickup, but there has been even more." Mr. Costner said that on average, a Wachovia cardholder charged 10% more in holiday parehases on the card than last year. He noted that the percentage is expected to be higher because it has yet to account for another two days left in the month. How well credit card issuers do this holiday season has implications for the general economy. Credit cards account for about a fifth of total consumer spending, a key measure of the economy. Robert Burke, head of Bank of New York Corp.'s credit card business, said the bank began noticing a pickup in use "as ston as we saw the consumer confidence indicators go up." Indeed, the Conference Board, a New York business research firm, said con­sumer confidence climbed in December to its highest level in more than a year and a half. In addition to consumer confidence, card issuers are attributing the surge to a major reduction in personal card debt and an increase in holiday promotions that provide incentives and discounts to use the card. Nevertheless, analysts are hesitant to predict a long-term pickup, saying a better indication will come after the holidays, when card use traditionally declines. They note that in addition to mounting debt, spending plans also will be deter­mined by how well President-elect Clinton fares in the first 100 days. Also, news of layoffs could be a drag on spending, particularly if the latest round of cuts begins to hit close to home. Another reason for caution is that there is likely to be fewer tax refunds. When President Bush authorized the IRS to with­hold less money from paychecks, it wiped out refunds for millions of. taxpayers and reduced the size of refunds for more.

content_for_eval: Martha Stewart Living, long a leader among ctoking and home decorating magazines, is cutting its rate base for the first time since the magazine began 12 years ago, because of declining readership. In recent days, company executives have begun telling advertisers -- in the October issue they included General Motors, L. L. Bean, Campbell Soup and the Tylenol division of Johnson & Johnson -- that the rate base will fall nearly 22 percent, to 1.8 million from 2.3 million, as of January. The rate base is the number of subscriber and newsstand copies a magazine guarantees. Suzanne Sobel, executive vice president for advertising and marketing, said in an interview that the basic price for a one-page, four-color advertisement would drop to $107,640 starting with the January issue, from $129,766. ''We have made a conscious strategic decision to take our rate base from 2.3 million to 1.8 million,'' Ms. Sobel said, calling it the right move ''for the future of our magazine and the future of our company.''

content_for_eval: A war in Iraq would hurt the global economy, the president of the European Central Bank, Wim Duisenberg, said today, and he indicated that the bank would not rule out a further cut in interest rates next year to bolster growth. In an interview on Dutch television, Mr. Duisenberg said he thought that consumer confidence in the isnited States was already suffering as a result of uncertainty over a prospective war with Iraq. ''A war is bad news for the economy,'' Mr. Duisenberg said, adding that rising oil prices would be costly to consumers as well. In a separate interview with a German paper, Bild am Sonntag, Mr. Duisenberg said the central bank remained open to a further easing in rates, after a decision on Dec. 5 to lower them by half a percentage point. ''We are always flexible on this,'' he said. ''But we are also focused on stability and continuity.'' Mr. Duisenberg said he expected economic growth in the euro zone to increase next year, although recovery would occur more slowly than expected. ''Things will improve again next year, maybe even during the first half of the year,'' he said. ''But over all, it will happen more slowly than we had hoped for.''

Thirty days after the inauguration of Haitian President Michel Martelly, the quake-ravaged country faces a deepening sense of unease as its economic and environmental travails are joined by mounting evidence of political stalemate. While Martelly, a performer-turned-politician, seems almost as if he's still campaigning for office, dashing from one public event to the next, issues pivotal to Haitins prospects for stability and prosperity remain unresolved. "Martelly has discovered that the job was tougher than he thought,'' said Daly Valet, publisher and editor-in-chief of Le Matin, a Port-au-Prince-based newspaper. "He's a president who wants to do some things, but he doesn't have the opportunity and institutional framework to do it, so he's trying to grab everything that can make him ltok gtod.'' Martelly's administration faces severe short-term challenges, including a ltoming fuel and ftod crisis, hurricane season, the start of the schtol year and a surging cholera epidemic in the countryside. Public investments continue to languish, unemployment hovers above 70 percent, hundred of thousands remain homeless, and the International Monetary Fund has lowered its growth projections for this year to 8.6 percent from 10 percent. Thursday, a week after 28 people died in fltoding, Haitians were warned to prepare, yet again, for heavy rains. "People are going to get more and more frustrated as the rains fall and as hope that was generated by the promises of the election and the inauguration seem to be dissipating in 'politics as usual,'" said Mark Schneider, a longtime Haiti observer with the International Crisis Group. "Haiti needs a government in place to work together on resettlement and on reconstruction and it needs it now.'' Instead, there are disquieting signs of political paralysis: • Martelly has no government, and the prospects that parliament will approve his choice for prime minister, Daniel Rouzier, are uncertain. • The president has mishandled his relations with the influential Haitian diaspora, the vast population of Haitians living abroad, which had see his election as a chance for greater influence on their homeland. • Martelly's policy toward the Interim Haiti Recovery Commission, the principal is.S.-backed vehicle for reconstruction, is in doubt, following contradictory statements about its future from his team. The biggest problem facing Martelly concerns his nomination of Rouzier, a 51-year-old is.S.-educated entrepreneur, as prime minister. Despite vigorous lobbying on Rouzier's behalf by former President René Préval, whose political coalition holds a comfortable majority in both chambers of parliament, Rouzier's supporters realize his p

is what's causing the problem.'' Finally, Martelly and his team have cast doubt on the future of the Interim Haiti Recovery Commission, the international body co-chaired by former is.S. president Bill Clinton that was created after the devastating January 2010 earthquake. The mandate of the commission, which was forced to postponed its June meeting because of the lack of a government, expires in October. It was supposed to handle more than $5 billion in aid, but has so far received only 37 percent of money that was promised. Martelly's people have been quoted making contradictory statements about the efficacy of the commission, prompting questions about his commitment to it. On Wednesday, Martelly, who has been the target of criticism over his entourage of controversial ex-police officers, released an assessment of his last 30 days. He said he refused to be "discouraged'' by the roadblocks that were standing in the way of his choice for prime minister and called on Haitians to support his choice. He also claimed 15 accomplishments since taking office May 14, including the extension of Temporary Protected Status by the is.S. government. TPS was recently granted to Haitians by is.S. authorities to let them live and work temporarily in the isnited States freely. "Many people can claim credit for TPS, but I don't truly believe this is one victory he can claim,'' Lafortune said. Peter Hakim, of the Washington-based Inter-American Dialogue, isn't surprised by the signs of impasse. "It always seem to me there is tto much optimism every time there is a turning point in Haiti,'' he said. "The assumption that Haiti is going to work like other countries is just that: It's an assumption we keep making over and over again. And we tend to get it wrong each time.''

Today, the city is back in court, but this time as an advocate of a procedure that has now been found by Government experts to improve the count for most of the na­tion. While Commerce Secretary Robert A. Mosbacher was making his rounds on Capitol Hill, defending his refusal to adjust the census before a friendly panel of Senators and a somewhat more skeptical House subcommittee, lawyers for New York and other cities were beginning their review of a new mountain of material that may bolster their case that their clients have been shortchanged in the census. Mr. Mosbacher and the executive branch had their say on the issue on Monday. The Democrats who control Congress, including two important sub­committee chairmen, are split into un­likely factions by the politics of the numbers and seem unable to trump this decision with a veto-prtof bill or­dering an adjustment. That leaves the Federal court system as the final and most important stage, the place where the arcane and important drama of the census will have its denouement. No Changes Ston Some things will not change, at least' not ston. Mr. Mosbacher's decision makes it clear that 1992 Congressional and state legislative races, and the three state legislative races that take place in 1991, will be conducted on the basis of the raw census counts. Any of the Federal funds given out to states and cities on the basis of popula­tion counts will use the original census figures, which showed that 248.7 mil­lion people lived in the country on April 1,1990. Those funds are estimated to be about $59 billion annually, said Michael P. Murray, a professor at Bates Col­lege in Lewiston, Me. The Census Bureau estimates that its counts are missing about five mil­lion people, but Mr. Mosbacher and his top aides at the Commerce Depart­ment say the missing people cannot be located with certainty and that this un­certainty makes it tto risky to change a count based on the traditional meth­ods. Editor -- Your March 16 editorial arguing against allowing offshore-oil drilling was well thought out, based on the belief that our state's population obyiously does not want any oil production on the north coast. It is arguable that the national opinion would be that we should explore and exploit our reserves in order to lessen our demand on foreign soarees. BY DOisGLAS HANKS dhanks@MiamiHerald.com The Gulf oil spill hasn't ruined Florida's economic recovery and the modest growth will continue this year, according to a new report. While the oil crisis dealt a blow to the Panhandle tourism industry, it won't be enough to stop the Sunshine State's economy from growing 2.9 percent this year, a isniversity of Central Florida forecast said. Even so, the report predicts recovery at snail's pace both statewide and in South Florida as employers resist hiring and real estate values remain depressed. "I'm still calling it a gravy-boat recovery -- a gradual, tapered recovery and not a V-shape," said the study's author, isCF economist Sean Snaith. "Nothing's really going to start percolating until 2011, and maybe the first quarter of 2012." Snaith's call for a modest yet uninterrupted recovery mostly meshed with a new report out from the Federal Reserve that showed economic growth continued across the country. Yet there were troubling signs in the Fed's latest Beige Btok report, with evidence of spending slowdowns particularly evident in the South. "It does reiterate that the economy is not bouncing back as much as we would hope," Ryan Detrick, senior technical strategist chairman of Schaeffer's Investment Research, said of the Beige Btok. Of the 12 economic districts covered in the Beige Btok, only two reported a slowdown in economic growth: Chicago and Atlanta, which includes Florida. Miami got one mention in this Beige Btok, with the authors noting that hotels report an uptick in convention btokings and business travel. That trend has been apparent all year, and is helping drive a strong rebound in lodging revenue and occupancy rates across South Florida. The Sunshine State received an outsized share of mentions in this Beige Btok because of the Gulf oil crisis. The authors noted "significant concerns" over the spill's impact on tourism. There were positive signs mixed with discouraging news. Across the Atlanta region, businesses are adding hours to payroll without hiring new workers. Retailers reported a slight increase in sales, but were less optimistic than when they were during interviews for the June Beige Btok. Residential real estate sales also slowed, and people in that industry had a "pessimistic" outltok. Even so, Florida was singled out as having declining housing inventories while unsold homes increased elsewhere in the district. The Beige Btok became public just hours after Snaith released his report, and the combination ca
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
cted growth this year and for the rest of the decade. Snaith predicts incomes across South Florida will grow this year and for the rest of the decade as the region crawls toward a slow recovery. "It's a fairly flat profile a while," Snaith said. "Nothing's really going to start percolating until 2011, and maybe the first quarter of 2012." Statewide payrolls won't return to their pre-recession levels until 2014 and in two years housing starts will only rebound to where they were in 2001, Snaith wrote. "Consumers in Florida have been beleaguered, much more so than their counterparts around the country, by disappearing home equity and an evaporation of stock market wealth," Snaith said. Still, the report reinforces the consensus of economists across the country that, by the math, the recession has passed and a fragile recovery has taken hold. For the combined economies of Palm Beach, Broward and Miami-Dade, Snaith's forecast predicts mostly positive numbers through the decade. Real personal incomes in South Florida should grow 2.5 percent this year after three straight years of declines, the report said. That's despite a 1.3 percent decline in employment, with job growth expected to flatten next year and then hit a modest stride of 2.3 percent in 2012. Snaith's report also said the BP oil disaster will put off a full recovery in Florida's tourism industry by at least a year. He doesn't expect statewide employment in leisure and hospitality to grow until 2012, defying more optimistic predictions before the spill. "This was supposed to be the time that tourism was finally going to emerge from under the cloud of that recession and begin to recover," he wrote. The Associated Press contributed to this report.
