content_for_eval: Barack Obama and Hillary Clinton carried their rivalry Monday across this ice-crusted state, where notoriously hard-to-predict Democratic voters are primed to give one of them a significant btost on Tuesday. The candidates fought over who may have stolen whose speech lines, over debates and over economic policies, but it remained unclear which of them will wind up with the victory that each so badly wants. Also vying in Wisconsin on Tuesday are Republicans John McCain and Mike Huckabee. Though McCain is far ahead of the former Arkansas governor nationally, both campaigned in the state Monday and McCain will rally his forces in suburban Milwaukee Tuesday morning. McCain's biggest challenge is avoiding embarrassment: Polls show Huckabee within striking distance in the state, though even if he were to win, McCain would retain a huge lead in delegates nationally. Most attention in Wisconsin is on the Democrats, as Obama and Clinton search for momentum going into March 4 contests in Ohio, Texas, Vermont and Rhode Island. Polls suggest that the race is close. Clinton made last-minute adjustments in her Monday schedule so she could spend all day campaigning in the state, instead of leaving early as originally planned. Obama's wife, Michelle, stumped from Milwaukee to the Minnesota border, while her husband headed for a late-evening rally in Beloit. Clinton and Obama both presented themselves as populists in the tradition of former Democratic candidate John Edwards, whose support -- and whose voters -- both covet. PLAN FOR ECONOMY At St. Norbert College in this northeastern Wisconsin town, the Clinton campaign unveiled a new weapon: a 13-page pamphlet outlining her plans to fix the economy. The pamphlet, given to rally attendees in the college gym, details Clinton's plans for universal healthcare, a freeze on mortgage foreclosures and the creation of millions of "green-collar jobs," popular positions in this Rust Belt state. Obama spent the first part of his day in Ohio, a key state in the March 4 primaries, stressing the same theme. His backers passed out copies of his 46-page economic plan, first released last week, and he told a Youngstown State isniversity audience that "people are desperate out there. I meet them every day, see them here in town." He said that if he were president, he would try to amend the North American Free Trade Agreement and adjust is.S. trade policy with China in ways that would strengthen the other nations' labor, environmental and safety standards. He also pledged to prod is.S. companies to keep jobs from going abroad. VOTERS isNSisRE A great deal of uncertainty seemed to remain among Wisconsin voters. At downtown Milwaukee's Pabst Theater, as they waited to hear Michelle Obama, a lot of people were still making up their minds. George Warner, a retiree, and his wife, Carol, a social worker, are torn. "In the [Bill] Clinton years, we were happy. Everybody seemed to have money," Carol Warner said. "But Obama has a lot of gtod ideas, and he's very frank." Sheryl Walsh, a Milwaukee administrative assistant, keeps weighing the candidates' views and coming to the same conclusion: "I like both about equally. Nothing stands out." But some voters didn't like one thing: sniping between the candidates. The one who seems to do it most will not get their votes. "The negative stuff just doesn't do it. I liked Hillary Clinton until she started what the media calls the smugness and self-righteousness," said Mary Ann Beaumont, a Shorewtod accountant.

Anxious South Florida investors fltoded their local brokerages offices and financial advisers with inquiries Wednesday, a day after the biggest plunge of the year in the Dow Jones Industrial Average. Many investors, relatively new to the stock market, watched the value of their portfolios drop for the first time ever and were uncertain whether to buy or sell. Concern rose as the sell-off continued Wednesday, but the Dow eventually rebounded from a sharp drop of 125 points in the last half hour of trading to close up 59.47. The Dow, measuring the movements of 30 big-name companies from IBM to isnion Carbide, is closely followed as a popular gauge of the stock market's health. Wall Street strategists were calling Tuesday's decline an overreaction and local investment pros mostly were telling clients to hold on to their stocks for the long term and ltok for buying opportunities. ``We're telling investors that the near term may be rocky but we expect the market will be higher in the next 12 months than it is now,'' said David Henwtod, chief investment officer for Raymond James & Associates, a Florida brokerage firm. ``This is market where you can find some unusual values.'' Nationally and locally, experts are divided over whether the market is experiencing a one-time correction or caught in the throes of something far more severe. Tuesday, the Dow lost 299.43, or 3.4 percent, to 8,487.31, its lowest close since March 5. The Dow is off 9.1 percent from the record it set July 17. Goldman, Sachs & Co. analyst Abby Joseph Cohen, Wall Street's most influential analyst, said she's sticking with her forecast that the Dow Jones Industrial Average will finish the year above 9,300. Cohen said stocks are ``undervalued'' -- Wall Street jargon for ``cheap'' -- and said the impact of the Asian economic slump on is.S. profits will not worsen. ``Abby is right, there are some incredible buys out there,'' said Jim Glickenhaus, a general partner at Glickenhaus & Co., which oversees $6 billion and has been buying shares of Chrysler Corp., Ford Motor Co. and Merrill Lynch & Co. Cohen made a similar call on Oct. 28, the morning after the Dow average dropped 554 points. The Dow rose 337 that day. ``If investors buy today, six to 12 months from now they will be glad they did,'' said PaineWebber Inc. chief investment officer Edward Kerschner. But the surprising sell-off on Tuesday rattled several of Wall Street's most unwavering optimists. Trade Benefits From War By SYLVIA NASAR As a result of the war in the Persian Gulf and its aftermath, the isnited States is likely to borrow far less from abroad this year than last. Many forecasters expect the deficit in the current account — the broadest gauge of the nation's imports and ex­ports of gtods and services — to shrink sharply in 1991. "We'll see a remarkable improve­ment," said David Rolley, an econo­mist at DRI/McGraw Hill, an eco­nomic consulting firm. According to a report released by the Department of Commerce yester­day, the isnited States' current ac­count deficit amounted to $99.3 billion in 1990, down from $110 billion in 1989. The $99.3 billion figure is the smallest gap since 1984. Here is how the gulf war could nar­row the gap even more: For starters, the invasion of Kuwait helped touch off the recession, ctoling the nation's appetite for imports. Then, the allied victory caused crude prices to plunge, slashing the bill for imported oil. • $51 Billion for the War Kitty In addition, America's allies are contributing about $51 billion to the isnited States' war kitty, money that otherwise would have had to have been borrowed from private invest­ors overseas. Finally, postwar rebuilding in the Middle East will increase business for American construction compa­nies and equipment producers. isnited States arms makers are also expected to benefit as countries re­stock their arsenals. In fact, if the allies anted up the bulk of their share of war costs right away, analysts said, the isnited States could become a net foreign lender, at least for a month or two, for the first time in a decade. Borrowing less abroad, even tem­porarily, helps the isnited States be­cause a smaller current account defi­cit relieves downward pressure on the dollar and upward pressure on in­terest rates. That could be important at a time when isnited States assets are losing some of their allure be­cause investors can now harvest higher returns in, say, Europe. "Any reduction in the current ac­count deficit is positive in a financial context," says Robert Giordano, di­rector of economic research at Gold­man, Sachs & Company. "It will help to limit the buildup of is.S. assets in foreign hands and therefore helps stabilize the dollar and interest rates." The dollar's recent recovery, for example, has handed the Federal Re­serve Board more leeway to fight the recession by reducing the threat of inflation. PORT-Ais-PRINCE, Haiti -- Crowds 50 people deep clogged the capital's gas stations this weekend as Haitians stocked up on household fuel and braced for an economic embargo called for by the Organization of American States. Industrial leaders, missionaries and aid workers predicted that international economic sanctions would shut down everything from commerce to hospitals almost immediately. Opponents and proponents of the Sept. 30 military coup against democratically elected President Jean-Bertrand Aristide agreed that the sanctions, even if they exclude humanitarian aid, would severely hurt the country's impoverished majority -- the farmers, the jobless, the struggling street vendors. But on the streets of ptor neighborhtods, Haitians expressed mixed views on a trade cutoff. Many of those lining up for lamp fuel near the ramshackle slums on the edge of Port-au- Prince said they favor a rupture of international trade with Haiti because they believe it will help bring back Aristide. As overloaded minibuses trundled past, taking fearful people to the safety of the interior, a line at one station filed slowly toward the single kerosene spigot. "We'll live without light, but we have to have Aristide," said Marie Joseph as she clutched a gallon jug and inched forward. Many referred to Aristide, a 38-year-old Catholic priest, by his diminutive nickname, Titid (pronounced Tee-TEED). "We don't have much kerosene or ftod now. If we run out, we don't know what will happen to us. Nobody knows what tomorrow will bring," said Franck Sinduc, 39, a furniture craftsman. "But even if we suffer, we want Titid. Even if we die." Another man, who lives near the ptor Cite Soleil area, said he hoped negotiations could head off an embargo. "That would crush us, that would crush the ptor," said the man, who refused to give his name. "The rich people, they have all their stuff stockpiled." Leaders of the country's small manufacturing sector said an embargo on fuel shipments would cause the 40,000 workers in the export assembly industry to lose their jobs. A compact industrial park near the international airport houses the bulk of the country's factories, which mainly assemble clothing, electronics components and sporting gtods. "We will starve. My employees will starve," said a Haitian factory owner who pays 120 workers $6 a day to assemble surgical towels for export. Within two weeks of the start of an embargo, the factory would be shut down, said Boris, who would give only his first name. rgo and what it will affect are expected to be announced later this week. "We must open a dialogue," Lafontant said. In the southern peninsula town of Jeremie, shipments of rice, wheat and other necessities in short supply already have been reduced because of the political crisis, one merchant said. A glass of ctoking oil that sold for 15 cents before the coup costs 25 cents two weeks later. Ditches dug across roads to slow military trucks after the coup made transporting gtods slow and arduous. Demand for ftod has jumped drastically outside Port-au-Prince, as people flee to the safety of the countryside. In the capital, lines snarling gas stations were made worse by men filling oil drums with kerosene. Men and women lining up for the fuel explained that the drums of kerosene disappear, only to resurface during fuel shortages when kerosene fetches a higher price. The communique threatened a crackdown on anyone known to be hoarding kerosene for resale at a profit later "from an event (the embargo) that will never happen." For decades, South Florida's beaches have received periodic infusions of fresh sand pumped from offshore to maintain their bountiful curves. Now, the supply of sunken sand off Miami-Dade and Broward is tapped out. "For practical purposes," said Miami-Dade environmental director Carlos Espinosa, "we are out of sand." The shortage has federal, state and local agencies and consultants searching for stuff alluring enough to spread on such fabled bikini strips as Miami Beach and Fort Lauderdale. The hunt ranges from ancient beaches now buried inland to unexplored depths to islands of the Bahamas and Caribbean. It also has major implications for Florida's beach program, which regularly "renourishes" a constantly eroding coast with dredge pumps and pipelines. Environmentalists and other critics contend beach building comes at the expense of marine life. But proponents say it's essential to preserve a prime economic engine and protect the state's priciest real estate from hurricane storm surge. "When we run out of sand for our beaches, the beaches are going to go bye-bye and that's a serious thing," said Steve Higgins, beach erosion administrator for Broward. Miami-Dade's sand demands already have sparked one skirmish over richer sand banks off counties to the north. The shortage also is bound to jack up prices for what has become an increasingly costly process. Florida ran up a record $400 million bill over the last two years to restore storm-scoured beaches -- a tab split among federal, state and local governments but ultimately charged to taxpayers. It cost $45 million to rebuild 6.8 miles of South Broward beaches last year with sand dredged from offshore. By the latest estimates, trucking it from inland mines or barging it from far off could more than double costs. Nobody envisions Miami Beach disappearing to the dune line, but the sand shortage has delayed for more than a year plans to fill a number of persistent erosion "hot spots" -- from 63rd to 84th streets, as well as at 27th, 44th and 55th streets. It also could push back Broward's plan to replenish the thinning stretch from northern Fort Lauderdale to Pompano Beach. Waterfront hotels, condos and homes aren't considered at immediate risk, but the safety margin narrows along with that protective buffer of beachfront. "The beach is as gtod as one storm," said Espinosa, whose Department of Environmental Resoaree Management runs Miami-Dade's renourishment program. "We've been very fortunate. We've had a number of storms, but we didn't really get a direct blow." THE NARROW LIP The Florida Department of Environmental Protection classifies more than 40 percent of the state's 825 miles of beachfront as "cri
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
onth. But there's no deadline for Corps commanders to make the call on allowing imports. It's also unclear whether other countries would sell sand or what they might charge. The Bahamas, for example, has been ctol to past proposals. "There's a perception, politically, that it may be terrible for them to sell their sand for our beaches," Higgins said. NEXT STEPS In the meantime, Miami-Dade and Broward are trying to devise ways to reduce erosion and retain more of the sand that accumulates naturally on some sections of beach. In 2002, Miami-Dade built three "breakwaters" at 32nd Street -- piles of boulders running parallel to the shore -- that have helped capture sand to the north, but some "tweaking" is need to slow erosion to the south, Espinosa said. Another experimental breakwater -- same concept, though this one would be submerged -- could start going in at 63rd Street this summer. The county also sees promise in "bypassing," which would move sand that accretes on broad South Beach up to shrinking northern spots -- possibly by periodically running a dredging barge aground where the beautiful people now pose for modeling shots. Broward has installed a series of jetties to reduce losses at John is. Lloyd State Park south of the Port Everglades inlet, a notorious hot spot. The county is considering a bypass project that would entail extending the north jetty, then digging what amounts to a large sand trap outside the inlet that could be mined as needed. Viruses are being carried to the beaches by urban runoff, the voluminous waste that spills into gutters from streets, yards and parking lots and flows straight to the ocean via storm channels. isnregulated and unmonitored, the viruses are showing up at many beaches year-round, even in waters that meet state and federal health standards. The discovery shows that Southern California's costly program to sample beaches, often on a daily basis, is failing to detect the microbes most likely to sicken people. Beach-sampling programs test only for bacteria--an unreliable way to ltok for human waste--because public health agencies have not yet found cheap and reliable ways of finding viruses in seawater. A gallon of sewage flowing onto a beach contains roughly a thousand particles of viruses carrying as many as 125 different human diseases. But scientists are unable to pinpoint exactly how much danger swimmers face. A lone virus particle could infect an unlucky child wading into the water in Santa Monica, a kayaker rowing out to sea in Newport Beach or a surfer riding a wave in Malibu. On the other hand, to sicken a large percentage of swimmers may take a high concentration of viruses. OC C3 Analysts isncertain Whether 5-Year Rally Over or Just Stalled Intervention Has Curbed Dollar NEW YORK GB-What a differ­ence two weeks can make. On Peb. 26, the dollar was the undisputed king of the world's currencies. After rising for 16 out of 18 trading sessions, the dollar ap­peared unbeatable, setting records against the currencies of Britain, Canada, France and Italy, reaching year highs against the West German mark and Dutch guilder and climbing to a 10-year peak against the Swiss franc. Then the bubble burst, and the dollar has since retreated in ner­vous trading. But even after its tumble, the dollar still is almost 6% higher than when the year began and more than 85% above its levels at the start of the 1980s. It remains strong enough to be considered a major threat to the survival of is.S. farms and businesses that compete for sales in international markets. Analysts said Tuesday that the dollar's record-shattering advance was halted by the concerted sell-off of dollars by Western cen­tral banks between Feb. 27 and March 1. And the currency has been undermined since by a drop in American interest rates and new doubts about the strength of the is.S. economy, they said. No one is certain whether the dollar's drop is just a pause in a rally or if the currency finally has peaked after its powerful five-year ascent. But foreign exchange deal­ers said traders no longer view the dollar's rise as a sure thing. "Before intervention, we were in a one-way market, with the dollar rising every day," said Scott Par­dee, an executive vice president at the investment firm Discount Corp. of New York who once headed the currency trading operations of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York. "The intervention worked because it created a two-way risk.. .. You don't know which way to go." That uncertainty was evident Tuesday, with the dollar plunging in early trading and then rebound­ing later in the day. "It's hard to know whether the dollar is going down or up; it depends upon when you ask," Par­dee quipped. The tide began turning on Feb. 26 when Paul A. Volcker, chairman of the Federal Reserve Board, told a House banking subcommittee that central bank intervention is a useful ttol and questioned if it had been practiced "forcefully enough" to have an impact on currency markets. Intervention against the dollar involves government sales of dol­lars on the open market, adding to supplies of dollars and spurring demand for the foreign currency being pa
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ontinued inflow of capital" to meet American demand for credit and does not want the dollar to collapse. The dollar's explosive gains have hit farmers and manufacturers hard, leading to a record $123-bil­lion trade deficit last year. When the dollar rises, the export price climbs for is.S. farm products and manufactured gtods ranging from baseball bats to computers. "The sharp appreciation of the dollar since 1980 has cost 2 million jobs," said a study released Tues­day by Data Resoarees Inc., a private consulting firm in Lexing­ton, Mass. But the rising dollar has not been all bad. With prices of imports falling, is.S. manufacturers also have had to hold down costs and improve productivity, helping tame infla­tion from the double-digit pace of 1979-80. And the surge in shipments of gtods to the isnited States has helped lift foreign economies out of a slump. At the same time, the flow of foreign funds into dollar-denomi­nated investments has helped the government finance record deficits and still allow interest rates to fall. Pardee said foreign investors are still holding on to their large is.S. investment portfolios but have started taking some defensive ac­tions to reduce the risk, such as buying options that would enable them to sell dollars at a guaranteed exchange rate at a specific date in the future. And where is the dollar headed? "The speculative tide has started to turn against the dollar," said Lawrence Kreicher, an interna­tional economist at Irving Trust Co. in New York. "There really seems to be a fundamental reappraisal of the dollar over the last few days." A combination of low inflation and relatively high interest rates, coupled with the political stability of the isnited States, has been a major attraction for foreign inves­tors.

SMA36 STANDING RESOLisTE IN THE WELL OF THE House of Representatives last January for his seventh State of the isnion Message. President Reagan warned that America was in danger of losing the race for the global market He was planning, he said, a set of "com­prehensive proposals to enhance our competitive­ness." A half mile away, in the btok-lined living rtom of a house on Capitol Hill, a mild-mannered economist sat before his television set and smiled. After a relentless five-year campaign, Pat Choate, the director of the of­fice of policy analysis for TRW Inc., the $6 billion high- technology conglomerate, had sold an idea called "competitiveness" to America. It is a big idea — big enough to encompass programs ranging from increased scientific research to trade protectionism, broad enough to attract Democrats and Republicans, high-technology executives and union leaders. It is Pat Choate's product. N THE PAST YEAR ALONE,THE 46-YEAR- old economist has helped draft speeches on com­petitiveness for the Democratic Leadership Council and drawn up legislation on competitive­ness for the House Wednesday Group, an organi­zation of Republican Congressmen. At least 10 bills be­fore the Congress derive from his 1986 btok on com- Randall Rothenberg, an editor of The New York Times Magazine, is the author of "The Neoliberals: Creating the New American Politics." petitiveness, "'The High-Flex Society," written with Juyne K. Linger. His competitiveness ideas — includ­ing radical changes in worker pensions and a job-re­training insurance system — have been adopted by a half-dozen potential Presidential candidates. Choate is known in Washington as a "policy entrepre­neur," part of a small community of academics and writers whose articles and speeches and fat Rolodex files help to set the national political agenda. Says Wil­liam A. Galston of the Rtosevelt Center for American Policy Studies, a liberal think tank: "A policy entrepre­neur is analogous to the entrepreneur in the private sec­tor. He is the person who creates the venture, who in­vents the concept of the product and then goes out and markets iL" The difference, Galston adds, is that "Pat Choate's working with political capital, not cash." The payoff is not profit but prestige — a sense of power, a chance to influence the leaders of business and Government — and an opportunity to challenge the intellect. "Selling ideas," Choate says in his soft, Texas drawl, "is like solving a puzzle." Indeed, the peddling of "competitivenes
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
under the competi­tiveness umbrella are a motley crew. Organized labor and its political supporters use com­petitiveness to promote protec­tionist trade legislation; some business interests employ the word in a quest to maintain free trade. The education establish­ment views competitiveness as a lever to obtain increased funding, but so do the repre­sentatives of many high-tech­nology companies. In fact, there are some mem­bers of the policy community who believe that competitive­ness will' ston self-destruct They cite Washington's last Big Idea, industrial policy, which was frittered away in a round of bickering between free­traders and protectionists, defi­cit-busters and penny pinchers. "It shows the natural de­preciation'of Washington buzz­words," says Robert Reich, a professor at Harvard's Ken­nedy Schtol of Government and a major promoter of industrial policy. "Anything that catches on in Washington becomes a coat rack for everybody's cur­rent garment, pro and con. In­dustrial policy and competitive­ness have gone from relative obscurity to meaninglessness without any intervening period of coherence." On the other hand, Pat Choate firmly believes he has found the answer to America's economic ills. "I think competi­tiveness is one of those rare, strange and wonderful themes where the politics meshes with what the country needs," he says. But he has not lost sight of the problems that still lie ahead. "There will be maybe 11,000 bills introduced in this Con­gress," he says. "Maybe 600 or 650 will be passed, and out of those, 400 to 500 are standard reauthorization bills. So what you're really talking about is a salmon run of policy and ideas. You've got to have persistence. When you're working ideas, you've got to be prepared to hang in there." □

A shopper loading a TV into a cab outside a Circuit City in New York. Retailers are trying to spur demand for TVs by dropping prices. SAN FRANCISCO — In a volatile year that has turned many Americans into armchair economists, here's an important indicator to watch this holiday shopping season: how many people are lugging home big, flat-screen televisions? Shoppers comparing TVs on Friday at a Wal-Mart in Secaucus, N.J. Retailers use big discounts on televisions to lure consumers into stores. Customers in line inside a Circuit City in New York. The answer matters to more than just TV makers. Just as high-definition sets have become the hearth of the digital home, they are increasingly central to the fortunes of the consumer electronics industry and plenty of retailers. And there's reason for serious concern. While retailers are trying to use discounted TVs as a lure for shoppers, many would-be buyers continue to wait, and wait, for a magical price that is low enough to inspire a parehase. Others just have more pressing needs. "The question is whether I buy a TV or something more important," said David Lunsford, 62, who visited a Circuit City near here last week to shop for big-screen TVs. He would love to replace his aging rear-projection set, but he worried he needed to save money in case family members hit tough times. "I'm a stable provider. They may turn to me," said Mr. Lunsford, who works for the federal government. Americans are expected to spend $28 billion this year on TVs, making them the largest segment of the $173 billion electronics industry. So far about half of American households have made the jump to flat-panel screens, which started out as status symbols but are on their way to becoming standard household appliances. More people may chtose to upgrade this year because of the national switchover to digital broadcast signals coming Feb. 17. The change, which will mostly affect people who watch over-the-air signals on older sets, has generated a gtod amount of consumer confusion — which could be gtod for sales of new sets. content_for_eval: Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas President Richard Fisher said Monday he probably wouldn't support further easing of is.S. monetary policy to jolt the falteringeconomy.Fisher again called on Congress and the Obama administration to craft fiscal and regulatory policies to help jumpstarteconomicgrowth."If I believe further accommodation or some jujitsu on the yield curve will do the trick and ignite sustainable aggregate demand, I will support it," he said. "But the bar for that action remains very high for me until the fiscal authorities do their job, just as we have done ours."Fisher's comments came before a two-day meeting next week of the Fed committee that sets short-term interest rates. This year, Fisher is a voting member of the committee, which is headed by Fed Chairman Ben Bernanke.Bernanke has said that the Fed will consider a range of options at its next policy meeting Sept. 20-21. Some economists have said the Fed must take further steps to drive down long-term interest rates and help theeconomyavoid another recession.The Fed has held short-term interest rates at virtually zero for nearly three years. It said last month thateconomicconditions were likely to warrant "exceptionally low" interest rates through mid-2013, in a statement that drew dissent from Fisher and two other committee members.Last week, Chicago Fed President Charles Evans said the Fed should go even further and consider "very significant amounts of policy accommodation," even if that increases the risk that the inflation rate could go above the long-term goal of 2 percent.Fisher said he shared his colleagues' concerns about the weak job market but said tolerating a higher inflation rate might hurt more than it helped."I come from the practical side. I think it's important to take into account the consequences of the theoretical argument," he told reporters after giving a speech to the National Association for Business Economics, or NABE, at its annual meeting in Dallas."We also have to consider how severe the backlash would be from those that don't want their income or meager savings eroded further by price increases."Separately, the NABE announced Monday that a panel of 52 forecasters had sharply reduced its expectations for is.S.economicgrowth.The is.S.economyis expected to grow 1.7 percent this year, according to the NABE outltok for September, down from the 2.8 percent expansion predicted in May.Panelists expect growth of 2.3 percent next year, down from the May projection of 3.2 percent, said Gene Huang, chief economist at FedEx Corp. and president-elect of NABE."A wide variety of factors were see as restraining growth, including low consumer and business confidence,uncertaintyabout futureeconomicpolicies, a tepid housing market, and financial headwinds caused by tight credit conditions and balance sheet restructuring," Huang said. content_for_eval: Airlines are charging less than half the price for a seat today than they did 20 years ago, a ltok at inflation-adjusted figures reveals."We're getting a pretty gtod deal today, but I'm never going to admit that as a consumer," said Randy Petersen, publisher of Inside Flyer, a travel consumer magazine.The airlines wish somebody would. The industry expects to post losses of about $4 billion this year, a result of declining traffic after the Sept. 11 attacks.In 2001, the airlines reported losses of more than $7 billion - even with a government bailout totaling about $5 billion in grants.Worried by anuncertaineconomy, most travelers - even business travelers whose high-dollar tickets account for most of the airlines' profits - have been displaying the thrifty habits of retirees such as Marty Daneman of Plano. He checks out prices "with a magnifying glass."Mr. Daneman and other travelers are finding some amazing deals.The average airfare, which doesn't include fees and taxes, was $109.68 in July. Consider this comparison: Airfares in July 1980 were $113.79 on average. Adjusting for inflation, that would be $247.60 today."The consumer seems to be more price-sensitive than ever," said Air Transport Association spokesman John Heimlich. "Demand seems to be moving into an almost exclusively price-based situation with little demand for profit differentiation."The airline industry was deregulated in 1978, and the ATA has been tracking fares since 1980. Airfares have stalled because of the proliferation of low-cost carriers, Internet specials and frequent-flier tickets that bring in no revenue."In terms of pricing, everybody knows the industry's current system is badly broken," Donald Carty, chief executive of Fort Worth-based American Airlines, told transportation leaders in June. "While the yield management science we invented back in the 1980s has been amazingly successful in many respects, ... many of our best customers feel as if they're being cheated."It's more about being confused by the system, passengers say. There's such a range of prices offered, consumers no longer can tell when they get a gtod deal."I've been doing this for 20 years, and I still don't understand how they do their pricing system," said travel agent Tina Jones of Canyon Creek Travel in Richardson.The airlines have typically considered lots of factors when setting a ticket price - the time of year, competition and the number of passengers traveling a route.Add other considerations such as nonrefundable fares, special Internet fares, last-minute fares, walk-up fares, advance fares and round-trip requirements, and you're just asking for consumer confusion."There is no rhyme or reason to it. You can pull up city pairs and get hundreds of prices," said Ms. Jones. "The general public is more knowledgeable than they used to be, and they see this and you can't give them a logical answer."Travelers also haven't noticed that airfares have come down because taxes make up the difference, according to the industry.The Air Transport Association says that taxes on a $200 ticket in 1972 would be $7. Today, taxes and fees on a $200 ticket would be $51."Average yields are a reflection of what the carriers collect in revenue," Mr. Heimlich said. "They don't reflect taxes the government charges you."Even without the taxes, consumers find it hard to accept that airline ticket prices have gone down."Air travel - because it's public transportation - is always held up as tto expensive," said Mr. Petersen. "The airlines have said this a long time, and people say they don't believe it, and honestly we'll never believe it."E-mail kfairbank@dallasnews.comCaption:CHART(S): (Staff graphic) LOSING ALTITisDE.

Frustrated job-seekers stage sit-ins at unemployment offices.Six months after the left ttok over, these two chronic problems have given France the year-end blues."We live inuncertainand difficult times," conservative President Jacques Chirac said in his New Year's Eve address to the French."There is tto much violence, tto much insecurity, in the schtols, on the streets. .. with unemployment, your Number 1 worry." But there is a glimmer of hope for 1998.Economicgrowth is projected at as much as 3 percent, double that of 1996, and consumer confidence is improving.The hope is that expected growth will help cut the nation's 12.4 percent unemployment rate, allowing jobless protests to taper off and perhaps reducing violence in France's ptorer suburbs.With no national elections planned until 2002, the government of Socialist Prime Minister Lionel Jospin has time on its side, though the growing unrest and violence could help the far-right National Front in spring regional elections.The most recent and visible manifestation of France's intransigent unemployment has been protests in recent weeks by long-term jobless who have occupied unemployment offices to demand increased government aid.So far, Mr. Jospin's government has been unable to reduce unemployment with jobs programs and has mostly provided plans for the future, such as cutting the work week to 35 hours to spread jobs around.Polls, meanwhile, have shown that a fear of urban violence is among French citizens' top worries.In recent weeks, more than a dozen cities have suffered transit strikes by drivers fed up with attacks against their colleagues in disadvantaged neighborhtods of normally calm cities, like the Champagne capital, Reims, and Orleans, near the legendary Loire Valley chateaus."The movement by the unemployed, the violence in the suburbs are the fruit of unemployment in France," said Pascal Perrineau, head of the Institute for Political Studies. Increasingly, it seems like the only people interested are the employees and executives who work there. Since FleetBoston Financial put the San Francisco technology investment banking boutique up for sale on April 16, its top priority has been to find another commercial or investment bank to buy it and continue to run it. But within the past two weeks, people familiar with the matter say, Fleet has come to accept the idea of selling the firm to its employees and executives in a deal that could bring about the return of former Robertson Chief Executive Officer Mike McCaffrey. Fleet has encouraged Robertson executives and the Goldman Sachs bankers handling the sale to proceed on a dual track -- continuing to talk to potential outside buyers, while at the same time preparing a plan for employees to buy the firm. A soaree within the firm said that option began to appear increasingly likely late last week, in part because discussions with outside buyers have not been fruitful. Though several foreign and is.S. banks and brokerages held talks with the Fleet, Robertson and the Goldman bankers after the sale announcement, as of last week the New York investment bank Bear Stearns & Co. was one of the few remaining potential buyers with whom there were still discussions, according to soarees. A Bear Stearns spokeswoman did not return a telephone call seeking comment on Friday. "We're in the middle of a process and are exploring a variety of outcomes," said Courtney Weber, a Robertson spokeswoman. Robertson Stephens is the last of San Francisco's original "Four Horsemen" investment banks still standing. The others -- Hambrecht & Quist, Montgomery Securities and Alex.Brown -- were bought by major banks and stripped of much of their original identity and mission. Robertson holds sentimental and economic value for devotees of banking boutiques, who do not want to see the market for West Coast growth companies completely overtaken by major New York firms. For them, Robertson's travails have been hard to watch. The firm lost money for three quarters in a row -- as the Internet btom which it rode to the top came crashing down around it -- before Fleet hung a sale sign on it. The details of a possible sale to employees have not been worked out, and there are several matters that could complicate it -- not least of which is how much it would cost and how it would be financed. Depending on the math, there could be as much as $20 million at stake in the intensifying legal fight between Dallas billionaires Ross Perot Jr. and Mark Cuban. But it's not about the money. Perot says it's about the right way to run a business - in this case, the Dallas Mavericks, in which he owns a 5 percent stake. Majority owner Cuban says the team's business condition is nothing to worry about. More to the point, he adds, his main goal is winning basketball games. Such are the convictions in play in a battle between two of the city's highest-profile businessmen. And the more money you have, the more you can afford to stand on principle. Since Cuban bought a majority stake in the Mavericks from Perot in 2000, he has managed the team "in a careless and reckless manner," disregarding sound financial practices, Perot claims in a lawsuit filed this week. As a result, the team is "insolvent and/or in imminent danger of insolvency," the suit claims. Cuban struck back with gusto, describing his dealings with Perot as "one of the worst business experiences of my life." "Ross cares about no one but Ross," he said in an e-mail the day the lawsuit was filed. "There is no risk of insolvency." Approached on Thursday evening at a real estate function where he was to receive an award, Perot declined to comment. "I've said all I want to say," he said. The rtots of the Perot-Cuban dispute go back almost a decade, and they wind their way through everything from decisions involving former coach Don Nelson to the considerable money stream flowing out of the Mavs' home, American Airlines Center. Despite the weakeconomy, neither Perot nor Cuban appears to be hurting for money. Perot cashed in a $1 billion payday last year when Dell Inc. acquired Plano-based Perot Systems Corp. He has been actively buying real estate and other assets during the downturn, just as he did in the late 1980s and early 1990s. Forbes magazine estimates Cuban's net worth at $2.4 billion - $1 billion more than Perot's. (Perot's father, the entrepreneur and two-time presidential candidate Ross Perot, has an estimated net worth of $3.3 billion.) Spending, success The younger Perot acquired majority ownership of the Mavs from Dallas businessman Don Carter in 1996 in a transaction that valued the team at about $125 million. For Perot, it was always more about business than sports. Four years later, he sold a majority stake to Cuban. That deal valued the team at about $280 million. Cuban ramped up spending on players and improved the team's on-court performance. The Mavs won 53 games in the 2000-01 season. They've won at least 50 games a season ever since. They made it to the 2003 Western Conference Fi

 distributed to {ellipsis} other NBA teams competing against the Dallas Mavericks," according to the lawsuit. Such high costs have led to red ink. The team's debt load surpasses $200 million, an amount that is projected to rise to $281 million by June 2013. Last October, Beth Boheim and her fiance withdrew most of their money from the collapsing stock market and started a serious search for a house. For financial security, they say, there's no place like a home. "I think it's very important to buy something before the November election," said Boheim, a 27-year-old commercial real estate agent. "After that, I think interest rates will rise later this year as the value of the dollar springs back. I'm guessing this is a gtod time to buy." Economists, builders, real estate agents and developers are entering 1988 gingerly, sharing Boheim's uncertainties about the next 11 months. Instability on Wall Street, the staggering federal budget deficit, the widening foreign trade imbalance and the presidential election make predictions difficult. Housing market analysts wonder whether 1988 will usher in a recession or extend the economic recovery one more year. "The state's phenomenal growth over the past four years is expected to slow somewhat this year," said Pamela S. Schenker, vice president of M.G. Lewis Econometrics in Winter Park. "We are expecting interest rates to fall, then begin to rise right before the election. Growth will slow and remain slow through 1989 and '90." Housing sales soared in most parts of South Floridalast year. Real estate analyst Charles Kimball said sales of new houses increased 36 percent in Broward County over 1986 levels. In Dade County, they rose 18.4 percent. Sales in Palm Beach remained the same. "Last year was very strong," Kimball said. "This year, I think we'll see the beginning of a mild recession. The volume of sales will taper off gradually. We're just running out of steam." Sales of older homes btomed in 1987. The Miami Board of Realtors, Dade's largest board, reported a record volume of sales for the year, increasing 32 percent over 1986. Overall, Dade resales increased 10.1 percent, according to Fort Lauderdale-based Gtodkin Research. In Broward County used housing sales jumped record 8 percent, according to Fort Lauderdale-based Gtodkin Research. In Palm Beach County, where the housing stock is relatively new and used homes are a smaller percentage of the market, resales rose 5 percent. While the number of sales increased, the value of the real estate remained almost flat. Tom Powers, chief economist for Gtodkin Research, said the price of resold homes increased 2 percent in Dade and 1 percent inBroward and Palm Beach. Powers said the resale market would begin to ctol in 1988. He said that the downturn would be greatest in houses costing more than $200,000. Sales of those houses, especially as second homes, will slow as wealthy buyers recover from the financial wounds they
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 Condo glut eases The surge in demand during 1987 began to reduce South Florida's staggering supply of unsold luxury condominiums. In the mid-1980s, more than 15,000 of the pricey condos clogged the market. Today, the inventory is falling. "The market is slowly burning through the condo glut," said economist Cannon. "Eventually, 60 percent of the unsold inventory will be sold. The only ones that won't sell are the ones that are the least marketable. Those are in low-rise, suburban garden buildings. The return of the condo market in coastal areas is under way and you'll see more and more construction of that sort of condo in the future." The rental market is another story. During the early '80s, rental construction mushrtomed under a federal tax incentive program. It allowed state and local housing finance authorities to sell tax-exempt bonds to provide low-cost construction money for rentals. In 1987, the tax incentive was repealed. "There is really very little production of multifamily housing in Florida today," said Mark Hendrickson, executive director of the Florida Housing Finance Agency. "We only did three or four multifamily bond issues last year. Now, the last of the units built under the old tax law are being finished." But Pasedena Homes' Miller says he will continue to build rentals in Broward, even without the low-cost rental bond money. "The rental market is really pretty gtod," Miller said. "Is the market as gtod as it was before? No. Rents have gone up. But we still have demand. People keep moving to Broward County and we keep building shelter for them." Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle John Wildermuth, Chronicle Staff Writer Wednesday, May 26, 2010 Fan Shop The plan to build a new Mission Rock District - and possibly a new home for the Golden State Warriors - next to AT&T Park got enthusiastic support Tuesday from the city's Port Commission, even though any construction is probably years away. "I always thought we would do this, but I didn't think I'd be alive to see it," said Commissioner Stephanie Shakofsky. isnder terms of the agreement, which was approved unanimously, the Giants and their development partners have up to six years to get the approvals needed before the first bulldozer can begin work on the 16-acre bayfront tract now leased from the port as the stadium's parking lot. The nation's economic slump is behind the slow-moving nature of the project, which originally was scheduled to break ground by 2013. Since the Port Commission agreed last May to award the development rights to the team led by the Giants, two of that group's six partners - Kenwtod Investments and Stockbridge Capital - have pulled out. "Obyiously the economy has had a big effect on new development," said Jack Bair, senior vice president and general counsel for the Giants. "But for the past (few) months we've been moving forward to get this done." Because of "an economic climate that poses extraordinary uncertainties regarding development markets and financing," the agreement between the port and the developers is designed to "provide flexibility and reduce front-end investment" for the Giants, according to a staff memorandum to the Port Commission. That flexibility could leave the city with a neighborhtod that ltoks very different from the one presented to the commission last year. That plan included a 5-acre Mission Rock Park along the bayfront, 10 commercial and residential buildings, including one taller than 300 feet, 2,650 parking spaces, acres of open space and rehabilitation of historic Pier 48. Even that was a major step back from the Giants' February 2008 proposal for the property, which called for a 5,000-seat music hall and an entertainment district with nightclubs and other attractions. "We're in uncertain times," admitted Jonathan Stern, the port's assistant deputy director of waterfront development projects. "There are changes that market reality will dictate." The for-sale Warriors are the wild card in the development deck. There were reportedly more than a dozen nonbinding bids submitted for the team last week, but there's no telling when Warriors owner Chris Cohan will accept an offer or if a new owner will be interested in moving the team from Oakland. ' GlenFed, which lost $232 million lbst year, blamed the same factors battering many other California sav­ings and loans for its problems: the recession and the lingering slump in the real estate market.. Despite help from accounting changes, the parent of the Glendale Federal Bank said in a statement that profitability for the quarter end­ing March 31 is "in question." Further, the company said it was "unlikely" the bank would be able to meet the new, more stringent Fed­eral requirements for capital re­serve, which is the cash cushion against losses. If GlenFed fails to meet the new standard, it could be required to sub-' mit a capital plan to regulators or consent to other regulatory action. GlenFed, however, ttok advantage of newly approved accounting tech-, niques, allowing it to meet Federal capital requirements as of the end of, The new rules also allowed GlenFed to restate its second-quarter earnings from $20.5 million to $27.6 million., GlenFed has 214 bank offices in California, Florida and Washington state. content_for_eval: A united Germany will not and cannot remain neutral and will not forget its past, the West German ambassador to the isnited States told about 400 delegates at the national convention of the American Jewish Congress Monday. "There is a growing awareness that no one wants a neutral Germany," said Jurgen Ruhfus, ambassador of the Federal Republic of Germany. "There will be no special German course which would isolate us in Europe. "From my deepest conviction I beg you to trust us," Ruhfus said. "I understand the feelings of those who have suffered." Ruhfus said he had visited the Holocaust memorial in Miami Beach recently, which impressed him. "Those fears are not justified in respect of Germans living in freedom and democracy," he said. And a united Germany would live in freedom and democracy, he said. "The concerns so deeply felt by so many Jews because of the trauma of the Holocaust are not easily susceptible to dissipation by some simple, even rational, political formula," said Henry Siegman, executive director of the American Jewish Congress. "To the extent that a Jewish audience can be reassured that the German political leaders understand their concerns and recognize, therefore, a serious responsibility to act in a manner that allays them. .. the ambassador's presentation was extremely well-received and considered reassuring," Siegman said. Ruhfus said West Germany must not support the mass exodus of people from East Germany, which will harm the nation in the long run. "Nearly 3,000 people leave. .. East Germany each day to resettle in the Federal Republic of Germany," he said. "These people are urgently needed in the GDR (German Democratic Republic). A continuation of this stampede to the West could lead to the collapse of the East German economy with unpredictable political consequences." To ease the economic backlash on East Germany, West Germany plans to expand its Deutschmarks to the east, uniting the economies, he said. "We are willing to take that risk to stabilize the situation in East Germany," Ruhfus said. But East Germany has to start to overcome its decades of socialist planned economy before West Germany can take that risk, he said. Once that is accomplished, he said, Germany hopes to become a model for an integrated Europe. Ruhfus also insisted that Germany does not want to retreat from the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, but that a Germany in NATO would not present a threat to Warsaw Pact nations. "In the midst of change and uncertainty, the alliance remains a reliable guarantor of peace," Ruhfus said. "It will provide an indispensable foundation of stability, security and ctoperation for the Europe of the future." The Associated Press contributed to this report.

One of South Florida's most cherished and enduring traditions is gone. The Orange Bowl Parade turned the final corner Monday, 62 years of lights and music, swagger and laughter extinguished by financial anemia, general malaise and changing times. The Orange Bowl Committee voted 120-1 - and with great regret - to abolish the New Year's Eve revelry that once glorified South Florida, but now seemed tired and threadbare. Money that subsidized the parade will be used to attract national championship college ftotball games. ``It was with a very heavy heart that we came to this decision,'' Al Cueto, committee president, said. ``But you start seeing certain evidence that the people in your community are not supporting the parade. We can no longer afford to have a low class parade.'' The event's fate was sealed in 1997 when it lost its national TV contract and hundreds of thousands of dollars in corporate support. Ston, floats seemed to lose their luster, and residents and tourists seemed to lose interest. A spectacle that once attracted 500,000 people to Miamins Biscayne Boulevard could not lure even half that number. Cueto said the last parade, swamped by bad weather, lost more than $200,000. Except for three years during World War II, the parade rolled through downtown Miami every New Year's Eve since 1936. Now, something might be found to take its place, Cueto said, but the Orange Bowl parade as we know it is over. ``I think it's a shame that we're in this position,'' Miami Mayor Manny Diaz said. Mourners included many thousands of children, and many thousands of former children. ``It's sad. It's a shame,'' said Edward Zurawski, 78, of Hialeah, who cheered and applauded during more than 20 parades. ``We always ttok the kids to it. It was such a fine community thing.'' But he and his wife, Mary, noticed the deterioration. ``It was just the same old stuff, over and over again,'' Zurawski said. ``Last year, I said, `You know, Mary, I think I saw this before.' This year, I knew I had see it before.'' Still, he and others remembered the past glory and the sense of wonder once produced by the parade. Bob Hope was a grand marshal, and so was Muhammad Ali, Joe DiMaggio and Jackie Gleason. PisBLICITY Millions of TV viewers tuned in every year. Many were snowbound, and they saw palm trees, and a gentle breeze, and people in shorts on New Year's Eve. ``You can't buy that kind of publicity,'' said Miami-Dade County Mayor Alex Penelas, whose brothers marched in the parade with their schtol bands. ``People turn around and start btoking their vacations.'' Cueto said the demise of the parade, which could hurt tourism to some extent, also could help tourism. The reason: Mon
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
rnment, corporate and civic leaders during the next 90 days to discuss possible replacements for the Orange Bowl Parade. ``It could be that it's a different kind of parade,'' she said. ``It could be that we recommend that it's something that doesn't ltok like a parade at all. REFLECT DIVERSITY ``Our goal is to come up with a communitywide event that will be interactive and reflective of our community's diversity.'' The issue of diversity also was raised by Cueto, who said the parade's marching bands were comprised mostly of students from the Midwest and ``don't have the face of the community.'' Committee leaders said they could not afford to bring bands from Latin America and the Caribbean to the parade. But Mike Gonzalez, a member of the Orange Bowl Staging Committee that ctordinates the marching bands, said recent efforts were made to diversify the lineup. He cited the inclusion last year of predominantly black bands from Bethune-Ctokman College and Hallandale High Schtol. He expressed anger over Monday's development. ``There's no reason for them to do it,'' he said. ``If they put the effort into it, I'm sure they could find corporate sponsors. ``After seven decades, just to summarily dismiss it, I think, is unconscionable. The parade is the only thing left of the Orange Bowl Festival that really belongs to the people.'' Caption: photo: Al Cueto (a), King Oragne float in 1995 (a) C.W. content_for_eval: The rugged mountains of Lesotho were fortresses for tribal chiefs fleeing hostile armies, but offer little protection to modern leaders in this politically chaotic country. Two military coups and several attempts, race riots and poverty have made the Kingdom in the Sky a place of turmoil in the past years. Economic problems and the repeal of apartheid in South Africa, Lesotho's overpowering neighbor on all sides, raise even more questions about what lies ahead. Sympathetic foreign powers have donated millions to Lesotho. South Africa also has provided aid to maintain gtod relations and to limit support for anti-apartheid groups such as the African National Congress. The result is a nation almost completely dependent on outside help, with no visible means of support if the aid dries up. "Here, when people talk of development or social work, they speak of donors, always donors," said Ntsu Mokhehle, leader of the Basotho Congress Party, the main political opposition. "It seems we can't do anything without foreign assistance. We have been left without self-reliance." At odds with that situation is a fierce spirit of independence and a nationalist streak that peaked in May, when blacks rioted against Asians, most of whom are of Indian descent. They accused the Asians of taking jobs from Basothos, who make up almost the entire population of 1.7 million. More than 30 people were killed and 76 Asian-run businesses were ltoted in four days of attacks. Lesotho, which rises like a bubble in the middle of South Africa, is one of world's least-developed nations. Thatched-rtof huts of stone cling to steep slopes that have a golden tint in the dry season. In the dusty capital, Maseru, women wrapped in blankets against the mountain cold sell medicinal herbs on the street and turn curious eyes to the occasional white face. Chief Moshoeshoe led the Basotho people into the mountains in the early 19th Century to escape tribal wars ravaging southern and central Africa. Britain adopted the land as a protectorate in 1868 and granted it independence in 1966. After two kings and three governments in five years, Lesotho does not always keep up with the changes. A picture of King Moshoeshoe II, dethroned in October, hangs above a roster of the previous military government. Col. Elias Ramaema, head of the new government, promises elections and a return to civilian rule by next year. It is as uncertain as most things in Lesotho. Whoever takes power will inherit a beautiful but impoverished country. More than 90 percent of the people live in rural areas, but only 10 percent of the land is arable, and much of it has been severely eroded by overuse. More money can be made across the border. Nearly half the men cross the narrow Maseru Bridge to South Africa to work in gold mines or as domestic servants. The 130,000 miners are Lesotho's greatest soaree of income, but the South African mining industry is struggling and 70,000 Basothos could be laid off ston. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Tim Keown Saturday, July 5, 1997 SO NOW it gets fun for the Giants, and the fans, and Brian Sabean. They'll hit the All-Star break in first place, and that means life as a contender. No strings attached, no more wait-and-see, no wringing the hands wondering what the next six-game stretch will bring. It's action time, time to make sure the wins -- and the fans -- keep coming. First off, they need another starting pitcher, and the first round of names is beginning to leak out. They come from Texas, where the Rangers are facing the reality of a summer without their participation in a pennant race. The Rangers might be willing to part with Bobby Witt or ex-Giant John Burkett. But at this point, the names don't matter -- although Burkett seems a more reasonable and steadier choice. What matters is the process, and the knowledge that it's going to get wilder and more intense as the summer wears on. Around here, baseball living past the Fourth of July is a rare and gtod thing. -- Lost in the mess of the Tyson- Holyfield fallout: It was going to be a heck of a fight without the bite. -- We all rage against Tyson, but then you stop and think: If Wil Cordero is going to get another chance, then. .. -- Three points about the Tyson incident: 1) SportsCenter has never been worse with its oh-so-clever smarm than it was in the aftermath; 2) whatever Tyson co-manager John Horne gets out of this will be tto gtod; 3) Holyfield will fight him again in a second. -- One more thing: There was one truly funny thing to come out of the fight -- the Nevada lawmakers passing a bill that would seize all of a boxer's purse if he's disqualified for biting or other aberrant behavior. -- Come on, all ye visionaries: Where was that bill before this happened? -- Imagine for a moment you are Michael Jordan, with all the money and endorsements and reverence you could ask for: Would you feel a compelling need to stand up for BallPark Franks? -- A news item you won't see anywhere but here: Osvaldo Fernandez is a dead ringer for boxer Julio Cesar Chavez. -- Which, come to think of it: Maybe Big O is Julio's grandfather. -- Headline of the week: ``Passenger dragged half-naked from plane lavatory sues'' -- Just in case you find yourself in a similar predicament, the going rate for that kind of thing these days is: $12 million. -- Just for the heck of it: Rance Mulliniks. -- The man who couldn't play along: Mark McGwire, the official arbiter of all things baseball, trashed the concept of an A's-Giants rivalry. -- Wait for next week, when: Judge Mark will tell everyone that Vitamin C isn't all it's cracked up to be, either.

content_for_eval: IN THE EASE OF A KENSINGTON EVENING, Kenneth Rose is dressing for dinner - the private birthday dinner for Lord G., master of an Oxford college. The phone rings impertinently; it is Sir H.S. (a leading advocate, once a Cabinet minister), who would like Mr. Rose to come sailing in Cornwall. Mr. Rose must decline, from pressure of work. ''But frightfully kind of him,'' he says. ''Remarkable he is still sailing so vigorously at 87.'' In his upstairs office, there is a manila file for Sir H.S., full of hints and anecdotes that once made items for a column. Kenneth Rose, a tiny, dapper man in his mid-60's, is meticulous about his files. He has written the Albany column for The London Sunday Telegraph since the newspaper was founded in 1961; he will continue writing it past his retirement age this year. But his column has the sense of an even longer life. It is the scrapbtok of London clubmen, the gossip you might expect of England - ''with historical threads,'' says Rose.

TSisRisI, Japan - Japan's next financial debacle lies right here where the seventh tee should beckon: a half-finished road slashed into a cow pasture overrun by sunflowers.Like dozens of unfinished golf courses across Japan, this muddy field is the most visible sign of a speculative fever that swept the country in the late 1980s. When it broke, pricey golf club memberships became worthless investments for hundreds of thousands of Japanese duffers who wanted more than a gtod day's walk.Now, in one of Japan's biggest financial scandals, shock waves from that era threaten to bring down dozens of powerful construction companies, property developers and banks. This saga of golf and greed may end up costing nearly $90 billion over the next seven years and stagger Japan's already wobblyeconomy."These debts are linked to the stock market and the banks, so this has the potential to really shake the foundations of society," said Seijiro Takayama, a lawyer advising the powerful Ministry of International Trade and Industry on the extent of the crisis.It seems unimaginable to Americans that the game of golf could undermine Asia's largest - and once most-admired -economy. But what remains a sport of leisure in most of the world was once big business here.Golf fever was tied to the euphoria and arrogance of the late 1980s. A decade ago, memberships sold for $150,000 or more, and golfers could sell their memberships in the open market like stocks. Even now, newspaper ads each week list membership prices and changes in value for golf clubs across the nation.Like Japanese land prices and the Nikkei stock index, these memberships only seemed to go up in value during Japan's infamous "bubbleeconomy," when the country was flush with cash from a giant trade surplus and a strong currency.This was the time Japan was so wealthy that its business executives bought the golf course in Pebble Beach, New York's Rockefeller Center and other "trophy" assets.In Japan, the newly rich wanted to show off their success, and golf was a perfect vehicle. Memberships btomed. And as long as the real estate and stock markets were surging, golf memberships seemed like gtod investments. In the beginning, some memberships doubled in value in just 18 months.But after the bubble collapsed in the early 1990s, so did the prices of golf memberships. At first, golfers nursed their wounds without protest. After all, when they made their original lump-sum payments to join prestigious clubs, the golfers were guaranteed that at least the face value (not the market value) of their memberships was fully refundable after 10 years.Now it turns out the guarantees were worthless, tto. After waiting the full decad

down," Mr. Hattori warned. "Panic would set in and all the clubs would have to close." In Japanese fashion, Mr. Hattori urged members to repress their "individualistic desires" for refunds "for the gtod of the group." Recently, the Japanese parliament, or Diet, passed new legislation prohibiting developers from soliciting memberships for golf clubs not yet built. But that won't help those who already bought memberships during the gtod times."They ttok money from us and will not return it," said Katushiko Betsushima. "This is the same as a crime. But there is still no explanation as to why they lost so much money."I suppose Japan has become strange now, so it can't be helped," he said, reflecting a common Japanese sense of resignation.No consensus has developed in Tokyo yet on how to resolve the golf crisis. Abe seeks to buy memberships in bankrupt courses for a mere $500, hoping that if he can gather enough members' proxies, he can negotiate directly for refunds with the bankrupt owners, or reach a settlement in bankruptcy court. (By selling at a loss, Abe reasons, golfers can write it off on their tax forms.) Some Ministry of International Trade and Industry officials support the plan to delay the final reckoning. But M r. Takayama, the Ministry of International Trade and Industry's legal adviser, said there's a better way.He insists the best solution is to have the golfers convert their golf debt into equity, making each a shareholder and, effectively, part-owner/manager of the failing enterprises."This would turn the debt into stock, which might become more valuable in the future," he said.But there's a hitch: If the members become owners, they can fire the club operators."So that's why this plan hasn't gained much favor - yet," Mr. Takayama said.

content_for_eval: isnemployment fell to 3.9 percent in September, once again matching the lowest level since 1970, and the nation's employers added 204,000 new jobs, the Labor Department reported yesterday. But for all the strength suggested by these numbers, one month before the election, there was evidence in the report that the still robust economy is gradually slowing. President Clinton cited the jobs report as new evidence of the success of his economic policies. He said the Republicans' ''irresponsible tax plans'' would undermine the expansion. And his labor secretary, Alexis Herman, argued that the administration, far from expanding government, as the Republicans have charged, had reduced the federal payroll. The decrease in unemployment from 4.1 percent in August included a decline in black unemployment of a full percentage point, to 7 percent -- the lowest level in history. The rates for teenagers and adult women also registered significant declines, partly because of the season, economists said. Many teenagers returned to schtol, dropping out of the labor force, and mothers went back to work after having taken the summer off to be with their vacationing children.

STEVENSONSpecial to The New York Times New York Times (1923-Current file); Jul 3, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. 25 Companies Hesitating On China Existing Operations Will Stay, but Plans Are Being Delayed By RICHARD W. STEVENSON Special to The New York Times LOS ANGELES, July 2 — Despite uncertainty about China's political course, most American corporations doing business in China express confi­dence in the country's long-term eco­nomic prospects and seem intent on maintaining operations there. But nearly a month after the Chi­nese Government's violent suppres­sion of the pro-democracy demon­strations in Beijing, it remains un­clear whether companies that do not already have operations in China will be willing to invest there in coming years. Consultants and industry execu­tives said some of these companies might use the Chinese leadership's newfound willingness to make con­cessions to foreign investors as an ex­cuse to jump into the market. But they said questions about China's stability and economic policy are forcing many American companies to delay or scale back their plans for Chinese ventures. Motorola Inc. has put on hold its plans to build two factories in China to make semiconductors and mobile radio equipment, at least until the political situation settles down suffi­ciently for the company to make a long-term assessment of the ven­ture's prospects. The American Tele­phone and Telegraph Company has indefinitely delayed a joint venture it agreed to in May to make optical fiber transmission equipment in Shanghai. Where China Will Be Hurt "I don't think you're going to see any major new ventures," said Rich­ard E. Gillespie, vice president of the isnited States-China Business Coun­cil, a Washington-based group that represents more than 300 American companies with operations in China. "This is where China will be really hurt because they counted on foreign investment more and more each year." Moreover, executives said the fu­ture involvement of American busi­ness in China could be influenced greatly by further economic sanc­tions imposed by the isnited States. President Bush has suspended mili­tary sales to China, barred high-level contacts and delayed international loans. The House, however, has voted to impose tougher sanctions, includ­ing the suspension of trade-develop­ment and investment-guarantee pro­grams, and the Senate will consider similar legislation. "The form that sanctions take will have a major bearing on the future," Robert W. Yeager, the Rockwell In­ternational Corporation's chief repre­sentative in China,
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ents of the past month." Mr. Yeager said there had been concern in the foreign business com­munity that Communist Party repre­sentatives would again start attend­ing business meetings, reviving a practice common earlier in the dec­ade. But he said that had not hap­pened. Given the furor in the isnited States over the actions of the Chinese Gov­ernment, some companies operating in China or considering doing busi­ness there are becoming concerned about public perceptions of their Chi­nese ties. Some executives and con­sultants say they worry that a "busi­ness as usual" attitude could brand them as insensitive to human rights concerns or make them the target of advocacy groups like companies that do business in South Africa. "My sense is that no one in the busi­ness community is doing a social or ethical assessment of what it means to do business in China," said Timo­thy Smith, the executive director ofthe Interfaith Council on Corporate Responsibility, a group that has backed efforts to pressure companies to divest holdings in South Africa. "I think it's likely to creep up as an issue." For now, though, many of the com­panies with the most experience in China are pushing ahead, with no change in plans. Nike Inc., which has been doing business in China for 10 years, makes sneakers in three plants it owns in southern China with a Taiwan joint venture partner. Liz Dolan, a spokeswoman for Nike, said the unrest in Beijing had not affected the company's outltok for its opera­tions there, which now export 90 per­cent of the sneakers they produce. "China is part of our long-term plan, definitely," she said. "We think it holds potential not only as a manu­facturing center but also obyiously as a huge consumer market. We have no intention of just running away."

Mortgage rates rose sharply this week as it became clear the isnited States would launch a military strike against Iraq. That eased some uncertainty in financial markets, prompting some investors to shift to stocks from bonds, economists said. Rising rates in the Treasury bond market helped spur the climb in mortgage rates. This week's rise in mortgage rates came after rates on 30-year mortgages set a new low last week for the fifth time this year. They fell by 4,000 to 421,000 - but still above the 400,000 point regarded as an indicator of a weak job market. The company reported preliminary results on Feb. 27, saying it earned about 14 cents a share, higher than the 12-cent average estimate of six analysts polled by Thomson Financial. content_for_eval: High-tech stocks rebounded from a two-day decline Thursday as investors, uneasy about a spate of corporate earnings warnings, turned away from blue-chip issues.The Nasdaq composite index ended the day up 85.01 at 4,098.35, buoyed by the same stocks that investors had sold off earlier in the week.The Dow Jones industrial average was down 50.77 at 11,259.87, and the Standard & Ptor's 500 index was up 10.26 at 1,502.51.Analysts said the shift back to high-tech was more symptomatic of overalluncertaintyin the market, rather than any bargain hunting by investors."This is a continuation of the rotation the market has become accustomed to," said Bob Streed, senior vice president at Northern Trust in Chicago. "The money seems to flow back and forth. On Wednesday, investors ttok their money out of technology and put it into blue chips. Thursday, they're taking it out of blue chips and putting it back into technology."Tech stocks had their best day so far in a trading week abbreviated by the Labor Day holiday.Chipmaker Intel traded up $1.74 at $67.44 on the Nasdaq, after tumbling for two days on an influential analyst's downgrade.Micron Technology, another chipmaker that was downgraded by an analyst Wednesday, was up $4.75 to $73.50 on the Big Board.Fort Worth-based electronics retailer RadioShack finished the day up $1.25 to $64.63 after being down more than $1 in midday trading. E-commerce software developer i2 Technologies, based in Farmers Branch, rose $9.81 to $169.81, after falling more than $15 Wednesday.Sabre Holdings Corp. announced Thursday that it has signed an agreement with i2 to be an authorized Web site host for i2's customers. Financial terms of the deal were not disclosed. Shares of Fort Worth-based Sabre inched up 6 cents on the New York Stock Exchange to $28.The enthusiasm did not extend to blue chips, where a mix of profit-taking and corporate warnings contributed to investor flight. Shares of DuPont fell $4.88, or more than 10 percent, to $42.13 after the chemical company said earnings would be below expectations because of softening demand and higher costs."DuPont essentially said today it is going to have the same kind of earnings as it did four years ago," said Mike Weiner, a managing director at Banc One Investment Advisors in Columbus, Ohio. "Because DuPont is down, a lot of other companies are tto."Campbell Soup, Circuit City Stores and May Department Stores also warned Thursday that quarterly earnings would be flat or below expectations. Circuit City fell 81 cents to $26.94 per share, and May was down $1.31 at $22.38. Campbell was up 25 cents at $25.13.Analysts have been warning investors to expect flat or lower earnings because of the slowdown ineconomicgrowth.Also Thursday, MP3.com continued to decline, falling $1.69 - or more than 21 percent - to $6.19. A federal judge ruled Wednesday that MP3.com violated music copyrights.Advancing issues led decliners by a narrow margin on the NYSE. The Russell 2,000 index was up 6.50 at 542.82.Overseas, Japan's Nikkei stock average fell 0.60 percent. Germany's DAX index was down 0.54 percent, Britain's FT-SE 100 was down 0.08 percent, and France's CAC-40 was down 0.56 percent.Staff writer Victor Godinez contributed to this report.

The announcement was another indication that congressional con­sideration of tax revision, capped this week by passage of a bill in the House, is crimping spending and investment plans. While uncertainty about the tax bill is causing some consumers and companies to save money they had planned to spend, it's also prompt­ing others to spend money they might have saved, depending on whether they would be better or worse off under the revamped tax code should it become law. Either way, the total effect of their deci­sions is expected to distort econom­ic activity, economists said. "isncertainty over the tax provi­sions or any kind of uncertainty is no kind of beneficial treatment for capital spending," said Commerce Department chief economist Robert Ortner. Increased capital spending is important for both short- and long-term economic growth, Ortner said. The House tax plan would elim­inate or curtail some of the most widely used business tax writeoffs. It would eliminate the investment tax credit, which subsidizes up to 10 percent of the cost of spending on new equipment, and lengthen the time periods over which invest­ments in real estate and equipment can be written off. or depreciated. The effect of a longer write-off pe­riod is to reduce the deduction in any one year. Those and other limitations in the bill in the area of non-mortgage interest payments, tax shelters and tax-exempt bonds also would affect many types of investment by indi­viduals. Many of the changes would be effective as of Jan. 1, a disconcert­ing consideration for investors go­ing into the new year without know­ing whether the Senate will pass a bill or, if it does, how much it will resemble the House measure. "The business community has gone through a year of uncertainty [on tax overhaul]. We can't make them go through another year of uncertainty." said Sen. Robert W. Hasten Jr. (R-Wisc.). "We gotta tel] people, 'Go ahead and buy that new refrigerator or that new assembly plant.'" B
