vestment tax credit, companies get caught in the middle. "It's always unfortunate to have uncertainty in the tax legislative process. But it's a fact of life," Pearlman said. His point was underscored yes­terday as Congress struggled dur­ing its late-night closing session to wrestle with another uncertainty issue: how to deal with 13 provi­sions of the current tax code that will expire at the end of this year unless they are extended. Among those provisions were the tax credit for increases in research spending, the credit for residential energy expenditures, the credit for hiring disadvantaged workers, and the tax exemptions for legal ser­vices provided by an employer and employer-paid educational ex­penses. The House was debating a proposal to extend and modify the provisions, while the Senate plan would simply extend them all for six months. The Commerce survey of busi­nesses showed that the decline in spending next year compares with a 5.6 percent increase in investment spending planned for this year. That rise is smaller than the 15.3 per­cent gain in 1984, the largest in 20 years. According to the Commerce re­port, manufacturers said their spending on new plant and equip­ment, adjusted for inflation, would drop from $68.59 billion this year to $65.83 billion next year. Spend­ing by durable-gtods industries, which make products lasting more than three years, would drop from $35.26 billion to $33.08 billion in 1986. Spending by nondurable gtods industries would fall from $33.33 billion this year to $32.75 billion in 1986, Commerce said. However, nonmanufacturing spending is projected to increase slightly, from $109.52 billion to $110.53 billion. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Reyhan Harmanci Thursday, August 21, 2008 Mike Maxwell "A Perfect Balance" is one of the paintings on display at "Human's Nature," an exhibition by San Diego artist Mike Maxwell at Gallery Three. San Diego artist Mike Maxwell doesn't equivocate on the man-versus-nature debate. Nature wins, hands down, all the time. It would be ftolish to think otherwise. "The title of the show is 'Human's Nature,' and the work in the show is about human's own naturalism, and the way we need to take ownership of it," he says. "For a long time, I think humans have separated themselves from nature, but everything we do, say, eat, build, is all natural. That's the basic premise." But Maxwell's paintings - 25 of which were made for this show - don't depict lush forests. Many of his figures evoke images of Steinbeck's "The Grapes of Wrath": migrant workers and lonely buildings devoid of people. His palette is rich with blues, greens and grays, and punctuated by red and yellow. There is a definite melancholy to these depictions of historical people and places. Gallery Three curator and owner Justin Giarla says Maxwell first approached him two years ago. "At the time in 2006, I really started to get into artists whose work was raw and edgy and lacked perfection," he says, "stuff that was more expressive and outsider underground than perfectly rendered or executed figurative work." Maxwell's homespun style fit right into that frame. Since they were first in touch, Maxwell has had two other group shows and one other solo show with Giarla, who also owns White Walls and the Shtoting Gallery. Before he paints, Maxwell says he researches a subject, usually by going to that old-fashioned repository of knowledge, the library. For this show, he was also drawing on his direct experience of paring down his lifestyle and trying to become more self-sustaining in an uncertain economy. "I like to tell people the work comes out of a personal re-education," he says. "Agriculturally, I'm trying to learn how to feed myself and become less reliant on stores, less reliant on outside resoarees, major corporations, that sort of thing." Is it working? Seems to be. "I learn from my stupid," he says. "Instead of banging my head against the wall I try and change my personal reality." Opening reception 7-11 p.m. today. Through Sept. 6. Gallery Three, 66 Sixth St., S.F. MANY Florida companies are leaner and meaner this year than last, having cut back payrolls and overhead costs. In the third quarter a year ago, the country was on the brink of war, and companies faced an uncertain marketplace. For some, this meant reduced revenues and profits. In the most recent quarter, things appear better. Despite the continuing recession, 65 percent of the 72 companies surveyed by The Miami Herald reported improved earnings -- either greater profits or reduced losses -- compared with the same quarter a year ago. As the recession deepened, many companies made drastic moves. Some offered early retirement or otherwise cut back on staff. Some sold off unprofitable divisions. The results are showing up in the latest quarter. "Many have done it (laid off employees) as a result of the recession," said Lynn Cambest, chief financial officer of Miamins SunBank. "With the failures of companies and earning performances, it's not only the lower-level workers who have lost jobs, but middle management and the higher-paid people. We haven't see that in other recessions." One of the brightest spots in South Florida's economy is in the medical-equipment and pharmaceuticals sector. Take Ivax Corp. A year ago, the Miami-based pharmaceuticals company lost more than $2 million. Since that time, the company's profits have soared. The kick-in of a worldwide distribution network and recent acquisitions have helped. Ivax has see its stock rise to more than $40 per share from $6 a year ago. "We have a strategy to build an international pharmaceutical company," said Phillip Frost, chairman and chief executive. "The two areas that can be developed within the (current) economy are efficiency and scale. We're in a period of rapid growth." All of the medical services companies in The Herald's informal survey reported profits for the quarter, and 90 percent recorded better earnings than a year ago. "The industry is basically recession prtof," said Peter G. Mintz, an analyst with Rosenkrantz Lyon and Ross in New York. "People get sick no matter how the economy is. In fact, if it's bad, they get sicker." The financial sector is another strong area now. Of the eight companies surveyed, seven turned a profit, outperforming their numbers of a year ago. JW Charles Securities improved its bottom line when it merged with JW Charles Financial Services. The combined Boca Raton-based company, which provides a wide range of financial services to individuals, businesses and municipalities, has upgraded its product line and expanded its sales force. "After the merger, we brought several new companies to the market, hired a lot of new brokers and opened new franchise offices," s
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 traditionally slow. "Crest's management has taken recent measures to streamline operations," he said. Mark Vitner, an economist with Barnett Bank, said Florida usually "sails through a recession" and generally is not affected as badly as the rest of the country. That's not the case this time. "The sectors that were hurt the most, like construction and retail, are more important to Florida than the rest of the nation," he said. One major company that benefited substantially from streamlining was FPL Group. The parent company of Florida Power & Light Co. had been dragged down in part by losses from its insurance subsidiary, Colonial Penn Group. With the sale of Colonial Penn earlier this year and a 2 percent growth in the number of its electric-power customers, FPL was able to turn a profit of $188 million this quarter. There weren't many signs of a turnaround for Florida's economy in the third quarter. Though the state's unemployment rate dropped to 7.2 percent from 8 percent, it was still higher than the nationwide rate of 6.8 percent. And while the state's growth rate generally approaches 6 percent a year, the number of jobs in Florida is off by 70,000 in the past year, half of them in Miami. Another indicator of things to come, the national Index of Leading Economic Indicators, which foreshadows the economy six to nine months down the road, fell by 1 percent in October. Vitner, the Barnett economist, said he isn't sure that falling interest rates will fuel a turnaround. "We're not out of the wtods yet. This economy has a lot of troubles," he said. "This recession is very broad." Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Kathleen Pender Sunday, August 19, 2007 The Federal Reserve poured Drano into the clogged-up credit markets on Friday. It offered to make cheaper, longer-term loans to banks and other financial institutions through its discount window. The move stothed the stock market, sending the Dow Jones industrial average up 233 points, or 1.82 percent, on Friday. That pared the Dow's loss for the week to 1.2 percent. Whether the Fed's move dissolves the real problem - a sudden reluctance by lenders to make anything but the safest loans - remains to be see. Most experts say a different drain cleaner - most likely a cut in the Fed's benchmark federal funds rate - will be necessary. Want to know more? Here are some answers to some questions about what the Fed did last week and what lies ahead. Q: What's behind the Fed's move? A: Rising delinquencies on subprime and other questionable mortgages have caused the price of mortgage-backed securities - even highly rated ones - to fall sharply. Many lenders have recently stopped taking mortgage- and other asset-backed securities as collateral for loans. This has caused a sharp slowdown in lending, especially in home mortgages but also in other credit sectors deemed risky, such as low-grade corporate bonds and some short-term commercial paper. Many lenders and other companies rely on these markets to finance their operations. Fearing that credit woes could spark a recession, investors started bailing out of stocks. "First, the credit markets got frozen, but the stock market tto was getting frozen. Therefore, normal innocent activity was not taking place. Everyone wanted to sell any asset, every asset, and jump into Treasury securities. That's not a healthy thing for the economy," says Brian Fabbri, chief economist with BNP Paribas. Q: What did the Fed do on Friday? A: One of the Fed's duties is to be lender of last resort to financial institutions in distress. One way it does this is by lending money for a very short term to banks and other deposit-taking institutions having temporary problems. The loans are made through the Fed's discount window at the discount rate. At times, the Fed will also lend to banks in severe distress at a higher rate. On Friday, the Fed cut the discount rate by half a percentage point to 5.75 percent. In a highly unusual move, it also said banks could borrow for up to 30 days and then renew their loans. The last time the Fed let banks borrow for more than a day was in 1999, when it was afraid banks could face a cash crunch as a result of Y2K computer problems.
